wildflowers.desc: California state flower. Brilliant orange cups that close at night and in overcast weather. Self-sows vigorously on disturbed ground.
wildflowers.hp: Pollen source only (no nectar). Visited by specialist mining bees (Andrena auricoma) — one of few bees that collects California Poppy pollen exclusively.

wildflowers.desc: Coastal scrub sunflower with dark brown center disk. Dominant shrubby wildflower on coastal slopes. Drought-deciduous in summer.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by Painted Lady butterflies, native sweat bees (Halictidae), and mining bees (Andrena). Important nectar for migrating butterflies. Visited by many butterfly species. Resin chewed as gum by Cahuilla. Heated leaves applied as poultice for pain by Tongva. Lesser Goldfinch eats seeds from spent flower heads. Painted Lady butterfly visits for nectar. California Gnatcatcher forages in coastal sage scrub stands.

wildflowers.desc: Silver-leaved desert shrub with bright yellow flowers. Resinous stems once used as incense by Native Americans. Desert indicator species.

fm.Color: Yellow rays, yellow disk (vs dark disk in E. californica)

wildflowers.hp: Important desert pollinator plant — silver-felted leaves reflect heat. Visited by native bees and Painted Lady butterflies. Foundation species in coastal sage scrub. Resin chewed as gum by Cahuilla. Heated leaves applied as poultice for pain by Tongva. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch. Important Costa's Hummingbird nectar in desert-coastal transition.

wildflowers.desc: Coiled scorpioid cyme of small orange-yellow flowers unfurls like a fiddlehead. Bristly-hairy stems. Abundant on disturbed grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Coiled flower clusters uncurl as flowers open (scorpioid cyme). Important early-season bee forage. Seeds contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids. Visited by mining bees (Andrena), bee flies (Bombyliidae), and sweat bees. Host plant for Painted Lady butterfly larvae. Nutlet seeds collected by harvester ants (Pogonomyrmex).

wildflowers.desc: Pink to lavender flowers with distinctive narrow-clawed petals. Woodland edges and openings. Important native pollinator plant.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native solitary bees — especially Andrena and Lasioglossum. Self-incompatible — requires cross-pollination for seed set. White-lined Sphinx Moth visits elegant Clarkia for nectar at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-lavender Clarkia blooming as other spring wildflowers fade. Cup-shaped flowers with darker center blotch.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native solitary bees (Andrena, Lasioglossum). Self-incompatible — requires insect pollination for seed set. Important late-spring nectar. Evening-opening species visited by White-lined Sphinx Moth at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Chunky annual lupine with deep blue-purple flower spikes. Succulent hollow stems. Dominant in superbloom years on slopes.

fm.Leaves: Thick, FLESHY, large — leaflets to 3 in (much larger than L. bicolor)
fm.Habitat: Clay soil grasslands, moist areas — the big fleshy lupine
fm.vs: Miniature Lupine (L. bicolor) is tiny (4-12 in), delicate, with small leaflets under 1 in

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee specialist — large flowers require strong bees to trip pollination mechanism. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly larvae. Bumble bee buzz-pollinated. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual lupine — flowers barely half an inch. Blue and white bicolored. Often carpets grasslands in spring.

fm.Habitat: Open grasslands, disturbed areas — the tiny lupine
fm.vs: Arroyo Lupine (L. succulentus) is much larger (1-2 ft) with thick fleshy leaves to 3 in

wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Painted Lady butterfly larvae. Bumble bee pollinated. Ballistic seed dispersal — twisted pods explosively eject seeds with an audible snap on hot days. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Shrubby perennial lupine of coastal scrub. Blue-purple flower spikes. Important early-succession species after fire.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds stimulated by heat/smoke; pioneer after fire

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Important bumble bee forage. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Seeds dispersed explosively. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta) caterpillars — woolly bears with orange-and-black bands — feed on bush lupine. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny golden daisy that carpets grasslands in wet springs. Among the most abundant superbloom species.
wildflowers.hp: California goldfields — primary superbloom carpet species. Visited by specialist mining bees (Andrena) and syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Horned Lark, and Lesser Goldfinch. Tongva gathered seeds for pinole flour.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy with distinctive white-tipped rays — looks hand-painted. Classic superbloom species.
wildflowers.hp: Major superbloom species. Important native bee forage during spring peak. Visited by mining bees and sweat bees during spring blooms. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple paintbrush-like annual. Hemiparasitic — draws nutrients from neighboring grass roots. Superbloom species.
wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring grass roots. Dramatic magenta bracts. Important Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly host.

wildflowers.desc: Perennial paintbrush with scarlet-tipped bracts. Hemiparasitic on nearby plant roots. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral edges.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — perennial root crown survives fire

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Pollinated by hummingbirds. Host for Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Large white trumpet flowers open at dusk, pollinated by hawkmoths. All parts extremely toxic — hallucinogenic alkaloids. Native ceremonial use.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by White-lined Sphinx Moth (hawkmoth). Sacred hallucinogen used in Tongva toloache coming-of-age ceremony. EXTREMELY TOXIC — all parts contain deadly tropane alkaloids. Not for consumption. Mexican Free-tailed Bat feeds on Tobacco Hornworm moths (Manduca) attracted to datura flowers at night.

wildflowers.desc: Stunning blue-purple flowers with long curving stamens wrapped in woolly purple fuzz. California endemic. Famous fire follower — mass blooms in chaparral 2–4 years after burns, producing spectacular displays on blackened slopes, then declining as the canopy closes back in. Obligate seeder killed by fire.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds germinate in post-fire soil. Famous fire follower — mass blooms 2–4 years after burns, then declines as chaparral closes

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees — especially bumble bees and large carpenter bees. Annual — killed by fire but seeds germinate prolifically from smoke-stimulated seed bank. Woolly blue curls — one of California's showiest native plants. Seeds require smoke compounds (karrikins) for germination.

wildflowers.desc: Giant white poppy — the largest native flower in California. Crepe-paper petals surround golden stamen boss. Fire follower.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — rhizome resprouter + smoke-germinated seed
fm.Flowers: 5–8 in across — largest native CA flower

wildflowers.hp: Pollen source for native bees (no nectar). Seeds eaten by seed-harvesting ants. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA/Baja endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Matilija poppy — Chumash used sap as pain remedy and seeds as food.

wildflowers.desc: Orange to salmon tubular flowers on sticky stems. Blooms nearly year-round in coastal areas. Hummingbird-pollinated.

fm.Habitat: Rocky slopes, road cuts, coastal bluffs

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by hummingbirds (especially Anna's Hummingbird) and native bees. Fire-adapted resprouter from root crown. Seeds germinate prolifically after fire. Host plant for Chalcedon Checkerspot and Common Buckeye butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Tall spikes of tubular scarlet flowers. Premier hummingbird magnet. Post-fire specialist in chaparral.
wildflowers.hp: Specialist hummingbird flower — tube length matches bill length of Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds. Host for Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly larvae. Tubular red flowers exclusively hummingbird-pollinated.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-violet tubular flowers in whorled clusters. Narrow leaves. Important garden native. Hummingbird and bee pollinated.
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee and hummingbird pollinated. Important foothill nectar source. Host for Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Elegant three-petaled lily of grasslands. White to lilac with dark basal spot on each petal. Catalina Island namesake.

fm.Color: White to lilac, dark basal spots

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel. Host for specialist moth larvae (Greya). Regional endemic (SoCal) — conservation value. Important spring food source.

wildflowers.desc: Large yellow three-petaled lily with hairy inner surfaces. Club-shaped hairs on petals. Chaparral and foothill specialist.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — bulb survives fire underground; often more abundant post-fire

wildflowers.hp: CNPS 4.3. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel. Host for specialist moth larvae (Greya). Bulbs harvested by Tongva and Chumash as food. Roasted or dried for storage.

wildflowers.desc: Round heads of tiny blue flowers atop wiry stems. Delicate but abundant. Classic spring wildflower of disturbed grasslands.

fm.Color: Pale blue to lavender spherical heads

wildflowers.hp: Globe gilia — dense blue flower heads attract bumble bees, mining bees (Andrena), and small butterflies including Acmon Blue. Widely used in wildflower restoration seed mixes.

wildflowers.desc: Whorled blue flower heads on wiry stems. Seeds were a staple food for indigenous Californians — highly nutritious. Desert and scrub species.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — annual; seeds germinate abundantly in post-fire ash beds

wildflowers.hp: Seeds historically a staple food of Tongva and Chumash peoples — highly nutritious. Critical hummingbird and carpenter bee (Xylocopa) nectar. Aromatic oils attract specific pollinators and deter herbivores. Fire follower — seeds germinate in post-fire ash beds.

wildflowers.desc: Iconic silver-white aromatic shrub. Sacred plant in indigenous ceremonies. White flower spikes attract many native bees. Coastal sage scrub indicator.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds germinate post-fire
fm.Cultural: Sacred in indigenous ceremony

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated primarily by native carpenter bees (Xylocopa). Critical nectar source for native bees. Fire-adapted — killed by fire but recruits heavily from soil seed bank stimulated by smoke compounds (karrikins). Sacred to Tongva and Chumash — used for ceremonial smudging and purification. Seeds (chia-like) ground into nutritious porridge. Seeds eaten by Lawrence's Goldfinch — a chaparral specialist that tracks buckwheat and sage seed crops. Wrentit forages in dense stands. Visited by Crotch bumble bee (Bombus crotchii) — a California candidate for endangered listing, declined >80%. Anna's Hummingbird visits white sage flower spikes — important nectar during chaparral bloom (Calscape).

wildflowers.desc: Dark green aromatic sage with pale blue-lavender whorled flowers. Major honey plant — "mellifera" means honey-bearing. Coastal sage scrub dominant.

fm.Color: Pale BLUE-WHITE flower whorls along stem
fm.Habitat: Dry slopes, CSS, chaparral — below 4,000 ft
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; soil seed bank germinates post-fire
fm.Ecology: Major honey source — mellifera = honey-bearing
fm.Leaves: Dark green, wrinkled, strongly aromatic — SMALLER than S. leucophylla
fm.vs: Purple Sage (S. leucophylla) has PINK-PURPLE flowers and GRAY-WHITE woolly leaves

wildflowers.hp: Major honey bee forage. Visited by native bumble bees, carpenter bees, and hummingbirds (especially Anna's Hummingbird). Obligate seeder — killed by fire, regenerates from smoke-stimulated seed bank. Foundation coastal sage scrub species. Tongva and Chumash brewed leaf tea for colds and sore throats. Seeds ground into pinole. Dried seed heads eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch (LA Audubon). Wrentit forages in dense stands. Rufous-crowned Sparrow nests at base. Orange-crowned Warbler breeds in dense sage scrub thickets, gleaning insects from foliage. Visited by declining Crotch bumble bee (Bombus crotchii).

wildflowers.desc: Intensely blue bell-shaped flowers. Desert annual that appears in wet years. One of the most vivid blues in the California flora.
wildflowers.hp: Among the best native bee forage plants in desert habitats. Attracts swarms of native bees including mining bees (Andrena), sweat bees (Halictidae), and bee flies (Bombyliidae).

wildflowers.desc: Coiled scorpioid cymes of white to lavender flowers resembling caterpillars. Bristly stems. Common post-fire colonizer.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — abundant colonizer 1–2 years post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Important post-fire bee forage — one of the first annuals to colonize burns, providing nectar when it's most needed. Major native bee forage — visited by specialist Andrena mining bees and bumble bees. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: White daisy-like flowers on coastal and rocky slopes. Blooms through summer when most other wildflowers are done.
wildflowers.hp: Coastal cliff specialist with milky latex. Cliff aster — important late-season nectar for migrating Painted Lady butterflies and native bees on coastal bluffs. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-white phlox flowers on prickly needle-leaved stems. Chaparral openings. Can bloom year-round with sufficient moisture.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — woody base resprouts after fire

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and butterflies. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery.

wildflowers.desc: Large soft-leaved sunflower of shady canyon bottoms. Distinctively large heart-shaped leaves. California endemic.
wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Canyon sunflower — one of few native Asteraceae tolerating deep shade. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Large magenta flower whorls in shady woodland understory. Powerfully fragrant foliage. Premier hummingbird plant.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — powerfully fragrant, attracting native bees as well. Rhizomatous resprouter — survives fire underground, rapidly regrows from rhizomes.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple flower clusters hugging desert sand. Key superbloom species in Antelope Valley. Fragrant in evening.
wildflowers.hp: Sand stabilizer. Pollinated by hawkmoths at dusk. Fragrant. Sand dune stabilizer. Desert sand verbena forming magenta carpets after good rains. Important Mojave superbloom species.

wildflowers.desc: Magenta trumpets opening in afternoon. Sprawling perennial of dry slopes. Hawkmoth-pollinated at dusk.
wildflowers.hp: Tuberous roots. Wishbone bush — flowers open at dusk, pollinated by hawkmoths. Tuberous perennial surviving drought underground.

wildflowers.desc: Delicate purple-blue flowers on weak scrambling stems with tiny hooked prickles. Shady slopes and stream banks in spring.
wildflowers.hp: Canyon shade specialist blooming prolifically under oaks. Visited by bumble bees, mining bees, and sweat bees. Sticky hairs on auricle-clasping leaves aid climbing. Dark-eyed Junco and Song Sparrow forage for seeds under fiesta flower stands in shady canyons. Tongva ate young greens. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Rounded clusters of blue-purple flowers atop bare stems from underground corm. One of the most common spring wildflowers in CA grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Geophyte — corm produces explosive post-fire flower displays. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

wildflowers.desc: Elegant swept-back petals like a dart in flight. Cyclamen-relative. Moist grassy slopes in spring. Goes dormant in summer.
wildflowers.hp: Shooting star — buzz-pollinated exclusively by bumble bees (Bombus) which vibrate the flower using thoracic muscles (sonication). Tongva ate the edible leaves raw as greens. Summer-dormant — completely vanishes above ground by June.

wildflowers.desc: Spectacular orange spotted lily to 6 ft. California endemic. Shady canyons. Declining from habitat loss and deer browsing.

fm.Color: Orange with maroon spots, recurved petals

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher. Largest native lily — Turk's cap flowers with maroon spots. Tongva roasted bulbs as food. Fire-adapted resprouter.

wildflowers.desc: Dense clusters of small golden flower heads. Woolly-leaved. One of the most common and persistent chaparral understory wildflowers.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown; persistent chaparral understory species

wildflowers.hp: Golden yarrow — persistent chaparral understory subshrub. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees (Halictidae), and small butterflies. Important post-fire pioneer. Tongva used flowers medicinally. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow pea flowers on sprawling green stems. Major post-fire colonizer and nitrogen fixer. Important habitat for El Segundo Blue butterfly.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer — critical post-fire pioneer

wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Marine Blue butterfly (Leptotes marina) — one of LA's smallest butterflies. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Pink five-petaled native rose of stream banks and moist slopes. Rose hips persist through winter. Important wildlife food.
wildflowers.hp: California wild rose — rose hips critical winter food for Cedar Waxwing, Hermit Thrush, and American Robin. Browsed by Mule Deer. Chumash used root bark tea medicinally. Host for Western Tussock Moth.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Spiny seed capsules and white trumpet flowers. Introduced weedy relative of Sacred Datura. All parts highly toxic.
wildflowers.hp: Seeds and leaves cause dangerous hallucinations and are potentially fatal. Pollinated by hawkmoths (Manduca) at dusk. HIGHLY TOXIC — all parts dangerous. wrightii. EXTREMELY TOXIC — ingestion causes delirium, seizures, death. Sacred hallucinogen used in Tongva toloache ceremony. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS — deadly tropane alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Purple daisy-like flowers into late fall when most wildflowers are done. Woolly leaves. Important late-season pollinator plant.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; important post-fire colonizer

wildflowers.hp: California aster — critical late-season (fall) nectar when few other native flowers bloom. Visited by bumble bees, Painted Lady butterflies, and syrphid flies. Important for migrating Monarch nectar. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Pink hibiscus-like flowers on wand-like stems emerging from chaparral. Fire follower — seeds can remain dormant in soil for decades until fire smoke and heat trigger germination. Vigorous post-fire colonizer.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower — seeds triggered by heat/smoke

wildflowers.hp: Fire follower — seeds dormant in soil for decades until heat/smoke triggers germination. CA endemic. Host for Gray Hairstreak butterfly larvae in addition to Painted Lady and West Coast Lady.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny magenta flowers that carpet wet areas in spring. Opens in sunshine, closes on cloudy days. Succulent stems.
wildflowers.hp: Indigenous food plant — seeds ground into pinole flour, young leaves eaten raw. Visited by mining bees and sweat bees.

wildflowers.desc: Vivid orange four-petaled poppy with dark basal spots. Burns and disturbed chaparral. Less common than California Poppy.

fm.Color: Vivid orange with dark basal spots
fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds stimulated by smoke/heat

wildflowers.hp: Pollen source for native bees (no nectar). Seeds eaten by seed-harvesting ants. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery.

wildflowers.desc: Dramatic cobwebby-white thistle heads with crimson-magenta flowers. Native — unlike invasive thistles. Important nectar source.

fm.Native: Yes — do not confuse with invasive thistles
fm.vs: Bull Thistle (C. vulgare, invasive) has CONTINUOUS spiny wings on stems; Cobwebby Thistle (native) has COBWEBBY hairs and no stem wings

wildflowers.hp: Critical bumble bee forage — large flower heads provide abundant nectar. Important for Painted Lady larvae. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Lavender-pink three-petaled lily. Among the showiest Calochortus. Grasslands and open woodland.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Seeds eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel. Host for specialist moth larvae (Greya).

wildflowers.desc: Dark chocolate-brown nodding bells. Unusual color for a flower. Grasslands and coastal scrub. Bulb was indigenous food source.

fm.Cultural: Bulb was Tongva food source
fm.Fire: Resprouter — deep bulb survives fire underground

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Deep bulb survives fire underground. Indigenous food source — bulbs roasted.

wildflowers.desc: Wavy-leaved rosette sending up tall stalk with small white flowers that open only in late afternoon. Bulb lathers when crushed — indigenous soap.

fm.Cultural: Bulb used as soap by indigenous peoples
fm.vs: Indian Soap Plant (Chenopodium californicum) is a goosefoot with fleshy triangular leaves — completely unrelated despite shared "soap plant" name

wildflowers.hp: Night-blooming — pollinated by hawkmoths (Manduca, Hyles). Indigenous multipurpose plant: bulb used as soap, fish poison, and food. Geophyte.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Striking crimson cone-shaped flower heads. European cover crop that escapes to roadsides. Nitrogen fixer.
wildflowers.hp: European crimson clover with dramatic flower heads. Important cover crop and bumble bee forage. Nitrogen fixer enriching agricultural soils.

wildflowers.desc: Pink to purple owl clover of grasslands. Denser flower heads than C. exserta. Hemiparasitic on grass roots.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic on grasses

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic — taps neighboring grass roots for water and nutrients while photosynthesizing independently. Visited by hummingbirds and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny blue-violet flowers on grass-like stems. Actually an iris, not a grass. Moist grasslands and openings.
wildflowers.hp: Tiny blue iris relative forming grassland carpets. Not a grass — member of Iridaceae. Important early native bee forage. Corms eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher. Mule Deer occasionally browse foliage.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow snapdragon-like flowers with red spots in throat. Stream banks and seeps. One of the most common streamside wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Creek monkeyflower with red-spotted yellow flowers. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly (Junonia coenia). Edible leaves with peppery taste. Visited by hummingbirds (Anna's Hummingbird).

wildflowers.desc: Golden plumes in fall — important late-season nectar source. Often blamed for hay fever but wind-pollinated ragweed is the real culprit.

fm.Ecology: Does NOT cause hay fever — ragweed does

wildflowers.hp: Important fall nectar source for native bees and migrating Monarchs. Does NOT cause hay fever — insect-pollinated (wind-pollinated ragweed is the real culprit). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Coastal buckwheat with ashy-gray woolly leaves and pink-white flowers. Coastal bluffs. Important for native butterflies.
wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Mormon Metalmark butterfly (Apodemia mormo). Important nectar for native bees. Obligate seeder — killed by fire, regenerates from seed bank. Seeds ground into flour by Tongva peoples.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-magenta flower clusters on prostrate stems on coastal dunes. Declining with beach development. Fragrant.
wildflowers.hp: Pink flower clusters on coastal sand dunes. Sand stabilizer. Evening-fragrant for hawkmoth pollination. Sand-adapted winged seeds dispersed by coastal winds.

wildflowers.desc: Pink to lavender pea flowers on climbing tendrils in chaparral and woodland. Pods snap open, flinging seeds.
wildflowers.hp: Climbing vine with lavender pea flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Seeds eaten by California Quail. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Climbs via tendrils. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Large purple to white iris flowers. Coastal and foothill species. Leathery strap-like evergreen leaves.
wildflowers.hp: Hybridizes freely with other native irises. Pollinated by native bees. Rhizomes contain toxic iridin — deer-resistant.

wildflowers.desc: Cream-yellow bells persist papery — whisper in the breeze. Fire follower.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower
fm.Fire: Fire follower — mass germination after fire; seeds viable decades in soil

wildflowers.hp: Persistent dry calyces rattle in wind. Fire follower appearing abundantly after burns. Fire follower. Important post-fire bee forage — visited by bumble bees and mining bees. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny orange poppy ONLY appearing after fire. Seeds wait decades in soil.

fm.Ecology: Obligate fire follower
fm.Fire: Fire follower — obligate; seeds germinate ONLY after fire

wildflowers.hp: Brilliant orange-red flowers appearing only after fire. Among the showiest fire-follower wildflowers. No opiate alkaloids. Pollen source for native bees (no nectar). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

wildflowers.desc: Bright yellow bleeding-heart flowers on tall stems in chaparral burns. Post-fire specialist appearing 1–3 years after fire. Related to Dutchman's Breeches. Tuberous root. One of the showiest fire followers.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower

wildflowers.hp: Fire follower. Deep tuberous roots survive intense burns. Re-emerges from underground. Pollen source for native bees (no nectar). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

wildflowers.desc: Golden desert sunflower carpeting Antelope Valley floors in wet springs. The defining superbloom species — millions per acre in good rain years. Annual that seeds can wait years for the right conditions.
wildflowers.hp: Major Mojave Desert carpet wildflower. Important bee forage during desert spring. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Yellow cruciform flowers blanketing hillsides. Mediterranean invasive — extremely common across LA.

fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — tall dense stands carry fire through native scrub

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Dominant invasive of coastal hillsides. Fire-follower. Visited by native bees and butterflies including Sara Orangetip.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native White to purple cruciform flowers. Escaped garden radish now invasive.
wildflowers.hp: Garden escape. Edible young pods. Hybridizes with R. raphanistrum. Very common roadside plant. Visited by native bees and butterflies including Sara Orangetip. Major roadside invasive.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Tall anise-scented plant with yellow umbels. Mediterranean invasive. Host for Anise Swallowtail.
wildflowers.hp: Host for Anise Swallowtail butterfly (invasive but ecologically important relationship).

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Succulent groundcover with magenta flowers. South African invasive smothering coastal bluffs.
wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Red and yellow nodding flowers with spurs. Hummingbird favorite. Mountain streams.
wildflowers.hp: Also visited by long-tongued bumble bees. Crimson columbine — spurred flowers precisely shaped for hummingbird (Anna's Hummingbird) pollination. Important high-elevation nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant red star-shaped flowers with fringed petals. Hummingbird-pollinated.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Hummingbird-pollinated.

wildflowers.desc: One of few native SoCal orchids. Green and purple-veined. Stream banks. Declining.
wildflowers.hp: Specialized pollination — often by specific bee or moth species. Pollinated by specialist moths attracted by fragrance. Conservation-sensitive. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flowers with yellow stamen cone. Buzz-pollinated. Mildly toxic.
wildflowers.hp: Most common native nightshade in SoCal. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees vibrating flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by California Thrasher and mockingbirds. Tobacco Hornworm (Manduca sexta) larvae feed on chaparral nightshade — large green caterpillars with horn at tail end.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white flowers on coiled stems. Popcorn-scented. Among most common spring wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Common cryptantha — visited by specialist mining bees (Andrena) and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Seeds dispersed by harvester ants (Pogonomyrmex). Among the most abundant native spring wildflowers.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tiny pink flowers. Corkscrew seeds drill into soil. Mediterranean weed but important early bee forage.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Corkscrew seed dispersal mechanism. Cal-IPC Limited invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Large white flowers opening at dusk on desert sandy flats. Petals turn pink as they wilt. Key superbloom species in Antelope Valley.
wildflowers.hp: Mojave Desert specialist with large white nocturnal flowers. Pollinated by White-lined Sphinx Moths at dusk. Seeds eaten by desert Botta's Pocket Gopher and woodrats. Important dune stabilizer. Specialist evening primrose bees (Andrena subgenus Onagrandrena) are the primary pollinators — they time emergence to evening primrose bloom. Pallid Bat gleans sphinx moth caterpillars and scorpions from desert floor near evening primrose stands. Immune to scorpion stings.

wildflowers.desc: Bright yellow daisy with woolly silver foliage. Blooms after any significant rain. Desert roadsides and washes.
wildflowers.hp: Toxic to livestock (sesquiterpene lactones). Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Scarlet-red paintbrush of desert margins and sagebrush flats. Hemiparasitic. More arid-adapted than C. affinis.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring shrub and grass roots. Pollinated by hummingbirds. More arid-adapted than coastal paintbrushes.

wildflowers.desc: Translucent cream snapdragon-like flowers with maroon spots. Desert endemic. Mimics Mentzelia flowers to lure pollinators.

fm.Ecology: Floral mimic — lures pollinators via deception

wildflowers.hp: Translucent cream flowers mimicking desert bees to attract pollinators via deception. Fascinating pollination biology. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Spectacular large pale yellow star flowers that open in late afternoon. Barbed hairs on leaves and stems stick to clothing.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees attracted to afternoon-opening flowers. Barbed hairs trap small insects — not carnivorous but creates micro-habitat for predatory mites.

wildflowers.desc: Gray-woolly paintbrush with orange-red tips. Perennial. More woolly than other paintbrushes. Chaparral and coastal scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Perennial root crown survives fire. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Nodding mottled brown-green bells. Related to Chocolate Lily but taller and more northern. Rare in LA County mountains.
wildflowers.hp: Edible but uncommon. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher. Important spring nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant scarlet-red larkspur — unusual color for the genus. Hummingbird-pollinated. Rocky chaparral slopes. Summer-dormant.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — tuberous root survives fire; more abundant post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — brilliant scarlet color rare in the genus. Tuberous root survives fire underground; more abundant post-fire. Scarlet larkspur — exclusively hummingbird-pollinated (Anna's Hummingbird). Red color invisible to most insects. TOXIC — all parts contain diterpenoid alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Tall spikes of deep blue flowers. Spring chaparral and grasslands. Toxic to livestock.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by bumble bees. Toxic to livestock — contains delphinine alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Scarlet tubular flowers on scrambling shrubby stems. Heart-shaped leaves. Premier hummingbird plant of shady canyons.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — scarlet tubular flowers on scrambling stems. Fire-adapted — resprouts from woody root crown after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Orange-gold star flowers in spherical clusters atop bare stems. From underground corm. Grasslands and open scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Bulbs were important indigenous food. Seeds eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel. Important early-season pollen source in grasslands. Geophyte — corm stores energy for explosive post-fire flowering. CA endemic.

wildflowers.desc: Flat white flower clusters with ferny aromatic foliage. Medicinal — "Achilles plant." Both native and introduced forms exist in LA.

fm.Cultural: Medicinal since antiquity — named for Achilles

wildflowers.hp: Important for beneficial insects — predatory wasps, ladybugs, hoverflies. Attracts pest-controlling insects to gardens. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Round leaf wrapping stem like a saucer. Edible — Gold Rush miners ate it to prevent scurvy. Tiny white flowers in center of disc.

fm.Color: White, tiny, in center of disc-leaf
fm.Edible: Yes — mild flavor, high in vitamin C
fm.Cultural: Gold Rush scurvy prevention

wildflowers.hp: Miner's lettuce — edible spring green, mild flavor. California gold rush miners ate it to prevent scurvy. Visited by syrphid flies and small native bees. Tongva gathered as food.

wildflowers.desc: Scarlet-orange tubes in late summer. Critical late-season hummingbird food when almost nothing else blooms.
wildflowers.hp: Critical late-season nectar for Anna's Hummingbird and migrating Rufous Hummingbird. Primary fall nectar plant for Anna's Hummingbird and Allen's Hummingbird — blooms Aug-Nov when almost nothing else flowers. Critical for Allen's pre-migration fueling. Lazuli Bunting visits California fuchsia during late-summer breeding season. White-lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) larvae — large green hornworms — feed on California fuchsia leaves.

wildflowers.desc: Dark maroon-red nodding flowers. Only native peony in SoCal. Burns, shady slopes. Dormant bulb in summer.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — deep tuberous root; fire follower — more abundant post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. California peony — one of few native peonies. Ant-dispersed seeds (myrmecochory). Summer-dormant. Underground tuber resists fire and drought.

wildflowers.desc: White to pink flower clusters on tall bare stems. Important late-season pollinator plant. Many local variants.
wildflowers.hp: Important late-season bee forage. Nectar source for blue and hairstreak butterflies. Dried seed heads provide winter bird food. Resprouter from deep taproot. Returns quickly after fire. Seeds ground into meal by indigenous peoples.

wildflowers.desc: Low yellow four-petaled flowers dotting coastal grasslands in spring. Red-spotted center. Ground-hugging.

fm.Color: Yellow with red spots at base

wildflowers.hp: Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Important early-season bee forage. Pollinated by native bees and hawkmoths at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Sky-blue bowl-shaped flowers with white center. Classic California spring wildflower. Moist grasslands and woodland edges.
wildflowers.hp: Important early-season native bee forage — visited by bumble bees, mining bees (Andrena), and sweat bees (Halictidae).

wildflowers.desc: White flower with dark purple spot at tip of each petal. Related to Baby Blue Eyes. Moist woodland openings.
wildflowers.hp: Less common than N. menziesii. Important early-season native bee forage — visited by bumble bees, mining bees, and sweat bees. Shade-tolerant. Seeds eaten by Dark-eyed Junco foraging in woodland understory.

wildflowers.desc: Tiered whorls of purple and white flowers like a pagoda. Shady slopes. One of the showiest spring annuals.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees — flowers too small for hummingbirds. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow.

wildflowers.desc: Pink four-petaled flowers with darker center. Blooms as spring fades into summer. Grasslands and open scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees including sweat bees (Halictidae) and small carpenter bees. Late-spring nectar source. Pollinated by specialist Andrena clarkiae — a bee that depends entirely on Clarkia for pollen. Evening-opening species visited by White-lined Sphinx Moth at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Slender annual lupine of desert edges and sandy washes. Blue-purple flowers. Less robust than Arroyo Lupine (L. succulentus).
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Native desert Datura with violet-throated white trumpets. Smaller than Sacred Datura. Desert washes.
wildflowers.hp: Distinguished from related species wrightii by purple throat markings and shorter stature. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Sacred hallucinogen used in Tongva toloache ceremony. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS — deadly tropane alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Primary Monarch butterfly host plant in LA. Narrow leaves, white-pink flower clusters. Critical for Monarch conservation. All milkweeds contain toxic cardiac glycosides.

fm.Ecology: Primary Monarch butterfly host plant in LA

wildflowers.hp: Primary host plant for Monarch butterfly (Danaus plexippus). Also visited by native bees, beetles, and wasps. Fiber from stems twisted into strong cordage by Tongva and Chumash. Milky latex used medicinally. Important nectar plant for Pipevine Swallowtail and Monarch butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Woolly milkweed with drooping maroon-cream flower clusters. Monarch host plant. More drought-tolerant than Narrowleaf Milkweed.

fm.Ecology: Monarch host plant

wildflowers.hp: Host for Monarch butterfly. Visited by native bees — buzz-pollinated. Stems provided fiber for cordage and nets. Latex used medicinally by Chumash.

wildflowers.desc: Tall aromatic herb of stream banks. Silver-backed leaves. Major indigenous medicinal plant — used for pain, dreams, and ceremony. Spreads by rhizomes.

fm.Cultural: Major Tongva/Chumash medicinal plant — dreams, pain relief

wildflowers.hp: Important indigenous ceremonial plant. Used for smudging. Provides hummingbird nesting material. Vigorous rhizomatous resprouter — rapidly recolonizes burned areas from underground stems. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Painful stinging hairs inject formic acid on contact. But: young leaves are edible when cooked (like spinach) and highly nutritious. Important larval host for butterflies.

fm.Edible: Young leaves edible when cooked — very nutritious
fm.Ecology: Host plant for Red Admiral butterfly
fm.Toxicity: Painful — formic acid injection on contact

wildflowers.hp: Larval host for Red Admiral and West Coast Lady butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: White snapdragon-like flowers on slender stems. Native annual of disturbed slopes. Important pollinator plant.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by large native bees that can open the snapdragon 'mouth.'.

wildflowers.desc: Dark purple-blue spikes on a leguminous shrub. Nitrogen fixer. Chaparral and woodland understory.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer (legume)

wildflowers.hp: Host plant for California Dogface butterfly (Zerene eurydice — CA state insect), Gray Hairstreak, and Northern Cloudy-wing. Nitrogen fixer. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Cream pea flowers on sprawling stems. Toxic to livestock ("locoweed" — causes neurological damage). Coastal sage scrub and grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Accumulates toxic selenium from soil. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple pea flowers on thorny stems. Only species in its genus. Fire-resprouting. Post-fire specialist.

fm.Ecology: Fire-resprouting — post-fire specialist
fm.Fire: Resprouter + fire follower — resprouts from root crown; also seed-activated

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Bright yellow flowers blanketing small shrubs in early spring when everything else is still brown. Among the first chaparral plants to bloom each year. Important early-season bee forage.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; early post-fire bloomer

wildflowers.hp: Among the first chaparral shrubs to bloom each year (Feb–Apr). Critical early-season bee forage when little else is flowering. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Pink to lavender mariposa lily of dry rocky slopes. Named for Sara Plummer Lemmon, pioneering CA botanist. Griffith Park specialty.

fm.Cultural: Named for Sara Plummer Lemmon — pioneering CA botanist

wildflowers.hp: CNPS 4.2. Named for Sara Plummer (Lemmon), pioneering CA botanist. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher. Bulbs harvested as food by Tongva peoples.

wildflowers.desc: Parasitic evergreen growing on oak branches. White berries in winter. Spread by Phainopepla bird. Traditional holiday decoration.

fm.Ecology: Parasitic on oaks — spread by Phainopepla
fm.Cultural: Traditional holiday plant

wildflowers.hp: Important winter food for many birds. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Phainopepla's primary food — this silky-flycatcher is the principal disperser of oak mistletoe, creating an obligate mutualism.

wildflowers.desc: Native dandelion-like flower of mountain meadows. Larger than introduced dandelion. Orange sap when broken.
wildflowers.hp: Important early-season bee forage in mountain meadows. Latex-producing. Visited by sweat bees, mining bees, and syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Wiry-stemmed plant with small pink flowers. Rush-skeleton appearance. Very common on dry slopes in late season.
wildflowers.hp: Late-blooming — provides fall nectar when few other wildflowers remain. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive White woolly herb with tiny white flowers in whorls. Mediterranean weed but historically important cough remedy. Very common on disturbed ground.

fm.Cultural: Traditional cough/throat remedy

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators and deter herbivores. Displaces native species — active management needed.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Tall yellow-flowered mustard blanketing LA hillsides. Even more aggressive than Hirschfeldia. Seeds produce the condiment.

fm.Cultural: Seeds are true mustard seeds
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — tall dense stands increase fire risk

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Mediterranean origin. Seeds yield mustard condiment. Visited by native bees and small butterflies including Sara Orangetip and Cabbage White. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Displaces native species — active management needed.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Dramatic spiny thistle with white-veined leaves. Mediterranean invasive. Silymarin extract used medicinally for liver health.

fm.Cultural: Silymarin extract — liver support

wildflowers.hp: Seeds contain silymarin, used in liver medicine. Important goldfinch food. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Larger-flowered relative of Filaree. Musky scent. Same corkscrew-seed trick. Very common lawn and field weed.
wildflowers.hp: Corkscrew-shaped seed awns hygroscopically drill seeds into soil. Important early-season native bee forage. Edible young leaves — mild flavor. Visited by native bees and syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Spiny yellow thistle invading grasslands and scrub. Mediterranean origin. Less aggressive than Yellow Star-thistle but still problematic.
wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Mediterranean origin. Displaces native annual grassland species. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Small pink flowers. Round "cheese wheel" seed capsules. Among the most common LA weeds. Edible leaves and immature seeds.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Host plant for Painted Lady and West Coast Lady butterflies. Edible — leaves and immature seeds.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive South American shrub-tree with yellow tubular flowers. Naturalized in washes and roadsides. All parts toxic. Hummingbird-visited.
wildflowers.hp: Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Hummingbird-pollinated. Cal-IPC Moderate invasive — displaces native vegetation. Used ceremonially by Tongva and Chumash. NOT smoked recreationally — sacred ritual context only. Tobacco Hornworm (Manduca sexta) and Tomato Hornworm (M. quinquemaculata) larvae feed on tree tobacco.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Large tropical-looking plant with palmate leaves and spiny red seed pods. Naturalized in LA washes. Seeds contain ricin — extremely toxic.

fm.Seeds: Contain ricin — among most toxic natural substances

wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and ants. Cal-IPC Limited invasive — displaces native vegetation. Castor bean — EXTREMELY TOXIC seeds contain ricin. Despite toxicity, Cooper's Hawk and other raptors nest in dense castor stands in LA River channel.

wildflowers.desc: White woolly stems with small pearly flower heads that persist when dried. Smells like maple syrup. Common post-fire species.

fm.Ecology: Post-fire colonizer

wildflowers.hp: Leaves used in traditional medicine. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds. Host plant for American Lady butterfly (Vanessa virginiensis) larvae — caterpillars build silk nests among woolly leaves. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Large yellow trumpet flowers and striped gourd fruits. Massive underground root. Native gourd — related to squash but extremely bitter.
wildflowers.hp: Huge tuberous root and sprawling vines. Visited by squash bees (Peponapis, Xenoglossa) — specialist pollinators. Gourd fruit inedible but used as soap by indigenous peoples. Extremely drought-tolerant.

wildflowers.desc: Low silver-gray dune plant. Important coastal sand stabilizer. Windblown sand collects around it forming embryo dunes.
wildflowers.hp: Bur fruits disperse on fur and clothing. Important dune stabilizer. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch, American Goldfinch, Song Sparrow, and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Striking lavender-blue flowers erupting from woolly thistle-like bracts. Desert annual. Among the most photogenic SoCal wildflowers. Tongva used seeds for eye treatment.

fm.Cultural: Tongva used seeds medicinally

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees in desert habitats. Tongva used seeds medicinally for eye treatment. Seeds nutritious — indigenous food plant. Desert annual appearing after fire or rain. Smoke-stimulated germination.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Spiny yellow thistle — California's most damaging invasive weed. Covers 15 million acres statewide. Toxic to horses (chewing disease).

fm.Toxicity: Toxic to horses — causes chewing disease
fm.Ecology: 15 million acres statewide

wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Bright yellow clover-like flowers carpeting lawns and gardens in spring. South African bulb nearly impossible to eradicate. Each plant makes 20+ tiny bulbils.
wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Extremely aggressive bulb invader from South Africa. Bulbils make eradication nearly impossible. Displaces native species — active management needed. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Native onion with papery red-skinned bulb. White-pink flowers in umbels. Edible — onion-scented. Grasslands and scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Native wild onion of rocky slopes. Pollinated by native bees. Edible — mild onion flavor. Red-skinned bulb coats (haematochiton = 'blood coat') are diagnostic. Bulbs harvested as food by Tongva and Cahuilla peoples. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tiny prostrate weed of sidewalk cracks and gardens. White milky sap. Among the most ubiquitous urban weeds in LA.
wildflowers.hp: Prostrate mat with milky sap. Tiny flowers visited by small sweat bees and ants.

wildflowers.desc: Golden daisies on woolly stems. More montane than Golden Yarrow. Mountain meadows and openings.
wildflowers.hp: Silvery foliage tolerates extreme drought. Important pollinator resource on dry rocky slopes. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Slender plant with tiny blue-purple snapdragon flowers. Sandy soil. Easy to overlook but common in the right habitat.
wildflowers.hp: Easily overlooked but common in sandy grasslands. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds. Several species are host plants for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Tall yellow flowers opening at dusk, wilting by morning. Hawkmoth-pollinated. Stream banks and moist meadows. Biennial.
wildflowers.hp: Night-pollinated by hawkmoths. Biennial with large rosette first year. Pollinated by specialist evening primrose bees (Andrena subgenus Onagrandrena) that have evolved in lockstep with Oenothera.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native White-pink trumpet flowers on aggressive creeping vine. European invasive nearly impossible to eradicate. Deep perennial roots.

fm.Root: Deep perennial — nearly impossible to eradicate

wildflowers.hp: Field bindweed — extremely persistent weed with deep roots (20+ ft). Nearly impossible to eradicate. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Morning glory species provide hummingbird nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Pink mallow flowers with five dark maroon basal spots. Mojave Desert annual after good rain years. Distinctive and photogenic.

fm.Color: Pink with 5 dark maroon spots at base

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees. Host plant for Painted Lady and West Coast Lady butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy with white-tipped rays — similar to L. platyglossa but more inland. Antelope Valley grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Fremont's tidytips — distinguished from L. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant scarlet tubular flowers at stream banks. Hummingbird specialist. Much showier than yellow Creek Monkeyflower. Mountain streams.
wildflowers.hp: Scarlet monkeyflower of mountain streams. Exclusively hummingbird-pollinated (Anna's and Allen's Hummingbird) — red color invisible to most insects. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Delicate maidenhair-like foliage with dangling stamens — no petals. Wind-pollinated. Shady mountain stream banks.
wildflowers.hp: Meadow rue — unusual wind-pollinated wildflower with pendant stamens. Dark-eyed Junco eats seeds. Provides shade for Pacific Tree Frog in mountain streamside habitat.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant red tubular flowers on tall spikes. Hummingbird pollinated. Stream banks — very rare in LA County, at southern range limit.

fm.Habitat: Stream banks — very rare in LA County

wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — brilliant red tubular flowers. Very rare in LA County — at extreme southern range limit.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow violet with purple-veined lower petals. Mountain forest understory and meadows. Sweet fragrance.

fm.Color: Yellow with purple veins on lower petals

wildflowers.hp: Host for fritillary butterflies. Seeds dispersed by ants (myrmecochory).

wildflowers.desc: Intensely aromatic purple flower heads. Mountain trails. Crushed leaves release powerful mint-oregano fragrance. Tea plant.

fm.Cultural: Traditional tea plant

wildflowers.hp: Traditional indigenous remedy for colds. Visited by hummingbirds, carpenter bees, and bumble bees. Aromatic oils attract pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy of high alpine talus and scree above treeline. Among the highest-elevation wildflowers in LA County. San Gabriel Mountains peaks.

fm.Habitat: Alpine talus above treeline — Mt. Baldy, Baden-Powell

wildflowers.hp: Important high-elevation pollinator resource. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Bright orange cruciform flower clusters on mountain slopes. Vibrant color at mid-elevation after snowmelt. Biennial.
wildflowers.hp: Mountain specialist with brilliant orange-yellow flowers. Host plant for Sara Orangetip butterfly (Anthocharis sara). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Fire follower.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Fleshy beach plant with small lavender cruciform flowers. European invasive now dominant on LA beaches. Edible — mustard family.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Edible — yes — peppery mustard flavor.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple trumpets on dune-binding vine with kidney-shaped leaves. Important sand stabilizer. Declining with coastal development.

fm.Ecology: Critical dune stabilizer

wildflowers.hp: Important dune stabilizer. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Morning glory funnels attract hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white-pink mallow flowers on prostrate silvery stems. Alkaline soils and disturbed ground. Extremely common but overlooked.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees. Host for Common Checkered Skipper butterfly larvae. Alkaline soil specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Low gray woolly plant of dry grasslands. Sticky star-shaped leaves. Seeds eaten by doves and quail — hence "turkey" mullein.
wildflowers.hp: Seeds avidly eaten by Mourning Dove and California Quail ('dove weed'). Visited by small native bees. Traditionally used as fish stupefacient.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flower heads with strong minty fragrance. Common on dry slopes. Tea plant — excellent fresh or dried.
wildflowers.hp: Important bee forage in summer months. Visited by hummingbirds, carpenter bees, and bumble bees. Aromatic oils attract pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: White papery flower heads that persist dried for months. Mountain openings. Important host for Painted Lady and American Lady butterflies.

fm.Ecology: Host for Painted Lady and American Lady butterflies

wildflowers.hp: Host for Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) and American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly larvae. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny three-toned flowers — violet ring, yellow throat, dark center eye. Classic California spring annual. Grasslands.

fm.Color: Violet ring, yellow throat, dark eye

wildflowers.hp: Bird's-eye gilia — tricolored petals (violet, yellow, purple). Pollinated by mining bees (Andrena) and small carpenter bees (Ceratina). Grassland specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny pink flowers in spiny involucres on wiry stems. Very common on dry slopes but easily overlooked. Annual.
wildflowers.hp: Fringed involucral bracts. Sandy coastal and foothill habitats. Important for native seed-harvesting ants. Important nectar for butterflies including Acmon Blue and Mormon Metalmark.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple flowers with yellow stamen cone. Shrubby nightshade of woodland edges. Buzz-pollinated by native bees.
wildflowers.hp: Blue witch — native nightshade of coastal sage scrub. Buzz-pollinated by carpenter bees and bumble bees. Berry fruit eaten by Cedar Waxwing and American Robin.

wildflowers.desc: Small yellow daisies on extremely sticky stems. Dominant late-summer annual on dry grasslands. Pungent tar-like smell.

fm.Ecology: Dominant late-summer grassland annual

wildflowers.hp: Fascicled tarplant — sticky resinous annual of grasslands. Important late-season native bee forage. Tarry scent. Visited by sweat bees and small native bees attracted to tarry-scented glands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tubular red flowers on nearly leafless green stems. Major desert hummingbird plant. "Chuparosa" = hummingbird in Spanish. Desert washes.

fm.Cultural: Chuparosa = hummingbird (Spanish)

wildflowers.hp: Major desert hummingbird plant — critical nectar source in desert washes where few other flowers bloom. 'Chuparosa' = hummingbird in Spanish. Tubular red flowers precisely match Costa's bill length. Greater Roadrunner shelters and hunts lizards in dense chuparosa thickets.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow fall-blooming shrub of coastal slopes. Important late-season nectar when most other plants are done. Aromatic resinous foliage.
wildflowers.hp: Critical late-season nectar source (Aug–Nov). Important for native bees and migrating butterflies when most plants have finished. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple mint family flowers with wrinkled hairy leaves. Shady woodland understory. Doesn't sting like true nettles despite the name.

fm.Note: Does NOT sting — not a true nettle

wildflowers.hp: Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators and deter herbivores.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flower clusters with carrot-like foliage. Early spring bloomer in grasslands. Important indigenous food plant — roots edible.

fm.Cultural: Roots edible — indigenous food plant

wildflowers.hp: Edible roots — important indigenous food plant. Early spring nectar source. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Host plant for Anise Swallowtail butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Wild ancestor of cultivated sunflower. Large yellow heads tracking the sun (heliotropism in young plants). Roadsides and disturbed areas. Native.

fm.Color: Yellow rays, dark disk — classic sunflower
fm.Ecology: Important bird food

wildflowers.hp: Major bee and bird plant. Visited by 50+ native bee species. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and American Goldfinch, Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, and sunflower specialist bees (Diadasia).

wildflowers.desc: Tall sticky-hairy plant with yellow flowers. Named for growing along old telegraph lines. Among the most common disturbed-ground plants in LA.

fm.Cultural: Named for growing along telegraph lines

wildflowers.hp: Late-season pollinator plant on disturbed ground. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny golden daisies on woolly white cushions. Sandy desert and grassland soil. Miniature but abundant — forms golden carpets.
wildflowers.hp: Tiny woolly daisy forming yellow carpet displays in desert. Superbloom indicator. Visited by mining bees (Andrena), sweat bees, and small butterflies. Important early-season nectar on dry slopes. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Golden-yellow mariposa lily with hairy petals and brown-red markings. Coastal and foothill slopes. Named for botanist C.M. Weed. Fire follower — often abundant 2–3 years post-fire.

fm.Color: Golden-yellow with brown-red markings and hairy inner surface
fm.Ecology: Fire follower — abundant post-fire
fm.Fire: Resprouter — bulb survives fire; fire follower — abundant 2–3 years post-fire

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Host for specialist moth larvae (Greya). Bulb provides food for Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Elegant nodding white globe-shaped flowers in shady woodland. Among the most beautiful native wildflowers. At southern range edge in San Gabriel Mountain canyons.

fm.Habitat: Shady woodland, canyon bottoms — at southern range edge
fm.Conservation: Rare in LA County — range edge

wildflowers.hp: Deep shade specialist. Among the most elegant native bulbs. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel. Host for specialist moth larvae (Greya).

wildflowers.desc: Yellow mariposa lily with no basal markings — unusually uniform color for the genus. Pine forest and chaparral openings. Mid-elevation.

fm.Color: Uniform yellow — no basal markings (unusual for genus)
fm.Habitat: Pine forest, chaparral openings at mid-elevation

wildflowers.hp: Uniform golden-yellow without dark markings. Less showy than C. clavatus. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel. Host for specialist moth larvae (Greya).

wildflowers.desc: Highly variable mariposa lily — white, yellow, pink, or red with intricate central markings resembling a butterfly. At range edge. California's showiest wildflower genus.

fm.Color: Highly variable — white, yellow, pink, or red with butterfly-like markings
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, open woodland — at range edge

wildflowers.hp: Variable colors (white/yellow/pink) with butterfly-like markings. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

wildflowers.desc: Cream to pale yellow poppy relative. Hairy stems with flowers nodding in bud, erect when open. Grasslands. Classic spring wildflower of open slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Grassland ephemeral with delicate cream-yellow poppy flowers. Pollen source only — no nectar. Seeds eaten by Horned Lark. Cream cups — pollinated by small native bees and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Important grassland wildflower.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flower clusters with distinctive burr-like seeds that hitchhike on clothing and fur. Very common woodland understory plant.
wildflowers.hp: Burr-like seeds hitchhike on clothing and animal fur. Umbel flowers attract parasitoid wasps that control agricultural pest caterpillars — important biological pest control. Host for Anise Swallowtail butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Dark magenta sand verbena forming prostrate mats on beach sand. Sticky stems trap sand grains. Less common than purple sand verbena. Important dune stabilizer declining with coastal development.

fm.Ecology: Dune stabilizer

wildflowers.hp: Important dune plant. Pollinated by hawkmoths at dusk. Fragrant. Sand dune stabilizer.

wildflowers.desc: Low gray-green coastal plant growing on bluffs and sandy ground. Once widespread on LA coastal strand — now largely lost to development. Important coastal sage scrub component.

fm.Conservation: Largely extirpated from LA coast by development

wildflowers.hp: California saltbush of coastal salt marshes and bluffs. Wind-pollinated. Important salt marsh structure for Belding's Savannah Sparrow habitat. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Important desert wildlife browse.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple snapdragon flowers on climbing stems in chaparral. Twining tendrils wrap around adjacent plants. Much more delicate than garden snapdragons.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by carpenter bees strong enough to open the closed flower.

wildflowers.desc: Small white flowers in shady moist canyons. Round scalloped basal leaves. One of the few LA wildflowers preferring deep shade and constant moisture.
wildflowers.hp: Shade and moisture specialist. Visited by native bees and syrphid flies in moist microhabitats.

wildflowers.desc: White dandelion-like flower with pink-striped ray undersides. Common spring desert annual. Often grows up through dead shrubs for support.

fm.Habitat: Desert scrub — often leans on dead shrubs

wildflowers.hp: Important early-season desert pollinator resource. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Spectacular white lily of desert sandy flats. Deeply buried bulb to 18 in deep. Only blooms after heavy rain — can wait years underground. The showiest desert wildflower.

fm.Color: Large white trumpets with green midstripes
fm.Bulb: 18 in deep — dormant for years between blooms
fm.Habitat: Desert sandy flats — Antelope Valley edge

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Bulb/corm provides food for Botta's Pocket Gopher.

wildflowers.desc: One of the earliest spring bloomers — white flower clusters appear before the huge round basal leaves. Moist mountain streambanks. At southern range limit.

fm.Height: 6–18 in (flower stalk), leaves to 1 ft across
fm.Ecology: Among earliest spring bloomers — before leaves
fm.Habitat: Mountain streambanks — at southern range limit

wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Intensely fragrant sage relative with tubular crimson flowers. Crushed leaves smell like a combination of sage and camphor. Dry chaparral openings.

fm.vs: Ross' Pitcher Sage (L. rossii) is much rarer, found only in Liebre Mtns, with kinked inflorescence axes and large leafy bracts equaling flowers.

wildflowers.hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Important hummingbird nectar plant.

wildflowers.desc: Wind-pollinated ragweed — one of LA's worst hay fever plants. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes in disturbed areas. Inconspicuous flowers, major allergen impact.
wildflowers.hp: Wind-pollinated. Important fall allergy culprit in LA basin. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch, American Goldfinch, Song Sparrow, and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Stiff golden-flowered shrub with saw-toothed leaves. Very common on disturbed slopes. Fall bloomer — important late-season nectar.
wildflowers.hp: Resinous shrubby perennial of coastal sage scrub. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow snapdragon-like flowers on spreading shrub. Dry chaparral and desert edge. More drought tolerant than most penstemons.
wildflowers.hp: Important hummingbird nectar (Anna's and Allen's Hummingbird) plant. Fire-adapted — resprouts from woody root crown after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Mojave Desert sunflower relative with silver-gray leaves. More inland and desert-edge than E. californica. Important bee forage on desert margins.

fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, Joshua tree woodland edges

wildflowers.hp: Visited by Painted Lady butterflies, native sweat bees (Halictidae), and mining bees (Andrena). Important nectar for migrating butterflies. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Bush Sunflower provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Resin chewed as gum by Cahuilla. Heated leaves applied as poultice for pain by Tongva. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisies with extremely sticky white-resinous buds. Medicinal — used to treat poison oak rash. Roadsides and disturbed ground. Resinous buds diagnostic.

fm.Cultural: Poultice for poison oak rash

wildflowers.hp: Resin used in traditional asthma remedies. Extremely sticky resinous heads — visited by bumble bees, honey bees, and soldier beetles. Important late-season nectar. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: White to pale pink pincushion flowers on gray-green aromatic foliage. Very common spring annual on burns and dry slopes. Distinctive flat-topped flower heads.
wildflowers.hp: Fire follower establishing after burns. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: White pincushion flowers in sandy desert. Classic spring annual of Antelope Valley and desert washes. Woolly stems.
wildflowers.hp: Important desert bee forage — visited by mining bees (Andrena), small carpenter bees (Ceratina), and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Coiled blue-lavender flower clusters — one of the most common spring annuals in coastal sage scrub and disturbed areas. Strong unpleasant scent. Attracts swarms of native bees.

fm.Habitat: Sage scrub, disturbed slopes — very common

wildflowers.hp: Among the most important native bee forage plants. Visited by specialist Andrena mining bees and dozens of generalist bee species. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Large lavender bell-shaped flowers with dark veins — among the showiest spring annuals. Sticky hairy stems. Common on burns and disturbed slopes. CA endemic.

fm.Color: Lavender bells with dark veins — showy

wildflowers.hp: CA endemic fire follower providing critical post-fire pollinator resources. Among California's most important pollinator plants. Major native bee forage — visited by specialist Andrena and over 50 generalist bee species. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Small white flowers in shaded understory of chaparral. Extremely common spring annual but overlooked. Ferny aromatic foliage. One of the first annuals after fire.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by bumble bees, mining bees, and sweat bees. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny blue globe-shaped flower clusters on wiry stems. Common but overlooked spring annual in grasslands and sage scrub openings.
wildflowers.hp: Local Asteraceae with limited coastal range. Pollinated by native bees and small butterflies. Important component of spring grassland pollinator networks.

wildflowers.desc: Tall spikes of blue-purple tubular flowers — among the showiest wildflowers on LA trails. Whorled flower clusters. Chaparral openings and road cuts. Bee and hummingbird pollinated.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by bumble bees and hummingbirds (especially Anna's Hummingbird). Nectar for Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Cream to white tubular flowers on spreading shrubby stems. Very common in chaparral throughout LA mountains.
wildflowers.hp: Bush penstemon of dry chaparral slopes. Pink gaping flowers attract carpenter bees and hummingbirds (Anna's Hummingbird). Fire-adapted resprouter from woody base.

wildflowers.desc: Small white star-shaped flowers with yellow stamens. Black berries (toxic). Extremely common on disturbed ground and trail edges throughout LA. Douglas's nightshade.

fm.Habitat: Disturbed ground, trail edges — very common

wildflowers.hp: White nightshade of moist shade. Berries eaten by California Towhee, Northern Mockingbird, and other birds when ripe. Visited by native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny prostrate spurge forming mats on dry ground. White-margined leaves. Extremely common but utterly overlooked — grows in every crack and trailhead parking lot.

fm.Habitat: Dry ground, trail edges, parking lots — ubiquitous

wildflowers.hp: Tiny flowers visited by minute sweat bees and parasitoid wasps.

wildflowers.desc: Bright pink-purple lupine of desert washes and sandy flats. Classic Antelope Valley superbloom species. Shorter than Arroyo Lupine.

fm.Habitat: Desert washes, sandy flats — superbloom species

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Nitrogen fixer. Key Antelope Valley superbloom species providing mass nectar resources. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white forget-me-not-like flowers covering grasslands in spring. Extremely common but small. Coiled cymes unfurling as flowers open.
wildflowers.hp: Important early-season foraging for native mining bees and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Nutlet seeds collected by harvester ants (Pogonomyrmex).

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow four-petaled evening primrose flowers. Miniature annual extremely common in grasslands — easy to overlook. Reddish stems.
wildflowers.hp: Among the smallest evening primrose relatives. Sandy soils. Pollinated by native bees and hawkmoths at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Cream-white flowers with orange-red streaks opening in afternoon heat. Sandpaper-rough leaves that stick to clothing. Desert washes.
wildflowers.hp: Bracts surround flower base. Pollinated by native bees attracted to afternoon-opening flowers. Barbed hairs trap small insects — not carnivorous but creates micro-habitat for predatory mites.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow sunflower-like heads nodding on slender stems. Chaparral openings and road cuts. More delicate than true Helianthus.
wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Slender Sunflower provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flowers with finely dissected carrot-like foliage. Shady woodland understory and chaparral edges. Often the most common carrot-family wildflower on shaded trails.
wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects including parasitoid wasps and hoverflies. Important shady-trail pollinator resource. Potential Anise Swallowtail host.

wildflowers.desc: Dense white to pale lavender coiled flower clusters. Extremely sticky hairy stems. Common on mountain road cuts and burns. Strong unpleasant odor.
wildflowers.hp: Post-fire specialist providing high-elevation pollinator resources. Major native bee forage — visited by mining bees, bumble bees, and sweat bees. Among California's most important pollinator plants. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Small white flowers with notched petals in sticky calyx tubes. Common spring annual in grasslands and sage scrub. Stems sticky below nodes — catches small insects.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Night-blooming species attract nocturnal pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple trumpet flowers in open umbels on leafless stalks. Spring bulb of grasslands and open woodland. Edible corms were important indigenous food. Formerly Brodiaea.

fm.Cultural: Edible corms — indigenous food plant

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Edible corms were important indigenous food. Geophyte — enhanced flowering after fire due to increased sunlight. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

wildflowers.desc: Red-orange tubular flowers — the red counterpart of Sticky Monkeyflower. More southern distribution. Hybridizes freely with D. aurantiacus where ranges overlap.

fm.Ecology: Hybridizes with D. aurantiacus — intermediate colors common

wildflowers.hp: Important hummingbird nectar (Anna's and Allen's Hummingbird) plant. Bush Monkey Flower (Red) contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources. Fire-adapted resprouter from root crown. Seeds germinate prolifically after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Cream to pale yellow tubular flowers. Very common in coastal sage scrub from LA to Ventura. Important pollinator plant. Formerly Mimulus longiflorus.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — very common SMM

wildflowers.hp: Visited by bumble bees and hummingbirds (especially Anna's Hummingbird). Host for Common Buckeye butterfly. Fire-adapted resprouter from root crown. Seeds germinate prolifically after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white four-petaled flowers on wiry stems with whorled narrow leaves. Extremely common in chaparral and sage scrub understory — one of the most ubiquitous plants on LA trails.
wildflowers.hp: Provides ground cover preventing erosion. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Low gray-green aromatic shrub on coastal bluffs and dunes. Important host for Painted Lady butterfly. Crushed foliage smells medicinal.
wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Painted Lady butterfly (Vanessa cardui). Stellate (star-shaped) hairs visible under hand lens.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tall yellow flower spike from huge fuzzy gray rosette. European weed now common on every disturbed mountain roadside. Leaves used as toilet paper substitute (hence "cowboy toilet paper").

fm.Cultural: Called "cowboy toilet paper" — soft fuzzy leaves

wildflowers.hp: Used medicinally for centuries. Flowers yield yellow dye. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white flowers in umbels on slender stems. One of the most common but overlooked spring annuals in grasslands. Miniature carrot-family plant.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands, sage scrub openings — very common

wildflowers.hp: Tiny umbels attract minute beneficial insects. Easily overlooked but ecologically important as a widespread nectar source. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Tall carrot-family plant with purple-spotted hollow stems. EXTREMELY toxic — killed Socrates. White umbrella flowers. Common along streams and disturbed wet areas. Often confused with wild carrot.

fm.Toxicity: EXTREMELY toxic — all parts. Killed Socrates. Even skin contact can cause reactions.
