wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract beneficial insects including parasitoid wasps, lacewings, and hoverflies. Cal-IPC Moderate invasive — displaces native vegetation. EXTREMELY TOXIC — killed Socrates. Invasive in riparian areas.

wildflowers.desc: Large lavender-blue asters — among the showiest desert wildflowers. Woody base with spiny-tipped leaves. Desert rocky slopes and washes.

fm.Color: Lavender-blue rays, yellow disk — large, showy

wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Mojave Aster is a distinctive component of its plant community. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Apricot-orange globemallow flowers on gray-green woolly stems. Common desert roadsides. Stellate hairs irritate skin like Flannel Bush. Multiple desert color morphs.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees in desert habitats. Host for Common Checkered Skipper butterfly. Stellate hairs irritate skin. Pollinated by globe mallow specialist bee Diadasia diminuta — a solitary ground-nesting bee that collects pollen exclusively from Sphaeralcea.

wildflowers.desc: Low mounding desert shrub covered in yellow flowers in spring. Very common in Joshua tree woodland and creosote scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Important late-season pollinator resource in Mojave. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant orange-red mariposa lily — the most vividly colored of all Calochortus. Desert rocky slopes after wet winters. Very rare and localized in LA County (Antelope Valley edge).

fm.Habitat: Desert rocky slopes — Antelope Valley edge

wildflowers.hp: Fire follower. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel. Host for specialist moth larvae (Greya).

wildflowers.desc: Purple lupine with sweetly fragrant flowers — unusual for lupines. Desert sandy flats. Major superbloom species at Antelope Valley.
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Major Antelope Valley superbloom species. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: White dandelion-like flowers seemingly floating 2 ft above ground on invisible thread-thin stems. Among the most ethereal desert wildflowers. Sandy-gravelly desert flats.
wildflowers.hp: Virtually leafless. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: White-pink flower clusters with milky sap. Spreading by rhizomes in moist areas. Fiber plant — Tongva used stems for cordage and nets. Related to milkweed.

fm.Cultural: Tongva cordage plant — fiber from stems

wildflowers.hp: Indian hemp — Tongva and Chumash twisted stem fibers into the strongest native cordage, used for fishing nets, bowstrings, and snares. Milky latex toxic to livestock.

wildflowers.desc: Woolly milkweed with dense cream-pink flower clusters. Largest-flowered milkweed in SoCal. Monarch host plant. Dry slopes and chaparral openings.
wildflowers.hp: Monarch butterfly host plant. Also used by Queen butterfly. Important nectar for native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Tall aromatic herb — the wild form of culinary tarragon. Narrow willow-like leaves. Late summer/fall bloomer. Widespread in many plant communities.

fm.Cultural: Wild form of culinary tarragon

wildflowers.hp: Source of tarragon spice. Wind-pollinated. Important browse for Pronghorn and Mule Deer. Provides Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Rhizomatous resprouter surviving fire underground. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Bristly golden-flowered perennial of dry slopes. Several subspecies in SMM. Late summer bloomer. Stems and leaves densely hairy.
wildflowers.hp: Late-season bee forage on dry slopes. Blooms Jul–Nov when few other plants flower. Visited by sweat bees and syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Slender yellow-flowered tarweed with extremely sticky stems. Common spring annual in grasslands and woodland openings. Strong tar-like smell.
wildflowers.hp: Slender tarweed — delicate glandular annual with flowers. Evening-opening blooms attract nocturnal pollinators. Visited by sweat bees and mining bees attracted to tarry-scented glands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: White dandelion-like flowers, larger than R. neomexicana. Common spring annual in chaparral burns and disturbed areas. Often confused with Desert Chicory.
wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. California Chicory provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy clusters on gray-green shrubby plant. Washes and dry stream beds. Mildly toxic — pyrrolizidine alkaloids.
wildflowers.hp: Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids toxic to livestock. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow goldenrod plumes in late summer and fall. Important late-season pollinator plant. Incorrectly blamed for hay fever — actually insect-pollinated.

fm.Ecology: Insect-pollinated — wrongly blamed for hay fever (that is ragweed)

wildflowers.hp: Important late-season nectar for native bees and butterflies including Painted Lady. Insect-pollinated — not a hay fever plant despite common misconception. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers producing showy silver-white seed puffs. Very common spring annual in grasslands. The seed heads are more conspicuous than the flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Important early seed source for Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: White forget-me-not-like flowers with prickly nutlets that stick to clothing. Very common spring annual in chaparral and coastal scrub openings.
wildflowers.hp: Dry slopes. Distinguished by warty (muricate) nutlets. Important early-season foraging for native mining bees and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Nutlet seeds collected by harvester ants (Pogonomyrmex).

wildflowers.desc: Fleshy blue-green plant with small white flowers curving in scorpioid cymes. Salt-tolerant — marshes, alkaline areas, beach edges. Spreads by rhizomes.

fm.Habitat: Salt marshes, alkaline areas, beach edges

wildflowers.hp: Important early-season foraging for native mining bees and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Wild Heliotrope (Seaside) contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources. Nutlet seeds collected by harvester ants (Pogonomyrmex).

wildflowers.desc: Among the earliest spring wildflowers — white four-petaled flowers appearing in January in shady woodland. Edible — peppery watercress relative. A harbinger of spring on shady trails.

fm.Ecology: Among earliest spring bloomers — January in mild years

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Edible — peppery — watercress relative. Host for Sara Orangetip butterfly. Among the first spring wildflowers in shaded woodland.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny plant with distinctive round flat lace-edged seed pods — more conspicuous than the small white flowers. Very common spring annual in grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Distinctive circular winged seed pods with lace-like veined margins. Visited by native bees and small butterflies including Sara Orangetip and Cabbage White. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

wildflowers.desc: Similar to Lace Pod but with deeply fringed seed pods. Spring annual in grasslands and rocky slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Distinguished from related species curvipes by fringed (not smooth) pod edges. Visited by native bees and small butterflies including Sara Orangetip and Cabbage White. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

wildflowers.desc: Small yellow cruciform flowers. Rare annual of heavy clay grasslands. Increasingly uncommon due to habitat loss. California endemic.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny kidney-shaped leaves forming dense ground cover. Almost invisible flowers. One of the most common ground-cover natives in shade — looks like a miniature lily pad carpet.

fm.Habitat: Shaded ground — very common ground cover

wildflowers.hp: Tiny kidney-shaped leaves forming dense mats. Native ground cover. Shade-tolerant. Visited by native bees and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Small gray-green subshrub with tiny white flowers. Salt-tolerant specialist of alkali flats and salt marshes. Indicator of saline soils.
wildflowers.hp: Tiny salt-tolerant shrublet of alkaline flats. Indicator of saline soils. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-magenta lupine covered in stinging hairs that cause painful rash on contact. Common on burns and dry slopes. The only stinging lupine. Fire follower.

fm.Toxicity: STINGING hairs — painful rash on contact

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated despite stinging hairs. Fire follower — mass germination post-fire. Nitrogen fixer. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple lupine with distinctly truncated (flat-topped) leaves. Common spring annual on burns and grassy openings in chaparral.
wildflowers.hp: Fire follower appearing abundantly on burned chaparral slopes. Bumble bee pollinated. Nitrogen fixer enriching post-fire soil. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Bright pink star-shaped flowers with white center. Stunning miniature gentian of grasslands and openings. One of the most beautiful small wildflowers.

fm.Color: Bright pink stars with white center

wildflowers.hp: Traditional indigenous medicine for fever and stomach ailments. Visited by native bees, butterflies, and bee flies.

wildflowers.desc: Deep purple bell-shaped flowers. Classic spring annual of coastal sage scrub and chaparral. More delicate than P. grandiflora. Common on every SMM trail in spring.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral — very common

wildflowers.hp: Important native bee forage in coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Visited by bumble bees, mining bees, and small carpenter bees. Popular garden plant for native bee support. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: White to lavender flowers on sprawling branching stems. Perennial phacelia of chaparral and pine forest. Less showy than annual phacelia species but persistent.
wildflowers.hp: Many-branched with coiled flower clusters. Important native bee forage on coastal bluffs and rocky slopes. Major native bee forage — visited by mining bees, bumble bees, and sweat bees. Among California's most important pollinator plants. Important riparian bee forage — visited by specialist Andrena and many generalist species. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Deep blue flowers with white center on extremely sticky hairy stems. One of the most beautiful annual wildflowers. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral openings.

fm.Color: Deep blue with white center — showy

wildflowers.hp: Excellent native bee forage — visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, and mining bees. Sticky stems may trap small invertebrates. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Blue flowers with long curving stamens on aromatic stems that smell like vinegar. Late summer/fall annual of dry grasslands. Annual relative of Woolly Bluecurls.

fm.Ecology: Annual relative of perennial Woolly Bluecurls

wildflowers.hp: Late-season bee forage — important when few other annuals bloom. Visited by hummingbirds, carpenter bees, and bumble bees. Aromatic oils attract pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow flowers on stems with sandpaper-rough leaves that stick to clothing. Common annual of chaparral openings and rocky slopes. Much smaller than M. laevicaulis.
wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees attracted to afternoon-opening flowers. Barbed hairs trap small insects — not carnivorous but creates micro-habitat for predatory mites. Small-flowered Blazing Star contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources.

wildflowers.desc: Strange root parasite with no green leaves — entirely parasitic on chamise and other chaparral shrubs. Purple-brown flower spikes emerging from soil. No chlorophyll.

fm.Ecology: Root parasite — no chlorophyll; parasitizes chamise (Adenostoma)
fm.Habitat: Chaparral — on roots of host shrubs

wildflowers.hp: Fully parasitic with no chlorophyll. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Bright pink flowers erupting from sandy soil in early spring — flowers seem too large for the tiny plant. One of the earliest and showiest spring annuals. Sandy grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Sandy soils. Night-fragrant — attracts small nocturnal moths. Pollinated by mining bees and small butterflies during day. Sandy soil specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Dense mats of tiny pink spiny-bracted flowers on dry slopes. Named for resemblance to Turkish rug patterns. Common but overlooked late-spring annual.
wildflowers.hp: Dense mats of dried involucres persist, creating 'carpet' effect. Visited by specialized small native bees. Seeds important for native harvester ants. Conservation concern — many species declining.

wildflowers.desc: Tall wiry stems with small white-pink flower clusters atop. Important late-season bee forage. Common on dry slopes and roadsides.
wildflowers.hp: Late-season pollinator resource — blooms Aug–Nov when few other plants flower. Visited by native bees, wasps, and butterflies including blues and hairstreaks. Resprouter from woody root crown.

wildflowers.desc: Low mounding buckwheat with cream-pink flower clusters on coastal bluffs. Small leaves. Important butterfly and bee plant at the coast.
wildflowers.hp: Coastal buckwheat of sea-bluffs. Host for Mormon Metalmark and Acmon Blue butterflies. Critical coastal pollinator plant — nectar source for dozens of native bee species. Coastal species — less fire-exposed but regenerates from root crown.

wildflowers.desc: Weak-stemmed scrambling plant covered in tiny backward-pointing hooks that stick to everything. Whorled leaves. Extremely common in moist shade — the original velcro plant.

fm.Stems: Covered in tiny hooks — sticks to clothing

wildflowers.hp: Clinging stems with Velcro-like hooked hairs disperse seeds on clothing and animal fur. Edible young shoots. Visited by minute native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Small native carrot relative with lacy white umbels. Fruit covered in hooked spines — hitchhikes on socks. The native wild carrot of chaparral and sage scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract parasitoid wasps and other beneficial insects. Hooked seeds hitchhike on socks and fur. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flowers with glossy divided leaves. Early spring bloomer on dry ridges. Less common than L. dasycarpum.
wildflowers.hp: Early spring nectar source for native bees emerging from winter dormancy. Host plant for Anise Swallowtail butterfly larvae (Papilio zelicaon).

wildflowers.desc: Delicate white umbel flowers with anise-scented roots. Shady oak woodland. Slender fruit with hooked tip. The anise smell when roots are crushed is diagnostic.

fm.Habitat: Shady oak woodland, yellow pine forest

wildflowers.hp: Umbel flowers attract small native bees and beneficial insects in woodland shade. Anise-scented roots — diagnostic field mark.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow umbrella flowers with sharply toothed basal leaves. Open grassy and rocky slopes below 2,000 ft. Burr-like fruits.
wildflowers.hp: Burr-like seeds dispersed by animals. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Tall vivid red-orange paintbrush of mountain meadows and stream banks. Larger and more robust than foothill paintbrushes. Hemiparasitic. SGM above 5,000 ft.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots
fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows, stream banks — above 5,000 ft

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Pollinated by hummingbirds at mountain elevations. Important high-altitude nectar source.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy clusters with distinctive triangular leaves. Mountain stream banks. One of the most common wildflowers along SGM streams above 6,000 ft.
wildflowers.hp: Mountain stream specialist with triangular leaves. Visited by bumble bees at high elevation when few other flowers bloom. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids — toxic to livestock. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Rare lemon-yellow fragrant lily of mountain streams. Among SoCal's rarest and most beautiful wildflowers. Federally sensitive species. SGM canyons at 4,000–8,000 ft. Severely declined from collection and habitat loss.

fm.Conservation: Federally sensitive. Severely declined from collection.

wildflowers.hp: CNPS 1B.2. Rare and declining. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flower heads with intense mint fragrance. Mountain trails in SGM. More common at higher elevations than M. villosa. Tea plant.

fm.Habitat: Mountain trails, openings — SGM above 5,000 ft

wildflowers.hp: Mountain mint with intensely aromatic purple flower heads. Important bumble bee and mining bee forage above 5,000 ft. Aromatic oils attract specialist pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Large lavender tubular flowers — among the showiest SGM penstemons. Named for naturalist Joseph Grinnell. Rocky chaparral and pine forest openings.

fm.Habitat: Rocky slopes, pine forest openings — SGM

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and hummingbirds (especially Anna's Hummingbird). SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant red tubular flowers in elongated clusters. Hummingbird specialist. Mountain slopes and openings. One of the most vivid mountain wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — brilliant scarlet tubular flowers. Also visited by long-tongued hawk moths.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple nodding flower clusters. Native wild onion of grasslands and chaparral openings. Strong onion scent. Edible — used by Tongva.

fm.Cultural: Edible — used by Tongva

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native bees. Edible — Tongva food plant. Geophyte. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

wildflowers.desc: Large pink-white flowers emerging from gravelly mountain soil — seemingly without leaves. Famous plant of Lewis and Clark expedition. At extreme southern range limit in SGM above 7,000 ft.

fm.Cultural: Lewis and Clark expedition plant. Montana state flower.
fm.Habitat: Gravelly mountain ridges — at extreme southern range limit
fm.Conservation: Very rare in LA County — southern range edge

wildflowers.hp: Montana state flower. Massive pink-white blooms from tiny succulent rosette. Extremely bitter raw roots were critical indigenous food when properly prepared. Visited by native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple mallow flowers on slender stems in grasslands and moist slopes. Variable — many subspecies. Attractive to native bees.
wildflowers.hp: Host for Painted Lady and West Coast Lady butterflies. Visited by native bees. Variable — many subspecies.

wildflowers.desc: Delicate pink-white flower sprays from rocky crevices. Basal rosette of scalloped leaves. Rock specialist at mid to high elevations. Coral bells relative.
wildflowers.hp: Rock crevice specialist. Visited by native bees and syrphid flies in moist canyon microhabitats.

wildflowers.desc: Bizarre inflated hollow stems topped with small purple flowers. Desert annual — classic Antelope Valley superbloom species. One of the strangest-looking wildflowers.

fm.Color: Purple flowers on swollen white/green stems

wildflowers.hp: Mojave Desert annual with dramatically inflated hollow stems. Stems edible when young — traditional indigenous food. Visited by mining bees and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow-flowered subshrub with pine-like needle leaves. Scarce — a single plant at Mt. Hollywood overlook may be only one in eastern SMM (Cooper 2013). Dry rocky slopes.

fm.Habitat: Dry rocky slopes — very scarce in SMM

wildflowers.hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Late-season nectar source. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Inconspicuous when flowering but transforms into dramatic silvery puffballs — like oversized dandelion globes. Spring grasslands. The puffball stage is the diagnostic field mark.
wildflowers.hp: Papery-scaled heads open into fluffy silver spheres. Important bee forage. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers with backward-pointing leaf teeth. More common than A. grandiflora at lower elevations. Open grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Spear-leaved Agoseris provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: True licorice-flavored roots. White pea flowers in spiky clusters. Moist low areas along streams. The actual licorice plant — sweet root used medicinally.

fm.Color: White-cream pea flowers in spiky clusters
fm.Cultural: Sweet licorice-flavored root — medicinal

wildflowers.hp: Wild licorice with roots containing glycyrrhizin — 50× sweeter than sugar. Traditional medicine for centuries. Nitrogen fixer. Tongva used root medicinally. Burred seed pods disperse on Mule Deer and coyote fur.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow sticky daisies with hairy stems. Grasslands and road banks. Similar to G. camporum but hairier. Important bee forage.
wildflowers.hp: Extremely sticky resinous heads — visited by bumble bees, honey bees, and soldier beetles. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Hairy Gumplant provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow flowers on wiry subshrub. The only rock rose native to SoCal. Dry slopes and ridges in coastal sage scrub and chaparral.

fm.Habitat: Dry slopes and ridges, CSS, chaparral

wildflowers.hp: Important native bee forage. Extremely drought-adapted, thriving on dry chaparral slopes where most plants cannot survive. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Peak rush-rose — visited by mining bees (Andrena) and small butterflies. Fire-adapted — seeds germinate after heat exposure. Seeds eaten by Dark-eyed Junco.

wildflowers.desc: Deep blue-violet hooded flowers close to ground. Tuberous roots. Dry openings in chaparral and forest. Often hidden among leaf litter.

fm.Habitat: Dry openings in chaparral, under oaks

wildflowers.hp: Distinctive cap-shaped calyx. Tuberous roots. Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators and deter herbivores.

wildflowers.desc: White woolly mint-family flowers on tall stems in wet to seepy places. Taller and whiter than S. bullata. Mountain seeps and moist canyons.
wildflowers.hp: Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators and deter herbivores. Hedge Nettle (White) contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow daisy with strongly reflexed rays and prominent dome-shaped disk. Moist meadows and stream margins. Causes sneezing when pollen is inhaled — hence the name.

fm.Color: Yellow, reflexed rays with raised disk
fm.Ecology: Pollen causes sneezing

wildflowers.hp: Somewhat toxic to livestock. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow flowers that open in late afternoon only. Rabbit-ear-shaped leaves. Sticky and aromatic. Dry grasslands and openings. Easily overlooked.

fm.Color: Yellow, tiny — open in afternoon only

wildflowers.hp: Extremely inconspicuous. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny woolly annual of dry burns and disturbed areas. Among the smallest wildflowers — easy to overlook. Cotton-ball-like clusters at stem forks.
wildflowers.hp: Among the most inconspicuous native wildflowers. Important in grassland soil ecology. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-pink flower heads with strong minty fragrance. Annual mint of rocky chaparral and oak woodland openings. Strong fragrance when stepped on.

fm.Fragrance: Strong mint when crushed or stepped on

wildflowers.hp: Open grasslands and rocky slopes. Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils deter herbivores.

wildflowers.desc: Flat-topped yellow flower clusters on tall stems in moist areas. Late summer bloomer. Often confused with Solidago but with flat-topped clusters.
wildflowers.hp: Late-season nectar source in moist habitats. Flat-topped clusters attract native bees and butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow wide-throated monkeyflower of desert washes. More compact than D. aurantiacus. Sandy areas at desert-chaparral transition.
wildflowers.hp: Desert monkeyflower of sandy washes. Visited by native bees — wide open throat accessible to small bee species. Important spring desert nectar. Fire-adapted resprouter from root crown. Seeds germinate prolifically after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Low subshrub of moist disturbed areas and stream terraces. Inconspicuous flowers. Important native groundcover for restoration in riparian zones.

fm.Ecology: Restoration groundcover for riparian zones

wildflowers.hp: Low coastal perennial of salt marsh and beach margins. Wind-pollinated. Salt-tolerant. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Trailing mint with sweet fragrance — the plant San Francisco was named for (Yerba Buena = "good herb"). Shady woodland. Makes excellent tea.

fm.Cultural: San Francisco was originally named "Yerba Buena" after this plant

wildflowers.hp: The plant for which San Francisco was named. Makes excellent tea. Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators and deter herbivores.

wildflowers.desc: Bright red tubular snapdragon flowers on scrambling shrub. Channel Islands endemic — planted on mainland for hummingbird gardens. Spectacular when in full bloom.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — tube length adapted to bill length of Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds. Channel Islands endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Federally endangered tiny daisy of clay grasslands. One of LA County's rarest plants. Known from fewer than 15 sites in LA and Ventura counties. Grasslands on clay soil.

fm.Habitat: Clay grasslands — fewer than 15 known sites
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered. CNPS 1B.1. Among LA County's rarest plants.

wildflowers.hp: FEDERALLY ENDANGERED. Few extant populations. Critical conservation priority. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Lavender bell flowers on erect stems. Endemic to SoCal. Dry rocky slopes in chaparral openings. Less common than other Phacelia species but distinctive.
wildflowers.hp: Important native bee forage — visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, and mining bees. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Uncommon — rocky slopes and washes. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Low salt-tolerant subshrub with tiny pink flowers. Salt marsh specialist — excretes salt crystals on leaf surfaces. Ballona Wetlands indicator species.

fm.Ecology: Salt-secreting leaves

wildflowers.hp: Upper salt marsh specialist. Salt-excreting glands create white leaf crusts. Important structural component of Belding's Savannah Sparrow nesting habitat.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-blue flower sprays on leathery-leaved salt marsh perennial. Important late-season salt marsh color when most other plants have finished. Dried flower arranging.
wildflowers.hp: Coastal salt marsh with lavender flower sprays. Dried flowers popular in arrangements. Visited by native bees and syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: Fleshy yellow-flowered succulent of salt marshes. Forms dense mats. One of the few native composites tolerant of full saltwater immersion.
wildflowers.hp: Important salt marsh habitat structure. Halophyte. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tall yellow plume of desert roadsides. Selenium indicator — grows on selenium-rich soils. Large feathery compound leaves. Toxic to livestock.

fm.Ecology: Selenium indicator plant — grows on seleniferous soils

wildflowers.hp: Prince's plume — tall yellow spikes indicating seleniferous soils. Important indicator plant for uranium prospecting. Selenium accumulator — important biological indicator of seleniferous soils. Visited by native bees. Toxic to livestock.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tall weed with small pale yellow dandelion-like flowers. Spiny-edged leaves align north-south ("compass plant"). Wild ancestor of cultivated lettuce. Extremely common on disturbed ground.
wildflowers.hp: Ancestor of cultivated lettuce. Milky latex. Leaves orient north-south (compass plant). Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Yellow dandelion-like flowers on milky-juiced stems. European weed found on every patch of disturbed ground in LA. Edible — eaten as salad green in Mediterranean countries.

fm.Edible: Young leaves edible as salad — Mediterranean tradition

wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Edible — young leaves edible as salad — mediterranean tradition.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Yellow dandelion-like flowers with extremely bristly prickly stems and leaves. Mediterranean weed common on roadsides and disturbed ground. Distinctive rough texture.
wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean origin. Common roadside weed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Dense clusters of tiny white fragrant flowers. Garden escape now on every coastal disturbed site. Extremely common as a weed but also planted intentionally. Honey-sweet scent.

fm.Habitat: Coastal disturbed areas, sidewalks — very common

wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean garden escape now common on roadsides. Major beneficial insect attractor — hover flies (syrphid flies) drawn to small flowers control aphids in gardens.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tiny white star-shaped flowers on weak trailing stems in shade. European weed found in every shady garden and trail edge. Edible — mild lettuce-like flavor.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Edible — mild — eaten as salad green.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny yellow-green cone-shaped flowers with no petals. Smells strongly of pineapple when crushed. Common on compacted paths and trailheads. One of the most distinctive trail-edge weeds.
wildflowers.hp: Crushed leaves smell strongly of pineapple. Trample-resistant. Native despite sometimes being listed as introduced. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native The familiar yellow puffball weed. European introduction now global. Edible — leaves, roots, and flowers all used. Among the most recognizable plants on Earth.

fm.Edible: Leaves, flowers, and roots all edible

wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Edible — leaves, flowers, and roots all edible.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tiny white flowers with distinctive heart-shaped seed pods. Among the most common weeds worldwide. The heart-shaped pods are the diagnostic feature.
wildflowers.hp: World's most widespread weed after dandelion. Heart-shaped seed pods diagnostic. Traditional herbal styptic for bleeding. Visited by small native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Yellow cruciform flowers on tall weed. Named for growing in London after the Great Fire of 1666. Extremely common LA winter weed — one of the first to appear after rain.

fm.Cultural: Named for colonizing London after Great Fire of 1666
fm.Habitat: Disturbed ground — among first winter weeds

wildflowers.hp: London rocket — named for colonizing rubble after the Great Fire of London (1666). Visited by native bees and Sara Orangetip butterfly. Host for Cabbage White butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Purple pea flowers on tall hairy stems in moist areas. Leathery trifoliate leaves. Stream banks and seeps. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

wildflowers.hp: Large riparian perennial with purple flower spikes. Nitrogen fixer important for stream bank ecology. Visited by bumble bees and carpenter bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Glandular-dotted trifoliate leaves used as tea by indigenous peoples and early settlers. Small purple flowers. Shaded woodland.

fm.Cultural: Tea plant — used by indigenous peoples and settlers

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Broad basal leaf rosette with rat-tail flower spikes. Called "white man's footprint" by Native Americans as it followed European settlement. Medicinal — leaf poultice for insect stings.

fm.Cultural: "White man's footprint" — followed European settlement. Medicinal leaf poultice for stings.

wildflowers.hp: Major medicinal herb worldwide. Wind-pollinated spike. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual with shiny round seed pods. Very common spring annual in grasslands — easy to miss. Important food for mourning doves and sparrows.
wildflowers.hp: Small native annual with peppery-tasting leaves. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Visited by native bees and small butterflies including Sara Orangetip and Cabbage White.

wildflowers.desc: Large white poppy flowers with yellow centers on spiny blue-green stems. All parts toxic — yellow sap. Beautiful but dangerous. Desert washes and disturbed areas.
wildflowers.hp: Extremely spiny. Latex contains toxic alkaloids. Visited by large carpenter bees.

wildflowers.desc: Small greenish-white star flowers on leafless stalks. The overlooked native bulb — extremely common but ignored. Anagram of Allium (onion) — related but no onion smell.

fm.Ecology: Name is anagram of Allium — related but no onion scent
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, sage scrub openings — very common

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by small native bees. Name is anagram of Allium. Extremely common but overlooked geophyte. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

wildflowers.desc: Slender purple flower spikes on tall branching stems. Common in moist disturbed areas and riparian edges. Important butterfly nectar plant.
wildflowers.hp: Important butterfly nectar — visited by Painted Lady, West Coast Lady, and Monarch.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tiny orange-red (rarely blue) flowers that close in overcast weather — the "poor man's weather glass." European weed common in disturbed areas and lawns. Mildly toxic.

fm.Cultural: "Poor man's weather glass" — flowers close when rain approaches

wildflowers.hp: Toxic to livestock. Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple clarkia with distinctive narrow petals. Less common than C. unguiculata. Shady chaparral openings in SGM foothills.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees in shady foothill habitats. Less common than C. unguiculata. Pollinated by specialist Andrena clarkiae — this bee depends entirely on Clarkia pollen. Evening-opening species visited by White-lined Sphinx Moth at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Small white clarkia of shady canyon understory. Self-pollinating — flowers barely open. One of the least showy but most common Clarkias.
wildflowers.hp: Self-pollinating — flowers barely open. Shade specialist requiring minimal pollinator visits. Pollinated by native bees and hawkmoths at dusk. Pollinated by specialist Andrena clarkiae.

wildflowers.desc: Wine-purple to magenta petals with a darker basal spot. Grasslands and open slopes. One of the most colorful spring annuals.

fm.Color: Wine-purple to magenta with dark basal spot

wildflowers.hp: Pollinated by native solitary bees — especially Andrena and Lasioglossum. Self-incompatible — requires cross-pollination for seed set. Purple Clarkia contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources. Evening-opening species visited by White-lined Sphinx Moth at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Brilliant sapphire-blue flowers on woolly stems. Small but intensely colored. Dry openings in chaparral and sage scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Open rocky areas. Visited by native bees and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Important desert pollinator resource.

wildflowers.desc: Purple-blue tubular flowers on sticky branching stems. Common spring annual in chaparral openings. Sticky and aromatic.
wildflowers.hp: Open grasslands and road cuts. Pollinated by native bees and small butterflies. Important spring nectar in grassland communities.

wildflowers.desc: Red tubular flowers in whorls on tall shrubby stems. Hummingbird specialist. SGM chaparral. Distinctive whorled leaf arrangement.
wildflowers.hp: Hummingbird specialist — tube length adapted to bill length of Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds. Fire-adapted — resprouts from woody root crown after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow five-petaled flowers on sticky glandular stems. Moist slopes and meadows. Rose family — strawberry-like leaves.
wildflowers.hp: Mountain cinquefoil of wet meadows. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small beetles. Fruit eaten by Song Sparrow and California Towhee.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny pink flowers on thread-thin trailing stems. Very common but utterly invisible unless you look closely. Dry slopes in chaparral and sage scrub.

fm.Habitat: Dry slopes — very common but easily overlooked

wildflowers.hp: Delicate annual with winged bracts. Among the most inconspicuous native wildflowers. Important for native seed-harvesting ants and Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Nectar source for native bees and butterflies including Acmon Blue.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual with leathery involucral bracts. Sandy disturbed areas. Among the smallest and most overlooked native wildflowers.
wildflowers.hp: Leathery involucral bracts. Important for specialized native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Small yellow daisies on slender stems. Desert annual that appears in good rain years. Antelope Valley and SGM desert slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Brilliant yellow desert annual forming carpet displays. Major spring superbloom species. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Low sprawling annual with spiny bur-like fruits that stick to socks and dog fur. Dry stream bottoms and waste areas. Wind-pollinated hay fever plant.

fm.Fruit: Spiny burs — stick to clothing and fur

wildflowers.hp: Spiny-fruited annual of disturbed sandy areas. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch, American Goldfinch, Song Sparrow, and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow monkeyflower with many small flowers. Seeps and wet stream banks. More flowers but smaller than E. guttata.
wildflowers.hp: Profusely blooming yellow monkeyflower of mountain stream banks. Visited by bumble bees and small native bees. Host for Common Buckeye butterfly.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Prostrate weed with devastating spine-covered seed pods that puncture bicycle tires and pierce shoes. European invasive. The most cursed weed on LA bike paths.

fm.Fruit: Spine-covered nutlets — puncture tires and skin
fm.Habitat: Disturbed areas, bike paths, vacant lots

wildflowers.hp: Seeds eaten by harvester ants and small Botta's Pocket Gopher and woodrats. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: White flowers on glandular-hairy stems. Rose family relative of cinquefoils. Sandy openings in chaparral. Uncommon — declining with development.
wildflowers.hp: Coastal and foothill species with wedge-shaped leaflets. Important for native mining bees (Andrena) and sweat bees (Halictidae). Sandy substrate specialist.

wildflowers.desc: Huge herbaceous plant with massive compound leaves — dies to ground each winter. Ball-like white flower clusters. Moist shaded canyons. Looks tropical.

fm.Form: Dies to ground each winter, resprouts
fm.Habitat: Moist shaded canyons — requires permanent water

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and wasps. Provides habitat structure.

wildflowers.desc: Curious hemiparasitic annual with hooked "beak" flowers. Purple to cream. Dry granitic chaparral slopes. Root parasite on neighboring shrubs.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic — taps neighboring plant roots

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic. Seeds require host root contact to germinate. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Blue lobelia of moist canyon walls. CNPS Rare Plant rank 1B.2. One of LA County's rarest wildflowers. Hangs from wet cliffs and seeps.

fm.Habitat: Moist canyon walls, wet cliffs, seeps
fm.Conservation: CNPS 1B.2 — rare. One of LA County's rarest wildflowers.

wildflowers.hp: CNPS 1B.2. Important hummingbird nectar (Anna's and Allen's Hummingbird) source at elevation. Conservation priority.

wildflowers.desc: Cream-white star flowers in elegant racemes. TOXIC — all parts contain steroidal alkaloids. Grasslands and open chaparral. Also called "death camas" — fatalities documented.

fm.Toxicity: HIGHLY TOXIC — steroidal alkaloids in all parts. Historical fatalities.

wildflowers.hp: Creamy white star flowers with green nectary glands. TOXIC — all parts contain steroidal alkaloids. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Large-leaved bedstraw endemic to the San Gabriel Mountains. Chaparral and oak woodland. California endemic — limited range.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees. Provides ground cover in chaparral understory. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: White flowers on stems with large round basal leaves. Moist canyon walls and seeps. One of the prettiest saxifrage relatives.
wildflowers.hp: Important shade-loving wildflower. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow dandelion-like flowers on hairy stems. Chaparral and pine forest openings. Common at mid-elevation in SGM.
wildflowers.hp: Southern California endemic. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny inconspicuous annual of moist shaded walls and rocks. Related to nettles but does not sting. Wind-pollinated — causes hay fever in some people. Also known as Rillita Pellitory.
wildflowers.hp: Host plant for Red Admiral and West Coast Lady butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny native plantain of grasslands. Slender upright spikes. Important larval food for the endangered Quino Checkerspot butterfly.
wildflowers.hp: Larval food for the endangered Quino Checkerspot butterfly (Euphydryas editha quino).

wildflowers.desc: Tiny white flowers with small round seed pods on branching stems. Common trailside weed. Peppery-tasting — edible as trail nibble. Bird food.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Edible — peppery taste — edible trail nibble.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow pincushion-shaped flower heads on desert annual. Key component of Antelope Valley superbloom. Chaparral openings and sandy areas.

fm.Habitat: Sandy areas, chaparral openings, desert grasslands

wildflowers.hp: Important desert bee forage — visited by mining bees (Andrena), small carpenter bees (Ceratina), and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Yellow Pincushion provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple daisy with many narrow rays. Common on dry chaparral slopes. Multiple subspecies. More robust than other fleabanes.
wildflowers.hp: Important late-season nectar source in chaparral. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive The original tumbleweed — breaks off at base when dry and rolls with wind, dispersing seeds. Russian immigrant that arrived in contaminated flax seed. Symbol of the American West.

fm.Origin: Russian — arrived in contaminated flax seed ca. 1870s

wildflowers.hp: Wind-pollinated. Cal-IPC Limited invasive — displaces native vegetation. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow and California Quail.

wildflowers.desc: Large white flowers aging to pink, opening at dusk. Desert and sandy areas. Sphinx moth pollinated at night. Fragrant.

fm.Color: White aging to pink — open at dusk

wildflowers.hp: Desert washes and sandy areas. Important hawkmoth forage in arid habitats.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny golden daisies with needle-thin leaves. Key superbloom species — carpets fields with yellow alongside poppies. Antelope Valley spring display.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands — key Antelope Valley superbloom species

wildflowers.hp: Distinguished from related species californica by needle-like foliage. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow star flowers on grass-like leaves. Wet meadows and seeps. Less common than blue-eyed grass. Not actually a grass — iris family.
wildflowers.hp: Moist grasslands and vernal pool margins. bellum. Pollinated by native bees. Important early-season nectar. Corms eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher. Mule Deer occasionally browse foliage.

wildflowers.desc: Large-leaved coral bells endemic to Channel Islands and coastal bluffs. Pink-white flower sprays. Popular native garden plant.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies in moist canyon habitats. Channel Islands endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Tall magenta-pink spikes — among the first plants to colonize burns and disturbed areas. Mountain species. Named because it's the first flower after fire.

fm.Ecology: First colonizer of burns — hence "fireweed"

wildflowers.hp: Iconic post-fire colonizer forming purple-pink meadows. Seeds with silky plumes travel miles on wind. Edible young shoots. Pollinated by native bees and hawkmoths at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Blue-purple lupine of mountain meadows and forest openings. Perennial - blooms later than annual species. Important late-season bee forage.
wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Important late-season mountain bee forage. Nitrogen fixer. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Large-leaved perennial lupine of mountain meadows and stream banks. Blue to lavender flowers. One of the more robust montane lupines. Moist areas.

fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows, stream banks - moist areas

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated at mountain elevations. Nitrogen fixer. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Short annual lupine with brilliant blue flowers and white banner spot. Grasslands. Common superbloom species - forms blue carpets alongside poppies.

fm.Color: Brilliant blue with white banner spot
fm.Habitat: Grasslands - forms blue carpets in superbloom

wildflowers.hp: Bumble bee pollinated. Classic superbloom species — forms blue carpets alongside poppies. Nitrogen fixer. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Variable annual lupine - flowers white, pink, yellow, or lavender. Small pods. Multiple varieties including the showy var. densiflorus. Grasslands and openings.

fm.Color: Variable - white, pink, yellow, or lavender

wildflowers.hp: Visited by bumble bees. Nitrogen fixer. Variable flower color — multiple varieties. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny hairy lupine of sandy desert grasslands. Flowers bicolored pink-purple and white. Among the smallest lupines - easy to miss. Sandy washes.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees. Among the smallest lupines — desert sand specialist. Nitrogen fixer. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Low mat-forming mountain lupine with blue flowers close to ground. Rocky alpine and subalpine areas. The lupine of SGM highest ridges.

fm.Habitat: Rocky alpine, subalpine - SGM highest ridges

wildflowers.hp: Visited by mountain bumble bees at alpine elevations. Mat-forming — stabilizes rocky alpine soils. Nitrogen fixer. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Low desert annual lupine with lilac flowers. Sandy flats in western Mojave. Named for botanist William Shockley who collected in the Mojave in the 1880s.

fm.Habitat: Sandy flats - western Mojave, Antelope Valley edge

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Western Mojave specialist. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Deep blue lupine with distinctive yellow spot on banner. Desert annual. Sandy washes and scrub. Smaller and darker than most desert lupines.

fm.Color: Deep blue with yellow banner spot

wildflowers.hp: Visited by desert native bees. Nitrogen fixer. Sandy wash specialist. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Deep violet-purple bowl-shaped flowers — among the showiest annual Phacelias. Chaparral openings. Named for botanist Charles Parry.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees including bumble bees and mining bees. Fire follower providing post-fire pollinator resources. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Lavender-blue coiled flower clusters with protruding stamens. Widely planted as bee forage cover crop. Native to SoCal but now cultivated globally for honeybees.

fm.Ecology: One of the best bee forage plants — widely cultivated for honeybees worldwide

wildflowers.hp: Native to SoCal but now planted globally. Attracts 50+ bee species (Xerces Society). Major native bee forage — visited by mining bees, bumble bees, and sweat bees. Among California's most important pollinator plants. Important forage for declining Crotch bumble bee (Bombus crotchii) and Western bumble bee (Bombus occidentalis). Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Small white-lavender phacelia with short-lobed leaves. Shady chaparral canyons. Less showy than other species but common.
wildflowers.hp: Visited by small native bees in shady canyon habitats where few other annuals provide early-season nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Low mat-forming buckwheat with white-pink flowers aging to rusty red. Dry rocky slopes at mid-elevation. Important late-season pollinator plant.
wildflowers.hp: Important late-season pollinator resource in arid habitats. Critical nectar for Acmon Blue, Mormon Metalmark, Gray Hairstreak, and California Scrub-Jay seed caches, and other native butterflies. Foundation pollinator plant. Desert subshrub resprouting from woody base after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Tiny annual buckwheat with delicate wiry stems and small white-pink flower clusters. Common but easily overlooked. Dry slopes.
wildflowers.hp: Slender desert buckwheat. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly (Icaricia acmon). Important nectar source for native bees and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by harvester ants. Annual — regenerates from soil seed bank after fire.

wildflowers.desc: Purple flower heads on subshrub with distinctive white-veined leaf undersides. SGM endemic subspecies. Dry chaparral openings.
wildflowers.hp: Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

wildflowers.desc: Yellow to orange paintbrush of moist areas and stream edges. More delicate than other paintbrushes. Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic — taps neighboring roots

wildflowers.hp: Hemiparasitic on neighboring plant roots. Visited by hummingbirds and native bees along stream edges.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Massive spiny thistle with purple artichoke-like flower heads — the wild ancestor of the globe artichoke. Cal-IPC Moderate. Forms impenetrable stands on coastal bluffs and grasslands. Roots to 6 ft deep.

fm.Cultural: Wild ancestor of the globe artichoke
fm.Ecology: Forms impenetrable stands. Roots to 6 ft deep — very difficult to eradicate.

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Massive spiny rosettes and purple flowers displacing native grassland. Mediterranean. Extremely difficult to control. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Spiny purple-flowered thistle with winged spiny stems. European weed common on disturbed ground. Distinguished from native thistles by continuous spiny wings running down the stems.

fm.Stems: Winged with continuous spines (diagnostic — native thistles lack this)

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Important goldfinch food. Purple flowers attract bumble bees despite invasive status.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Tall perennial with dense white flower clusters. Cal-IPC High. Extremely aggressive in wetlands and riparian areas — spreads by root fragments. Nearly impossible to eradicate once established.

fm.Ecology: Spreads by root fragments. Nearly impossible to eradicate.

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC High. Aggressive invader of wetlands and riparian areas. Deep roots make eradication very difficult. Visited by native bees and butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Pale yellow mustard devastating Mojave Desert spring wildflower displays. Cal-IPC High. Germinates earlier than natives, forming dense stands that shade out poppies and desert annuals. Rapidly expanding through Antelope Valley.

fm.Origin: North Africa/Middle East — Cal-IPC High. CDFA C-rated.
fm.Ecology: Germinates earlier than natives — shades out wildflower displays
fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, Antelope Valley — rapidly expanding

wildflowers.hp: Visited by native bees and small butterflies including whites and sulphurs. Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Purple thistle with clusters of narrow flower heads atop winged spiny stems. Cal-IPC Moderate. One of the most hated invasive thistles in LA — forms impenetrable stands on roadsides, trailheads, and disturbed grasslands. Distinguishable from Bull Thistle by narrower clustered heads.

fm.Color: Purple, narrow heads in clusters of 2-5 (vs solitary in Bull Thistle)
fm.Habitat: Roadsides, trailheads, disturbed grasslands — very common
fm.vs: Bull Thistle (C. vulgare) has solitary larger heads; Italian has clustered narrow heads

wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean origin. Less aggressive than bull thistle. Roadside colonizer. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: White woolly everlasting with felt-covered leaves and stems. Fall bloomer — flowers when most other wildflowers have finished. Dry chaparral slopes. Leaves gray-white below, greener above.

fm.vs: California Everlasting (P. californicum) blooms Jan-Jul and has broader, less woolly leaves with a two-tone appearance

wildflowers.hp: Higher elevation than P. californicum. Host for American Lady butterfly. Woolly stems provide nesting material for Anna's Hummingbird. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Bicolored everlasting — leaves green above and white-woolly beneath, giving a striking two-tone effect. Dry slopes in chaparral. One of the more attractive everlastings.

fm.Leaves: Distinctly two-tone — GREEN above, WHITE-WOOLLY beneath (diagnostic)
fm.vs: Feltleaf (P. canescens) is woolly on BOTH surfaces; Two-tone has green upper surface

wildflowers.hp: Woolly stems provide nesting material for hummingbirds. Visited by small native bees. Host plant for American Lady butterfly larvae. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Tall white-woolly everlasting with many tiny flower heads in dense clusters. The whitest and most conspicuous of the LA everlastings. Fall bloomer. Dry open chaparral.

fm.Color: Dense white clusters — many tiny heads
fm.vs: Feltleaf (P. canescens) similar but typically shorter; White Everlasting is taller with more dense flower clusters

wildflowers.hp: White everlasting — woolly stems provide critical nesting material for Anna's Hummingbird and Allen's Hummingbird. Visited by small native bees. Host plant for American Lady butterfly larvae. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Rose-pink tinged everlasting — the only pink-flowered Pseudognaphalium in LA County. Uncommon on dry slopes. The pink tinge is subtle and best seen in bud.

fm.Color: Rose-pink tinged flower clusters (unique among LA everlastings)
fm.vs: All other LA everlastings are white; Pink Everlasting has distinctive rose-pink tinge

wildflowers.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Pink Everlasting provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Small yellowish-white annual cudweed — European weed common in disturbed areas. Distinguished from native everlastings by annual habit and yellowish (not pure white) flower heads.

fm.Color: Yellowish-white heads (vs pure white in native species)
fm.vs: Native everlastings (Pseudognaphalium) are perennials with pure white heads; Jersey Cudweed is an annual with yellowish heads

wildflowers.hp: European. Common garden and roadside weed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

wildflowers.desc: Pink-purple thistle-like flower heads on spiny subshrub. Dry chaparral slopes. Tongva name "sacapellote" (hair-puller) refers to the spiny involucral bracts that snag clothing and hair. Unusual sweet vanilla fragrance when fresh.

fm.Cultural: Tongva name "sacapellote" = hair-puller (spiny bracts snag clothing)

wildflowers.hp: Traditional medicinal plant. Ethnobotanically important. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and other seed-eating birds.

wildflowers.desc: Small pink-purple four-petaled flowers with notched petals. Common along streams and moist areas. Produces cottony seeds that drift on wind. Closely related to fireweed. Distinguished from E. canum by small flowers and moist habitat.

fm.vs: California Fuchsia (E. canum) has large red tubular flowers and grows in DRY habitats; Willowherb has small pink flowers in MOIST habitats

wildflowers.hp: Moist disturbed areas. Important early colonizer of newly created wetlands. Pollinated by native bees and hawkmoths at dusk.

wildflowers.desc: Extremely rare pitcher sage endemic to the western Transverse Ranges — described new to science in 2006. Known from only four populations: two in the Liebre Mountains (LA County) east of Castaic Lake. Fewer than 1,000 plants total.

fm.Color: White to purplish, bell-shaped in racemes
fm.Leaves: Toothed oval blades to 13 cm, hairy and glandular
fm.Inflorescence: Axis bent at 60-90° angles to stem (diagnostic — unique in genus)
fm.Bracts: Large, leaf-like, equaling or exceeding flowers (diagnostic)
fm.Conservation: CNPS 1B.1 — extremely rare. Only ~4 populations known worldwide.
fm.vs: Fragrant Pitcher Sage (L. fragrans) has straight inflorescence axes and smaller bracts much shorter than flowers. Ross' has geniculate (kinked) axes.

wildflowers.hp: CNPS 1B.1 — known from very few populations in Santa Monica Mountains. Important hummingbird nectar (Anna's Hummingbird). Conservation priority.

wildflowers.desc: Perennial goosefoot with fleshy triangular leaves and a large starchy root used by Tongva and Chumash as soap. Not related to Chlorogalum despite sharing the "soap plant" name. Moist shady slopes.

fm.Leaves: Fleshy, triangular to diamond-shaped, dark green
fm.Root: Large, starchy tuber — crushed for saponin-rich lather
fm.Cultural: Tongva/Chumash soap plant — root crushed for cleaning lather
fm.Habitat: Moist shady slopes, oak woodland understory
fm.vs: Wavyleaf Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) is a lily relative with wavy strap leaves and white flowers — completely unrelated despite shared common name

wildflowers.hp: Wind-pollinated. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow and California Quail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native small melilot (Fabaceae). Sweet clover — common roadside plant.

fm.Flowers: Tiny yellow pea-flowers in slender racemes, 2-3 mm

wildflowers.hp: Sweet clover — common roadside plant. Nitrogen fixer. When improperly cured as hay, produces dicoumarol (blood thinner). Honeybee forage plant. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native white clover (Fabaceae). ubiquitous lawn plant.
wildflowers.hp: Ubiquitous lawn plant. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Important honeybee and native bee forage. Edible flowers. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive bur clover (Fabaceae). Bur clover with distinctive spiral seed pods.

fm.Fruit: Distinctive spiral bur pods with hooked spines

wildflowers.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Bur clover with distinctive spiral seed pods. Nitrogen fixer. Common pasture plant but displaces native annuals in grasslands. Visited by bumble bees and sweat bees. Important agricultural species. Host for Marine Blue butterfly larvae.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native White Sweetclover (Fabaceae). White sweet clover.
wildflowers.hp: Tall biennial nitrogen fixer. Important honeybee forage. Contains coumarin — precursor to the blood thinner warfarin when fermented. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native black nightshade (Solanaceae). black nightshade with small white flowers and black berries.

fm.Flowers: Small white stars with yellow stamens, 6-8 mm
fm.Fruit: Glossy black berries in drooping clusters, 6-8 mm

wildflowers.hp: Berries toxic when green, edible when ripe in some traditions. Common garden weed worldwide. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native hairy vetch (Fabaceae). nitrogen-fixing cover crop escaping to roadsides.
wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen-fixing cover crop escaping to roadsides. Important honeybee forage. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees.

wildflowers.desc: Silver bird's-foot trefoil (Fabaceae). Silver lotus with silvery-hairy leaves.
wildflowers.hp: Important nitrogen fixer in coastal sage scrub. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Spanish clover (Fabaceae). widespread annual nitrogen fixer in grasslands and open areas.
wildflowers.hp: Widespread annual nitrogen fixer in grasslands and open areas. Host for several blue butterfly larvae. Important bee forage. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Black Medick (Fabaceae). tiny yellow clover of lawns and disturbed ground.
wildflowers.hp: Tiny yellow clover of lawns and disturbed ground. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and sweat bees. Important agricultural species.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native oceanblue morning glory (Convolvulaceae). vigorous vine smothering native vegetation in riparian areas and coastal bluffs.

fm.Flowers: Large blue-purple funnels, 6-8 cm, fading pink

wildflowers.hp: Vigorous vine smothering native vegetation in riparian areas and coastal bluffs. Visited by native bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. Vigorous vine.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive needle-leaved broom (Fabaceae). Flax-leaved broom naturalizing on hillsides and road cuts.

fm.Flowers: Bright yellow pea-flowers in terminal clusters

wildflowers.hp: Flax-leaved broom naturalizing on hillsides and road cuts. Nitrogen fixer. Forms dense thickets excluding native shrubs. Related to Cal-IPC High-rated scotch broom. Visited by native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native western coastal wattle (Fabaceae). western coastal wattle from Australia.

fm.Fruit: Coiled pods with bright red arils on seeds

wildflowers.hp: Western coastal wattle from Australia. Nitrogen fixer. Forming dense thickets in some coastal areas. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Strigose lotus (Fabaceae). Strigose lotus of dry open areas.

fm.Habitat: Dry open areas, coastal sage scrub

wildflowers.hp: Strigose lotus of dry open areas. Important early-season nitrogen fixer. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly larvae. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Rose Clover (Fabaceae). rose clover from Mediterranean — planted for forage but naturalizing in grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Rose clover from Mediterranean — planted for forage but naturalizing in grasslands. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Longleaf Wattle (Fabaceae) — Non-native Sydney golden wattle — phyllode-leaved acacia naturalizing in coastal areas.

fm.Phyllodes: Bright green, 5-15 cm, with 2-4 prominent veins
fm.Size: Large shrub or small tree, 3-8 m

wildflowers.hp: Sydney golden wattle — phyllode-leaved acacia naturalizing in coastal areas. Nitrogen fixer. Dense growth excludes native species. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Cootamundra wattle (Fabaceae). spectacularly showy golden flowers in winter.

fm.Flowers: Brilliant golden balls in axillary racemes, winter

wildflowers.hp: Widely planted ornamental. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Braunton's milkvetch (Fabaceae). FEDERALLY ENDANGERED.
wildflowers.hp: FEDERALLY ENDANGERED. Known from fewer than 20 populations (USFWS Recovery Plan). Fire follower dependent on recently burned chaparral. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native common morning-glory (Convolvulaceae). Common morning glory — annual vine with showy purple trumpet flowers.

fm.Flowers: Purple trumpet funnels, 4-6 cm, with white throat

wildflowers.hp: Common garden escape. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Morning glory species provide hummingbird nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Tomcat clover (Fabaceae). Important early-season nitrogen fixer and bee forage in California grasslands.

fm.Flowers: Purple-tipped white heads with inflated calyces

wildflowers.hp: Important early-season nitrogen fixer and bee forage in California grasslands. Distinctive inflated calyx. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Mexican palo verde (Fabaceae). Mexican palo verde with distinctive green bark (photosynthetic).

fm.Leaves: Bipinnate with tiny leaflets on long rachis
fm.Flowers: Yellow with orange-spotted banner, in racemes

wildflowers.hp: Mexican palo verde with distinctive green bark (photosynthetic). Thorny. Seeds eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and woodrats and birds. Visited by native bees and carpenter bees. Important desert bee forage.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native vanilla-scented wattle (Fabaceae). low-growing ground cover acacia widely planted in LA landscapes.

fm.Size: Prostrate ground cover, 30-60 cm tall, spreading 3-5 m

wildflowers.hp: Low-growing ground cover acacia widely planted in LA landscapes. Nitrogen fixer. Relatively low invasion risk compared to tree acacias. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Common Vetch (Fabaceae). agricultural nitrogen-fixing cover crop escaping to roadsides.

fm.Flowers: Purple-pink, 10-20 mm, paired in leaf axils

wildflowers.hp: Agricultural nitrogen-fixing cover crop escaping to roadsides. Important honeybee forage. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native tomato (Solanaceae). cultivated tomato — occasionally persisting in compost piles and disturbed areas.
wildflowers.hp: Cultivated tomato — occasionally persisting in compost piles and disturbed areas. Minimal ecological impact. Important agricultural crop. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Island broom (Fabaceae). Channel Islands endemic that has spread to mainland coastal sage scrub.
wildflowers.hp: Channel Islands endemic that has spread to mainland coastal sage scrub. Shrubby nitrogen fixer. Important pollinator plant. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Visited by native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Coastal bird's-foot trefoil (Fabaceae). Coastal lotus of sandy and rocky coastal habitats.
wildflowers.hp: Coastal lotus of sandy and rocky coastal habitats. Important nitrogen fixer in coastal strand. Host for Acmon Blue butterfly. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Peanut-Butter Cassia (Fabaceae). peanut-butter cassia from tropical Africa — distinctive peanut butter scent when leaves crushed.

fm.Flowers: Bright yellow spikes with dark brown buds, 30-50 cm
fm.Scent: Strong peanut butter odor from crushed leaves

wildflowers.hp: Peanut-butter cassia from tropical Africa — distinctive peanut butter scent when leaves crushed. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Important ecosystem service provider.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Strawberry clover (Fabaceae). distinctive inflated fruiting heads resemble tiny strawberries.

fm.Flowers: Pink heads becoming inflated and strawberry-like in fruit

wildflowers.hp: Salt-tolerant. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees, honey bees, and long-tongued native bees. Important forage plant.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Alfalfa (Fabaceae). world's most important forage crop.
wildflowers.hp: Deep-rooted nitrogen fixer. Important commercial honeybee forage. Escaping to roadsides and river terraces. Visited by bumble bees and sweat bees. Important agricultural species.

wildflowers.desc: Douglas' milkvetch (Fabaceae). Douglas' milkvetch of mountain chaparral margins.
wildflowers.hp: Douglas' milkvetch of mountain chaparral margins. Important nitrogen fixer. Several varieties with different habitat preferences. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native bugweed (Solanaceae). bugweed from South America.
wildflowers.hp: Bugweed from South America. Purple flowers and yellow berries spread by birds. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Bladder-senna (Fabaceae). bladder-senna with inflated papery seed pods.
wildflowers.hp: Bladder-senna with inflated papery seed pods. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing on disturbed slopes. Mildly toxic seeds. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Indian tobacco (Solanaceae). culturally significant to Tongva and Chumash peoples for ceremonial use.

fm.Flowers: White tubular, 3-5 cm, opening at dusk

wildflowers.hp: Culturally significant to Tongva and Chumash peoples for ceremonial use. Small white tubular flowers visited by hawkmoths at night.

wildflowers.desc: Canyon Dodder (Convolvulaceae). completely parasitic with no chlorophyll.

fm.Size: Parasitic vine, no roots once established
fm.Habitat: On native shrubs in chaparral and coastal sage scrub

wildflowers.hp: Completely parasitic with no chlorophyll. Often parasitizes native shrubs. Part of the natural ecosystem despite parasitic lifestyle. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Chaparral bird's-foot trefoil (Fabaceae). Chaparral lotus with large showy yellow-and-red flowers — the showiest native Acmispon.
wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Visited by small native bees. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: American black nightshade (Solanaceae). distinguished from introduced S.

fm.Fruit: Glossy black berries (not dull like S. nigrum)

wildflowers.hp: Distinguished from related species nigrum by glossy (not dull) berries. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native silver senna (Fabaceae). silver senna from Australia — gray fern-like foliage with yellow flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Drought-tolerant. Naturalizing from plantings. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native silverleaf nightshade (Solanaceae). extremely difficult to eradicate due to deep root system.
wildflowers.hp: Extremely difficult to eradicate due to deep root system. Toxic berries. Serious agricultural weed in desert regions. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Silver wattle (Fabaceae). showy golden flower sprays in winter.

fm.Flowers: Bright golden balls in large sprays, winter

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing on hillsides. Important early-season honeybee forage. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native arabian pea (Fabaceae). crushed leaves smell strongly of asphalt (bitumen).
wildflowers.hp: Crushed leaves smell strongly of asphalt (bitumen). Mediterranean origin. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native yellow bird-of-paradise shrub (Fabaceae). Yellow bird-of-paradise — showy yellow-and-red flowers with long stamens.

fm.Flowers: Yellow petals with spectacular long red stamens, 8-10 cm

wildflowers.hp: Yellow bird-of-paradise — showy yellow-and-red flowers with long stamens. South American. Mildly toxic. Common ornamental naturalizing in washes. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Peirson's morning-glory (Convolvulaceae). SoCal endemic vine with large white-pink funnel flowers.

fm.Flowers: White to pale pink funnels, 3-5 cm

wildflowers.hp: SoCal endemic morning glory. Host for Painted Lady butterfly (Vanessa cardui). Visited by native bees and hummingbirds (Anna's Hummingbird).

wildflowers.desc: San Miguel Island milkvetch (Fabaceae). Channel Islands endemic.
wildflowers.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Important nitrogen fixer in island ecosystems. Conservation concern. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Asian ponysfoot (Convolvulaceae). small round-leaved ground cover used as lawn substitute.
wildflowers.hp: Small round-leaved ground cover used as lawn substitute. Low ecological impact. Shade-tolerant. Visited by native bees and small butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Big deervetch (Fabaceae). big deervetch of mountain meadows and forest openings.
wildflowers.hp: Big deervetch of mountain meadows and forest openings. Yellow flowers visited by native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native buffalo-bur (Solanaceae). buffalo-bur with extremely spiny stems and fruit.

fm.Flowers: Yellow, 2-3 cm, with one elongate curved stamen

wildflowers.hp: Ancestral host of Colorado Potato Beetle. Toxic solanine alkaloids. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native broad-leaved sweet pea (Fabaceae). vigorous perennial vine with showy pink flowers.

fm.Flowers: Pink-magenta, large, 2-3 cm, in racemes of 5-15
fm.Leaves: One pair of leaflets + branching tendril + winged stem

wildflowers.hp: Escaping from gardens and smothering native vegetation on hillsides. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Climbs via tendrils.

wildflowers.desc: San Clemente Island milkvetch (Fabaceae). Channel Islands endemic.
wildflowers.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Important nitrogen fixer. Conservation concern — restricted island distribution. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native lanceleaf nightshade (Solanaceae). lanceleaf nightshade naturalizing in disturbed areas.
