wildflowers.hp: Lanceleaf nightshade naturalizing in disturbed areas. Toxic berries. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Peach Thorn (Solanaceae). important desert shrub with fleshy orange-red berries eaten by birds.
wildflowers.hp: Important desert shrub with fleshy orange-red berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Provides nesting structure for desert songbirds. Thorny. Visited by native bees. Provides nesting habitat in thorny branches.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Sweet pea (Fabaceae). iconic garden flower occasionally persisting on road cuts and disturbed slopes.

fm.Flowers: Fragrant, variable colors (purple, pink, white, red)
fm.Habitat: Road cuts, old gardens; garden escape

wildflowers.hp: Iconic garden flower occasionally persisting on road cuts and disturbed slopes. All parts mildly toxic. Many cultivated varieties. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Climbs via tendrils.

wildflowers.desc: Tall silky lupine (Fabaceae). Tall silky lupine of mountain meadows.

fm.Flowers: Blue-purple with white banner, in dense racemes

wildflowers.hp: Important bumble bee forage at elevation. Nitrogen fixer. Seeds dispersed explosively — pods twist open audibly on hot days. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native blackwood (Fabaceae). blackwood acacia — one of the most invasive acacias in California.

fm.Phyllodes: Dark green, lanceolate, 6-13 cm (replaces true leaves)
fm.Habitat: Streets, parks; dense shade suppresses native understory

wildflowers.hp: Blackwood acacia — one of the most invasive acacias in California. Dense shade and leaf litter suppress native seedlings. Hard durable wood. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Guadalupe Island Lupine (Fabaceae). rare lupine associated with islands and coastal bluffs.

fm.Flowers: Blue-purple with white banner, in racemes

wildflowers.hp: Rare lupine associated with islands and coastal bluffs. Nitrogen fixer. Limited distribution. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native potato (Solanaceae). cultivated tuber crop occasionally volunteering from gardens.
wildflowers.hp: Cultivated tuber crop occasionally volunteering from gardens. Minimal ecological impact. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Streambank bird's-foot trefoil (Fabaceae). Streambank lotus — moist meadow and riparian specialist.
wildflowers.hp: Streambank lotus — moist meadow and riparian specialist. Stream bank stabilizer. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Baja desert-thorn (Solanaceae). spiny shrub with fleshy red berries important for desert birds.
wildflowers.hp: Spiny shrub with fleshy red berries important for desert birds. At northern range limit in southern LA County. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Visited by native bees. Provides nesting habitat in thorny branches.

wildflowers.desc: Tree Clover (Fabaceae). tall annual clover of grasslands and oak woodlands.

fm.Flowers: Pink-purple heads with pointed calyx teeth
fm.Leaves: Trifoliate, narrow leaflets with fringed margins

wildflowers.hp: Tall annual clover of grasslands and oak woodlands. Important bee forage. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow and California Quail. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Coyote Tobacco (Solanaceae). fire follower germinating abundantly after burns.

fm.Flowers: White tubular, 2-3 cm, opening at dusk

wildflowers.hp: Fire follower germinating abundantly after burns. Produces nicotine as herbivore defense. Night-blooming — visited by hawkmoths. Used ceremonially by Tongva and Chumash. NOT smoked recreationally — sacred ritual context only.

wildflowers.desc: Southern island clover (Fabaceae). Southern island clover of Channel Islands and coastal areas.
wildflowers.hp: Southern island clover of Channel Islands and coastal areas. Important nitrogen fixer. Limited mainland distribution. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Persian silk tree (Fabaceae). showy pink powder-puff flowers.

fm.Leaves: Bipinnate, fern-like, 20-40 cm, fold at night

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Persian silk tree contributes nitrogen to soil communities.

wildflowers.desc: Freckled Milkvetch (Fabaceae). extremely variable species with 40+ varieties across western US.
wildflowers.hp: Extremely variable species with 40+ varieties across western US. Some varieties accumulate toxic selenium from soil. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Pacific goldenthread (Convolvulaceae). Pacific dodder — golden-thread parasite on coastal shrubs, especially pickleweed and saltbush.

fm.Size: Parasitic vine — no roots once established

wildflowers.hp: Pacific dodder — golden-thread parasite on coastal shrubs, especially pickleweed and saltbush. Important component of salt marsh ecology. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: Pinpoint clover (Fabaceae). Slender annual clover of grasslands and serpentine soils.
wildflowers.hp: Slender annual clover of grasslands and serpentine soils. Tiny but ecologically important nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Clammy clover (Fabaceae). Sticky-glandular clover of moist grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Important nitrogen fixer. Declining. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native golden wreath wattle (Fabaceae). aggressive Australian acacia naturalizing in coastal areas.
wildflowers.hp: Aggressive Australian acacia naturalizing in coastal areas. Dense growth displaces native coastal sage scrub. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Peirson's Lupine (Fabaceae). desert specialist with yellow flowers.

fm.Flowers: Blue-purple with yellow-white banner, in racemes

wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Important early-season desert bee forage. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: San Diego bird's-foot trefoil (Fabaceae). San Diego lotus — small annual of coastal sage scrub.
wildflowers.hp: San Diego lotus — small annual of coastal sage scrub. Important nitrogen fixer. Subtle but ecologically significant. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Visited by native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tangier Pea (Fabaceae). showy annual sweet pea with crimson flowers.

fm.Leaves: One pair of leaflets + tendril + winged stem

wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean. Escaping from gardens to road cuts. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Important ecosystem service provider.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Queensland silver wattle (Fabaceae). showy golden flower balls on silvery foliage.

wildflowers.desc: Heermann's Lotus (Fabaceae). woolly-leaved annual of sandy and rocky habitats.

wildflowers.desc: American vetch (Fabaceae). climbing vine of mountain meadows with purple flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Important bee forage. Seeds eaten by California Quail. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Cowbag clover (Fabaceae). Small annual clover of grasslands.

fm.Flowers: Tiny purple heads inflating in fruit

wildflowers.hp: Small annual clover of grasslands. Important nitrogen fixer in native grassland ecology. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native bird's-foot trefoil (Fabaceae). European nitrogen fixer planted for forage.

wildflowers.desc: Rancheria clover (Fabaceae). annual clover of grasslands with distinctive bicolored flowers.

fm.Fruit: Green tomatillo in papery husk, 3-5 cm

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Knife-leaf Wattle (Fabaceae). distinctive triangular phyllodes.
wildflowers.hp: Distinctive triangular phyllodes. Australian ornamental. Nitrogen fixer. Limited invasion potential. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Cock's-eggs (Solanaceae). creeping vine with small white bell flowers and white berry fruit.

wildflowers.desc: Sierra Lotus (Fabaceae). mat-forming perennial of mountain openings.

wildflowers.desc: Two-seeded milkvetch (Fabaceae). Small annual of grasslands.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native White leadtree (Fabaceae). fast-growing nitrogen-fixing tree from tropical America.

fm.Phyllodes: Long, narrow, 5-15 cm, with single vein

wildflowers.desc: Non-native reddish tufted vetch (Fabaceae). showy purple-red flower clusters.

fm.Flowers: Deep reddish-purple, 1-2 cm, in dense racemes

wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean. Nitrogen fixer. Roadside colonizer. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Plume Albizia (Fabaceae). feathery cream flower spikes.

wildflowers.desc: Field Dodder (Convolvulaceae). yellow-thread parasite of agricultural crops.

fm.Habitat: On agricultural crops and weedy hosts

wildflowers.hp: Yellow-thread parasite of agricultural crops. Economically important crop pest. Part of natural ecosystems on native hosts. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds.

wildflowers.hp: Cairo morning glory — vigorous vine smothering native vegetation. Difficult to control once established. Visited by native bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. Vigorous vine.

wildflowers.desc: Big Bear Valley woollypod (Fabaceae). San Bernardino Mountains endemic.
wildflowers.hp: Conservation concern. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees. Many species accumulate selenium — toxic to livestock. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Paiute false bindweed (Convolvulaceae). white funnel flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Carpet clover (Fabaceae). Tiny mat-forming mountain clover.

fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows, moist areas above 6,000 ft

wildflowers.hp: Important nitrogen fixer in alpine meadows. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native manyflower tobacco (Solanaceae). small tubular flowers.
wildflowers.hp: South American. Occasional garden escape. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids. Used ceremonially by Tongva and Chumash. NOT smoked recreationally — sacred ritual context only.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Woodland Tobacco (Solanaceae). tall with fragrant white trumpet flowers attracting hawkmoths at night.

wildflowers.desc: Chile trefoil (Fabaceae). Chile lotus — small annual nitrogen fixer of grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Chile lotus — small annual nitrogen fixer of grasslands. Important bee forage. Formerly Lotus wrangelianus. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Visited by native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native black wattle (Fabaceae). one of the world's worst invasive trees in some regions.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Sweet acacia (Fabaceae). fragrant golden ball flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Tara (Fabaceae). Andean tree with red pods yielding tannins and gums.

wildflowers.desc: Small-flowered morning-glory (Convolvulaceae). Small-flowered morning glory — tiny white flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Red Clover (Fabaceae). familiar garden clover with pink-red flower heads.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Lesser hop trefoil (Fabaceae). Lesser hop clover — tiny yellow flower heads.
wildflowers.hp: European. Common lawn weed. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Slender vetch (Fabaceae). Louisiana vetch — delicate climbing vine of moist grasslands and woodlands.

wildflowers.desc: Harlequin Lupine (Fabaceae). bicolored yellow-and-pink flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Christmas Senna (Fabaceae). Climbing senna — yellow flowers on sprawling shrub.

wildflowers.desc: Gambel's milkvetch (Fabaceae). small annual of grasslands and coastal sage scrub.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Shrubby Scorpion-vetch (Fabaceae). Scorpion vetch — yellow flowers.

wildflowers.desc: Spider Lupine (Fabaceae). Bentham's lupine — slender annual of mountain meadows.
wildflowers.hp: Bentham's lupine — slender annual of mountain meadows. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Turkey Berry (Solanaceae). prickly tropical shrub with white flowers and yellow berries.
wildflowers.hp: Southeast Asian vegetable. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Lucky morning glory (Convolvulaceae). Lucky morning-glory — rare SoCal endemic.

fm.Habitat: Moist areas; SoCal endemic, very rare

wildflowers.hp: Lucky morning-glory — rare SoCal endemic. Known from few localities. Conservation priority. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Morning glory funnels attract hummingbirds.

wildflowers.desc: California goldenbanner (Fabaceae). California false lupine — yellow lupine-like flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Toxic to livestock. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Host for California Dogface butterfly (Zerene eurydice) — California's state insect. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Blue palo verde (Fabaceae). Stunning blue-green bark tree of desert washes.
wildflowers.hp: Blue palo verde — photosynthetic green bark. Important desert hummingbird nectar (Costa's Hummingbird). Nitrogen fixer. Seeds eaten by Gambel's Quail. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Silky California Broom (Fabaceae). Silky lotus — prostrate annual of dry rocky habitats.
wildflowers.hp: Silky lotus — prostrate annual of dry rocky habitats. Nitrogen fixer. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Visited by native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Thickpod Milkvetch (Fabaceae). Thick-podded milkvetch — low shrubby milkvetch of chaparral openings.
wildflowers.hp: Thick-podded milkvetch — low shrubby milkvetch of chaparral openings. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native arrowleaf clover (Fabaceae). large white flower heads.

fm.Flowers: White heads becoming inflated and bladder-like

wildflowers.hp: European forage crop. Nitrogen fixer. Occasional volunteer from agricultural plantings. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native purple crownvetch (Fabaceae). Crown vetch — pink-white flowers.
wildflowers.hp: European nitrogen fixer planted for erosion control. Can smother native vegetation. Visited by native bees and butterflies.

wildflowers.desc: Parish's nightshade (Solanaceae). endemic to SoCal mountains.
wildflowers.hp: Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — bees vibrate flowers to release pollen.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Dwarf Morning-glory (Convolvulaceae). Dwarf morning glory — tricolored blue-white-yellow flowers.

fm.Flowers: Tricolored — blue, white, yellow center, 3-5 cm

wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean. Annual. Garden flower. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Morning glory species provide hummingbird nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Jerusalem cherry (Solanaceae). ornamental with toxic orange berries resembling cherry tomatoes.

fm.Warning: Berries toxic — mistaken for cherry tomatoes

wildflowers.hp: Ornamental with toxic orange berries resembling cherry tomatoes. TOXIC to children and pets. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native blue potatobush (Solanaceae). Blue potato bush — blue-purple flowers.
wildflowers.hp: South American. Very common LA garden shrub. Attracts butterflies. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native chili pepper (Solanaceae). Pepper — cultivated vegetable occasionally volunteering.
wildflowers.hp: Pepper — cultivated vegetable occasionally volunteering. Includes bell peppers, chilis. Minimal ecological impact. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native apple-of-Peru (Solanaceae). Shoo-fly plant — pale blue flowers.

fm.Fruit: Brown berry in inflated lantern-like calyx

wildflowers.hp: Peruvian. Occasional escape. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Sharpleaf groundcherry (Solanaceae). Sharpleaf ground cherry — papery-husked fruits.
wildflowers.hp: Sharpleaf ground cherry — papery-husked fruits. Edible when fully ripe. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees. Fruit in papery lantern-like husk.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Common Pea (Fabaceae). Garden pea — cultivated crop occasionally volunteering.

fm.Leaves: Pinnate, 2-3 pairs + terminal tendril + large stipules

wildflowers.hp: Garden pea — cultivated crop occasionally volunteering. Nitrogen fixer. Minimal ecological impact. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Spotted medick (Fabaceae). heart-shaped dark spot on leaflets diagnostic.

fm.Leaves: Trifoliate with dark blotch on each leaflet (spotted)

wildflowers.hp: Heart-shaped dark spot on leaflets diagnostic. Mediterranean nitrogen fixer. Common lawn weed. Visited by bumble bees and sweat bees. Important agricultural species.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native woolly clover (Fabaceae). fuzzy inflated flower heads.

fm.Flowers: Pink heads becoming woolly-inflated in fruit

wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees, honey bees, and long-tongued native bees. Important forage plant. Important ecosystem service provider.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Golden Wattle (Fabaceae). Australia's national flower.

fm.Flowers: Large golden balls in racemes; Australia's national flower

wildflowers.hp: Australia's national flower. Showy golden balls. Nitrogen fixer. Naturalizing. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native narrow-leaf bird's-foot trefoil (Fabaceae). Narrow-leaved trefoil — slender yellow-flowered clover relative.
wildflowers.hp: European. Moist disturbed areas. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by small native bees (Halictidae). Host for several blue butterfly species (Lycaenidae). Important ecosystem service provider.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Reversed clover (Fabaceae). flowers face downward (unique among clovers).
wildflowers.hp: Flowers face downward (unique among clovers). Mediterranean. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees, honey bees, and long-tongued native bees. Important forage plant. Important ecosystem service provider.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Spiny Broom (Fabaceae). thorny Mediterranean shrub.
wildflowers.hp: Thorny Mediterranean shrub. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native giant devil's-fig (Solanaceae) — Non-native giant devil's fig — large fuzzy-leaved tropical nightshade.
wildflowers.hp: Giant devil's fig — large fuzzy-leaved tropical nightshade. Occasionally persisting from cultivation. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native marriage vine (Solanaceae) — Non-native Costa Rican nightshade — spectacular lilac flower clusters on climbing vine.
wildflowers.hp: Costa Rican nightshade — spectacular lilac flower clusters on climbing vine. Tropical. Frost-sensitive. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Dwarf Tamarillo (Solanaceae). small shrubby nightshade.
wildflowers.hp: Small shrubby nightshade. South American. Rare garden plant. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native violet tubeflower (Solanaceae). Mini-brugmansia — purple tubular flowers.
wildflowers.hp: South American tube flower. Visited by Anna's Hummingbird. Pendant violet clusters attract specialist pollinators. Garden plant occasionally naturalizing.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native porcupine tomato (Solanaceae). dramatic orange spines covering stems and leaves.

fm.Leaves: Lobed with dramatic orange thorns on leaf midribs

wildflowers.hp: Madagascar. Rare curiosity plant. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids.

wildflowers.desc: Invasive Scotch Broom (Fabaceae). One of California's worst invasive shrubs.
wildflowers.hp: One of California's worst invasive shrubs. Nitrogen fixer. Very difficult to eradicate. Visited by bumble bees. Cal-IPC High — displaces native shrubs.

wildflowers.desc: Foothill deervetch (Fabaceae). Short-pod lotus — small annual of grasslands.
wildflowers.hp: Short-pod lotus — small annual of grasslands. Nitrogen fixer. Important for native soil fertility. Host for Acmon Blue and other lycaenid butterflies. Visited by native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native hop trefoil (Fabaceae). Hop clover — golden-yellow flower heads.
wildflowers.hp: European. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees, honey bees, and long-tongued native bees. Important forage plant. Important ecosystem service provider.

wildflowers.desc: Bajada Lupine (Fabaceae). Agardh's lupine — slender mountain lupine.
wildflowers.hp: Agardh's lupine — slender mountain lupine. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Peanut (Fabaceae). occasionally volunteering near agricultural areas.

fm.Fruit: Underground pods (peanuts) — pegs grow down after flowering

wildflowers.hp: Occasionally volunteering near agricultural areas. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Kangaroo thorn (Fabaceae). thorny Australian acacia.
wildflowers.hp: Thorny Australian acacia. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Basalt milkvetch (Fabaceae). Threadstalk milkvetch — pendant threadlike fruit stalks.
wildflowers.hp: Threadstalk milkvetch — pendant threadlike fruit stalks. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: San Jacinto Lupine (Fabaceae). blue-purple mountain lupine.
wildflowers.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Important bumble bee forage. Seeds dispersed explosively — pods twist open audibly on hot days. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: California Indian breadroot (Fabaceae). tuberous root important indigenous food plant.
wildflowers.hp: Prairie-like habitats. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees. Important indigenous food — starchy tuber ('Indian breadroot'). Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Sweet broom (Fabaceae). Madeira broom — yellow flowers on arching stems.
wildflowers.hp: Canary Islands. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees. Invasive broom species — Cal-IPC concern.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Argentine Senna (Fabaceae). bright yellow flowers.

fm.Flowers: Bright yellow, 3-4 cm, in terminal clusters

wildflowers.hp: South American. Common LA ornamental. Butterfly nectar source. Buzz-pollinated by carpenter bees and bumble bees — must vibrate flowers for pollen release.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Blackbean (Fabaceae). Moreton Bay chestnut — Australian rainforest tree.
wildflowers.hp: Moreton Bay chestnut — Australian rainforest tree. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Flinders Range Wattle (Fabaceae). Willow-leaved acacia — weeping form.
wildflowers.hp: Willow-leaved acacia — weeping form. Australian. Drought-tolerant ornamental. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees. Seeds with nutritious arils dispersed by birds.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Woolly Pod Vetch (Fabaceae). Woolly-pod vetch — large purple flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Mediterranean. Nitrogen fixer. Occasional roadside plant. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees.

wildflowers.desc: Honey mesquite (Fabaceae). Western honey mesquite — recently split from Prosopis.
wildflowers.hp: Western honey mesquite — recently split from Prosopis. Desert tree with edible pods. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees. Important desert wildlife — pods eaten by coyotes, Desert Cottontail, and Gambel's Quail. Honey mesquite — sweet pods were critical indigenous food for Cahuilla — ground into flour (mesquite meal). Costa's Hummingbird feeds from flowers. Deep taproot reaches groundwater 50+ ft down (USDA, recorded to 190 ft).

wildflowers.desc: Pacific False Bindweed (Convolvulaceae). white-pink funnel flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Important native bee forage. Visited by native bees, small butterflies, and hummingbirds. Pacific False Bindweed contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native green cestrum (Solanaceae). yellow-green tubular flowers.

fm.Flowers: Greenish-yellow tubular, 2 cm, fragrant at night

wildflowers.hp: Chilean. Toxic — all parts. Buzz-pollinated by bumble bees and carpenter bees — bees vibrate flowers to release pollen.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native ivy-leaved morning-glory (Convolvulaceae). Ivy-leaved morning glory — annual vine with blue trumpet flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Agricultural weed. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Morning glory species provide hummingbird nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native orange cestrum (Solanaceae). Orange tubular flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Central American night-blooming orange cestrum. Pollinated by hawkmoths at night. Visited by hummingbirds (Anna's Hummingbird). Garden escape — all parts toxic.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native kidney weed (Convolvulaceae). tiny round-leaved ground cover.
wildflowers.hp: Tiny round-leaved ground cover. Australian. Used as lawn substitute. Low ecological impact. Visited by native bees and small butterflies. Morning glory species provide hummingbird nectar.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native jasmine tobacco (Solanaceae). fragrant white trumpet flowers opening at night.

fm.Flowers: White tubular, 5-8 cm, intensely fragrant at night

wildflowers.hp: Jasmine tobacco — intensely fragrant at night. Pollinated by hawkmoths (Manduca spp.) attracted to white tubular flowers and sweet scent after dark. Used ceremonially by Tongva and Chumash. NOT smoked recreationally — sacred ritual context only.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native Cultivated tobacco (Solanaceae). large pink tubular flowers.
wildflowers.hp: Tropical American. Occasional volunteer. Contains nicotine. Buzz-pollinated by native bees. Berry fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Many species contain toxic alkaloids. Used ceremonially by Tongva and Chumash. NOT smoked recreationally — sacred ritual context only.

wildflowers.desc: White bog orchid (Orchidaceae). Fragrant white flower spikes in mountain meadows.

fm.Flowers: White, spurred, in dense spike, fragrant
fm.Habitat: Mountain bogs, wet meadows above 5,000 ft

wildflowers.hp: Mycorrhizal dependent. Pollinated by hawkmoths attracted to fragrant white flowers. Rare native orchids.

wildflowers.desc: Cooper's Rein Orchid (Platanthera) (Orchidaceae). Cooper's rein orchid — tall green flower spikes in montane chaparral.
wildflowers.hp: Important for native bee pollination. Mycorrhizal. Pollinated by hawkmoths attracted to fragrant white flowers. Rare native orchids.

wildflowers.desc: Sparse-flowered bog orchid (Orchidaceae). Green flowers in moist mountain meadows.
wildflowers.hp: Mycorrhizal. At southern range limit. Pollinated by specialized insects. Conservation-sensitive habitat indicator. Pollinated by hawkmoths attracted to fragrant white flowers. Rare native orchids.

wildflowers.desc: Non-native centipede grass orchid (Orchidaceae). tiny terrestrial orchid from Asia.

fm.Flowers: Tiny white, 3-5 mm, in dense spike

wildflowers.hp: Tiny terrestrial orchid from Asia. Naturalizing in irrigated lawns and parks. One of few naturalized orchids. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

wildflowers.desc: Denseflower Rein Orchid (Orchidaceae). green flower spikes in mountain forest.
wildflowers.hp: Mycorrhizal dependent. Uncommon. Pollinated by specialized insects. Conservation-sensitive habitat indicator. Pollinated by hawkmoths attracted to fragrant white flowers. Rare native orchids.

wildflowers.desc: Alaska Rein Orchid (Orchidaceae). at extreme southern range limit in SGM.
wildflowers.hp: Important mycorrhizal relationship. Pollinated by hawkmoths attracted to fragrant white flowers. Rare native orchids.

trees.desc: LA's signature tree. Evergreen with holly-like convex leaves. Massive spreading canopy. Foundation species supporting 300+ insect species.

fm.Ecology: Foundation species — 300+ insect species
fm.Fire: Resprouter — resprouts vigorously from root crown after fire

trees.hp: Supports 300+ insect species (UC Berkeley studies) including California Sister butterfly, many moth species, and Acorn Woodpecker. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Host for Mournful Duskywing skipper larvae. Crown resprouter — even when top-killed by intense fire, resprouts vigorously from root crown burl. Acorns second only to Valley Oak in importance as indigenous food. Tongva maintained oak groves by burning understory. Ash-throated Flycatcher nests in larger oak cavities. Acorns cached by California Scrub-Jay. Western Screech-Owl nests in natural tree cavities and old woodpecker holes. California Oak Moth (Phryganidia californica) caterpillars defoliate coast live oaks in fall — trees recover but repeated defoliation stresses them. Band-tailed Pigeon and Red-shouldered Hawk nest in coast live oak canopy. Cooper's Hawk hunts songbirds through oak woodland.

trees.desc: Largest North American oak. Deciduous with deeply lobed leaves. Majestic spreading form. Once dominant in LA's valley floors — now mostly lost to development.

fm.Conservation: Historically dominant — now rare in LA basin
fm.Fire: Resprouter — thick bark provides fire resistance

trees.hp: Acorns were the primary food of California's indigenous peoples — ground into flour. Once dominant on LA's valley floors, now largely lost to development. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species. Fire-resistant thick bark on mature trees. Young trees resprout from base after fire. Oak Titmouse forages on trunk and branches. Nuttall's Woodpecker drills bark for insects. Band-tailed Pigeon eats acorns. Largest North American oak. Northern Flicker feeds on acorn-boring beetle larvae in bark. Polyphemus Moth (Antheraea polyphemus) — California's largest native moth (6-inch wingspan) — larvae feed on oak leaves. Red-shouldered Hawk nests in valley oak canopy. Great Horned Owl roosts in dense crown.

trees.desc: Dense shrubby oak of chaparral. Evergreen with small spiny leaves. Often forms impenetrable thickets. Fire-resprouting.

fm.Leaves: Spiny-toothed, convex, dull green. Interior species — found miles from coast.
fm.vs: Nuttall Scrub Oak (Q. dumosa) is restricted to within a few miles of the coast, has flatter leaves, and is CNPS 1B.1 rare

trees.hp: Crown-sprouter after fire — resprouts vigorously from underground burl. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species. California Thrasher forages under scrub oak canopy. Wrentit nests in dense stands. Striped Skunk digs for white grub beetle larvae in soil beneath scrub oak. Western Tent Caterpillar (Malacosoma californicum) forms silk tent colonies on scrub oak branches in spring.

trees.desc: Evergreen oak of inland foothills. Flat leaves (vs convex in Coast Live Oak). Often at slightly higher elevations.

fm.Leaves: Evergreen, flat, spiny or smooth margin

trees.hp: Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Mule Deer, and Western Gray Squirrel. Fire-adapted resprouter. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species. Dusky-footed Woodrat builds elaborate stick houses (middens) 4-6 ft tall under interior live oak canopy. Middens host unique invertebrate communities.

trees.desc: Gold-scaled acorn cups diagnostic. Evergreen. Higher elevation than Coast Live Oak. Fire-resistant thick bark.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — thick bark + root crown sprouting
fm.Bark: Gray, scaly to furrowed — thick, fire-resistant

trees.hp: Gold-scaled acorn cups diagnostic. Thick fire-resistant bark. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, American Black Bear, California Scrub-Jay, Mule Deer, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species. Mountain Lion ambush sites in canyon live oak woodland — Mule Deer are primary prey. Bobcat hunts Brush Rabbit in understory.

trees.desc: Deciduous oak of mountain elevations. Deeply lobed leaves turn gold-brown in fall. Important acorn source for wildlife and indigenous peoples.

fm.Fall: Golden-brown — best fall color of LA oaks
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown and epicormic sprouting

trees.hp: Acorns were most important food source for mountain-dwelling indigenous peoples. Fire-adapted resprouter. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Foundation woodland species. Gilbert's Skink forages in leaf litter under California black oak canopy. California Oak Moth and Western Tent Caterpillar (Malacosoma californicum) both use black oak as larval host. Tent caterpillars form conspicuous silk colonies.

trees.desc: Massive white-barked riparian tree. Mottled bark peels in patches. Leaning, multi-trunked form. Defines LA's canyon bottoms.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — thick bark, resprouts from base

trees.hp: Host for Western Tiger Swallowtail butterfly. Cavity-nesting habitat for Great Horned Owl, Nuttall's Woodpecker, and Wood Ducks. Tiny seeds with hairs dispersed by wind and water — sycamore balls break apart releasing hundreds. Fire-resistant thick bark on mature trees. Black Phoebe nests under overhanging sycamore roots along streams. House Wren nests in small natural cavities. Western Screech-Owl uses larger cavities in mature sycamores. Tongva used sycamore bark to make bowls and containers. California Sister butterfly sometimes uses sycamore as larval host (primarily oaks). Red-shouldered Hawk nests in sycamore canopy. Hooded Oriole sews pendant nest to sycamore leaf.

trees.desc: Endemic Southern California tree. Small spreading form with pinnate leaves. Threatened by urbanization and hybridization with English Walnut.
trees.hp: SoCal endemic threatened by urbanization and hybridization with cultivated English Walnut. Walnuts eaten by Western Gray Squirrel and California Scrub-Jay. Bark foraging habitat for Nuttall's Woodpecker. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Large deciduous riparian tree. Heart-shaped leaves flutter in any breeze. Cotton-like seeds in spring. Fast-growing but short-lived.
trees.hp: Host for Western Tiger Swallowtail, Mourning Cloak, and Lorquin's Admiral butterfly larvae. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Vigorous root crown resprouter after fire. Suckers from roots can form extensive clonal groves. Black Phoebe hawks insects from cottonwood perches. Red-tailed Hawk and Cooper's Hawk nest in canopy. Cavity habitat for woodpeckers and owls. Northern Flicker excavates nest cavities in dead cottonwood snags — these are later used by Western Screech-Owl, American Kestrel, and other cavity nesters. Bushtit builds pendant nests in outer cottonwood branches. Yellow Warbler nests in cottonwood canopy (riparian breeding obligate).

trees.desc: Most common native willow in LA. Multi-stemmed riparian tree. Important caterpillar host plant and streambank stabilizer.

fm.Ecology: Host plant for many moth and butterfly species
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous root and stem sprouting

trees.hp: Host for Lorquin's Admiral, Western Tiger Swallowtail, Mourning Cloak butterflies and many moth species. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Rapid resprouter after fire — among the first riparian trees to recover. Stems used in Tongva and Chumash basketry. Bushtit builds elaborate pendant nests in arroyo willow canopy. Black Phoebe forages from streamside perches. Orange-crowned Warbler breeds in dense arroyo willow thickets. Wilson's Warbler forages during migration.

trees.desc: Tall single-trunked willow with reddish twigs. Larger and more tree-like than Arroyo Willow. Riparian corridors.
trees.hp: Important Lorquin's Admiral and Mourning Cloak host. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Rapid resprouter after fire — among the first riparian trees to recover. Stems used in Tongva and Chumash basketry. Black Phoebe nests under willow root overhangs along streams. Bushtit builds hanging nests in outer willow branches. Arroyo Toad (federally endangered) breeds in sandy-bottomed streams shaded by red willow canopy. Larvae require slow, shallow water.

trees.desc: Aromatic evergreen with pungently fragrant leaves. Crushing leaves releases camphor-like scent. Can cause headaches. Canyon understory tree.
trees.hp: California bay laurel — aromatic leaves contain umbellulone, which causes 'headache tree' response. Aromatic oils deter herbivores. Tongva and Chumash roasted nuts as food. Bay fruit eaten by Band-tailed Pigeon.

trees.desc: "Christmas Berry" — bright red berries in winter gave Hollywood its name. Evergreen shrub-tree. Important winter bird food.

fm.Cultural: Hollywood named for Toyon berries

trees.hp: Red berries critical winter food for American Robin, Cedar Waxwing, Northern Mockingbird, and Hermit Thrush. Summer-blooming white flowers visited by native bees. Vigorous root crown resprouter after fire.

trees.desc: Stunning magenta-pink flowers on bare branches in early spring before leaves emerge. Heart-shaped leaves turn yellow in fall.
trees.hp: Visited by early-season bumble bees. Host for Henry's Elfin butterfly (Las Pilitas confirmed). Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

trees.desc: Produces the heaviest pine cone in the world — up to 11 lbs. Long gray-green needles in bundles of 3. Mountain slopes.

fm.Cones: Massive — to 11 lbs, heaviest pine cone on Earth
fm.Bark: Dark brown-gray, deeply furrowed — thick plates

trees.hp: Produces the heaviest pine cone on Earth — up to 11 lbs (Jepson eFlora). Fire-adapted thick bark. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine. Merriam's Chipmunk caches Coulter Pine seeds. Mountain Lion uses Coulter pine woodland as travel corridor between territories.

trees.desc: Mountain pine with butterscotch-vanilla scented bark. Stick your nose in the bark crevices to smell it. Three needles per bundle.

fm.Fire: Fire-adapted — thick bark, self-pruning lower limbs

trees.hp: Fire-adapted self-pruning. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine. Steller's Jay caches Jeffrey Pine seeds, sometimes forgetting cached seeds — unintentional tree planting. Mountain Chickadee — a montane cavity nester — forages for insects in Jeffrey Pine bark crevices.

trees.desc: Tallest pine in the world. Enormous hanging cones to 2 ft long. John Muir called it "the noblest pine." High San Gabriels.

fm.Bark: Red-brown, deeply furrowed into rectangular plates

trees.hp: John Muir called it 'the noblest pine.' Tallest pine species worldwide. Enormous cones to 2 ft. Fire ecology varies by species — some need fire for cone opening. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine. White-headed Woodpecker pries open cones for seeds — a montane pine specialist.

trees.desc: Aromatic cedar of mountain forests. Pencil-like scent when foliage is crushed. Flat fan-like sprays. Actually used to make pencils.
trees.hp: Important for pencil manufacture. Fire-resistant thick bark. Seeds eaten by Red Crossbill and Clark's Nutcracker. Dense foliage provides bird roosting cover. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake.

trees.desc: Deciduous riparian tree with smooth gray bark. Nitrogen-fixing root nodules enrich stream soils. Catkins in late winter.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer — enriches riparian soils

trees.hp: Important for stream shading and cooling. Host for swallowtail butterfly larvae. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Nitrogen fixer via Frankia actinomycete root nodules — enriches riparian soil. Resprouter after fire. California Newt breeds in pools beneath white alder canopy — toxic skin deters all predators except introduced crayfish. Pine Siskin feeds on alder catkins. Red-breasted Sapsucker drills sap wells in alder bark.

trees.desc: Flat-topped cream flower clusters followed by blue-black berries. Important wildlife food. Valley Elderberry Longhorn Beetle host.

fm.Ecology: Host for Valley Elderberry Longhorn Beetle (ESA)

trees.hp: Berries important for 30+ bird species. Flowers visited by native bees and flower beetles. Berries cooked into food and beverages by Tongva and Chumash (raw berries mildly toxic). Wood used for flutes and clapper sticks.

trees.desc: Only palm native to California. Natural groves at desert oases. Thatch skirt of dead fronds. Most "palm trees" in LA are imported species.

fm.Habitat: Desert oases — only native CA palm
fm.Bark: Stout, with persistent leaf base thatch skirt

trees.hp: Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and California Towhee. CA/AZ endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering. Only palm native to California. Hooded Oriole weaves pendant nest from palm fiber — obligate association in urban LA. Dead fronds provide critical roosting for Western Yellow Bat (state Species of Special Concern).

trees.desc: SoCal endemic conifer. Larger cones than Pacific Northwest Douglas-fir. Fire-adapted with thick bark. North-facing mountain slopes.

fm.Fire: Fire-adapted — thick bark provides resistance; survives moderate fire
fm.Bark: Thick, deeply furrowed red-brown — fire resistant

trees.hp: SoCal endemic conifer — fire-adapted with thick bark. Larger cones than Pacific Northwest Douglas-fir. Important raptor nesting habitat. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake.

trees.desc: Deciduous oak with distinctive blue-gray leaves. At southern range limit in northern LA County. Drought-adapted — drops leaves in severe drought.

fm.Habitat: Foothill woodland — at southern range edge

trees.hp: Acorns are keystone food for Acorn Woodpeckers, Scrub-Jays, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Host for California Sister butterfly. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Fire-resistant thick bark. Resprouts from crown after moderate fire. Lark Sparrow nests on ground beneath blue oak canopy in savanna habitat.

trees.desc: Gnarled desert tree-shrub with scale-like leaves and fleshy blue "berries" (actually cones). Desert edge of LA County.
trees.hp: Drought-tolerant. Seeds eaten by Red Crossbill and Clark's Nutcracker. Dense foliage provides bird roosting cover. Berries used as food flavoring and medicine by Cahuilla and other indigenous peoples. Wood used for bows and implements.

trees.desc: Iconic Mojave Desert tree-yucca at LA County's northeastern edge. Pollinated exclusively by Yucca Moth. Climate-threatened.

fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert woodland, northeast LA County

trees.hp: Obligate mutualism with Yucca Moth. Pollinated exclusively by Tegeticula synthetica. Desert Woodrat builds stick houses at base of Joshua trees. Desert Night Lizard (Xantusia vigilis) lives under exfoliating bark. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

trees.desc: White-pink flower spikes in spring. Toxic seeds. Summer-deciduous — drops leaves in drought. Ghostly silver bare trunks in summer.

fm.Behavior: Summer-deciduous — bare silvery trunks in drought
fm.Bark: Smooth, pale gray — silvery when leafless in summer

trees.hp: Toxic seeds. Host for Echo Blue and Spring Azure butterflies (Las Pilitas). Early-season bumble bee nectar. California buckeye — TOXIC to honey bees — the only native California plant known to kill them (UC Davis). Tongva leached toxic nuts for food.

trees.desc: Non-native Towering blue-gray bark tree planted since 1850s. Monarch roost sites but displaces natives.
trees.hp: Flowers visited by hummingbirds and honeybees. Non-native — may displace native vegetation. Monarch butterfly winter roosting — thousands cluster in eucalyptus groves along CA coast. Cooper's Hawk and Red-tailed Hawk nest in tall eucalyptus. Important urban canopy despite invasive status.

trees.desc: Non-native Purple flower canopy iconic to LA. South American. Unofficial symbol of the city.
trees.hp: Flowers attract native bees and hummingbirds. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat.

trees.desc: Invasive Graceful weeping tree with feathery compound leaves and pink peppercorn clusters. Planted by Spanish missions throughout California — now naturalized. Gnarled bark with age. Drought-tolerant but allergenic sap related to poison oak.

fm.Size: Medium tree with weeping habit, 10-15 m

trees.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Iconic LA street tree with weeping form. Displaces native vegetation on hillsides. Provides raptor nesting habitat. Fruit eaten by Cedar Waxwing, American Robin, and Northern Mockingbird and California Ground Squirrel and Brush Rabbit. Visited by native bees.

trees.desc: Invasive Gnarled Mediterranean tree planted across LA since the mission era. Gray-green evergreen foliage. Highly allergenic pollen in spring — some cities ban new plantings. Fruit requires processing before eating.

fm.Leaves: Opposite, lanceolate, gray-green above, silvery below
fm.Bark: Gray, gnarled and twisted with age

trees.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Mediterranean olive naturalizing on hillsides via bird-dispersed fruit. Dense shade excludes native understory. Pollen is significant allergen. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

trees.desc: Not a true willow. Showy orchid-like pink flowers. Desert washes. Hummingbird-pollinated.
trees.hp: Hummingbird-pollinated. Host for Catalpa Sphinx Moth. Not a true willow despite the name. Desert willow — important Costa's Hummingbird nectar in desert washes. Seeds wind-dispersed. Cahuilla used bark fiber for basketry and construction.

trees.desc: SoCal endemic deciduous oak. Blue-gray leaves. Fewer than 10,000 trees remain — among California's rarest oaks.
trees.hp: SoCal endemic — fewer than 10,000 trees remain (CNPS). Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species. Root crown resprouter. Fire-resistant bark on mature trees. Endangered — <1% of original range remains.

trees.desc: Tall mountain conifer with blue-green flat needles. Christmas tree shape. Upper montane forests of San Gabriel Mountains.

fm.Bark: Gray, smooth when young — thick corky furrows with age

trees.hp: Important timber tree. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Fire-sensitive — bark thickens with age. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Western Toad breeds in montane meadow pools near white fir forest. Declining due to chytrid fungus.

trees.desc: Slender mountain pine with short needles in pairs. At southern range limit on highest San Gabriel peaks.

fm.Habitat: Mountain peaks above 7,000 ft — southern range edge

trees.hp: Some populations have serotinous cones. Fire ecology varies by species — some need fire for cone opening. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Gnarled high-altitude pine with extremely flexible branches. Krummholz form near treeline. Mt. Baden-Powell and Baldy.

fm.Bark: Gray-white, smooth when young — furrowed when old

trees.hp: High-elevation specialist. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Tall riparian tree with balsam-scented buds in spring. Larger leaves than Fremont Cottonwood. Mountain streams.
trees.hp: Black cottonwood — sticky resinous buds. Host for Western Tiger Swallowtail, Mourning Cloak, Lorquin's Admiral. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Vigorous root crown resprouter after fire. Suckers from roots can form extensive clonal groves.

trees.desc: Only native maple in SoCal. Compound leaves unlike typical maples. Riparian corridors. Boxelder bugs congregate on trunks.

fm.Bark: Pale gray-green when young, furrowed with age

trees.hp: The only maple native to LA County lowlands. Seeds eaten by Evening Grosbeak. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Small deciduous tree with two-petaled white flowers. Dry foothill canyons. More drought-tolerant than other ashes.
trees.hp: California ash — only two-petaled ash species in the world. Host for Two-tailed Swallowtail butterfly. Visited by native bees. Important riparian wildlife corridor tree.

trees.desc: Invasive The tall slender palm lining LA boulevards. Mexican, not Californian. Taller and thinner than native Washingtonia filifera.

fm.Trunk: Slender gray, to 25 m, narrower than W. filifera
fm.Fruit: Small black drupes in hanging clusters
fm.Habitat: Riparian corridors; naturalizing from bird-dispersed seeds

trees.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Naturalizing in riparian corridors from bird-dispersed seeds. Dense stands alter hydrology and displace native willows and cottonwoods. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering. Hooded Oriole sews nest to underside of palm frond using plant fiber. Dead frond skirts provide roosting habitat for bats.

trees.desc: Non-native Massive palm with thick trunk and arching fronds. Planted throughout LA parks and estates. Not from California.

fm.Trunk: Stout, patterned with diamond-shaped leaf scars
fm.Habitat: Parks, streets; threatened by palm weevil

trees.hp: Iconic LA silhouette. Threatened by South American palm weevil. Dates eaten by birds. Fire hazard from accumulated dead fronds. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Non-native Massive buttress-rooted fig. Largest in LA is a 1874 specimen in Santa Monica. Strangler fig ecology in native range.

fm.Cultural: 1874 Santa Monica specimen is famous

trees.hp: Moreton Bay fig — massive buttressed roots. Australian. Dramatic specimen trees in LA parks (e.g., Santa Monica). Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Non-native Mediterranean evergreen with leathery pods. Pods are chocolate substitute. Seeds historically used as carat weight standard.

fm.Cultural: Seeds = original carat weight standard

trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Seeds and pods eaten by wildlife. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

trees.desc: Largest maple leaves of any maple species — up to 2 ft across. Confirmed at Chantry Flat and Santa Anita Canyon. Golden fall color. One of the few native trees offering real autumn display in LA County.

fm.Fall: Golden yellow — best fall color of LA native trees
fm.Habitat: Moist canyons — Chantry Flat, Santa Anita Canyon

trees.hp: Massive leaves support diverse epiphyte communities. Important for moths and beetle larvae. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Tall pine with puzzle-piece bark that smells like butterscotch or vanilla. Long needles in bundles of 3. Mid-elevation San Gabriels.
trees.hp: Massive tree with jigsaw-puzzle bark smelling of butterscotch. Fire-adapted thick bark. Most widely distributed NA pine. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Non-native Australian tree with ferny leaves and spectacular orange comb-like flowers. Widely planted as street tree across LA. Not a true oak.
trees.hp: Acorns are critical food for Acorn Woodpecker, Western Scrub-Jay, and Band-tailed Pigeon. Supports hundreds of insect species including moth larvae and gall wasps. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Smooth red peeling bark and evergreen leaves. At extreme southern range limit in LA County mountains. Iconic Pacific Northwest tree barely reaching SoCal.

fm.Habitat: Mountain north-facing slopes — at southern range edge

trees.hp: Evergreen. Berries important for band-tailed pigeon. Fire-sensitive. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake.

trees.desc: Non-native Hybrid plane tree dominating LA street plantings. Mottled bark like native Sycamore but with more uniform canopy. Highly allergenic.

fm.Origin: Hybrid — most planted LA street tree

trees.hp: Extremely pollution-tolerant. Major LA street tree. Visited by native bees when flowering. Fire-resistant thick bark on mature trees. Cavity-nesting habitat for woodpeckers, owls, and Wood Ducks.

trees.desc: Non-native Red cylindrical flower brushes. Australian native widely planted. Important urban hummingbird food year-round.
trees.hp: Australian. Major hummingbird nectar source in LA neighborhoods.

trees.desc: Non-native Dense evergreen street tree increasingly planted across LA. Australian. White flowers. Replacing aging Ficus as preferred city tree.

fm.Origin: Australian — increasingly popular LA street tree

trees.hp: Australian street tree with white flower clusters. Attracts honeybees. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds.

trees.desc: Non-native Dense evergreen fig with massive buttress roots that famously crack sidewalks. Asian origin. Among the most common LA street trees. Aerial roots.

fm.Roots: Massive surface buttress roots — crack sidewalks
fm.Origin: Asian — one of LA's most common street trees

trees.hp: Common LA street tree with curtain of aerial roots. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Relict Channel Islands endemic with ferny leaves and peeling red bark. Only native to Catalina, San Clemente, Santa Cruz, Santa Rosa Islands. Living fossil — closest relatives are 20 million year old fossils.
trees.hp: Channel Islands endemic — living fossil. Closest relatives are 20-million-year-old fossils. Provides nesting habitat for island endemic birds. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and beetles. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

trees.desc: Non-native Widely planted pine with naturally tiny range (3 mainland CA populations). Dense dark green canopy. Most planted pine worldwide for timber.

fm.Origin: Native CA but planted far outside range in LA

trees.hp: Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and chickarees. Provides nesting habitat for raptors and cavity nesters. Non-native but provides some urban wildlife habitat. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Rarest pine in North America — wild populations only at Torrey Pines and Santa Rosa Island. Long needles in bundles of 5. Planted as specimen across LA.

fm.Needles: 8–13 in, bundles of 5 — longest of any pine

trees.hp: Torrey pine — rarest pine in North America, native to only two locations (Torrey Pines and Santa Rosa Island). Seeds eaten by California Scrub-Jay and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi.

trees.desc: Invasive Feathery pink-flowered tree invading desert waterways. Drops salt on soil, excluding native plants. Major target of removal programs.

fm.Ecology: Drops salt — poisons soil for native plants. Massive water consumer.

trees.hp: Cal-IPC High. Salt-excreting leaves raise soil salinity. Massive water consumption. Active removal programs. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: At extreme southern range limit on Mt. San Antonio (Baldy). Few trees persist above 9,000 ft. Blue-green needles. Among the rarest trees in LA County.

fm.Habitat: Mt. Baldy above 9,000 ft — extreme southern range limit
fm.Conservation: Very few trees in LA County

trees.hp: High-elevation specialist at extreme southern range limit in San Gabriel Mountains. Sharp square needles. Cool-climate relict. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake.

trees.desc: Extremely rare native cypress at a few sites in the Santa Ana Mountains edge of LA County. Fire-dependent — serotinous cones open only after fire. Federally threatened.
trees.hp: Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and chickarees. Provides nesting habitat for raptors and cavity nesters. CA/Baja endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Dense foliage provides bird roosting cover.

trees.desc: Invasive Aggressive invasive tree with compound leaves that smell like rancid peanut butter when crushed. Grows through pavement. Root-suckers extensively. Impossible to eradicate once established. Origin of "A Tree Grows in Brooklyn."

fm.Leaves: Pinnately compound, 13-25 leaflets, glandular teeth at base
fm.Habitat: Disturbed urban areas, roadsides, riparian corridors

trees.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Tree-of-heaven — aggressive non-native producing allelopathic chemicals from roots that inhibit native plant growth. Vigorous root suckering. Difficult to eradicate. Displaces native species — active management needed. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Slender-leaved willow forming dense thickets along streams. Spreads aggressively by root suckers. Critical riparian habitat — first colonizer of sand and gravel bars after floods.
trees.hp: Important Mourning Cloak larval host. Bank stabilizer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Rapid resprouter after fire — among the first riparian trees to recover. Stems used in Tongva and Chumash basketry.

trees.desc: Low scrubby oak with thick leathery convex leaves. Rare in LA County — Griffith Park has unusual occurrences. More common on serpentine soils in NorCal.

fm.Habitat: Rocky slopes — rare in LA County; Griffith Park
fm.Conservation: Rare in LA — unusual disjunct occurrence

trees.hp: CNPS 4.2. Serpentine soil specialist at range edge in LA County. Important acorn producer for wildlife. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species.

trees.desc: Desert tree with distinctive spiral coiled bean pods. More compact than honey mesquite. Desert springs and washes with high water table.
trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Seeds and pods eaten by wildlife. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

trees.desc: Ash tree of desert washes and canyon bottoms. Velvety-hairy leaflets. More drought-tolerant than F. dipetala. SGM canyons and Antelope Valley edge.
trees.hp: Arizona ash — important riparian cavity nester habitat for Ash-throated Flycatcher, Western Screech-Owl, and Nuttall's Woodpecker. Browsed by Mule Deer. Fire-adapted — resprouts from root crown.

trees.desc: The largest native willow — to 60 ft. Dark deeply furrowed bark. Major riparian tree along LA rivers and larger streams. Provides critical shade for stream habitats.

fm.Habitat: Along rivers, larger streams — major riparian tree

trees.hp: Goodding's willow — largest native willow. Important raptor nesting tree. Host for butterfly larvae and moth species. Host for Mourning Cloak, Lorquin's Admiral, and Western Tiger Swallowtail butterflies. Critical riparian shade. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Rapid resprouter after fire — among the first riparian trees to recover. Stems used in Tongva and Chumash basketry. Yellow Warbler — a riparian breeding obligate — nests in willow canopy. Wilson's Warbler forages in willow understory during migration.

trees.desc: Tallest native cottonwood — can reach 100 ft. Sticky resinous buds with sweet balsam fragrance in spring. Mountain streams. Fast-growing pioneer species.
trees.hp: Balsam poplar — strongly balsam-scented buds. Host for swallowtail and admiral butterfly larvae. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Vigorous root crown resprouter after fire. Suckers from roots can form extensive clonal groves.

trees.desc: Only pine in the world with single needles (all other pines have 2+). Edible pine nuts — Paiute/Shoshone staple food. Desert-mountain transition. Round open crown.

fm.Needles: Single (unique — no other pine has this)
fm.Cultural: Major indigenous food source — Paiute pine nut harvest

trees.hp: Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and chickarees. Provides nesting habitat for raptors and cavity nesters. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Steller's Jay and Clark's Nutcracker cache pinyon pine nuts — critical indigenous food plant. Pinyon Jay (rare in LA County) is an irruptive specialist.

trees.desc: True coastal scrub oak — much rarer than Q. berberidifolia which is often misidentified as this species. CNPS 1B.1. Restricted to coastal sage scrub within a few miles of the ocean.

fm.Leaves: Small, holly-like, FLAT (not convex like Q. berberidifolia)
fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — within a few miles of ocean only
fm.Conservation: CNPS 1B.1 — much rarer than commonly realized. Often confused with Q. berberidifolia.
fm.vs: Inland Scrub Oak (Q. berberidifolia) is found far from coast, has convex spiny leaves, and is very common

trees.hp: CNPS 1B.1. Rare coastal scrub species declining due to development. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species.

trees.desc: Non-native Red berry clusters on weedy tree. Cal-IPC Limited. Related to CA Pepper Tree but more aggressive. Berries spread by birds. Contact can cause dermatitis in sensitive people — related to poison oak.

fm.Toxicity: Contact dermatitis in sensitive people — Anacardiaceae (poison oak family)

trees.hp: Bird-dispersed. Less common than S. Fruit eaten by Cedar Waxwing, American Robin, and Northern Mockingbird and California Ground Squirrel and Brush Rabbit. Visited by native bees.

trees.desc: Golden trembling leaves on white-barked tree. At extreme southern range limit on highest SGM peaks. Clonal — entire groves share one root system. The most widely distributed tree in North America.

fm.Fall: Brilliant gold — best fall color in SGM
fm.Habitat: SGM above 7,000 ft — extreme southern range limit

trees.hp: Clonal organism — entire groves connected by single root system. Host for Mourning Cloak and Tiger Swallowtail butterflies. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Vigorous root crown resprouter after fire. Suckers from roots can form extensive clonal groves.

trees.desc: Multi-stemmed alder of mountain streams above 5,000 ft. Distinguished from White Alder by hairy twigs and leaves. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer — enriches mountain stream soils

trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Host for various moth larvae. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake.

trees.desc: Large willow with glossy dark green leaves and prominent stipules at leaf base. Mountain streams. Distinguished from Red Willow by glossy leaves.
trees.hp: Host for Mourning Cloak, Lorquin's Admiral, and Western Tiger Swallowtail butterfly larvae. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Rapid resprouter after fire — among the first riparian trees to recover. Stems used in Tongva and Chumash basketry.

trees.desc: Deciduous mountain mahogany with birch-like leaves. More common at higher elevations than C. betuloides. Feathery spiral seed tails.
trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Hard wood used traditionally for tools. Feathery seed tails wind-dispersed. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and beetles. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

trees.desc: White flower racemes followed by dark red-black cherries. Important wildlife food. Mountain canyons and stream banks. Bark and seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides.

fm.Toxicity: Bark and seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides

trees.hp: Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, California Towhee, Cedar Waxwing, and Western Tanager. Fruit eaten fresh and kernels leached and ground into flour by Tongva. Important food plant. Gray Fox — the only canid that climbs trees — eats chokecherry fruit and caches manzanita berries. Host for Pale Swallowtail and Western Tiger Swallowtail butterfly larvae.

trees.desc: Non-native Massive evergreen with aromatic camphor-scented leaves. Asian origin — widely planted as street tree across LA. Dense shade. Naturalizing in some riparian areas.

fm.Leaves: Glossy, aromatic — camphor scent when crushed
fm.Origin: East Asian — common LA street tree

trees.hp: But provides significant urban canopy and shade. Fruits eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and California Towhee. Naturalizing in some riparian areas. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Non-native Mottled bark street tree — one of LA's most common planted trees. Small leaves, graceful form. Semi-evergreen in LA's mild climate. More resistant to Dutch Elm Disease than American Elm.

fm.Bark: Mottled — patches of gray, orange, brown (attractive)
fm.Origin: Chinese — extremely common LA street tree

trees.hp: But heavily used by urban birds for nesting. Mottled bark provides habitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Non-native Fast-growing South American shade tree with yellow flowers and helicopter-like winged seeds. Increasingly popular LA street tree. Nitrogen fixer. Aggressive surface roots.

fm.Origin: South American — popular LA street tree
fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Non-native but provides shade canopy and flower resources for native bees in urban areas.

trees.desc: Non-native Spectacular pink flowers on thorny green trunk in fall. South American. Silky floss from seed pods. Increasingly common LA ornamental — unmistakable fat thorny trunk.

fm.Trunk: Green, fat, covered in conical thorns

trees.hp: Fall-blooming non-native providing late-season nectar for hummingbirds and bees when few other trees flower.

trees.desc: Non-native World's tallest tree species. Not native to LA County but planted specimens thrive in moist canyons — some reaching 80+ ft. Natural range begins in Big Sur, 200 miles north.

fm.Habitat: Planted in moist canyons — not native to LA

trees.hp: Not native to LA (range begins in Big Sur) but planted specimens provide habitat for native birds and invertebrates. Fire-resistant bark. Seeds eaten by Red Crossbill and Clark's Nutcracker. Dense foliage provides bird roosting cover.

trees.desc: Non-native Distinctive flat-topped umbrella canopy — iconic Mediterranean pine planted throughout LA parks and boulevards. Edible pine nuts. Heavy cones can be hazardous.
trees.hp: But provides significant urban canopy. Seeds eaten by California Scrub-Jay, Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Gnarled mountain juniper at high elevations in SGM. More common in Sierra Nevada — at range edge in LA County. Blue berry-like cones. Extremely slow-growing.
trees.hp: Seeds dispersed by Cedar Waxwing, American Robin, and Phainopepla including Townsend's Solitaire. Provides nesting habitat for mountain birds. Very slow-growing — some individuals centuries old. Seeds eaten by Red Crossbill and Clark's Nutcracker. Dense foliage provides bird roosting cover. Berries used as food flavoring and medicine by Cahuilla and other indigenous peoples. Wood used for bows and implements.

trees.desc: Non-native Graceful Himalayan conifer with weeping branch tips — LA's most popular ornamental conifer. Massive specimens in Pasadena and Altadena neighborhoods. Blue-green needles.

fm.Origin: Himalayan — most popular ornamental conifer in LA

trees.hp: But provides significant urban habitat. Dense canopy used for raptor nesting. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel.

trees.desc: Non-native Mediterranean pine widely planted across LA — tolerates extreme drought. Light green needles in pairs. Asymmetric cones. Very common on hillsides and in parks.

fm.Habitat: Hillsides, parks — very common in LA

trees.hp: But heavily used by Cooper's Hawk and other raptors for nesting. Drought-tolerant urban canopy tree. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Southern Live Oak (Fagaceae). Fagaceae family species of LA County habitats.

fm.Leaves: Spiny-toothed evergreen, smaller than var. agrifolia

trees.hp: Interior live oak — adapted to drier inland valleys with smaller, spiny-margined leaves. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks. Fire-resistant thick bark.

trees.desc: Desert scrub oak of Mojave margin — at western range limit in northeastern LA County. Small spiny leaves. Extremely drought-tolerant. Named for pioneering CA oak botanist John Tucker.

fm.Habitat: Desert margin — northeastern LA County edge

trees.hp: Desert oak providing acorns for wildlife at Mojave-chaparral transition. Named for UC Davis botanist John Tucker. Acorns eaten by Acorn Woodpecker, California Scrub-Jay, Western Gray Squirrel, and Mule Deer. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Host for California Sister butterfly. Foundation woodland species.

trees.desc: Non-native Spectacular golden flower clusters in summer — one of LA's showiest ornamental trees. Brazilian origin. Increasingly popular street tree.
trees.hp: But provides important summer nectar for native bees and carpenter bees when few other trees flower.

trees.desc: Non-native Brilliant orange-red flowers on bare branches in late winter — official tree of the City of Los Angeles. South African. Soft corky bark. Thorny branches.

fm.Cultural: Official tree of the City of Los Angeles

trees.hp: Official tree of LA. Critical winter nectar for Anna's Hummingbird during breeding season.

trees.desc: Non-native common fig (Moraceae). common fig naturalizing aggressively in riparian corridors.

fm.Bark: Smooth gray, becomes furrowed with age
fm.Size: Spreading tree or large shrub, 3-10 m

trees.hp: Common fig naturalizing aggressively in riparian corridors. Fruits consumed and dispersed by birds and mammals. Dense canopy shades out native riparian vegetation. Important wildlife food tree. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Gray pine (Pinaceae). Gray pine (formerly digger pine — name changed due to cultural insensitivity).

fm.Needles: Gray-green, in bundles of 3, drooping, 20-30 cm
fm.Cones: Very large heavy cones, 15-25 cm, armed with hooks

trees.hp: Distinctive gray-green foliage. Large heavy cones. At southern range limit on SGM north slopes. Fire ecology varies by species — some need fire for cone opening. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Non-native Canary Island pine (Pinaceae). tall columnar form widely planted in LA.

fm.Needles: In bundles of 3, dark green, 20-30 cm

trees.hp: Fire-adapted — resprouts from trunk after burning. Important raptor nesting tree. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Non-native Paper mulberry (Moraceae). paper mulberry naturalizing in riparian areas.

fm.Leaves: Variably lobed, rough sandpapery above, velvety below
fm.Bark: Pale tan, inner bark fibrous (tapa cloth)

trees.hp: Paper mulberry naturalizing in riparian areas. Bark used for traditional tapa cloth in Polynesia. Wind-pollinated — significant allergen. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Non-native Roxburgh fig (Moraceae). large-leaved tropical fig occasionally naturalizing near urban plantings.

fm.Leaves: Very large, heart-shaped, to 40 cm, rough-textured
fm.Fruit: Cauliflorous — figs grow directly on trunk, 5-8 cm

trees.hp: Large-leaved tropical fig occasionally naturalizing near urban plantings. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Non-native white mulberry (Moraceae). among the worst pollen allergen trees in LA.

fm.Leaves: Variable — unlobed to deeply lobed, glossy green

trees.hp: Among the worst pollen allergen trees in LA. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Cedar Waxwing, and Northern Mockingbird. Hybridizes with native M. rubra. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Knobcone pine (Pinaceae). Serotinous cones remain closed for years, opening only after fire.

fm.Cones: Asymmetric, serotinous — remain closed for years

trees.hp: Serotinous cones remain closed for years, opening only after fire. Fire-dependent reproduction. At extreme southern range limit in LA County. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Non-native Korean mulberry (Moraceae). dense shade tree with edible fruit.
trees.hp: Dense shade tree with edible fruit. Less common than M. Important bird food. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Non-native Sacred fig (Moraceae). sacred fig (Bodhi tree) — heart-shaped leaves with long drip tip.

fm.Leaves: Heart-shaped with distinctive long drip tip, 10-17 cm
fm.Size: Large spreading tree, 15-30 m, often with aerial roots
fm.Habitat: Temple gardens; religious significance in Buddhism
