trees.hp: Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Arizona cypress (Cupressaceae). blue-gray foliage.

fm.Habitat: Planted as windbreak; native to AZ mountains

trees.hp: Planted as ornamental and windbreak. Native to AZ, at edge of natural range. Seeds eaten by Red Crossbill and Clark's Nutcracker. Dense foliage provides nesting and roosting cover for birds.

trees.desc: Smooth Arizona cypress (Cupressaceae). similar to H.
trees.hp: Arizonica but with smooth (not rough) bark. Seeds eaten by Red Crossbill and Clark's Nutcracker. Dense foliage provides nesting and roosting cover for birds.

trees.desc: Non-native hairy crabweed (Moraceae). small Asian mulberry relative spreading rapidly as urban weed.
trees.hp: Small Asian mulberry relative spreading rapidly as urban weed. Explosive seed dispersal. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Non-native Siberian elm (Ulmaceae). tough drought-tolerant street tree.

fm.Leaves: Small, elliptic, nearly symmetrical base (unlike most elms)
fm.Flowers: Small clusters before leaves, late winter

trees.hp: Tough drought-tolerant street tree. Seeds prolifically. Wood brittle — storm damage risk. Provides nesting habitat for birds. Visited by native bees when flowering.

trees.desc: Non-native Indian banyan (Moraceae). massive spreading tree with aerial roots.

fm.Trunk: Massive with aerial prop roots forming secondary trunks

trees.hp: Massive spreading tree with aerial roots. Can grow to enormous size. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Monterey Cypress (Cupressaceae). naturally restricted to two groves near Monterey but widely planted.

fm.Habitat: Coastal plantings; native to Monterey coast only

trees.hp: Naturally restricted to two groves near Monterey but widely planted. Iconic windswept form. Seeds eaten by Red Crossbill and Clark's Nutcracker. Dense foliage provides nesting and roosting cover for birds.

trees.desc: Non-native Scots pine (Pinaceae). European.  Orange upper bark distinctive.

fm.Needles: Blue-green, in bundles of 2, 3-7 cm, twisted

trees.hp: European. Fire ecology varies by species — some need fire for cone opening. Seeds eaten by Clark's Nutcracker, Red Crossbill, and Western Gray Squirrel. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi. Provides critical montane wildlife habitat. Pine nuts harvested as food by Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples. Pitch used as waterproof adhesive and medicine.

trees.desc: Non-native Common Wild Fig (Moraceae). African strangler fig.

fm.Trunk: Smooth gray, sometimes with aerial roots

trees.hp: African strangler fig. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Non-native Port Jackson fig (Moraceae). Australian fig with rusty-hairy leaves.
trees.hp: Planted as street tree in some LA neighborhoods. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Pollinated by species-specific fig wasps — fascinating mutualism.

trees.desc: Non-native black locust (Fabaceae). nitrogen fixer with fragrant white flower clusters.

fm.Flowers: Fragrant white pea-flowers in pendant racemes, Apr-Jun
fm.Thorns: Paired stipular spines at leaf bases

trees.hp: Nitrogen fixer with fragrant white flower clusters. Aggressively suckering from roots. Hard rot-resistant wood used for fence posts. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

trees.desc: Non-native Coast Coral Tree (Fabaceae). red flowers.

fm.Flowers: Orange-red tubular, in dense clusters, winter-spring

trees.hp: South African. Less common than E. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Important ecosystem service provider.

trees.desc: Non-native honey locust (Fabaceae). long twisted pods.

fm.Leaves: Pinnately or bipinnately compound, small leaflets
fm.Bark: Gray-brown with clusters of branching thorns (wild type)
fm.Fruit: Long twisted brown pods to 45 cm
fm.Habitat: Street tree; thornless cultivars common in LA

trees.hp: Eastern US native planted as street tree. Thornless cultivars common. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria — enriches soil. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Important ecosystem service provider.

shrubs.desc: The signature scent of coastal sage scrub. Gray-green threadlike aromatic leaves. Drought-deciduous in summer. Foundation species of CSS.

fm.Ecology: Drought-deciduous — drops leaves in summer
fm.Fire: Resprouter — weak; also colonizes from seed after fire

shrubs.hp: Foundation species for Coastal California Gnatcatcher (federally threatened) habitat. Signature scent of coastal sage scrub. Host for many moth species. Drought-deciduous. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Obligate seeder — killed by fire. Rapid post-fire recruitment from smoke-stimulated seed. Wrentit (chaparral obligate) depends entirely on sage scrub — pairs defend 1-acre territories for life and never leave the scrub. Southern Alligator Lizard hunts insects and spiders under sage canopy. Western Whiptail forages in open ground between shrubs. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco. Lazuli Bunting breeds in dense sage scrub (Apr–Aug). Orange-crowned Warbler nests in sagebrush thickets.

shrubs.desc: Pioneer colonizer of disturbed ground. Dioecious — female plants produce cotton-like seed puffs. Wind-dispersed.

fm.Ecology: Pioneer species — first to colonize bare ground
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown; also seeds vigorously into burned areas

shrubs.hp: Coyote brush — dioecious pioneer after fire and disturbance. Fall-blooming — critical late-season nectar for migrating Painted Lady and native bees when almost nothing else flowers. Wrentit nests in dense stands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Most widespread chaparral shrub in California. Needle-like leaves. Highly flammable but resprouts vigorously after fire.

fm.Habitat: Chaparral — most widespread CA chaparral shrub

shrubs.hp: Most widespread chaparral shrub in California — foundation species. Host for Ceanothus Silk Moth. Facultative seeder — resprouts from burl AND seeds germinate from heat/smoke (Keeley 1991). Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Fire-adapted resprouter. Host plant for Gray Hairstreak butterfly larvae. Wrentit nests deep inside dense chamise. California Thrasher forages under chamise canopy. Mountain Quail forages in chamise understory at higher elevations.

shrubs.desc: Evergreen shrub with taco-folded aromatic leaves. White flower plumes. Frost-sensitive — its distribution marks mild coastal climate zone.

fm.Ecology: Frost-sensitive — climate indicator

shrubs.hp: Frost-sensitive — distribution marks mild coastal climate zone. Taco-shaped leaf folds are diagnostic. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Visited by native bees. Bark and leaves used medicinally by Chumash for skin conditions. Southern Pacific Rattlesnake shelters in leaf litter under laurel sumac. Coast Horned Lizard forages for harvester ants in adjacent open ground. Wrentit and California Thrasher nest in dense laurel sumac thickets. One of the fastest chaparral species to recover after fire — resprouts from massive lignotuber within weeks.

shrubs.desc: Coastal evergreen with sticky reddish berries. Berry coating tastes like lemon when soaked in water — hence the name. Important bird food.
shrubs.hp: Berries important for Northern Mockingbird, robin, and wrentit. Early-season bee forage (Feb–Apr bloom). Visited by native bees. Berries eaten by birds. Root crown resprouter. Foundation coastal sage scrub species. Berries soaked to make tart lemonade-like beverage by Tongva and Chumash. California Kingsnake shelters under lemonade berry thickets — immune to rattlesnake venom, it preys on Southern Pacific Rattlesnake. Spotted Towhee and Dark-eyed Junco forage in leaf litter under lemonade berry. California Gnatcatcher (ESA threatened) uses dense stands for cover. Hermit Thrush eats lemonade berry drupes in winter.

shrubs.desc: Inland relative of Lemonade Berry with ovate folded leaves. Similar lemon-flavored berries. Chaparral and sage scrub.
shrubs.hp: Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and California Towhee including Northern Mockingbird, robin, and wrentit. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery. Visited by native bees. Important for erosion control. Berries soaked to make tart lemonade-like beverage (Indian lemonade) by Tongva and Chumash.

shrubs.desc: Smooth red bark on twisted trunks. Urn-shaped white-pink flowers. Large red berries. Classic chaparral species — the name means "little apple."

fm.Fire: Resprouter — basal burl; also seeds germinated by fire
fm.Cultural: Manzanita = "little apple" (Spanish)

shrubs.hp: Nectar for Monarch, Painted Lady, California Tortoiseshell, and Mourning Cloak butterflies. Early-season bumble bee forage — flowers Dec–Feb when almost nothing else blooms. Berries eaten by American Black Bear, coyotes, California Thrasher, Spotted Towhee, and American Robin. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Fire ecology varies by species — some (A. glauca) are obligate seeders killed by fire, others (A. glandulosa) resprout from basal burl. Coastal Rosy Boa shelters in rock crevices among manzanita roots. Gopher Snake hunts Botta's Pocket Gopher through burrow systems beneath chaparral. Manzanita mason bee (Osmia ribifloris) — a specialist solitary bee — times its emergence to manzanita bloom and nests in hollow stems. Fox Sparrow (winter) scratches for seeds and insects in leaf litter under manzanita.

shrubs.desc: Blue-green bark and dense pale blue flower clusters. Nitrogen-fixing. Deer browse resistant. One of the largest Ceanothus species.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Unusual for Ceanothus — resprouts from lignotuber (most Ceanothus are obligate seeders killed by fire). Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell. Host plant for Echo Blue and Hedgerow Hairstreak butterfly larvae.

shrubs.desc: Deep blue flower clusters in spring — among the most intensely blue flowers in the chaparral. Nitrogen-fixing root nodules.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; heat-activated seed bank

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Obligate seeder — killed by fire; heat-activated seed bank. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell. Host for Pale Swallowtail and California Tortoiseshell butterfly larvae. Elegant Sheep Moth larvae also feed on Ceanothus. Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) cocoons overwinter on branches.

shrubs.desc: Evergreen cherry with holly-like spiny leaves. Small white flower clusters. Dark red cherries eaten by birds. Dense shade-casting.
shrubs.hp: Dark red cherries eaten by Band-tailed Pigeon, Northern Mockingbird, and other American Robin, Cedar Waxwing, and Band-tailed Pigeon. Host for Pale Swallowtail butterfly (Las Pilitas). Fire-adapted resprouter. Fruit flesh eaten fresh by Tongva. Pits leached and ground into flour — important food source. Black-headed Grosbeak feeds on holly-leaved cherry fruit. Bullock's Oriole nests in outer canopy branches. Western Tent Caterpillar (Malacosoma californicum) forms silk tent colonies on holly-leaved cherry in spring. California Thrasher eats cherry fruit. Ash-throated Flycatcher nests in hollow trunks.

shrubs.desc: Rust-brown flower heads that persist year-round. Foundation plant of coastal sage scrub. Critical for native butterflies and bees.

fm.Flowers: White to pink clusters, turning rust
fm.Ecology: Foundation species for pollinators — critical for native bees and butterflies

shrubs.hp: Host for Bramble Hairstreak, Mormon Metalmark, and many other butterflies. Foundation nectar source for native bees. Facultative seeder — some individuals resprout from root crown, others recruit from seed after fire. Tongva brewed flowers and stems into tea. Seeds ground into flour. Seeds primarily ant-dispersed (myrmecochory) — ants carry seeds to nests, eat the elaiosome, discard the seed in nutrient-rich waste. Seeds eaten by Lawrence's Goldfinch — tracks buckwheat crops across chaparral landscapes. Dark-eyed Junco forages under buckwheat stands. Critical late-season forage for Crotch bumble bee (Bombus crotchii) — a California candidate for endangered listing. Host plant for Gray Hairstreak butterfly larvae (Strymon melinus).

shrubs.desc: Gray-purple aromatic sage of coastal scrub. Produces allelopathic chemicals that inhibit grass growth. Important bee plant.

fm.Leaves: GRAY-WHITE, woolly-hairy (much lighter than S. mellifera)
fm.Ecology: Allelopathic — inhibits nearby grasses
fm.Habitat: Dry open hills, CSS, chaparral — below 2,500 ft
fm.vs: Black Sage (S. mellifera) has pale BLUE-WHITE flowers and DARK GREEN wrinkled leaves

shrubs.hp: Produces allelopathic chemicals from roots that inhibit grass growth in surrounding soil — creates bare 'halo' zones around shrubs. Important bee forage. Fire-adapted resprouter. Visited by hummingbirds (especially Anna's Hummingbird), carpenter bees, and bumble bees. Aromatic oils attract pollinators. Wrentit nests in dense sage stands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch. Rufous-crowned Sparrow breeds in purple sage habitat.

shrubs.desc: Willow-like riparian shrub. Among first plants to colonize gravel bars. Important soil stabilizer. Dense white flower clusters.

fm.Ecology: Critical streambank stabilizer
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous root crown and rhizome sprouting

shrubs.hp: Mulefat — among the first plants to colonize gravel bars after floods. Black Phoebe perches on streamside mulefat to hawk insects. Important Lorquin's Admiral butterfly nectar source. Tongva used stems for arrow shafts. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Yellow flowers and inflated balloon-like seed pods. Drought-deciduous. Desert edges and coastal bluffs. Malodorous foliage.
shrubs.hp: Important butterfly nectar plant in coastal sage scrub. Visited by many pollinator species. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds feed heavily on bladderpod nectar (LA Audubon). Unusual spider-like flowers.

shrubs.desc: Yellow flowers in fall when most shrubs are done blooming. Important late-season nectar. Coastal and disturbed areas.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; colonizes burns

shrubs.hp: Goldenbush — critical fall nectar source (Sep–Nov) for Painted Lady, bumble bees, and syrphid flies. California Gnatcatcher (federally threatened) depends on goldenbush-dominated coastal sage scrub. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: "Holy herb" — sticky aromatic leaves used medicinally by indigenous peoples. Lavender tubular flowers. Post-fire resprouter.

fm.Cultural: Yerba Santa = "holy herb" (Spanish)
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown and rhizome sprouting

shrubs.hp: Top-ranking nectar plant for adult butterflies (Las Pilitas top 5). Visited by native bees and butterflies. Visited by specialist bee Habropoda depressa — a large fuzzy bee that depends on yerba santa pollen. Thick-leaved yerba santa — Chumash and Tongva brewed leaf tea for respiratory ailments ('sacred herb'). Vigorous resprouter from rhizomes after fire.

shrubs.desc: Hard-wooded chaparral shrub with feathery spiral-tailed seeds. Among the hardest woods of any CA plant. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer
fm.Color: Inconspicuous — feathery seed tails are showier

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Among the hardest woods of any CA plant — traditionally used for tools. Feathery spiral-tailed seeds are diagnostic. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and beetles. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Root crown resprouter. Mountain mahogany — Bushtit forages extensively for insects on leaves and twigs. Extremely hard wood — 'mountain ironwood.' Browsed by Mule Deer. Seeds wind-dispersed by feathery twisted awns.

shrubs.desc: Glossy evergreen shrub with coffee-bean-shaped berries turning red to black. Bird food. Shade-tolerant understory species.
shrubs.hp: Berries ripen green → red → black sequentially — important multi-season bird food. Shade-tolerant understory species. Fire-adapted resprouter. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Berries eaten by Cedar Waxwing, American Robin, and Band-tailed Pigeon.

shrubs.desc: Dramatic hanging catkin chains in winter — male plants with catkins to 8 in. Evergreen. Dry slopes.
shrubs.hp: Silk-tassel — long pendant catkins are wind-pollinated. Bitter fruit eaten by Band-tailed Pigeon and American Robin. Dark-eyed Junco nests in dense foliage. Tongva used bark medicinally.

shrubs.desc: Bright yellow four-petaled poppy flowers on an evergreen shrub. Fire follower — seeds require fire or smoke to germinate.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower — seeds need smoke/heat
fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds require fire or smoke to germinate

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Pollen source for native bees (no nectar). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

shrubs.desc: The smell of desert rain. Clonal rings to 11,000 years old (King Clone).

fm.Ecology: Clonal rings to 11,000 years old
fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown sprouting; clonal ring expansion

shrubs.hp: Foundation species of Mojave Desert scrub. Clonal rings live to 11,000 years (King Clone — oldest living organism). Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Pollinated by specialist Colletes and Andrena bees — over 20 bee species depend on creosote for pollen. Le Conte's Thrasher — desert obligate — forages under creosote canopy. Verdin builds round stick nests in creosote branches — multiple nests used for roosting and breeding. Say's Phoebe hunts insects from creosote perches.

shrubs.desc: Spectacular large golden-yellow flowers — among the showiest native shrubs. Dense stellate (star-shaped) hairs covering leaves and stems are highly irritating to skin and eyes. Fire follower. California endemic. Chaparral slopes.

fm.Ecology: Fire follower
fm.Fire: Fire follower — seeds require heat/smoke to germinate; killed by fire but seed bank persists

shrubs.hp: Fire follower — seeds require heat/smoke. CA endemic. Host for Painted Lady and West Coast Lady butterflies. Visited by native bees. Flannel bush — stellate hairs cause severe skin irritation. Important bumble bee forage — some of the showiest native flowers. Fire-adapted resprouter from root crown.

shrubs.desc: "Leaves of three, let it be." Urushiol causes severe dermatitis. Brilliant red fall color.

fm.Leaves: Trifoliate — "leaves of three, let it be." Terminal leaflet has a STALK (vs sessile in Skunkbrush).
fm.Toxicity: Urushiol oil — severe contact dermatitis in 85% of people. All parts toxic year-round.
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous regrowth. Urushiol in smoke causes lung damage.
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, chaparral, riparian — very common
fm.Fruit: White berries — important bird food (birds immune)
fm.vs: Skunkbrush (Rhus trilobata) also has 3 leaflets but terminal leaflet is SESSILE (no stalk), leaves are smaller, and crushed leaves smell skunky. Skunkbrush is harmless.

shrubs.hp: Berries are critical fall/winter food for American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and California Towhee (birds immune to urushiol). Host for Spring Azure butterfly. Smoke inhalation causes lung damage. Western Fence Lizard and side-blotched lizard shelter in poison oak thickets. Yellow-rumped Warbler eats poison oak berries in winter — unaffected by urushiol.

shrubs.desc: Red-barked chaparral shrub with shredding bark that peels in long strips — diagnostic field mark. More open growth form than its relative Chamise. Higher elevations. Aromatic resinous foliage. Resprouts vigorously after fire.
shrubs.hp: Higher elevation than chamise. Resprouter from basal burl. Important bee forage — late-summer bloomer (Jul–Sep). Foundation chaparral species. Visited by small native bees and syrphid flies. Fire-adapted resprouter. Host plant for Gray Hairstreak butterfly larvae. Gray Fox dens in rock outcrops within red shanks chaparral. Merriam's Chipmunk forages among shrubs at elevation.

shrubs.desc: Intensely fragrant blue-purple sage. Possibly the most aromatic California native. Popular garden plant.

fm.Fragrance: Intensely aromatic — most fragrant native sage

shrubs.hp: Cleveland sage — the most intensely aromatic native sage. Critical hummingbird (Anna's Hummingbird) and carpenter bee (Xylocopa) nectar plant. Important native garden restoration species. Killed by fire but rapidly recolonizes from seed. Important post-fire nectar source for pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Wine-red fragrant flowers. Crushed bark smells like cinnamon. Rare in LA County — moist mountain canyons only.

fm.Habitat: Moist mountain canyons — very rare in LA

shrubs.hp: Seeds dispersed by water. Indigenous medicinal plant. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Pink drooping flower clusters in fall-winter — one of the earliest-blooming shrubs. Important for Anna's Hummingbird.
shrubs.hp: Critical for Anna's Hummingbird which breeds in winter. Important early-season nectar when few other plants flower. Resprouter from root crown after fire. Among the first shrubs to flower post-fire, providing early nectar.

shrubs.desc: Bright red fuchsia-like flowers on thorny stems. Hummingbird specialist. Among the showiest native shrubs.
shrubs.hp: Critical winter nectar for Anna's and Allen's Hummingbirds when almost nothing else blooms. CA endemic. Resprouter from root crown after fire. Among the first shrubs to flower post-fire, providing early nectar.

shrubs.desc: Large gray-green shrub of alkaline and coastal areas. Important Quail habitat. Salt-tolerant. Used in restoration of degraded sites.

fm.Ecology: Important Quail habitat — salt-tolerant

shrubs.hp: Quail bush — critical cover and food for California Quail and Gambel's Quail. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow. Important habitat structure for California Gnatcatcher in alkaline sage scrub.

shrubs.desc: Tree-like daisy to 10 ft with succulent trunk. Channel Islands and coastal bluffs. Spectacular in bloom — looks like a yellow coral tree.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs — Channel Islands and mainland

shrubs.hp: Giant coreopsis — tree-like succulent Asteraceae of coastal bluffs. Important early-season nectar for Anna's Hummingbird, native bees, and Painted Lady butterflies. Channel Islands and mainland sea-bluff specialist. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: White-flowered ceanothus of chaparral. Thick leathery leaves with white undersides. Obligate seeder — killed by fire but regenerates from seed bank.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire, regenerates from seed

shrubs.hp: Obligate seeder — killed by fire, regenerates from soil seed bank. Heat-activated seeds. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell. Host plant for Echo Blue and Hedgerow Hairstreak butterfly larvae. Wrentit nests deep inside hoaryleaf ceanothus. California Thrasher forages underneath. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule actinomycetes.

shrubs.desc: Dense green broom-like shrub of desert edges. Nearly leafless — photosynthesizes through green stems.
shrubs.hp: Important late-season nectar when few other plants bloom. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Green broom-like shrub of dry washes. Scale-like leaves. Important habitat for Coastal California Gnatcatcher.

fm.Ecology: Gnatcatcher habitat

shrubs.hp: Critical habitat for Coastal California Gnatcatcher (ESA threatened). Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Thorny coastal shrub with small purple flowers and red berries. Related to goji berry. Salt-tolerant.
shrubs.hp: Fleshy red berries important for desert birds. Salt-tolerant. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Visited by native bees. Provides nesting habitat in thorny branches.

shrubs.desc: Low gray desert shrub. Dominant co-dominant with Creosote Bush in Mojave scrub. Among the most drought-tolerant plants known.

fm.Ecology: Co-dominant with Creosote in Mojave

shrubs.hp: Dominant low shrub of Mojave Desert. Important habitat structure for desert Western Fence Lizard and side-blotched lizard and kangaroo rats. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch, American Goldfinch, Song Sparrow, and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green desert shrub of alkaline flats. Important wildlife browse. Extremely salt and drought tolerant.

fm.Ecology: Important wildlife browse

shrubs.hp: Many-seeded. Important browse for desert bighorn sheep. Extremely salt-tolerant. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail.

shrubs.desc: Thorny desert shrub with cat-claw-shaped thorns. Yellow puffball flowers. Important bee forage. Desert washes.

fm.Ecology: Important bee forage

shrubs.hp: Curved spines catch on clothing ('wait-a-minute' bush). Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

shrubs.desc: Wiry green shrub of desert washes. Named for cheese-wheel-shaped fruit. Wind-pollinated — major hay fever plant in desert areas.

fm.Pollen: Major hay fever source in desert

shrubs.hp: Cheesebush — wind-pollinated with pungent odor. Pioneer on disturbed desert soils. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Desert shrub with inflated papery calyx enclosing seeds. Purple-blue flowers. Dry rocky desert slopes.
shrubs.hp: Mexican bladder sage — inflated papery seed pods. Unusual for the mint family. Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils attract pollinators and deter herbivores.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green desert shrub with distinctive four-winged fruit. Extremely salt and drought tolerant. Important browse for wildlife and livestock.
shrubs.hp: Alkali-tolerant. Browse for Pronghorn. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Important desert wildlife browse.

shrubs.desc: Intensely fragrant gray-leaved desert shrub. Purple flower spikes. Strong lavender scent — attracts swarms of native bees. Desert washes and rocky slopes.
shrubs.hp: Frost-sensitive — indicator of mild microclimates. Important native bee and hummingbird forage. Aromatic oils deter herbivores.

shrubs.desc: Evergreen shrub with holly-like spiny leaves and small red berries. Important bird food. Dense chaparral component.
shrubs.hp: Holly-like leaves with red berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Visited by native bees. Host plant for Pale Swallowtail butterfly larvae. Root crown resprouter after fire.

shrubs.desc: Thorny pale-barked ceanothus with blue-white flower clusters. White bark diagnostic — ghost-like in chaparral. Important for nitrogen cycling.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: White bark diagnostic — 'ghost ceanothus.' Nitrogen fixer. Resprouter from root crown (unusual thorny Ceanothus). Important post-fire habitat structure. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell.

shrubs.desc: Inconspicuous maroon flowers but one of the most important native bee plants. Square stems. Woodland shade. Blooms in winter when little else does.

fm.Ecology: Premier winter bee forage — blooms when nothing else does

shrubs.hp: Critical early-season nectar for native bumble bees and carpenter bees when few other plants bloom. Host plant for Chalcedon Checkerspot butterfly — one of its most important host plants in SoCal.

shrubs.desc: Compact rosette succulent of desert slopes. Massive flower stalk 8–15 ft after 20+ years, then dies (monocarpic). Indigenous food plant — hearts roasted in pit ovens.

fm.Lifecycle: Monocarpic — blooms once after 20+ years, then dies
fm.Cultural: Hearts roasted in pit ovens (indigenous food)

shrubs.hp: Massive rosette blooming once after decades then dying. Important hummingbird nectar. Indigenous food plant (roasted hearts). Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Yellow pea flowers on green stems forming dense thickets that exclude all native plants. Mediterranean invasive. Seeds remain viable in soil for decades. Active removal programs across California.

fm.Ecology: Seeds viable 30+ years in soil bank; allelopathic
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — burns hot; reseeds aggressively after fire
fm.Habitat: Coastal slopes and disturbed woodland; invasive in natural areas

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen-fixing shrub forming impenetrable thickets. Fire hazard. Major invasive across coastal California. Visited by native bees. Invasive broom species — Cal-IPC concern.

shrubs.desc: Small evergreen shrub with bright red berries. Important bird food. Spiny stems. Very common in chaparral understory.

fm.Fruit: Small bright red berries — bird food

shrubs.hp: Important bird food. Provides protective nesting cover. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Visited by native bees. Nitrogen fixer via root nodules. Host plant for Pale Swallowtail butterfly larvae. Root crown resprouter after fire.

shrubs.desc: Evergreen shrub with red shredding bark and small urn-shaped flowers. Red berries. Chaparral in Santa Monica Mountains. Less common than manzanita.
shrubs.hp: Summer holly — one of few chaparral species with fleshy fruit. Berries eaten by California Thrasher, Spotted Towhee, and American Robin. Fire-adapted — resprouts from massive basal burl.

shrubs.desc: Deciduous shrub with three-lobed leaves resembling poison oak — but not toxic. Crushed leaves smell skunky. Red berries used by Tongva to make lemonade-like drink. Important basketry material.

fm.Leaves: Three-lobed — resembles poison oak but terminal leaflet is SESSILE (no stalk) vs stalked in Poison Oak
fm.Fragrance: Skunky when crushed (Poison Oak has no odor)
fm.Cultural: Tongva basketry material and beverage plant
fm.vs: Poison Oak has a STALKED terminal leaflet, no odor when crushed, and causes severe rash. Skunkbrush is harmless.

shrubs.hp: Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and California Towhee including Northern Mockingbird, robin, and wrentit. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples. Visited by native bees. Important for erosion control.

shrubs.desc: White-flowered ceanothus dominating the seaward slopes of the Santa Monica Mountains. Large pods. Among the most common chaparral shrubs on south-facing SMM slopes. Nitrogen fixer.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds require fire/heat to germinate

shrubs.hp: Obligate seeder — killed by fire but seedlings better adapted to post-fire drought than resprouting species (Frazer & Davis 1988). Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell. Host plant for Echo Blue and Hedgerow Hairstreak butterfly larvae. Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) — large green caterpillars feed on leaves; adults are enormous (5-inch wingspan) with crescent eyespots. Fox Sparrow (winter) scratches in leaf litter under bigpod ceanothus.

shrubs.desc: Inconspicuous fall-blooming shrub with cream-colored flower clusters. Important late-season nectar for butterflies and bees. Dry washes and rocky slopes.
shrubs.hp: California brickellbush of chaparral and woodland. Important late-season (fall) nectar source for migrating native bees and butterflies when few other plants bloom. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Small yellow-flowered subshrub of dry slopes. Sticky resinous stems. Very common in coastal sage scrub and chaparral openings. Late summer bloomer.
shrubs.hp: Matchweed — sticky resinous stems historically used as fire-starting tinder by indigenous peoples. Late-season nectar for native bees and syrphid flies. Rangeland indicator species. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Low spreading shrub with small pink bell flowers and waxy white berries. Shady chaparral and oak woodland understory. Berries mildly toxic but important for birds.
shrubs.hp: Important food for Hermit Thrush and Cedar Waxwing. Spreads via stolons. Nectar source for hummingbirds. Berries eaten by birds.

shrubs.desc: Tall herb of dry stream beds with clusters of tiny yellow-green flowers. Unusual — one of only two species in its family worldwide. Source of yellow dye.

fm.Cultural: Source of yellow dye
fm.Ecology: One of only two species in family Datiscaceae worldwide

shrubs.hp: Unusual riparian herb with yellowish-green flowers. Traditional indigenous medicine. Nitrogen-fixing root nodules. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Thorny white-flowered ceanothus dominating post-fire montane chaparral. Dense impenetrable thickets at 5,000–9,000 ft. Nitrogen fixer. Key component of SGM upper montane.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer. Dominant post-fire upper montane shrub.
fm.Habitat: Montane chaparral 5,000–9,000 ft — San Gabriel Mts
fm.Fire: Resprouter — vigorous root crown sprouting after fire

shrubs.hp: Dominant post-fire montane shrub — forms impenetrable thickets. Nitrogen fixer. White flowers visited by mountain bumble bees.

shrubs.desc: Golden-backed evergreen leaves with spiny-burred fruits. Mountain chaparral. Closely related to golden chinquapin trees of NorCal. Key SGM montane shrub.

fm.Habitat: Montane chaparral, rocky ridges — San Gabriel Mts

shrubs.hp: Edible nuts in spiny burs. Important food for Clark's Nutcrackers and Western Gray Squirrel. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Small white-pink tubular flowers with waxy red berries. Mountain shrub. Berries edible but insipid. Important wildlife food.

fm.Fruit: Red waxy berries — edible but bland
fm.Habitat: Mountain slopes, pine forest — SGM above 5,000 ft

shrubs.hp: Mountain wax currant with flowers. Important early-season nectar for Rufous Hummingbird during spring migration. Berries eaten by Hermit Thrush and Clark's Nutcracker. Resprouter from root crown after fire. Among the first shrubs to flower post-fire, providing early nectar.

shrubs.desc: Drooping clusters of pink flowers in mountain forests. Blue-black berries. Moist shady areas near streams at mid-elevation. Beautiful ornamental.

fm.Habitat: Moist shady mountain slopes near streams

shrubs.hp: Mountain pink currant of moist forest edges. Drooping flower racemes attract Anna's Hummingbird at elevation. Tart berries eaten by American Robin and Band-tailed Pigeon. Resprouter from root crown after fire. Among the first shrubs to flower post-fire, providing early nectar.

shrubs.desc: Red berry clusters (toxic raw) on mountain shrub. Cream dome-shaped flower clusters. Higher elevation than Blue Elderberry. SGM canyons.

fm.Fruit: Red berries — toxic raw; cooked for jelly
fm.Habitat: Mountain canyons, moist sites — SGM 4,000–8,000 ft

shrubs.hp: Important bird food. Berries eaten by Cedar Waxwing, American Robin, and Band-tailed Pigeon. Visited by native bees and syrphid flies. Berries cooked into food and beverages by Tongva and Chumash (raw berries mildly toxic). Wood used for flutes and clapper sticks.

shrubs.desc: Leathery curled evergreen leaves on stiff shrub. Feathery corkscrew seed tails to 3 in. Rocky ridges and exposed slopes at upper montane. Very slow-growing.

fm.Habitat: Rocky ridges, exposed upper montane slopes

shrubs.hp: Hard dense wood (heaviest NA wood). Important Mule Deer browse. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and beetles. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Higher-elevation snowberry with round leaves and white berries. Pink bell flowers. Mountain pine forest understory. More robust than S. mollis.
shrubs.hp: Important grouse food. Nectar source for hummingbirds. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Thorny desert tree with feathery compound leaves and sweet edible bean pods. Massive taproot to 175 ft deep. Critical desert shade and food. Tongva ground pods into flour.

fm.Fruit: Sweet edible pods — ground into flour by indigenous peoples
fm.Cultural: Tongva/Cahuilla staple food — pods ground into pinole

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Flowers visited by native bees. Ethnobotanically significant — used by indigenous peoples. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

shrubs.desc: Gray leafless tree looking like a plume of smoke in desert washes. Purple pea flowers. Spine-tipped branches. Seeds require flash-flood scarification to germinate.
shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and long-tongued native bees. Smoke Tree contributes nitrogen to soil communities. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

shrubs.desc: Bright red winter stems and white berry clusters. Mountain stream banks. One of the most visually striking winter shrubs. Attracts birds to white berries.
shrubs.hp: White berries eaten by 40+ bird species. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Rosette of long tough grass-like leaves with tall plume-like flower stalk. Chaparral slopes. Important nesting material for woodrats. CA endemic.
shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, Cedar Waxwing, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: White tubular flower clusters on sticky shrub. Smoother leaves than hairy yerba santa. Same medicinal properties. Dry chaparral slopes.

fm.Cultural: Same medicinal uses as hairy yerba santa

shrubs.hp: Traditional cough remedy. Fire follower resprouting vigorously after burns. Visited by native bees and butterflies. Traditional medicine — respiratory ailments. Visited by specialist Habropoda depressa bee. Important yerba santa for restoration.

shrubs.desc: CNPS Rare Plant List 1B.1. Cream flowers on uncommon shrub of dry washes and slopes. Known from Griffith Park, Verdugo Mountains, and scattered SGM sites. Federally endangered.

fm.Habitat: Dry slopes, washes — scattered rare populations

shrubs.hp: CNPS 1B.2. Conservation priority. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Three-toothed leaves on aromatic shrub. Important deer browse — named "antelope" bitterbrush. Yellow flowers. High desert transition zone.

fm.Ecology: Important deer browse
fm.Habitat: High desert transition — Antelope Valley edge

shrubs.hp: Critical winter browse for Mule Deer and Pronghorn. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and beetles. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Blue-lavender flowers on aromatic desert shrub. Mojave Desert endemic. Intensely fragrant dry leaves. More drought-tolerant than coastal sages.
shrubs.hp: Among the most drought-tolerant native sages. Critical hummingbird and carpenter bee nectar. Important restoration species.

shrubs.desc: CAUTION: causes severe contact dermatitis worse than poison oak. Tall purple-flowered shrub colonizing burns — can dominate post-fire chaparral for years. Beautiful but untouchable.

fm.Toxicity: SEVERE contact dermatitis — worse than poison oak. Do not touch any part.
fm.Habitat: Post-fire chaparral — mass colonizer of burns
fm.Fire: Fire follower — germinates prolifically after fire, dominates 2–5 years

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Fire follower — seeds germinate after fire, contributing to post-fire recovery. Visited by bumble bees, mining bees, and sweat bees. Visited by bee flies (Bombyliidae) — important early-season parasitoid of native bee larvae.

shrubs.desc: Cascading sprays of tiny cream-white flowers. Mountain chaparral and woodland edges. Named for the foam-like appearance of flower clusters. Important browse for deer.
shrubs.hp: Pollinated by native bees and syrphid flies. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing and mammals. Hard wood used for arrow shafts by indigenous peoples.

shrubs.desc: Pink-white urn flowers on smooth red-barked shrub. Glandular-hairy leaves distinguish from Bigberry Manzanita. Common SGM chaparral.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — fire-killed; seeds germinate from heat

shrubs.hp: Important post-fire resprouter. Berries fermented into cider by indigenous peoples. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Early-season bumble bee forage (Dec-Feb). Berries eaten by California Thrasher, American Robin, and Spotted Towhee. Wrentit nests in dense stands. Visited by specialist manzanita mason bee (Osmia ribifloris).

shrubs.desc: Small pendulous red-green flowers on spiny shrub. Shady chaparral understory. Earlier bloom than other Ribes. Small berries attract birds.
shrubs.hp: Hillside gooseberry with pendant fuchsia-like flowers — among earliest-blooming shrubs. Critical nectar for Anna's Hummingbird in February when almost nothing else flowers. Resprouter from root crown after fire. Among the first shrubs to flower post-fire, providing early nectar.

shrubs.desc: Dense yellow flower clusters on resinous shrub. Late summer/fall bloomer — important when few other shrubs flower. Thread-like leaves.
shrubs.hp: Among the showiest fall-blooming native shrubs. Critical late-season (fall) nectar for native bees when few other plants bloom. Visited by bumble bees and sweat bees. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Yellow-flowered subshrub of dry chaparral slopes. Named for early SoCal botanist Samuel Parish. Fall bloomer. Interior chaparral.

fm.Cultural: Named for botanist Samuel Parish (1838–1928)

shrubs.hp: Important late-season desert pollinator resource. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Sulfur-yellow flowers on compact white-woolly shrub. CNPS 1B.2 — endemic to Conejo Grade area of Ventura/LA county line. One of the rarest buckwheats in California.

fm.Habitat: Rocky volcanic soils — Conejo Grade area

shrubs.hp: Critical nectar source for native bees and butterflies including hairstreaks and metalmarks. SoCal endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: Desert shrub producing liquid wax "oil" seeds. Rare at eastern LA County edge (Mojave/Sonoran transition). The only plant that produces liquid wax. Major commercial crop.

fm.Fruit: Acorn-like — produces liquid wax, not oil
fm.Cultural: Major commercial crop — cosmetics, lubricant
fm.Habitat: Desert — extreme eastern LA County edge

shrubs.hp: Liquid wax from seeds used in cosmetics. The only known plant producing liquid wax esters. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Small white flower clusters and white berries on smooth-stemmed riparian shrub. Mountain stream banks. Less showy than Red-twig Dogwood but more common at lower elevations.
shrubs.hp: Less showy than C. sericea. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Yellow flower clusters and blue-purple grape-like berries on holly-leaved shrub. Shady woodland. Berries tart but edible — make good jelly. Inner bark yields bright yellow dye.

fm.Cultural: Inner bark yields bright yellow dye

shrubs.hp: Berries edible. Root bark yields yellow dye. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Brilliant purple-blue flowers on compact silvery shrub. High desert specialist. Intensely aromatic. One of the showiest desert sages. Mojave Desert edge of LA County.

fm.Habitat: High desert - Mojave edge of LA County

shrubs.hp: Important sage grouse forage in Great Basin (not in LA County). Visited by hummingbirds (especially Anna's Hummingbird), carpenter bees, and bumble bees. Aromatic oils attract pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Rose-purple bracts and blue flowers on compact shrub. Desert-mountain transition. Thick gray aromatic leaves. Drought specialist.
shrubs.hp: Mountain desert transition above 5,000 ft. Critical hummingbird and carpenter bee nectar. Important in native garden restoration.

shrubs.desc: Red-barked manzanita of mid-elevation SGM chaparral. Distinguished from Bigberry by smaller fruits and non-glandular leaves. Named for botanist Charles Parry. Common at 3,500-6,500 ft.

fm.Fruit: Small berries (vs large in A. glauca)
fm.Fire: Obligate seeder - killed by fire; seeds require heat

shrubs.hp: Prostrate form on exposed ridges. Important soil stabilizer on steep slopes. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Fire ecology varies by species — some (A. glauca) are obligate seeders killed by fire, others (A. glandulosa) resprout from basal burl.

shrubs.desc: Showy pink flower bracts distinguish this from all other SGM manzanitas. Glandular-hairy twigs. Interior chaparral at 4,000-7,000 ft. One of the most beautiful manzanitas when flowering.

fm.Fire: Burl sprouter - survives fire via lignotuber

shrubs.hp: Important early-season bee forage. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Fire ecology varies by species — some (A. glauca) are obligate seeders killed by fire, others (A. glandulosa) resprout from basal burl.

shrubs.desc: Bright green smooth leaves on spreading shrub - the greenest manzanita in the SGM. Upper montane at 6,000-9,000 ft. Common along Angeles Crest Highway at Dawson Saddle area.

fm.Leaves: Bright green, smooth, nearly round (diagnostic - greenest manzanita)
fm.Habitat: Upper montane 6,000-9,000 ft - common along Angeles Crest Hwy

shrubs.hp: Bright green leaves (not gray like most species). Important American Black Bear food. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Fire ecology varies by species — some (A. glauca) are obligate seeders killed by fire, others (A. glandulosa) resprout from basal burl. Mountain Quail forages for manzanita berries at high elevation. Clark's Nutcracker visits for berries in lean seed years.

shrubs.desc: Critically imperiled manzanita endemic to one tiny area at Mill Creek Summit divide in the San Gabriel Mountains. Known from a single population. Bright green shiny leaves with smooth edges.

fm.Habitat: Mill Creek Summit divide only - single population
fm.Conservation: CRITICALLY IMPERILED. Endemic to one site in SGM, LA County.

shrubs.hp: Early-season bumble bee forage. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing, American Black Bear, and coyotes. CA (extreme) endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake. Fire ecology varies by species — some (A. glauca) are obligate seeders killed by fire, others (A. glandulosa) resprout from basal burl.

shrubs.desc: Sharp-pointed leaves on dense spreading shrub. Desert-chaparral transition on north slopes of SGM. More drought-tolerant than other manzanitas. Extends into Mojave edge.
shrubs.hp: Fire-adapted — both resprouting and reseeding populations. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake.

shrubs.desc: Not a true Arctostaphylos but closely related. White-felted leaf undersides and hard woody fruits. Southern LA County coastal chaparral. More common in San Diego County.

fm.Habitat: Coastal chaparral - southern LA County edge

shrubs.hp: Mission manzanita — one of the rarest chaparral shrubs. Important early-season bumble bee forage (flowers Dec–Feb). Berries eaten by California Thrasher. Fire-killed — obligate seeder.

shrubs.desc: White-flowered ceanothus with small wedge-shaped leaves. Very common throughout chaparral and foothill woodland. More drought-tolerant than most ceanothus species.

fm.Fire: Obligate seeder — killed by fire; seeds require heat
fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Obligate seeder — seeds require fire heat to germinate. Nitrogen fixer. More drought-tolerant than most Ceanothus. Important for native bees.

shrubs.desc: Deciduous ceanothus with white or blue flowers. Important deer browse — hence the name. Mountain canyons and forest openings. The only common deciduous ceanothus in LA County.

fm.Ecology: Important deer browse

shrubs.hp: The only common deciduous Ceanothus in LA County. Important Mule Deer browse — hence the name. Nitrogen fixer. Resprouter. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell.

shrubs.desc: Thorn-tipped branches with small blue flowers. Interior chaparral on dry rocky slopes. Among the most drought-tolerant ceanothus species.
shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer via root nodule bacteria. Important native bee forage. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell. Obligate seeder — killed by fire but seeds germinate massively from heat-cracked seed coat. Some species store seed banks viable for decades.

shrubs.desc: Leafless green-stemmed desert shrub. Ancient gymnosperm lineage — related to conifers not flowering plants. Stems brewed as stimulant tea by indigenous peoples. Contains pseudoephedrine precursors.

fm.Ecology: Gymnosperm — more closely related to pines than to flowering plants
fm.Cultural: Mormon Tea — stems brewed as stimulant drink

shrubs.hp: Ancient gymnosperm lineage. Related to Chinese ma huang. Wind-pollinated. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Bright green broom-like desert shrub with no leaves. Higher-elevation than E. californica. Joshua tree woodland and pinyon-juniper zone. Yellow cone-like structures.
shrubs.hp: Mormon tea — green photosynthetic stems replace leaves. Wind-pollinated gymnosperm. Seeds eaten by California Quail and Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by native bees when pollen-shedding.

shrubs.desc: Thorny desert shrub with small tubular flowers and red berries. Important bird food. Related to goji berry. Tongva ate berries fresh. Desert washes and rocky slopes.

fm.Cultural: Related to goji berry. Tongva ate berries fresh.

shrubs.hp: Important bird food in desert. Spring nectar source. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Visited by native bees. Provides nesting habitat in thorny branches.

shrubs.desc: Spectacular spiny wand-like stems tipped with flame-red flowers. Leafs out after rain, drops leaves in drought — multiple cycles per year. Barely reaches eastern LA County (Mojave edge).

fm.Color: Flame-red tubular clusters at stem tips
fm.Form: Cluster of wand-like spiny stems from base
fm.Ecology: Drought-deciduous — multiple leaf cycles per year
fm.Habitat: Desert rocky slopes — barely reaches eastern LA County

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Hummingbird-pollinated. Ocotillo — leafs out after rain, drops leaves in drought. Flower clusters attract Costa's Hummingbird. Cahuilla used stems for fencing and building.

shrubs.desc: Blue-purple lupine that smells exactly like grape soda. Desert-mountain transition. Silvery-hairy leaves. One of the most pleasantly fragrant wildflowers.
shrubs.hp: Supposedly smells of grape soda. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

shrubs.desc: Silvery-leaved perennial bush lupine with blue-purple flower spikes. Chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Host plant for the federally endangered Mission Blue Butterfly.

fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, sandy slopes

shrubs.hp: Host plant for the federally endangered Mission Blue Butterfly (Icaricia icarioides missionensis). Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

shrubs.desc: Silvery coastal bush lupine of sand dunes and coastal bluffs. Blue-lavender flowers. Important dune stabilizer. Less common than L. albifrons inland.
shrubs.hp: Important dune stabilizer. Nitrogen fixer. Visited by bumble bees and native bees. Browsed by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

shrubs.desc: White flowers on stiff shrub. Channel Islands and coastal bluffs. The only member of its family endemic to California. Extremely rare on mainland LA County.

fm.Ecology: Sole genus of family Crossosomataceae — CA endemic

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Channel Islands endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Drooping clusters of white flowers in winter — blooms November through March when almost nothing else flowers. Critical early-season bee and hummingbird nectar.

fm.Bloom: Nov–Mar — flowers when almost nothing else does

shrubs.hp: Critical winter nectar — flowers Nov–Mar. Among the most important plants for overwintering Anna's Hummingbird. Resprouter from root crown after fire. Among the first shrubs to flower post-fire, providing early nectar.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Yellow pea flowers on bright green leafless stems. Cal-IPC High. Aggressively colonizes roadcuts and burns throughout SMM and SGM. Seeds viable for decades in soil. Extremely difficult to eradicate.

fm.Ecology: Colonizes roadcuts and burns. Seed bank lasts decades.
fm.Habitat: Roadcuts, burns, disturbed slopes — SMM and SGM

shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC High. Nitrogen fixer. Aggressively colonizes road cuts and disturbed slopes. Fire hazard. Visited by bumble bees and native bees.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Fast-growing New Zealand shrub/tree planted as windbreak. Cal-IPC Moderate. Being devastated by thrips (Klambothrips myopori) since 2005 — dying across LA. Still invasive in riparian areas.

fm.Ecology: Being killed by imported thrips since 2005 — widespread dieback across LA
fm.Habitat: Planted as windbreak — escaping to riparian areas

shrubs.hp: Provides cover, nesting habitat, and forage for native wildlife. Cal-IPC Moderate invasive — displaces native vegetation. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Fragrant golden tubular flowers in spring. Edible dark berries. Important hummingbird plant. Riparian and canyon edges.
shrubs.hp: Golden currant of riparian corridors. Fragrant yellow flowers attract bumble bees and hummingbirds. Berries ripen yellow to black — edible, used in jams by settlers. Resprouter from root crown after fire. Among the first shrubs to flower post-fire, providing early nectar.

shrubs.desc: Dark blue flowers on sticky-leaved shrub. Warty leaf surfaces give the name. Nitrogen fixer. Chaparral in Santa Monica Mountains.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Warty leaf surfaces are diagnostic. Obligate seeder — killed by fire but seeds germinate from heat-activated soil bank. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell.

shrubs.desc: Yellow-flowered subshrub of dry inland slopes. Fall bloomer. More common inland than coastal Isocoma. Important late-season pollinator resource.
shrubs.hp: Late-season nectar source for native bees and butterflies. Fire-adapted resprouter. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green shrub with masses of yellow fall flowers. Desert-mountain transition. Contains rubber — WWII rubber extraction attempted. Important fall pollinator plant.

fm.Cultural: Contains rubber — WWII extraction attempted

shrubs.hp: Critical fall nectar for native bees and migrating Monarchs. Pre-hibernation bumble bee queens depend on this late bloom (Xerces Society). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Spiny thorny shrub with small white flowers and small almonds. Desert washes. More drought-tolerant than other native Prunus. Indigenous food plant.
shrubs.hp: Indigenous food plant — bitter almonds eaten after leaching. Early spring bee forage in desert. Important bird food. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and early-season butterflies. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Cedar Waxwing, and Band-tailed Pigeon and mammals.

shrubs.desc: Dense thorny white-barked ceanothus of maritime chaparral. White flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Coast-facing slopes in Santa Monica Mountains.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen fixer

shrubs.hp: Nitrogen fixer. Obligate seeder — killed by fire. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and butterflies including California Tortoiseshell including California Tortoiseshell.

shrubs.desc: Yellow sunflower-like heads on shrubby stems in coastal sage scrub. Distinguished from Encelia by deeply lobed leaves. Southern LA County.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub — southern LA County

shrubs.hp: Year-round bloomer providing continuous nectar for native bees. Important Coastal Sage Scrub component. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Silver holly-shaped leaves on low desert shrub. One of the most drought and heat-tolerant plants — thrives in Death Valley. At western range edge in NE LA County.

fm.Habitat: Extreme desert — at western range edge in NE LA County
fm.Drought: Among most heat-tolerant plants on Earth

shrubs.hp: Among the most heat and drought-tolerant plants on Earth. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Important desert wildlife browse.

shrubs.desc: Small citrus-family shrub with white flowers. Strong citrus scent when leaves crushed. Very rare in LA County — at northern range limit on coast-facing slopes.

fm.Fragrance: Strong citrus scent — crushed leaves smell like lemon
fm.Habitat: Coastal slopes — at northern range limit

shrubs.hp: Only member of citrus family native to SoCal chaparral. Aromatic oils attract native bees. Rare at northern range limit.

shrubs.desc: Completely white-woolly paintbrush endemic to Channel Islands. White bracts — unusual for paintbrush. Maritime chaparral.
shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Hemiparasitic on neighboring shrub roots. Important hummingbird nectar.

shrubs.desc: Close relative of Matilija Poppy with bristly-hairy calyx (vs smooth in R. coulteri). Large white flowers. Very rare — a few LA County canyon populations.

fm.Flowers: Large white, yellow center — like R. coulteri
fm.Calyx: Bristly-hairy (vs smooth in R. coulteri — diagnostic)

shrubs.hp: Close relative of Matilija Poppy distinguished by bristly calyx. Fire follower. Pollinated by native bees and beetles. Very rare.

shrubs.desc: Island form of Holly-leaf Cherry with larger leaves, fruit, and tree-like form. Planted widely on mainland as ornamental. Native to Channel Islands.

fm.Fruit: Large dark cherries — edible flesh around stone
fm.Habitat: Channel Islands native; widely planted on mainland

shrubs.hp: Fruit eaten by American Robin, and Cedar Waxwing. Widely planted on mainland for wildlife gardens. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and early-season butterflies. Fruit eaten fresh and kernels leached and ground into flour by Tongva. Important food plant.

shrubs.desc: Pink flowers on spiny thorny shrub at desert-mountain transition. Small fuzzy peach-like fruit. At extreme southern range limit in NE LA County.

fm.Habitat: Desert-mountain transition — at southern range limit

shrubs.hp: Early spring bee forage in desert-mountain transition. Fruit eaten by American Robin, California Ground Squirrel, and Cedar Waxwing. Visited by native bees, hover flies, and early-season butterflies. Fruit eaten fresh and kernels leached and ground into flour by Tongva. Important food plant.

shrubs.desc: Gray-green leafless desert shrub. Ancient gymnosperm. Distinguished from E. californica by gray-green (not bright green) stems. High desert.

fm.Ecology: Gymnosperm — ancient lineage

shrubs.hp: Ancient gymnosperm lineage. Stems brewed as 'Mormon Tea' by early settlers. Desert habitat structure for small reptiles and mammals. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: White woolly desert shrub — important winter browse for livestock and wildlife. Desert flats and alkaline areas at NE LA County edge.

fm.Ecology: Critical winter browse — name refers to livestock fattening

shrubs.hp: Critical winter browse for Mule Deer, Pronghorn, and jackDesert Cottontail. White woolly stems provide winter food when other forage is scarce. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow and California Quail.

shrubs.desc: White rose-like flowers followed by dramatic feathery pink seed plumes. Desert-mountain transition. Extremely showy seed heads persist for months.

fm.Seeds: Feathery pink plumes — dramatic (persist for months)

shrubs.hp: Feathery pink seed plumes are among the showiest of any desert shrub. Pollinated by native bees and syrphid flies. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing and mammals. Important for erosion control.

shrubs.desc: Fragrant cream-yellow rose-family flowers on evergreen shrub. Important desert browse. At extreme western range limit at NE LA County edge.

fm.Habitat: Desert-mountain transition — at western range limit
fm.Ecology: Important wildlife browse

shrubs.hp: Important browse for Mule Deer. Fragrant flowers visited by native bees. At extreme western range limit in LA County.

shrubs.desc: Hairy-leaved manzanita of interior chaparral. Distinguished from A. glandulosa by non-glandular hairs and birch-like leaves. SGM north slopes.
shrubs.hp: Early-season bumble bee forage — blooms Dec–Mar. Obligate seeder killed by fire. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing and coyotes. Ectomycorrhizal with native fungi — depends on underground fungal networks for nutrient uptake.

shrubs.desc: Parasitic plant with no chlorophyll — entirely dependent on host plant roots. Purple-brown flower spikes emerge from ground near host (usually Isocoma or Eriodictyon). Coastal sage scrub.

fm.Ecology: Root parasite — no chlorophyll; parasitizes CSS shrubs

shrubs.hp: Holoparasite — entirely dependent on host plant roots for all nutrients. No chlorophyll. Indicator of intact native shrub communities. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Large rosette buckwheat with red fruits. Channel Islands endemic — planted on mainland for restoration. White to pink flower clusters. Important butterfly nectar.

fm.Color: White to pink clusters, then red fruit

shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Important nectar source for native bees and butterflies. Red fruits eaten by island birds.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Crofton Weed (Asteraceae) — Highly invasive Mexican shrub (Cal-IPC Limited) spreading through riparian and shaded canyon habitats.

fm.Flowers: White fluffy heads in dense clusters

shrubs.hp: Highly invasive Mexican shrub (Cal-IPC Limited) spreading through riparian and shaded canyon habitats. Forms dense monocultures excluding native understory. Toxic to horses (Numinbah disease). Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Garland Daisy (Asteraceae). garland daisy from Mediterranean — showy golden flowers but forms dense stands displacing native coastal sage scrub species.
shrubs.hp: Garland daisy from Mediterranean — showy golden flowers but forms dense stands displacing native coastal sage scrub species. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native American century plant (Asparagaceae). Century plant from Mexico.

fm.Flowers: Yellow in massive candelabra stalk, 6-10 m tall
fm.Size: Rosette 1-2 m; monocarpic (blooms once after 10-30 yrs)

shrubs.hp: Century plant from Mexico. Despite the name, blooms after 10–30 years (not 100), produces massive flower stalk to 25 ft, then dies. Pups spread vegetatively. Visited by native bees and other pollinators. Visited by Lesser Long-nosed Bat (Leptonycteris yerbabuenae) — a nectar bat rare in LA County. Hooded Oriole feeds from flowers.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Lawn daisy (Asteraceae). lawn daisy from Europe.

fm.Flowers: White rays with yellow disk, 2-3 cm

shrubs.hp: Lawn daisy from Europe. Edible flowers. Low ecological impact — mostly confined to maintained landscapes. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Rough cocklebur (Asteraceae). Cocklebur with distinctive hooked bur fruits.

fm.Fruit: Oval burs covered in hooked spines, 1-2 cm

shrubs.hp: Cocklebur with distinctive hooked bur fruits. Important waterfowl food despite being toxic to livestock. Burs disperse on animal fur and clothing. Wind-pollinated. Fruit eaten by Lesser Goldfinch. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Common Cotula (Asteraceae). Australian cotula forming dense mats in lawns and disturbed ground.
shrubs.hp: Australian cotula forming dense mats in lawns and disturbed ground. Low ecological impact — mostly confined to urban areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Big Sagebrush (Asteraceae). foundation species of the Intermountain West.

fm.Flowers: Tiny yellowish in narrow panicles, fall

shrubs.hp: Dominant shrub at desert-mountain transition in NE LA County. Critical sage grouse habitat (not in LA). Foundation species for Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat. Important for sage scrub ecosystem. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Fire-sensitive — killed by fire and slow to recover. Old-growth sagebrush stands take decades to return. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Common Asparagus Fern (Asparagaceae). common asparagus fern (not a true fern) — delicate feathery foliage.

fm.Foliage: Feathery cladodes (not true leaves), flat sprays
fm.Stems: Climbing, woody at base, small hooked thorns

shrubs.hp: Common asparagus fern (not a true fern) — delicate feathery foliage. Bird-dispersed red berries spreading into shaded canyons. Smothering native understory. Visited by native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Whitedaisy tidytips (Asteraceae). Whitedaisy tidytips with showy white ray flowers tipped yellow.

fm.Flowers: White rays with yellow center, 2-3 cm

shrubs.hp: Important bee pollinator plant. Grassland and open woodland specialist. Visited by mining bees and sweat bees during spring blooms. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Belladonna Lily (Amaryllidaceae). belladonna lily from South Africa — dramatic pink flowers on bare stems in late summer ('naked lady').

fm.Flowers: Large pink trumpets in umbels, 8-10 cm, on leafless stalk
fm.Habitat: Old garden sites, road cuts; extremely persistent

shrubs.hp: Toxic bulb. Extremely drought-tolerant once established. Visited by native bees and sweat bees. Bulbs provide wildlife forage.

shrubs.desc: Invasive horseweed (Asteraceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.

fm.Flowers: Tiny greenish-white heads in elongate panicles
fm.Habitat: Disturbed ground; one of first glyphosate-resistant weeds

shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Pioneer on disturbed ground. Wind-dispersed seeds travel long distances. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Flax-leaved Horseweed (Asteraceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.
shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. South American fleabane naturalizing on disturbed ground worldwide. Among the most common urban weeds in LA. Evolving herbicide resistance. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Late-season nectar source when other wildflowers have finished blooming. Displaces native species — active management needed.

shrubs.desc: Non-native common groundsel (Asteraceae). Groundsel — common year-round weed of gardens and disturbed areas.
shrubs.hp: Groundsel — common year-round weed of gardens and disturbed areas. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids toxic to livestock. Wind-dispersed seeds. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native prickly sowthistle (Asteraceae). prickly sowthistle.
shrubs.hp: Prickly sowthistle. Common garden weed with milky sap. Seeds wind-dispersed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Important early-season food for goldLesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Cretanweed (Asteraceae). Cretanweed from Mediterranean — becoming common in disturbed coastal areas.
shrubs.hp: Cretanweed from Mediterranean — becoming common in disturbed coastal areas. Increasing concern as grassland invader. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Silver rock-lettuce (Asteraceae). Silver rock-lettuce forming distinctive silvery rosettes on rock faces and road cuts.
shrubs.hp: Milky latex. Important habitat specialist — indicator of undisturbed rocky slopes. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Bigelow's tickseed (Asteraceae). Bigelow's tickseed forming brilliant yellow desert carpet displays in good wildflower years.

fm.Heads: Yellow, 3-5 cm, on long naked peduncles
fm.Leaves: Pinnately divided into linear segments, basal
fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, sandy flats; superbloom species

shrubs.hp: Bigelow's tickseed forming brilliant yellow desert carpet displays in good wildflower years. Important desert pollinator resource during spring bloom. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Nevin's Woolly Sunflower (Asteraceae). Channel Islands and coastal bluff endemic.
shrubs.hp: Channel Islands and coastal bluff endemic. Rare — few mainland populations remain. Conservation concern. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Desert dandelion (Asteraceae). Desert dandelion forming low yellow carpets in Mojave and Colorado Desert.
shrubs.hp: Important spring bee forage. Fire follower in some habitats. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Slender sunflower (Asteraceae). Slender sunflower of chaparral and oak woodland margins.
shrubs.hp: Slender sunflower of chaparral and oak woodland margins. Important late-season nectar source for native bees and butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Island Tarplant (Asteraceae). Channel Islands endemic.
shrubs.hp: Important pollinator resource on Channel Islands. Limited mainland populations. Visited by sweat bees and small native bees attracted to tarry-scented glands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Invasive African Asparagus (Asparagaceae). aggressive spreader via bird-dispersed berries.

fm.Foliage: Dense bright green cladodes on arching stems

shrubs.hp: Active removal programs in coastal habitats. Visited by native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Cal-IPC Moderate — bird-dispersed berries create new populations far from parent plants. Dense root mat prevents native seedling establishment. Smothers native understory in oak woodland.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Cornflower (Asteraceae). iconic blue wildflower of European grain fields.
shrubs.hp: Garden escape occasionally naturalizing on disturbed ground. Important bee forage. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Redray alpinegold (Asteraceae). Showy mountain wildflower with red-centered yellow rays.

fm.Heads: Bicolored — red rays, yellow tips, 3-5 cm

shrubs.hp: Important high-elevation pollinator plant. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Island senecio (Asteraceae). Channel Islands endemic now planted on mainland.
shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic now planted on mainland. Important for island pollinator communities. Drought-tolerant. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Smooth Cat's Ear (Asteraceae). common lawn and grassland weed from Mediterranean.

fm.Leaves: Basal rosette, smooth (not hairy like H. radicata)

shrubs.hp: Common lawn and grassland weed from Mediterranean. Displaces native annuals. Visited by native bees and syrphid flies. Dandelion-like weed.

shrubs.desc: Valley Lessingia (Asteraceae). purple-pink heads in open grasslands and disturbed areas.
shrubs.hp: Important late-season bee forage. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Emory's rockdaisy (Asteraceae). small annual of desert rock crevices.
shrubs.hp: Small annual of desert rock crevices. Among the first desert annuals to bloom after rain. Important early bee forage. Visited by small native bees and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Hairy Beggarticks (Asteraceae). Tropical beggartick with barbed seeds that adhere to clothing and animal fur.

fm.Heads: White rays (small or absent), yellow disk, 1 cm

shrubs.hp: Tropical beggartick with barbed seeds that adhere to clothing and animal fur. Colonizes disturbed moist areas. Used medicinally in some cultures. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Onion-Leafed Asphodel (Asphodelaceae). Mediterranean weed spreading rapidly in desert and coastal sage scrub.

fm.Flowers: White-pink stars with pink midstripe, 1-2 cm
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, coastal sage; spreading rapidly

shrubs.hp: Mediterranean weed spreading rapidly in desert and coastal sage scrub. Forms dense monocultures. Cal-IPC Watch List. Displaces native species — active management needed. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native feverfew (Asteraceae). traditional migraine remedy (contains parthenolide).

fm.Flowers: White daisies with yellow disks, 1-2 cm, in flat-topped clusters

shrubs.hp: Traditional migraine remedy (contains parthenolide). Garden escape naturalizing in disturbed areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Blair's munzothamnus (Asteraceae). Channel Islands endemic shrub in the lettuce tribe. Known only from San Clemente Island.

fm.Habitat: San Clemente Island — rocky coastal bluffs

shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic shrub in the lettuce tribe. Known only from San Clemente Island. Rare. Conservation priority. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Yellow Iris (Iridaceae). invasive in wetlands.
shrubs.hp: Invasive in wetlands. Dense rhizome mats exclude native marsh plants. All parts contain irisin glycoside toxin. Cal-IPC rated. Pollinated by native bees. Deer-resistant due to toxic rhizomes. Early-season nectar.

shrubs.desc: Crowned muilla (Asparagaceae). Small onion relative of desert and foothill grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Small onion relative of desert and foothill grasslands. Important early-season bee pollinator plant. Easily overlooked. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, Cedar Waxwing, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: Santolina pincushion (Asteraceae). santolina pincushion of mountain forests.
shrubs.hp: Mountain pincushion of conifer forests above 5,000 ft. Visited by bumble bees and mining bees. White woolly foliage adapted to alpine UV exposure. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Cape Smilax (Asparagaceae). Cape smilax (bridal creeper) — aggressive vine from South Africa smothering native vegetation.

fm.Foliage: Glossy, ovate leaf-like cladodes on twining stems
fm.Habitat: Shaded coastal canyons; serious ecological threat

shrubs.hp: Cape smilax (bridal creeper) — aggressive vine from South Africa smothering native vegetation. Bird-dispersed berries. Serious ecological threat. Visited by native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Spring starflower (Amaryllidaceae). spring starflower from South America — pale blue star flowers in lawns.
shrubs.hp: Garlic-scented foliage. Garden escape. Low ecological impact. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Stinkwort (Asteraceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.
shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Rapidly spreading stinkwort — strong unpleasant odor. Spreading explosively along CA roadsides since 2000s. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Displaces native species — active management needed.

shrubs.desc: Santa Susana tarplant (Asteraceae). STATE RARE.  Known only from sandstone outcrops in western San Fernando Valley.

fm.Habitat: Sandstone outcrops, western San Fernando Valley

shrubs.hp: STATE RARE. Known only from sandstone outcrops in western San Fernando Valley. Seriously threatened by development. Conservation priority. Visited by sweat bees and small native bees attracted to tarry-scented glands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Firewheel (Asteraceae). Showy red-and-yellow pinwheel flowers.
