shrubs.hp: Important pollinator plant. Increasingly planted in wildflower mixes and naturalizing. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: San Clemente Island brodiaea (Asparagaceae). Channel Islands endemic.

fm.Flowers: Purple-blue tubular bells, 2-3 cm, in umbels

shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Federally listed as endangered. Very few populations remain. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, Cedar Waxwing, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: Scale Bud (Asteraceae). distinctive desert annual with dandelion-like heads surrounded by papery bracts.

fm.Flowers: Yellow dandelion-like heads surrounded by papery bracts

shrubs.hp: Distinctive desert annual with dandelion-like heads surrounded by papery bracts. Good wildflower year indicator. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native cowpen daisy (Asteraceae). aggressive yellow daisy spreading along roadsides and washes.
shrubs.hp: Aggressive yellow daisy spreading along roadsides and washes. Forms dense stands. Native to central US, expanding range westward. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Mexican Fleabane (Asteraceae). Mexican fleabane widely planted in LA gardens.

fm.Flowers: Small daisies aging white to pink, 1-2 cm
fm.Habitat: Walls, rock faces; popular garden plant

shrubs.hp: Mexican fleabane widely planted in LA gardens. Cascading habit. Naturalizing on walls and rocky areas but relatively benign ecologically. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Late-season nectar source when other wildflowers have finished blooming.

shrubs.desc: Wedgeleaf Goldenbush (Asteraceae). wedgeleaf goldenbush of rocky outcrops.
shrubs.hp: Wedgeleaf goldenbush of rocky outcrops. Important late-season nectar source for native bees. Rock crevice specialist. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: San Diego Wirelettuce (Asteraceae). slender stems with small pink ray flowers.

fm.Leaves: Reduced on wiry stems, basal leaves pinnate

shrubs.hp: Important nectar source in coastal sage scrub. Milky latex. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Rush Skeletonweed (Asteraceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.

fm.Flowers: Yellow, dandelion-like, 1-2 cm, on wiry stems

shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Deep-rooted perennial spreading via milky-sapped runners. Extremely difficult to eradicate. Agricultural weed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Displaces native species — active management needed.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Wedding Treasureflower (Asteraceae). South African gazania — common ornamental ground cover escaping to roadsides and coastal bluffs.

fm.Leaves: Linear, dark green above, white-woolly below

shrubs.hp: South African gazania — common ornamental ground cover escaping to roadsides and coastal bluffs. Displaces native annuals. Visited by native bees and hover flies.

shrubs.desc: California cottonrose (Asteraceae). inconspicuous woolly annual of open areas.
shrubs.hp: Inconspicuous woolly annual of open areas. Despite being tiny, plays important ecological role in soil crust formation. Visited by minute native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Leafy Desertdandelion (Asteraceae). Leafy desert-dandelion — Channel Islands and coastal bluff endemic.

fm.Habitat: Channel Islands, coastal bluffs; rare endemic

shrubs.hp: Leafy coastal bluff-dandelion — Channel Islands and coastal bluff endemic. Conservation concern. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Early Onion (Amaryllidaceae). among the first spring wildflowers in coastal sage scrub.
shrubs.hp: Important early-season bee forage. Early Onion contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources. Visited by native bees and sweat bees. Onion scent deters Mule Deer browsing. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

shrubs.desc: Pumice alpinegold (Asteraceae). Mountain specialist of volcanic and granitic soils above 6,000 ft.

fm.Habitat: Mountain pumice and gravel, above 7,000 ft

shrubs.hp: Mountain specialist of volcanic and granitic soils above 6,000 ft. Alpine adaptation to harsh conditions. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native pot marigold (Asteraceae). edible orange flowers.

fm.Heads: Orange to yellow, 4-7 cm, double in cultivars

shrubs.hp: Edible orange flowers. Traditional medicinal herb. Occasional garden escape. Minimal ecological impact. Visited by native bees and hover flies. Medicinal herb.

shrubs.desc: Pringle's woolly sunflower (Asteraceae). tiny desert annual forming yellow carpets in good wildflower years.
shrubs.hp: Visited by mining bees (Andrena), sweat bees, and small butterflies. Important early-season nectar on dry slopes. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Pringle's woolly sunflower provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Fleshy Pincushion (Asteraceae). desert annual with white to pale pink pincushion heads.

fm.Heads: White to pale pink pincushion, 1-2 cm

shrubs.hp: Important desert bee forage — visited by mining bees (Andrena), small carpenter bees (Ceratina), and bee flies (Bombyliidae). Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Fleshy Pincushion provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Yellow Cosmos (Asteraceae). yellow cosmos from Mexico — garden escape with orange-yellow flowers.

fm.Leaves: Bipinnately divided, broader segments than C. bipinnatus

shrubs.hp: Occasionally naturalizing on disturbed ground. Pollinator-friendly. Visited by native bees and butterflies including Painted Lady. Important garden pollinator plant.

shrubs.desc: Common Madia (Asteraceae). sticky tarweed with showy yellow ray flowers.

fm.Heads: Yellow rays with maroon base, 3-5 cm

shrubs.hp: Sticky tarweed with showy yellow ray flowers. Important late-season native bee and butterfly forage. Visited by sweat bees and mining bees attracted to tarry-scented glands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: San Clemente Island triteleia (Asparagaceae). Channel Islands endemic with blue-purple flowers.

fm.Flowers: Blue-purple tubular bells, 2-3 cm, in umbels

shrubs.hp: Very rare. Federally listed. Conservation priority. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, Cedar Waxwing, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Spineless Yucca (Asparagaceae). spineless yucca from Central America — common LA ornamental.
shrubs.hp: Spineless yucca from Central America — common LA ornamental. Lacks the sharp-pointed leaves of native yuccas. No obligate moth pollinator relationship. Visited by native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Dwarf coastweed (Asteraceae). Small coastal annual with yellow button flowers.
shrubs.hp: Important component of coastal strand and bluff plant communities. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Hairy Horsebrush (Asteraceae). white-woolly desert shrub.

fm.Warning: Toxic to sheep when combined with sagebrush

shrubs.hp: Toxic to sheep when combined with sagebrush browsing (causes 'bighead' disease). Visited by native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Everlasting neststraw (Asteraceae). Tiny woolly annual of open areas.
shrubs.hp: Among the most inconspicuous CA native plants but ecologically significant in soil ecology. Visited by minute native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Greata's aster (Asteraceae). LA County area endemic! Purple-rayed fall-blooming aster of shady mountain canyons.
shrubs.hp: LA County area endemic! Conservation concern. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: California Goldenbush (Asteraceae). low shrub of coastal dunes and bluffs.
shrubs.hp: Low shrub of coastal dunes and bluffs. Important late-season nectar. Stabilizes sand. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Burlew's Onion (Amaryllidaceae). mountain endemic with pink-purple flowers.
shrubs.hp: Important early-season bee pollinator plant. Visited by native bees and sweat bees. Onion scent deters Mule Deer browsing. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

shrubs.desc: Cheesebush (Asteraceae). low desert shrub with pungent odor when crushed.
shrubs.hp: Low desert shrub with pungent odor when crushed. Visited by specialist native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native chicory (Asteraceae). striking blue flowers along roadsides.

fm.Leaves: Dandelion-like basal rosette; stem leaves clasping
fm.Habitat: Roadsides; root used as coffee substitute

shrubs.hp: Tap root used as coffee substitute. European origin. Important bee forage. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and hover flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native red-seeded dandelion (Asteraceae). similar to common dandelion but with red-brown seeds.

fm.Heads: Yellow dandelion, 2-3 cm; deeply cut phyllaries
fm.Fruit: Red-brown achenes (vs olive in T. officinale)

shrubs.hp: European. Less common than T. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native purple salsify (Asteraceae). edible root (oyster plant).
shrubs.hp: Edible root (oyster plant). European. Roadside colonizer. Visited by native bees and hover flies. Massive seed puffs.

shrubs.desc: Pappose Tarweed (Asteraceae). annual of seasonal wetland margins and grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Annual of seasonal wetland margins and grasslands. Declining due to habitat loss. Some subspecies rare. Visited by sweat bees attracted to glandular resinous stems. Late-season nectar. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: San clemente island hazardia (Asteraceae). Channel Islands endemic shrub.
shrubs.hp: Conservation priority. Very limited distribution. Visited by native bees. Channel Islands endemic — conservation priority. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Mojave hole-in-the-sand plant (Asteraceae). grows through sand surface with only flower heads visible.

fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, sand; grows through sand surface

shrubs.hp: Grows through sand surface with only flower heads visible. Strong creosote-like scent. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: False Daisy (Asteraceae). small white-flowered plant of moist disturbed areas.
shrubs.hp: Used in traditional medicine (Eclipta alba) for hair darkening in Asian traditions. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Western marsh cudweed (Asteraceae). Woolly annual of seasonal wet areas.

fm.Habitat: Seasonal wet areas, vernal pool margins

shrubs.hp: Inconspicuous but ecologically important in vernal pool communities. Visited by small native bees. Woolly stems used as hummingbird nesting material. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Plummer's baccharis (Asteraceae). coastal sage scrub shrub endemic to SoCal.
shrubs.hp: Coastal sage scrub shrub endemic to SoCal. Important late-season pollinator resource. Conservation concern. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Spanish False Fleabane (Asteraceae). Mediterranean weed spreading in moist disturbed areas.
shrubs.hp: Mediterranean weed spreading in moist disturbed areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Camphor (Lauraceae). camphor tree — recently split from Cinnamomum.

fm.Habitat: Riparian corridors and stream banks; naturalized from cultivation

shrubs.hp: Camphor tree — recently split from Cinnamomum. Widely planted LA street tree. Naturalizing in some riparian areas. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: San Bernardino Mountains onion (Amaryllidaceae). mountain endemic.
shrubs.hp: Important early-season bee pollinator. Visited by native bees and sweat bees. Onion scent deters Mule Deer browsing. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

shrubs.desc: Non-native spiny cocklebur (Asteraceae). Triple-spined nodes and bur fruits.

fm.Thorns: Triple yellow spines at leaf bases (diagnostic)

shrubs.hp: More aggressively spiny than native X. strumarium. Livestock hazard. Roadside weed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Dunn's Lobelia (Campanulaceae). slender blue-flowered perennial of moist mountain meadows and seeps.
shrubs.hp: Dunn's lobelia — SoCal endemic of moist mountain meadows. Declining due to habitat loss. Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds (Anna's Hummingbird). Conservation concern.

shrubs.desc: Fringed Onion (Amaryllidaceae). fringed onion of rocky chaparral slopes.

fm.Flowers: Pink-white in loose umbel; fringed tepals

shrubs.hp: Fringed onion of rocky chaparral slopes. Distinctive fringed tepals. Important early-season native bee forage. Several varieties. Visited by native bees and sweat bees. Onion scent deters Mule Deer browsing. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

shrubs.desc: Non-native stinknet (Asteraceae). rapidly spreading South African weed.

fm.Heads: Yellow balls, 5-8 mm, in dense clusters

shrubs.hp: Rapidly spreading South African weed. Strong unpleasant odor. Causes contact dermatitis. Active eradication programs in AZ and CA deserts. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native tropical horseweed (Asteraceae). similar to E.
shrubs.hp: Becoming one of the most common urban weeds globally. Herbicide resistance evolving. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Late-season nectar source when other wildflowers have finished blooming.

shrubs.desc: Fremont's syntrichopappus (Asteraceae). small desert annual with yellow ray flowers.

fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, sandy and gravelly areas

shrubs.hp: Good spring bloom indicator. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Southern mule's ears (Asteraceae). Large-leaved perennial of mountain meadows with showy yellow sunflower heads.
shrubs.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. southern mule's ears provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Prostrate Capeweed (Asteraceae). mat-forming South African daisy invading coastal dunes and disturbed areas.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sand dunes; naturalized from cultivation

shrubs.hp: Mat-forming South African daisy invading coastal dunes and disturbed areas. Displacing native strand plants. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Snake's-head (Asteraceae). Distinctive nodding yellow buds resemble snake heads.
shrubs.hp: Distinctive nodding yellow buds resemble snake heads. Desert bee forage — visited by mining bees and small carpenter bees (Ceratina). Spring ephemeral. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native narrowleaf cottonrose (Asteraceae). tiny Mediterranean weed spreading in open grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Tiny Mediterranean weed spreading in open grasslands. Displacing native Logfia species. Visited by minute native bees. Important in grassland soil communities.

shrubs.desc: Malibu baccharis (Asteraceae). Locally endemic shrub known from Santa Monica Mountains area.
shrubs.hp: Locally endemic shrub known from Santa Monica Mountains area. Conservation concern due to limited range. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Naples garlic (Amaryllidaceae). showy white star flowers.

fm.Flowers: White stars in loose umbel, 1-2 cm, showy

shrubs.hp: Mediterranean. Garden escape colonizing road cuts and grassy areas. Visited by native bees. Bulbs eaten by California Ground Squirrel. Onion scent deters Mule Deer.

shrubs.desc: Seaside daisy (Asteraceae). Showy lavender-blue flowers on coastal bluffs.
shrubs.hp: Salt-tolerant. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Important late-season nectar source. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Cottonbatting plant (Asteraceae). Woolly-white everlasting of grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Provides nesting material for hummingbirds. Retains form when dried. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Mojave cottonthorn (Asteraceae). woolly desert shrub of high-desert flats.
shrubs.hp: Important habitat structure for desert reptiles. Visited by native bees. Toxic to sheep — woolly leaves deter browsing. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Coulter's horseweed (Asteraceae). annual of washes and disturbed areas.
shrubs.hp: Annual of washes and disturbed areas. Wind-dispersed seeds. Pioneer on recently disturbed ground. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Rayless Goldenhead (Asteraceae). rounded yellow button heads on low desert shrub.
shrubs.hp: Important fall nectar source in Joshua tree woodland. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Cape marigold (Asteraceae). orange-yellow daisies.
shrubs.hp: South African. Common garden flower escaping to disturbed areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native African flag (Iridaceae). orange tubular flowers attracting hummingbirds.

fm.Flowers: Orange-red tubular, 5-7 cm, in zigzag spike
fm.Habitat: Coastal areas; South African garden escape

shrubs.hp: Orange tubular flowers attracting hummingbirds. South African. Garden escape naturalizing on road cuts.

shrubs.desc: Non-native common soliva (Asteraceae). Lawn burweed — spiny fruits that embed painfully in bare feet.

fm.Fruit: Spine-tipped achenes that penetrate bare feet

shrubs.hp: Lawn burweed — spiny fruits that embed painfully in bare feet. South American. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Prairie sunflower (Asteraceae). Annual of sandy soils.

fm.Heads: Yellow, 4-6 cm; smaller than H. annuus

shrubs.hp: Annual of sandy soils. Visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, sunflower bees (Diadasia), and leaf-cutter bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and American Goldfinch. Distinguished from common sunflower by smaller size.

shrubs.desc: Cottonthorn (Asteraceae). Rigid spiny desert shrub.
shrubs.hp: Important structure for desert wildlife. Visited by native bees. Toxic to sheep — woolly leaves deter browsing. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Summer snowflake (Amaryllidaceae). white bell flowers.

fm.Flowers: White bells with green spots, 1-2 cm, in umbels

shrubs.hp: European bulb garden escape. Persists for decades in old garden sites. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Coast Range triteleia (Asparagaceae). Dark triteleia — distinctive dark blue-purple flowers.

fm.Flowers: Yellow to white tubular, 1-2 cm, in umbels

shrubs.hp: Among the darkest-flowered native bulbs. Pollinated by native bees. Bulbs were an important indigenous food source. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Blue Pigroot (Iridaceae). small blue flowers.
shrubs.hp: South American. Pollinated by small native bees (Halictidae and Andrenidae). Important early-season grassland nectar source.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Stinking chamomile (Asteraceae). similar to true chamomile but with foul odor and no medicinal properties.

fm.Heads: White rays, yellow disk, 2-3 cm; stinking odor

shrubs.hp: Similar to true chamomile but with foul odor and no medicinal properties. Roadside weed. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native blessed thistle (Asteraceae). spiny Mediterranean herb historically used medicinally.

fm.Heads: Yellow, 2-3 cm, surrounded by spiny bracts

shrubs.hp: Spiny Mediterranean herb historically used medicinally. Now a roadside weed in disturbed areas. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Tehachapi ragwort (Asteraceae). Yellow daisy of mountain openings.
shrubs.hp: Important pollinator resource at mid-elevations. Visited by native bees and syrphid flies. Mountain meadow species. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native trailing African daisy (Asteraceae). purple daisies.
shrubs.hp: South African ground cover escaping from gardens to coastal bluffs. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Spineless Horsebrush (Asteraceae). gray-woolly non-spiny desert shrub.
shrubs.hp: Important browse for Pronghorn and Mule Deer. Visited by native bees. Toxic to sheep — woolly leaves deter browsing. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Scarlet Kammetjie (Iridaceae). Scarlet freesia — red tubular flowers.
shrubs.hp: South African. Garden escape persisting in moist shaded areas. Visited by native bees. Non-native but provides some early-season nectar.

shrubs.desc: Grassland silverpuffs (Asteraceae). Dandelion-like puff balls in grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Dandelion-like puff balls in grasslands. CA endemic. Important indicator of intact grassland. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Oriental false hawksbeard (Asteraceae). tiny yellow composite of shaded disturbed areas.
shrubs.hp: Tiny yellow composite of shaded disturbed areas. Asian origin. Rapidly spreading urban weed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Thread-leaved brodiaea (Asparagaceae). FEDERALLY THREATENED.

fm.Habitat: Clay soils, vernal pool margins; federally threatened

shrubs.hp: FEDERALLY THREATENED. Disappearing due to development. Important for vernal pool habitat restoration. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, Cedar Waxwing, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: Slender woolly-marbles (Asteraceae). Tiny woolly annual of vernal pool margins.
shrubs.hp: Tiny woolly annual of vernal pool margins. Inconspicuous but important vernal pool indicator species. Visited by minute sweat bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Trailing bellflower (Campanulaceae). lavender star flowers cascading over walls.
shrubs.hp: Lavender star flowers cascading over walls. European garden escape. Minimal ecological impact. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Common Cat's-ear (Asteraceae). dandelion-like rosette weed of lawns and grasslands.

fm.Heads: Yellow dandelion-like, 2-3 cm; branching flower stalk

shrubs.hp: Dandelion-like rosette weed of lawns and grasslands. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Chinchweed (Asteraceae). aromatic desert annual with lemon-scented foliage.
shrubs.hp: Important desert bee forage after summer rains. Visited by small native bees. Lemon-scented desert annual. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Paperwhite (Amaryllidaceae). paperwhite narcissus — strongly fragrant white flowers.

fm.Flowers: White, fragrant, 2-3 cm, in clusters of 5-15

shrubs.hp: Mediterranean. Common garden bulb occasionally persisting in old garden sites. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native False Hawkbit (Asteraceae). Mediterranean weed with pale yellow dandelion-like flowers.
shrubs.hp: Mediterranean weed with pale yellow dandelion-like flowers. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Onion Weed (Amaryllidaceae). white star flowers from grass-like leaves.

fm.Flowers: White stars, 1-2 cm, in loose umbel
fm.Leaves: Grass-like, no onion smell (diagnostic vs Allium)

shrubs.hp: South American bulb. Extremely persistent once established. Faint garlic scent. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Three-cornered garlic (Amaryllidaceae). distinctive triangular flower stems with drooping white bells.

fm.Flowers: White bells with green stripe, in pendant umbel

shrubs.hp: Strong garlic scent. European. Visited by native bees. Bulbs eaten by California Ground Squirrel. Onion scent deters Mule Deer.

shrubs.desc: Common hillside daisy (Asteraceae). Showy yellow carpets on grassy slopes in good wildflower years.
shrubs.hp: Important bee forage during spring bloom. Visited by mining bees and sweat bees during spring superbloom displays. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Fragrant Everlasting (Asteraceae). woolly-white plant with sweet vanilla fragrance.

fm.Leaves: Oblanceolate, green above, woolly below, sweet-scented

shrubs.hp: Fragrant Everlasting provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Woolly stems provide nesting material for hummingbirds. Visited by small native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Willow baccharis (Asteraceae). Riparian shrub similar to B.
shrubs.hp: Riparian shrub similar to B. salicifolia. Important streamside habitat structure. Visited by native bees when flowering. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Erect Goldenaster (Asteraceae). Goldenaster — yellow daisy of dry slopes.
shrubs.hp: Goldenaster — yellow daisy of dry slopes. Late-season bloomer providing fall nectar for native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Common cocklebur (Asteraceae). Bur-fruited annual of moist disturbed areas.

fm.Fruit: Oval bur, 2-3 cm, covered in hooked spines

shrubs.hp: Bur-fruited annual of moist disturbed areas. Burs disperse on animal fur. Seeds toxic in early growth stage. Wind-pollinated. Fruit eaten by Lesser Goldfinch. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Brownplume Wirelettuce (Asteraceae). slender desert perennial with pink ray flowers.
shrubs.hp: Important late-season nectar source for desert pollinators. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Slimleaf bursage (Asteraceae). Desert shrub providing habitat structure for lizards and small mammals.
shrubs.hp: Desert shrub providing habitat structure for Western Fence Lizard and side-blotched lizard and Desert Cottontail and kangaroo rats. Wind-pollinated — contributes to desert allergy season. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch, American Goldfinch, Song Sparrow, and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native White rain-lily (Amaryllidaceae). white crocus-like flowers after rain.
shrubs.hp: South African. Garden escape. Low ecological impact. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Devil's Beggarticks (Asteraceae). barbed flat seeds ('stick-tights') dispersed on clothing and animal fur.
shrubs.hp: Barbed flat seeds ('stick-tights') dispersed on clothing and animal fur. Moist disturbed areas. Important waterfowl food. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Harlequin flower (Iridaceae). tricolored flowers.
shrubs.hp: South African. Garden escape. Low ecological impact. Pollinated by native bees and syrphid flies. Rhizomes/bulbs provide wildlife forage.

shrubs.desc: Annual saltmarsh aster (Asteraceae). Slender fall-blooming aster of moist saline soils.
shrubs.hp: Slender fall-blooming aster of moist saline soils. Important late-season nectar in salt marsh habitats. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Lemmon's syntrichopappus (Asteraceae). tiny yellow-rayed annual of desert sandy soils.
shrubs.hp: Tiny yellow-rayed annual of desert sandy soils. Mojave specialist. Important desert bee forage — visited by mining bees and small carpenter bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Pitted onion (Amaryllidaceae). Several varieties on rocky slopes.
shrubs.hp: Several varieties on rocky slopes. Important early native bee forage. Visited by native bees and sweat bees. Onion scent deters Mule Deer browsing. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher.

shrubs.desc: Q-tips (Asteraceae). tiny woolly annual with distinctive woolly round heads resembling cotton swabs.
shrubs.hp: Among the smallest composites. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Bigelow's sneezeweed (Asteraceae). showy yellow mountain wildflower of wet meadows.

fm.Heads: Yellow reflexed rays, raised brown disk, 3-5 cm

shrubs.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Bigelow's sneezeweed provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: White rabbit-tobacco (Asteraceae). Woolly-white everlasting of stream banks and washes.
shrubs.hp: Traditional indigenous medicinal plant. Woolly stems provide nesting material for hummingbirds. Visited by small native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Small Wirelettuce (Asteraceae). slender pink-flowered annual of open sandy areas.
shrubs.hp: Milky latex. Late-season pollinator resource. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Common Spikeweed (Asteraceae). Common tarweed — spiny-glandular annual of alkali flats and grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Common tarweed — spiny-glandular annual of alkali flats and grasslands. Important late-season bee forage despite unpleasant resin. Visited by sweat bees attracted to glandular resinous stems. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Island Sagebrush (Asteraceae). Channel Islands endemic.
shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Important island pollinator habitat. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Provides Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Bunch-flowered Daffodil (Amaryllidaceae). Polyanthus narcissus — fragrant clusters.

fm.Flowers: White with yellow cup, 3-4 cm, in clusters

shrubs.hp: Polyanthus narcissus — fragrant clusters. Mediterranean. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native black-eyed Susan (Asteraceae). Showy orange-yellow flowers with dark cone.

fm.Heads: Orange-yellow rays, dark brown-black cone, 5-7 cm

shrubs.hp: To eastern US, planted in wildflower mixes. Important bee forage. Visited by native bees, butterflies, and syrphid flies. Important garden pollinator. Important Gulf Fritillary and Painted Lady nectar source.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Safflower (Asteraceae). ancient oil and dye crop occasionally volunteering.
shrubs.hp: Ancient oil and dye crop occasionally volunteering. Source of food-grade orange dye. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Russian knapweed (Asteraceae). aggressive perennial spreading by deep creeping roots.
shrubs.hp: Aggressive perennial spreading by deep creeping roots. Contains sesquiterpene lactones toxic to horses (nigropallidal encephalomalacia). Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Desert Pincushion (Asteraceae). white pincushion heads in Mojave Desert.

fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, sandy and rocky areas

shrubs.hp: Important spring desert bee forage. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Poverty weed (Asteraceae). Povertyweed — low shrubby perennial of alkaline soils.

fm.Leaves: Fleshy, opposite below, alternate above, 2-5 cm

shrubs.hp: Povertyweed — low shrubby perennial of alkaline soils. Indicator of alkali habitat. Wind-pollinated. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native plains coreopsis (Asteraceae). bicolored yellow-and-red flowers.
shrubs.hp: Great Plains native. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Inyo threadplant (Campanulaceae). Curved-thread lobelia — tiny S-curved flower stems.
shrubs.hp: Curved-thread lobelia — tiny S-curved flower stems. Among the most diminutive native wildflowers. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Invasive Slender Thistle (Asteraceae). Mediterranean.
shrubs.hp: Mediterranean. Cal-IPC Moderate. Displaces native annuals. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Deltoid balsamroot (Asteraceae). Large arrow-shaped leaves and sunflower-like heads.
shrubs.hp: Important indigenous food plant — roots edible. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Yellow tack-stem (Asteraceae). Yellow tackstem — desert annual with distinctive tack-shaped glands on stems.
shrubs.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. yellow tack-stem provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Yellow Salsify (Asteraceae). Western salsify — yellow dandelion-like flowers.

fm.Heads: Yellow, 4-6 cm; long pointed bracts exceed rays

shrubs.hp: European. Edible tap root. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: California desertdandelion (Asteraceae). California desert-dandelion — yellow flowers with red-striped buds.
shrubs.hp: Desert bee forage — visited by mining bees and small carpenter bees (Ceratina). Spring ephemeral. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. California desertdandelion provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Silver Wormwood (Asteraceae). White sagebrush — silver-white aromatic leaves.

fm.Leaves: Lanceolate, entire or lobed, silver-white on both sides

shrubs.hp: Important ceremonial plant for indigenous peoples. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Provides Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native blue-and-white daisybush (Asteraceae). African daisy — white-purple daisies.

fm.Heads: White above, blue-purple below, yellow disk, 5-7 cm
fm.Habitat: Coastal areas; South African garden escape

shrubs.hp: South African. Very common LA ornamental. Garden escape. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Garden Lobelia (Campanulaceae). Edging lobelia — popular trailing garden annual with blue flowers.
shrubs.hp: South African. Occasional escape. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Parry's Wirelettuce (Asteraceae). slender desert perennial with pink ray flowers.
shrubs.hp: Milky latex deters herbivores. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Parry's Wirelettuce provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Dwarf Brodiaea (Asparagaceae). small ground-level flowers.

fm.Flowers: Purple bells in umbel, 1-2 cm, at ground level
fm.Size: Bulb, flowering nearly at soil surface

shrubs.hp: Important early-season pollinator resource. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, Cedar Waxwing, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel. Bulbs were a staple 'Indian potato' — roasted in earth ovens by Tongva and Chumash.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Willowleaf Lettuce (Asteraceae). Willow-leaved lettuce — slender European weed.
shrubs.hp: Willow-leaved lettuce — slender European weed. Milky sap. Roadside colonizer. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native bristleleaf pricklyleaf (Asteraceae). Dahlberg daisy — tiny aromatic yellow daisies.

fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, and woodland; naturalized from cultivation

shrubs.hp: Mexican. Popular ornamental occasionally naturalizing. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Heermann's tarweed (Asteraceae). Heermann's tarplant — sticky-glandular annual.
shrubs.hp: Host for the rare Heermann's checkerspot butterfly. Conservation interest. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native wild asparagus (Asparagaceae). Cultivated asparagus — culinary vegetable occasionally persisting from old plantings.
shrubs.hp: Cultivated asparagus — culinary vegetable occasionally persisting from old plantings. Bird-dispersed red berries. Visited by native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Dune Kammetjie (Iridaceae). Wild freesia — yellow fragrant flowers.

fm.Flowers: Yellow to cream, fragrant, 3-4 cm, in one-sided raceme

shrubs.hp: South African. Pollinated by native bees and syrphid flies. Rhizomes/bulbs provide wildlife forage.

shrubs.desc: Low woolly marbles (Asteraceae). Dwarf woolly-marbles — one of CA's smallest native plants.

fm.Size: Annual, 1-5 cm; one of smallest CA plants

shrubs.hp: Dwarf woolly-marbles — one of CA's smallest native plants. Inconspicuous but important. Visited by minute sweat bees. Vernal pool indicator species. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Brewer's fleabane (Asteraceae). Mountain perennial with lavender ray flowers.
shrubs.hp: Rocky slopes above 5,000 ft. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Late-season nectar source when other wildflowers have finished blooming. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Opposite-leaved Tarweed (Asteraceae). — one of the smallest composite plants.

fm.Size: One of smallest composites — annual, 2-8 cm

shrubs.hp: — one of the smallest composite plants. Tiny annual of open grassland. Easily overlooked but ecologically important. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native petite-licorice (Asteraceae). Silver bush everlasting — gray-leaved South African shrublet.

fm.Habitat: Coastal areas; South African garden escape

shrubs.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. petite-licorice is a distinctive member of its community.

shrubs.desc: Thorny Skeletonweed (Asteraceae). Thorny skeleton-weed — spiny leafless desert shrub.
shrubs.hp: Thorny skeleton-weed — spiny leafless desert shrub. Important habitat structure for small desert animals. Visited by native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Red Sunflower (Asteraceae). Mexican sunflower — large orange flower heads attracting butterflies.
shrubs.hp: Garden escape. Important late-season pollinator resource. Visited by native bees, butterflies including Painted Lady, and hummingbirds.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Bietou (Asteraceae). Trailing gazania — South African daisy naturalizing on coastal bluffs.
shrubs.hp: Trailing gazania — South African daisy naturalizing on coastal bluffs. Displaces native strand plants. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Giant Century Plant (Asparagaceae). Pulque agave — massive Mexican agave.

fm.Size: Very large rosette, 2-3 m wide; monocarpic

shrubs.hp: Pulque agave — massive Mexican agave. Source of pulque and mezcal. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Woolly fishhooks (Asteraceae). Tiny annual with hooked bracts.

fm.Heads: Tiny woolly, 3 mm, with hooked phyllaries

shrubs.hp: Tiny annual with hooked bracts. Among the most inconspicuous native wildflowers. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native field marigold (Asteraceae). small orange-yellow calendula.
shrubs.hp: European. Less showy than garden calendula. Roadside weed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Sticky snakeweed (Asteraceae). Threadleaf snakeweed — low shrub with tiny yellow heads.

fm.Heads: Tiny yellow, 1 ray + 1-2 disk flowers per head

shrubs.hp: Threadleaf snakeweed — low shrub with tiny yellow heads. Indicator of overgrazed rangeland. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Desert threadplant (Campanulaceae). Reddish thread-plant — tiny desert annual turning reddish in age.
shrubs.hp: Extremely inconspicuous. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Throatwort (Campanulaceae). blue throatwort — Mediterranean garden escape with dense blue flower clusters.

fm.Flowers: Dense flat-topped clusters of tiny blue-violet

shrubs.hp: Blue throatwort — Mediterranean garden escape with dense blue flower clusters. Naturalizing on walls and cliffs.

shrubs.desc: Star-flowered lily-of-the-valley (Asparagaceae). Starry false Solomon's seal — white star flowers and red berries along arching stems.

fm.Flowers: White stars in terminal raceme, 5-8 mm

shrubs.hp: Star-flowered lily-of-the-valley. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Hermit Thrush, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel. Visited by native bees. Rhizomatous — forms colonies in moist mountain woodland.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Bicolor Cobra Lily (Iridaceae). Two-toned African flag — orange-and-yellow flowers.
shrubs.hp: South African. Garden escape. Pollinated by native bees and syrphid flies. Rhizomes/bulbs provide wildlife forage.

shrubs.desc: Coast tarweed (Asteraceae). Sticky-glandular annual with small yellow flowers.
shrubs.hp: Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Strong tarry scent. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Kellogg's Tarweed (Asteraceae). Kellogg's tarplant — small annual of open grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Kellogg's tarplant — small annual of open grasslands. Visited by sweat bees and small native bees attracted to tarry-scented glands. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Sea Dahlia (Asteraceae). large yellow daisy of coastal bluffs.

fm.Heads: Large yellow, 5-8 cm, on thick peduncles

shrubs.hp: Important spring nectar for mining bees, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Superbloom carpet species. Sea Dahlia provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Arizona cottonrose (Asteraceae). small woolly annual of desert and grassland openings.
shrubs.hp: Small woolly annual of desert and grassland openings. Soil crust contributor. Visited by minute native bees. Important in grassland soil communities. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Trailing Treasureflower (Asteraceae). Trailing gazania — orange-yellow daisies.

fm.Heads: Orange to yellow, 5-8 cm, dark basal spots

shrubs.hp: South African ground cover. Naturalized on bluffs. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Red Hot Poker (Asphodelaceae). tall orange-red flower spikes attracting hummingbirds.

fm.Flowers: Dense bicolored spike — red buds opening yellow, 10-15 cm

shrubs.hp: Tall orange-red flower spikes attracting hummingbirds. South African. Important non-native hummingbird resource.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Stinking iris (Iridaceae). orange seed pods split open for winter display.

fm.Flowers: Dull lilac, 5-7 cm; brilliant orange seeds exposed in fall
fm.Leaves: Sword-shaped, dark green, foul-smelling when crushed
fm.Fruit: Capsule splitting to reveal orange-red seeds

shrubs.hp: European. Shade-tolerant. Garden escape. Pollinated by native bees. Deer-resistant rhizomes.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Mauritius hemp (Asparagaceae). massive agave relative with huge flower stalk.

fm.Flowers: Greenish-white bells in massive terminal panicle, 6-10 m

shrubs.hp: Massive agave relative with huge flower stalk. Tropical American. Occasionally persisting from old plantings. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Karoo Tulp (Iridaceae). Blue Cape iris — blue flowers.
shrubs.hp: South African. Garden escape naturalizing in disturbed areas. Pollinated by native bees and syrphid flies. Rhizomes/bulbs provide wildlife forage.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Swan-neck Agave (Asparagaceae) — Non-native foxtail agave — spineless agave with dramatic arching flower stalk.

fm.Leaves: Blue-green rosette, soft (no terminal spine), 50-70 cm

shrubs.hp: Foxtail agave — spineless agave with dramatic arching flower stalk. Mexican. Very common LA ornamental. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native common ragweed (Asteraceae). major pollen allergen.
shrubs.hp: Wind-pollinated. Produces enormous quantities of airborne pollen. Fall allergy culprit. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch, American Goldfinch, Song Sparrow, and White-crowned Sparrow. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Newhall sunflower (Asteraceae). Unexpected sunflower — recently described species from San Fernando Valley area.

fm.Habitat: San Fernando Valley area; recently described endemic

shrubs.hp: Unexpected sunflower — recently described species from San Fernando Valley area. Limited distribution. Conservation interest. Visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, and sunflower specialist bees (Diadasia). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and American Goldfinch.

shrubs.desc: Non-native grey-leaved euryops (Asteraceae). Golden shrub daisy — bright yellow flowers year-round.
shrubs.hp: South African. Very common LA ornamental. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Parikh's Thread-plant (Campanulaceae). rare desert Nemacladus.
shrubs.hp: Limited distribution. Conservation interest. Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds.

shrubs.desc: Palos Verdes Daisy (Asteraceae). Palos Verdes fleabane — local endemic known only from Palos Verdes Peninsula.

fm.Habitat: Palos Verdes Peninsula; critically rare endemic

shrubs.hp: Palos Verdes fleabane — local endemic known only from Palos Verdes Peninsula. Critically rare. Conservation priority. Visited by syrphid flies, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native soap aloe (Asphodelaceae). spotted leaves with coral flower spikes.

fm.Flowers: Orange-red tubular in flat-topped racemes, 3 cm
fm.Leaves: Triangular rosette, spotted, toothed margins, 15-25 cm

shrubs.hp: South African spotted aloe. Visited by hummingbirds (Anna's Hummingbird) and carpenter bees. Orange tubular flowers provide winter nectar when few other plants bloom.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Gallant Soldier (Asteraceae). Gallant-soldier — small white-rayed composite.

fm.Heads: Tiny, 5 white rays, yellow disk, 5 mm

shrubs.hp: Gallant-soldier — small white-rayed composite. Tropical American. Common garden weed. Edible leaves used in South American cuisine. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: New Mexico thistle (Asteraceae). pinkish flower heads.
shrubs.hp: Native thistle of desert-mountain transition. Important bumble bee forage — large flower heads provide abundant nectar and pollen. Visited by hummingbirds (Anna's Hummingbird). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Pennsylvania Cudweed (Asteraceae). Spoonleaf cudweed — woolly rosette annual.
shrubs.hp: Spoonleaf cudweed — woolly rosette annual. South American. Common urban weed of disturbed moist areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Featherweed (Asteraceae). Native cudweed of mountain openings.
shrubs.hp: Native cudweed of mountain openings. Important for native bee nesting material. Visited by small native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Holly dandelion (Asteraceae). Mojave keyflower — small desert annual with intricately lobed leaves pressed flat to ground.

fm.Leaves: Basal rosette, pinnately lobed with spiny tips, pressed flat

shrubs.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. holly dandelion provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: California Coreopsis (Asteraceae). yellow daisy of coastal sage scrub.
shrubs.hp: Important spring nectar for mining bees, sweat bees, and small butterflies. Superbloom carpet species. California Coreopsis provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native upright prairie coneflower (Asteraceae). Prairie coneflower — tall columnar flower cones.

fm.Heads: Tall columnar cone with reflexed yellow or red rays, 3-5 cm

shrubs.hp: Prairie coneflower — tall columnar flower cones. Great Plains native planted in wildflower mixes. Important bee forage. Visited by native bees and butterflies. Prairie pollinator plant.

shrubs.desc: Littleleaf horsebrush (Asteraceae). Smooth-leaved desert shrub.
shrubs.hp: Toxic to sheep. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Paris Daisy (Asteraceae). African bush daisy — bright yellow daisies year-round.
shrubs.hp: African bush daisy — bright yellow daisies year-round. South African. Very common LA ornamental. Minimal invasion risk. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native White beggarticks (Asteraceae) — Non-native white beggartick — tropical American weed with white ray flowers and barbed seeds.
shrubs.hp: White beggartick — tropical American weed with white ray flowers and barbed seeds. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Turkish Iris (Iridaceae). tall white iris.
shrubs.hp: Garden escape persisting in moist areas. Low ecological impact. Pollinated by native bees. Rhizomes contain toxic iridin — deer-resistant. Hybridizes freely, complicating taxonomy.

shrubs.desc: Non-native bitter lettuce (Asteraceae). Wild lettuce — tall European weed.
shrubs.hp: Wild lettuce — tall European weed. Contains lactucarium — mild sedative historically called 'lettuce opium.'. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Brewer's ragwort (Asteraceae). yellow mountain daisy of rocky slopes.
shrubs.hp: Alpine specialist above 7,000 ft. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: California ragwort (Asteraceae). yellow daisies on moist mountain slopes.
shrubs.hp: Important early-season pollinator resource. California ragwort provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Bud Sagebrush (Asteraceae). low spiny desert shrub.

fm.Leaves: Tiny, 3-lobed, deciduous; old leaf bases persist as spines

shrubs.hp: Important Pronghorn browse. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Desert Twinbugs (Asteraceae). Desert dicoria — gray-leaved annual of desert sand dunes.
shrubs.hp: Desert dicoria — gray-leaved annual of desert sand dunes. Important sand stabilizer. Wind-dispersed seeds. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Hoary Tansyaster (Asteraceae). Hoary aster — purple-rayed perennial of dry rocky slopes.
shrubs.hp: Hoary aster — purple-rayed perennial of dry rocky slopes. Important late-season pollinator plant in arid habitats. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Licorice plant (Asteraceae). silver-felted leaves with licorice scent.

fm.Habitat: Coastal areas; South African garden escape

shrubs.hp: South African ground cover escaping to coastal areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Leafy stemmed coreopsis (Asteraceae). Leafy-stemmed coreopsis — large yellow daisies of grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Leafy-stemmed coreopsis — large yellow daisies of grasslands. Important spring pollinator resource. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Mason's neststraw (Asteraceae). extremely rare vernal pool endemic.
shrubs.hp: Extremely rare vernal pool endemic. Among California's rarest plants. Conservation priority. Visited by minute native bees. Grassland soil ecology contributor. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Peck neststraw (Asteraceae). Baretwig neststraw — tiny annual of open areas.
shrubs.hp: Baretwig neststraw — tiny annual of open areas. Related to Micropus. Difficult to identify. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Venus's looking-glass (Campanulaceae). Small Venus' looking-glass — tiny annual with cleistogamous (self-pollinating) and open flowers on same stem.

fm.Flowers: Purple stars, 8-12 mm, in leaf axils

shrubs.hp: Small Venus' looking-glass — tiny annual with cleistogamous (self-pollinating) and open flowers on same stem. Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Mexican sunflower (Asteraceae). Tree marigold — large orange sunflower heads on tall stems.
shrubs.hp: Tropical American. Becoming invasive in some areas. Visited by native bees, butterflies including Painted Lady, and hummingbirds. Major Gulf Fritillary and Monarch nectar source. Important late-season butterfly magnet.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Fern-leaf Yarrow (Asteraceae). Fernleaf yarrow — flat golden flower clusters.
shrubs.hp: Fernleaf yarrow — flat golden flower clusters. Asian. Garden plant. Important bee forage. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native nipplewort (Asteraceae). small yellow composites.
shrubs.hp: European woodland weed naturalizing in shaded disturbed areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: California sunflower (Asteraceae). tall perennial of moist areas.
shrubs.hp: Important native bee and butterfly forage. Visited by bumble bees, sweat bees, and sunflower specialist bees (Diadasia). Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and American Goldfinch.

shrubs.desc: Non-native garden cosmos (Asteraceae). popular ornamental occasionally naturalizing.

fm.Heads: White, pink, or magenta rays, 5-8 cm

shrubs.hp: Popular ornamental occasionally naturalizing. Important pollinator garden plant. Mexican origin. Visited by native bees and butterflies including Painted Lady.

shrubs.desc: Sweetbush (Asteraceae). yellow button flowers on rush-like green stems.

fm.Leaves: Linear to absent; green rush-like stems

shrubs.hp: Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Sweetbush provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Royal Goldfields (Asteraceae). yellow grassland annual.
shrubs.hp: Important spring bee forage. Superbloom species. Visited by mining bees (Andrena) and sweat bees (Halictidae) in spring grassland blooms. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Brazilian bachelor's button (Asteraceae). Brazilian button flower — purple button heads.
shrubs.hp: Tropical garden escape. Limited naturalization. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Spreading chinchweed (Asteraceae). Prostrate chinchweed — mat-forming aromatic desert annual.

fm.Leaves: Linear, opposite, oil glands on margins

shrubs.hp: Prostrate chinchweed — mat-forming aromatic desert annual. Important soil surface stabilizer. Visited by small native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Barberry-leaf Goldenbush (Asteraceae). Barberry-leaved hazardia — Channel Islands endemic shrub.
shrubs.hp: Barberry-leaved hazardia — Channel Islands endemic shrub. Rare. Conservation concern. Visited by native bees. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native creeping groundsel (Asteraceae). aggressive South African vine smothering native vegetation in riparian corridors.
shrubs.hp: Aggressive South African vine smothering native vegetation in riparian corridors. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Short-Flowered Threadplant (Campanulaceae). Short-flowered thread-plant — tiny desert annual.
shrubs.hp: Short-flowered thread-plant — tiny desert annual. Among smallest CA native plants. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Western pearlflower (Campanulaceae). Rare-flowered heterocodon — tiny annual bellflower of vernal pool margins.
shrubs.hp: Rare-flowered heterocodon — tiny annual bellflower of vernal pool margins. Important vernal pool indicator species. Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds.

shrubs.desc: Mesa brodiaea (Asparagaceae). Jolon brodiaea — rare.
shrubs.hp: Jolon brodiaea — rare. Limited distribution. Conservation interest. Visited by native bees. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, Cedar Waxwing, Botta's Pocket Gopher, and California Ground Squirrel.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Wild Kopieva (Asphodelaceae). Stalked bulbine — yellow star flowers.

fm.Flowers: Yellow or orange stars, 1-2 cm, in slender racemes

shrubs.hp: South African. Common drought-tolerant ground cover in LA. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native common mugwort (Asteraceae). Mugwort — aromatic European herb.

fm.Leaves: Pinnately lobed, dark green above, white-woolly below

shrubs.hp: Traditional medicinal plant. Rhizomatous spreader in moist disturbed areas. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Provides Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Douglas' silverpuffs (Asteraceae). Douglas' microseris — yellow dandelion-like heads in grasslands.

fm.Leaves: Basal, linear to pinnately lobed, 10-20 cm

shrubs.hp: Important early-season bee forage. Edible roots — indigenous food plant. Visited by mining bees and sweat bees. Important grassland nectar source. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Low Pussytoes (Asteraceae). mat-forming alpine cushion plant.

fm.Habitat: Exposed alpine ridges, dry rocky slopes

shrubs.hp: Important soil stabilizer on exposed mountain slopes. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Smooth Beggarticks (Asteraceae). Smooth beggartick — showy yellow daisies in marsh edges.
shrubs.hp: Smooth beggartick — showy yellow daisies in marsh edges. Important waterfowl food. Barbed seeds disperse on fur and clothing. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Invasive spotted knapweed (Asteraceae). Cal-IPC High.
shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC High. Allelopathic root exudates inhibit native plant germination. Very difficult to eradicate. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Displaces native species — active management needed.

shrubs.desc: Green Rabbitbrush (Asteraceae). Needleleaf goldenbush — narrow-leaved desert shrub with yellow button heads.
shrubs.hp: Critical late-season (fall) nectar for native bees when few other plants bloom. Visited by bumble bees and sweat bees. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Green Rabbitbrush provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native rain daisy (Asteraceae). white daisies opening only in sun.

fm.Heads: White above, purple below, 4-5 cm; close in cloud

shrubs.hp: South African. Common LA wildflower mix component. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Marguerite daisy (Asteraceae). white or yellow daisies.
shrubs.hp: Canary Islands. Widely planted ornamental. Occasional garden escape. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native velvet groundsel (Asteraceae). Large velvety leaves with yellow flower clusters.
shrubs.hp: Mexican. Naturalizing in shaded riparian areas. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Shrubby alkali aster (Asteraceae). Succulent-leaved aster of alkaline soils and desert springs.
shrubs.hp: Important indicator of alkali seep habitat. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native New Zealand cabbage tree (Asparagaceae). Cabbage tree from New Zealand — planted in LA landscapes.

fm.Leaves: Long narrow sword-shaped, 30-100 cm, in terminal rosette

shrubs.hp: Cabbage tree from New Zealand — planted in LA landscapes. Palm-like form. Minimal ecological impact. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Grape hyacinth (Asparagaceae). dense blue flower spikes.

fm.Flowers: Dense raceme of dark blue grape-like bells, 3-5 mm

shrubs.hp: European bulb. Garden escape persisting for decades. Low ecological impact. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Garlic Chives (Amaryllidaceae). white flower clusters.

fm.Flowers: White stars in flat umbel, fragrant, 1 cm

shrubs.hp: Chinese culinary herb. Garden escape. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Sprenger's Asparagus (Asparagaceae). aggressive spreader via bird-dispersed red berries.

fm.Foliage: Dense bright green cladodes, arching stems
fm.Habitat: Gardens; South African — less invasive than A. aethiopicus

shrubs.hp: Aggressive spreader via bird-dispersed red berries. Smothering native understory in coastal canyons. Visited by native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Thread agave (Asparagaceae) — Non-native thread-leaf agave — distinctive white threads curling from leaf margins.

fm.Leaves: Dark green rosette with white filaments peeling from margins

shrubs.hp: Thread-leaf agave — distinctive white threads curling from leaf margins. Mexican. Ornamental. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Large Fortnight Lily (Iridaceae). Large wild iris — white iris-like flowers.

fm.Flowers: White with purple/yellow markings, iris-like, 8-10 cm
fm.Habitat: Gardens; South African — very common LA plant

shrubs.hp: South African. Common LA landscape plant. Minimal escape risk. Pollinated by native bees and syrphid flies. Rhizomes/bulbs provide wildlife forage.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Bay laurel (Lauraceae). Mediterranean culinary herb.

fm.Habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub, and woodland; naturalized from cultivation

shrubs.hp: Mediterranean culinary herb. Large planted specimens in older LA neighborhoods. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native shaggy soldier (Asteraceae). Shaggy-soldier — hairy variant of G.

fm.Heads: Tiny, 5 white rays, 5 mm; hairy stems (vs smooth G. parviflora)

shrubs.hp: Parviflora. Tropical American garden weed. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Wirestem (Asteraceae). Wireweed — slender wiry annual of open grasslands.

fm.Heads: Tiny, 5-8 mm, yellow or white rays

shrubs.hp: Wireweed — slender wiry annual of open grasslands. Inconspicuous but important in grassland ecology. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Alkali goldenbush (Asteraceae). Shrub of alkaline soils and desert washes.
shrubs.hp: Shrub of alkaline soils and desert washes. Visited by native bees. Important late-season desert nectar source. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Cleveland's desert dandelion (Asteraceae). Cleveland's desert-dandelion — yellow flowers.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, sandy areas; SoCal endemic

shrubs.hp: Named for San Diego naturalist Daniel Cleveland. Southern CA endemic. Desert bee forage — visited by mining bees and small carpenter bees (Ceratina). Spring ephemeral. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: San Gabriel bluecup (Campanulaceae). Bluecup — small blue bell-shaped flowers in grasslands.
shrubs.hp: Important native bee forage. Several varieties. Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds.

shrubs.desc: Non-native absinthe wormwood (Asteraceae). Wormwood — intensely bitter aromatic herb.

fm.Heads: Tiny yellow globose, in drooping panicles
fm.Leaves: Bipinnately divided, silky gray-green, intensely bitter

shrubs.hp: Wormwood — intensely bitter aromatic herb. European. Source of absinthe flavoring. Garden escape. Foundation species for Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat. Important for sage scrub ecosystem. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Invasive woolly distaff thistle (Asteraceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.

fm.Heads: Yellow, surrounded by long spiny bracts

shrubs.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Livestock hazard. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Displaces native species — active management needed.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Canary Island Marguerite (Asteraceae). Fennel-leaved daisy — feathery foliage with white daisies.
shrubs.hp: Fennel-leaved daisy — feathery foliage with white daisies. Canary Islands. Garden escape. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Desert baccharis (Asteraceae). Bright green rush-like stems.

fm.Leaves: Reduced or absent; green broom-like stems

shrubs.hp: Important habitat structure. Important late-season nectar for migrating butterflies, native bees, and beneficial wasps. Dioecious — separate male and female plants. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Yellow Rabbitbrush (Asteraceae). sticky-leaved desert shrub.
shrubs.hp: Important fall nectar for native bees when few other desert plants bloom. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Beautiful Woolly Sunflower (Asteraceae). Yellowray goldfields — tiny desert annual forming yellow carpets.
shrubs.hp: Yellowray goldfields — tiny desert annual forming yellow carpets. Spring superbloom species. Visited by mining bees (Andrena), sweat bees, and small butterflies. Important early-season nectar on dry slopes. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Curlycup Gumweed (Asteraceae). Curly-cup gumweed — sticky resinous flower heads with curled bracts.

fm.Heads: Yellow rays, 2-3 cm; strongly recurved (squarrose) bracts

shrubs.hp: Curly-cup gumweed — sticky resinous flower heads with curled bracts. Important late-season bee forage. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Broom Snakeweed (Asteraceae). Low desert shrub.  Yellow button heads.
shrubs.hp: Indicator of overgrazed or disturbed rangeland. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Island hazardia (Asteraceae). Channel Islands endemic.
shrubs.hp: Channel Islands endemic. Rare. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native max chrysanthemum (Asteraceae). Shasta daisy — classic white garden daisy.
shrubs.hp: Shasta daisy — classic white garden daisy. Hybrid origin. Occasional garden escape. Popular cut flower. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Sowthistle desert dandelion (Asteraceae). Sowthistle desert-dandelion — yellow flowers.
shrubs.hp: Desert bee forage — visited by mining bees and small carpenter bees (Ceratina). Spring ephemeral. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator visits. sowthistle desert dandelion provides late-season nectar in open habitats. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native German Chamomile (Asteraceae). traditional medicinal tea herb.

fm.Heads: White rays, yellow cone disk, 1-2 cm; receptacle hollow

shrubs.hp: Traditional medicinal tea herb. Garden and field weed. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Nuttall's threadplant (Campanulaceae). Branching thread-plant — tiny many-branched annual of sandy soils.
shrubs.hp: Branching thread-plant — tiny many-branched annual of sandy soils. Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds.

shrubs.desc: Annual turtleback (Asteraceae). Turtleback — small grey rounded annual of alkaline desert soils.
shrubs.hp: Turtleback — small grey rounded annual of alkaline desert soils. Distinctive turtle-shell growth form. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and Dark-eyed Junco.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Creeping Bellflower (Campanulaceae). aggressive European invader of gardens.

fm.Habitat: Urban landscapes and gardens; naturalized from cultivation

shrubs.hp: Aggressive European invader of gardens. Deep roots make eradication nearly impossible. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

shrubs.desc: Non-native fringed sagebrush (Asteraceae) — Non-native fringed sage — silvery aromatic subshrub.

fm.Habitat: Dry rocky slopes; Great Plains native

shrubs.hp: Fringed sage — silvery aromatic subshrub. Great Plains native planted ornamentally. Extremely cold-hardy. Foundation species for Coastal California Gnatcatcher habitat. Important for sage scrub ecosystem. Wind-pollinated — major pollen allergen. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Tricolor Daisy (Asteraceae). Painted daisy — colorful concentric-ringed flowers.

fm.Heads: Concentric rings of red, yellow, white, 4-6 cm

shrubs.hp: Moroccan. Annual garden flower occasionally naturalizing. Composite flower heads maximize pollinator efficiency — visited by native bees, butterflies, and beneficial syrphid flies.

shrubs.desc: Non-native Succulent Bush Ragwort (Asteraceae). Succulent bush senecio — pencil-like succulent stems.
shrubs.hp: South African. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids toxic to livestock. Visited by syrphid flies, small native bees, and day-flying moths. Some species are significant invasives.

grasses.desc: California state grass. Once dominant across millions of acres of CA grassland — now reduced to fragments by exotic annual grasses.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands, oak woodland — the iconic California bunchgrass
fm.Conservation: CA state grass — once dominant, now rare
fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with long twisted awns — awns 2-3 in (longer than S. lepida)
fm.vs: Foothill Needlegrass (S. lepida) is smaller (1-2 ft), finer, with shorter awns, and prefers drier slopes

grasses.hp: California state grass. Once dominant across millions of acres — now reduced to fragments by exotic annual grasses. Deep roots stabilize soil and sequester carbon. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by California Towhee, Lesser Goldfinch, and Song Sparrow. Important native bunchgrass. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Barn Owl hunts California Vole in purple needlegrass grassland. Voles create runway systems through grass bases.

grasses.desc: Large bunchgrass forming dense tussocks. Important basket-weaving material for indigenous peoples. Stream margins.

fm.Cultural: Used in Tongva/Chumash basketry

grasses.hp: Important basket-weaving material for Tongva and Chumash peoples. Stream margin stabilizer. Wind-pollinated. Native bunchgrass of moist mountain slopes. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Dense tussocks provide cover for California Quail chicks.

grasses.desc: Massive native grass to 10 ft. Forms tall clumps in coastal and foothill areas. Fire-resprouting. Important erosion control plant.

fm.Ecology: Fire-resprouting, erosion control

grasses.hp: Massive native grass providing cover for ground-nesting birds (Horned Lark, Western Meadowlark) and California Ground Squirrel and Brush Rabbit. Fire-adapted — vigorous rhizome resprouting. Wind-pollinated. Important restoration bunchgrass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch.

grasses.desc: Native perennial bunchgrass of oak woodland understory. Finer than Purple Needlegrass. More shade-tolerant.

fm.Habitat: Dry foothill slopes, coastal sage scrub
fm.Inflorescence: Slender open panicle with sharp-awned seeds — awns shorter than S. pulchra
fm.vs: Purple Needlegrass (S. pulchra) is taller (2-3 ft), more robust, with longer 2-3 in awns

grasses.hp: Bunchgrass providing habitat structure for ground-nesting birds (Horned Lark, Western Meadowlark) and California Ground Squirrel and Brush Rabbit. More shade-tolerant than S. pulchra. Wind-pollinated. Important native bunchgrass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch.

grasses.desc: Blue-green native grass of woodland openings. Important forage grass. Relatively shade-tolerant for a grass.
grasses.hp: Shade-tolerant. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, Lesser Goldfinch, and California Quail. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Important component of native grassland restoration.

grasses.desc: Non-native Dominant lawn and park grass across LA. African/Asian origin. Extremely aggressive — impossible to eradicate once established.
grasses.hp: Extremely aggressive via stolons and rhizomes. Pollen allergen. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive European annual grass that dominates most CA grasslands. Golden in summer. Largely replaced native perennial bunchgrasses.
