fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — annual grass creates flash fuel

grasses.hp: Dominant invasive — largely replaced native perennial bunchgrass prairie statewide. The golden grass defining California's summer hillsides is not native. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, Lesser Goldfinch, and California Quail. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Wild oat — covered California's lowlands by 1850, replacing native bunch grasses. Creates fire-prone fuel load.

grasses.desc: Invasive Aggressive annual grass with sharp-awned seeds that penetrate animal skin. Fire-promoting — increases fire frequency in native habitats.

fm.Awns: Long, sharp — can injure livestock mouths
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, roadsides — dominant invasive grass in coastal LA
fm.Ecology: Fire-promoting invasive
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — creates continuous fine fuel layer; increases fire frequency
fm.Inflorescence: Large open drooping panicle with stiff awns — spikelets to 3 in long (larger than Red Brome)
fm.vs: Red Brome (B. madritensis) is smaller, more compact, with reddish-purple tinge at maturity

grasses.hp: Fire-cycle driver — creates continuous fine fuel that increases fire frequency in native chaparral and sage scrub, converting shrubland to annual grassland. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Ripgut brome — barbed awns can penetrate eyes and mouths of livestock and pets, causing serious injury.

grasses.desc: Invasive Massive bamboo-like grass to 30 ft choking riparian corridors. Active removal programs.

fm.Ecology: Consumes enormous water
fm.Fire: Fire-promoting — burns intensely; resprouts rapidly after fire

grasses.hp: Among the most damaging invasive plants in California riparian corridors. Consumes enormous water, displaces native willows, and burns intensely. Active removal statewide. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Despite ecological damage, provides nesting structure for Least Bell's Vireo (ESA endangered) in some degraded riparian corridors — a conservation paradox.

grasses.desc: Low-growing native grass of salt marshes and alkaline flats. Tolerates extreme salt concentrations that kill most plants. Key species for coastal wetland restoration. Spreads by rhizomes.
grasses.hp: Salt-excreting leaves create distinctive white crusts. Foundation species of salt marsh and alkali flat communities. Seeds eaten by shorebirds. Important Belding's Savannah Sparrow habitat.

grasses.desc: Native bunchgrass with distinctive sideways-hanging seed heads resembling eyelashes. Extremely drought-tolerant.
grasses.hp: Important for soil carbon sequestration. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Delicate purple-tinged bunchgrass with three-pronged awns. Desert and dry foothill grasslands.
grasses.hp: Purple three-awn with distinctive three-pronged seed. Important soil stabilizer on arid slopes. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Large bunchgrass of alkaline flats. Open airy seed heads. Important for alkali meadow restoration.
grasses.hp: Extremely salt-tolerant grass of alkaline flats and desert springs. Important for salt marsh and alkaline wetland restoration. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, Lesser Goldfinch, Gambel's Quail, and Horned Lark. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Alkali sacaton — deep-rooted bunchgrass of alkaline soils. Provides cover for Burrowing Owl near burrows.

grasses.desc: Native meadow grass of moist areas. Short bristly heads. Important component of native grass restoration.
grasses.hp: Moist meadows and alkali flats. One of few native barley species. Important wetland indicator. Wind-pollinated. Barbed awns disperse on animal fur. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Tough dark green stems in wet areas. Round stems (not triangular like sedges). Important wetland component.
grasses.hp: Important for wetland restoration. Indigenous peoples used stems for basket weaving. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees.

grasses.desc: Fine-textured native grass used as lawn alternative. Much less water than Kentucky Bluegrass. Coastal and foothill meadows.
grasses.hp: Fine-textured native grass of coastal bluffs and meadows. Important soil binder on sandy soils. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Native bunchgrass with nodding awns. More compact than Purple Needlegrass. Dry grasslands and openings.
grasses.hp: Graceful drooping inflorescence. Deep-rooted native bunchgrass. Important for California grassland restoration. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by California Towhee, Lesser Goldfinch, and Song Sparrow. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Elegant desert bunchgrass with large nutritious seeds. Major food source for indigenous peoples — ground into flour. Sandy desert flats.

fm.Cultural: Staple food for Paiute and other peoples

grasses.hp: Highly nutritious seeds were staple indigenous food. Drought-tolerant. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Rhizomatous native grass forming sod-like patches. Tolerates seasonal flooding and alkaline soil. Restoration workhorse.

fm.Ecology: Important restoration species

grasses.hp: Rhizomatous. Important for wetland and alkali site restoration. Salt-tolerant. Dense cover for nesting birds. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Compact native bunchgrass with one-sided panicle. Very widespread — found coast to alpine. Drought-tolerant.
grasses.hp: Extremely widespread native grass. Tolerates heavy soils. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Provides ground-level habitat for native bees.

grasses.desc: Attractive native bunchgrass with delicate one-sided panicles nodding from slender stems. Common in oak woodland understory and rocky slopes. Excellent drought-tolerant garden native and restoration species.
grasses.hp: One of few grasses thriving in shrub shade. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Wind-pollinated. Chaparral understory grass. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Desert bunchgrass with delicate open panicles emerging from leaf sheaths. Important sand-stabilizing grass. Seeds eaten by birds.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle, often partly enclosed in sheath
fm.Ecology: Sand stabilizer, bird food

grasses.hp: Tiny hidden seeds. Important desert stabilizer on loose sandy soils. Wind-pollinated. Salt-tolerant — salt marsh and cordgrass habitats. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Robust native brome grass common on road cuts and woodland openings. Flat, somewhat drooping panicle. Important restoration species.

fm.Ecology: Important restoration species

grasses.hp: Large native bunchgrass of woodland openings. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Feathery silver seed heads on tall cane-like stems. Native bunchgrass of dry rocky slopes. More common in SoCal than other bluestems.
grasses.hp: Warm-season grass with distinctive silver-hairy nodes. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Dense clumps of smooth cylindrical stems in wet areas. Flowers emerge from side of stem. One of the most common rushes in LA riparian areas. Not a true grass — rush family.

fm.Form: Dense clumps of smooth cylindrical stems
fm.Flowers: Small brown clusters emerging from stem side
fm.Ecology: Important wetland indicator species

grasses.hp: Pith used for rush candles. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Important wetland habitat. Soft rush — Black Phoebe perches on stems along streams. Provides ground-level habitat for Pacific Tree Frog.

grasses.desc: Iris-like flat leaves on rush stems. Wet meadows and stream margins. Among the most common LA County rushes. Not a grass — rush family.
grasses.hp: Distinguished from other rushes by iris-like foliage. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Iris-leaved rush — dense clumps provide Song Sparrow and Common Yellowthroat nesting habitat in wetland margins.

grasses.desc: Slender dark sedge forming dense stands in moist meadows. Rhizomatous — spreads to form sod. Important restoration species for seasonal wetlands.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense narrow spike of clustered spikelets

grasses.hp: Sedge of moist meadows and stream banks. Rhizomatous — forms dense sods. Important wetland restoration species. Provides cover for ground-nesting birds. Wind-pollinated sedge. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Tiny native annual grass of grasslands and openings. One of the most common native grasses but completely overlooked due to small size.
grasses.hp: Important in intact native grassland communities. Short-lived. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch, House Finch, and Song Sparrow. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. One of the most widespread native annual grasses.

grasses.desc: Invasive Bushy foxtail seed heads that break apart and embed in dog fur and socks. European invasive. Extremely common on every disturbed ground. Seeds can injure dogs' ears and feet.
grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Foxtail barley whose barbed awns embed painfully in dog paws, ears, and noses. Common veterinary hazard. Dominates disturbed urban areas. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Displaces native species — active management needed.

grasses.desc: Invasive Slender rat-tail-like seed heads. European invasive annual grass dominating disturbed grasslands. One of the most common grasses in LA County.
grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Fine fuel creating continuous fire load in grasslands. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: The largest native needlegrass — tall dense clumps. Important bunchgrass of chaparral understory and oak woodland. Fire-adapted — resprouts vigorously.

fm.Ecology: Important bunchgrass — fire-adapted

grasses.hp: Bunchgrass providing habitat structure for ground-nesting birds (Horned Lark, Western Meadowlark) and California Ground Squirrel and Brush Rabbit. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch.

grasses.desc: Graceful drooping panicle with small spikelets. The most common woodland grass in LA County. Shady chaparral and oak woodland understory.
grasses.hp: Perennial grass of chaparral understory and rocky slopes. Important for erosion control on steep slopes. One of few native grasses thriving in chaparral shade. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Green umbrella-like flower heads on triangular stems. Common in moist disturbed areas and stream margins. Not a grass — sedge family.

fm.Inflorescence: Pale green spikelets in umbel-like clusters

grasses.hp: Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native insects in wetland communities.

grasses.desc: Non-native Mediterranean grass invading LA chaparral and sage scrub. Dense clumps with drooping seed heads. Increasingly problematic — converting native shrubland to grassland.
grasses.hp: Mediterranean perennial displacing native bunchgrasses. Less invasive than annual grasses. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Tall native grass with distinctive bushy white seed heads in fall. Moist areas and seeps. Among the most visually striking native grasses.
grasses.hp: Moist areas and stream banks. Warm-season grass providing fall structure. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Delicate purple-tinged native annual grass. Open disturbed areas. Often overlooked but common in grasslands and chaparral openings.
grasses.hp: Annual muhly grass of rocky desert slopes. Important early-season seed source for desert birds. Wind-pollinated. Native bunchgrass of moist mountain slopes. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Fine stems used in Cahuilla and Tongva basket weaving — provides texture and pattern.

grasses.desc: Tall smooth rush — the primary basket-weaving rush of indigenous SoCal peoples. Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla all used for basket construction. Disappearing from the LA River.

fm.Stems: Round, smooth, very tough, 1-2 m (tallest native rush)
fm.Habitat: Wet areas; important basket-weaving material for indigenous peoples

grasses.hp: Primary basket-weaving rush of Tongva, Chumash, and Cahuilla peoples — disappearing from the LA River due to channelization and water diversion. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Important wetland habitat.

grasses.desc: Tufted native bunchgrass of mountain meadows and moist grasslands. Important component of native grassland restoration. Wider leaves than needlegrasses.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with few large spikelets

grasses.hp: Important native grass of coastal and montane meadows. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Compact silvery spike on slender native bunchgrass. Dry grasslands. Named for its June peak bloom. Important component of California native grasslands.
grasses.hp: Important forage for wildlife. Less showy than other native grasses but ecologically important in intact grassland communities. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive South African grass aggressively invading LA County shaded habitats — oak woodland, riparian, north-facing slopes. Cal-IPC Moderate. Shade-tolerant, which makes it uniquely destructive among invasive grasses. Displacing native understory across the SMM and SGM.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with distinctive 6 glumes per spikelet
fm.Ecology: SHADE-TOLERANT invasive — uniquely destructive because it invades intact woodland understory
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, riparian, north-facing slopes — spreading rapidly
fm.vs: Perennial Veldt Grass (E. calycina) prefers sun and sandy soils; Upright Veldt Grass (E. erecta) invades SHADE

grasses.hp: Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Cal-IPC High — uniquely destructive because it invades intact SHADED native woodland understory where most invasive grasses cannot grow. Displaces native ferns and wildflowers under oaks.

grasses.desc: Invasive South African grass invading coastal dunes and sandy habitats. Cal-IPC High. More sun-tolerant than E. erecta. Particularly destructive at El Segundo Dunes and coastal strand habitats.

fm.Ecology: Sand dune and coastal strand invader — FULL SUN species
fm.Habitat: Coastal dunes, sandy habitats — devastating at El Segundo Dunes
fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle, spikelets with prominent hairy lemmas
fm.vs: Upright Veldt Grass (E. erecta) is shade-tolerant and invades woodland understory; Perennial Veldt (E. calycina) needs SUN

grasses.hp: Sand dune and coastal strand invader — particularly devastating at El Segundo Dunes, threatening habitat for the federally endangered El Segundo Blue butterfly. Stabilizes sand that native dune species require to shift.

grasses.desc: Invasive Giant South American grass with showy white plume seed heads to 10 ft. Cal-IPC High. Each plume produces 100,000 wind-dispersed seeds. Widely planted ornamentally, now invading coastal bluffs and disturbed areas.

fm.Inflorescence: WHITE plumes — requires pollination to set seed
fm.vs: Jubata Grass (C. jubata) has PURPLE-PINK plumes and reproduces asexually (no pollen needed)

grasses.hp: Each plume produces ~100,000 wind-dispersed seeds. Widely planted ornamentally but escaping to coastal bluffs where it displaces native vegetation. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive Reddish-purple annual brome dominating disturbed areas and desert grasslands. Cal-IPC High. Fuels fire cycle in desert — converts native shrubland to annual grassland through repeated burning.

fm.Inflorescence: Compact, reddish-purple at maturity (vs large and drooping in Ripgut)
fm.Ecology: Fire cycle driver in desert — converts shrubland to grassland
fm.Habitat: Disturbed areas, desert grasslands — extremely common
fm.vs: Ripgut Brome (B. diandrus) is taller with larger drooping spikelets; Red Brome is compact and purplish

grasses.hp: Fire-cycle driver in desert ecosystems — converts native shrubland to annual grassland through repeated burning. Dries 4–6 weeks before natives, creating early fire fuel. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive The West's most destructive invasive grass. Dries early, creating fire fuel weeks before natives. Has shortened fire return intervals from 60-110 years to 3-5 years across millions of acres. Present in SGM and Antelope Valley.

fm.Inflorescence: Drooping, purplish, with barbed awns that embed in socks
fm.Origin: Eurasian — Cal-IPC High. Most destructive invasive grass in western North America.
fm.Ecology: Dries 4-6 weeks before natives, creating continuous fire fuel. Shortened fire intervals from decades to years.
fm.Habitat: SGM slopes, Antelope Valley, disturbed areas

grasses.hp: The West's most destructive invasive grass. Shortened fire return intervals from 60–110 years to 3–5 years across millions of acres, converting sagebrush to grassland. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive Dominant annual grass of California grasslands — more common than A. fatua. Golden dried stalks define the California summer hillside. Replaced native perennial bunchgrass prairie across the state.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with long twisted awns
fm.Ecology: Replaced native perennial bunchgrass prairie statewide
fm.Habitat: Grasslands, hillsides — the golden grass of California summer

grasses.hp: Dominant invasive — replaced native perennial bunchgrass across California. More common than A. fatua. The golden summer hillsides are this species. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Cal-IPC Moderate — creates fine fuel that increases fire frequency, which favors annual grasses over native perennial bunchgrasses. Self-reinforcing invasion cycle.

grasses.desc: Invasive Purple-pink plumed pampas grass — even more invasive than white pampas grass. Cal-IPC High. Reproduces asexually (no pollination needed). Each plant produces millions of wind-dispersed seeds. Coastal bluffs and disturbed slopes.

fm.Inflorescence: PURPLE-PINK plumes (vs white in C. selloana)
fm.Ecology: Asexual reproduction — no pollination needed. Even more invasive than C. selloana.
fm.vs: Pampas Grass (C. selloana) has WHITE plumes and requires pollination to seed

grasses.hp: Even more invasive than white pampas grass — reproduces asexually without pollination. Each plant produces millions of wind-dispersed seeds. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Cal-IPC High — reproduces asexually (apomictic), meaning every plant produces viable seed without pollination. Invades coastal bluffs, displacing native coastal sage scrub. No effective biological control known.

grasses.desc: Invasive Fountain Grass (Poaceae). Extreme fire hazard — burns hot and fast, converting native scrub to invasive grass-fire cycle.

fm.Habitat: Roadsides, dry slopes; extreme fire hazard

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC High. Extreme fire hazard — burns hot and fast, converting native scrub to invasive grass-fire cycle. Rapidly reestablishes after burning. Active eradication programs statewide. Originally planted as ornamental. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Native giant wild rye (Poaceae) — Giant native bunchgrass forming dramatic clumps to 10 ft.
grasses.hp: Giant native bunchgrass forming dramatic clumps to 10 ft. Seeds eaten by California Quail and Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Important erosion control on steep slopes. Provides cover for ground-nesting birds. Seeds harvested as food by indigenous peoples. Tough stems used in construction.

grasses.desc: Non-native Umbrella Papyrus (Cyperaceae). Umbrella sedge commonly escaped from water gardens into riparian areas.

fm.Leaves: Reduced to sheaths; bracts are the 'leaves'
fm.Habitat: Pond margins, wet areas; escaped water gardens

grasses.hp: Umbrella sedge commonly escaped from water gardens into riparian areas. Spreads via rhizome fragments in flood debris. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native insects in wetland communities.

grasses.desc: Non-native Red Brome (Bromus) (Poaceae). Annual grass converting native desert scrub to grassland through fire-grass cycle.

fm.Habitat: Dry open areas; major invasive fine fuel

grasses.hp: Annual grass converting native desert scrub to grassland through fire-grass cycle. Dries earlier than native plants, creating fine fuel. Among the most ecologically damaging invasive grasses in Mojave Desert. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Smilo Grass (Oloptum) (Poaceae). Perennial grass from Mediterranean.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with large shiny spikelets

grasses.hp: Perennial grass from Mediterranean. Colonizes disturbed areas and roadsides. Less ecologically damaging than annual bromes but displaces native bunchgrasses. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native goldentop grass (Poaceae). Mediterranean grass with showy golden inflorescences.

fm.Habitat: Rocky areas, road cuts; ornamental value

grasses.hp: Mediterranean grass with showy golden inflorescences. Common in disturbed areas and desert washes. Forms dense stands excluding native annuals. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive rabbitfoot grass (Poaceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense silky-fluffy spike, 3-8 cm, pale green to golden

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Grass of moist disturbed areas. Colonizes wetland margins, potentially displacing native rush and sedge communities. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive common soft brome (Poaceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.
grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Dominant invasive annual grass in coastal grasslands. Contributes to annual grass fire cycle that has converted millions of acres of native perennial grassland statewide. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Rescue Brome (Poaceae). South American brome occasionally used for erosion control.

fm.Inflorescence: Open drooping panicle, large flat spikelets
fm.Spikelets: Large, flat, 2-3 cm, awnless or short-awned

grasses.hp: South American brome occasionally used for erosion control. Less invasive than other bromes but colonizes disturbed riparian areas. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Dallis grass (Poaceae). South American grass naturalizing in moist disturbed areas and lawns.

fm.Spikelets: Paired, round, 3 mm, with sticky silky hairs

grasses.hp: South American grass naturalizing in moist disturbed areas and lawns. Sticky seed clusters adhere to clothing and animal fur for dispersal. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Spiny rush (Juncaceae). Spiny rush of coastal salt marshes and alkaline springs.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense lateral clusters near stem tip

grasses.hp: Spiny rush of coastal salt marshes and alkaline springs. Important nesting cover for marsh birds. Salt-tolerant. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Important wetland habitat.

grasses.desc: Non-native Annual Meadow-grass (Poaceae). Annual grass — the most widespread grass weed globally.

fm.Habitat: Lawns, sidewalk cracks; year-round seed production

grasses.hp: Annual grass — the most widespread grass weed globally. Ubiquitous in lawns, sidewalk cracks, and disturbed areas. Produces seed year-round. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive harding grass (Poaceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.
grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Perennial grass planted for forage but escaping to wetlands. Contains alkaloids toxic to livestock (phalaris staggers). Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Native purple needlegrass (Poaceae) — California state grass.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with nodding branches, awned

grasses.hp: California's state grass. Deep-rooted native bunchgrass that evolved with indigenous burning. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and California Towhee. Important grassland restoration anchor species. Dark-eyed Junco and White-crowned Sparrow forage for seeds in purple needlegrass. Provides ground-nesting habitat for Horned Lark and Western Meadowlark. Burrowing Owl nests in California Ground Squirrel burrows in native grassland. Grasshopper Sparrow (state Species of Special Concern) nests on ground in native bunchgrass — one of LA County's rarest breeding birds.

grasses.desc: Non-native Italian Ryegrass (Poaceae). Annual ryegrass planted for erosion control and forage but outcompetes native grasses in wildlands.

fm.Inflorescence: Flat spike, spikelets edge-on to stem

grasses.hp: Annual ryegrass planted for erosion control and forage but outcompetes native grasses in wildlands. Rapidly establishes on disturbed ground. Wind-pollinated. Major lawn and turf grass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Desert Needlegrass (Poaceae). desert needlegrass with long twisted awns.
grasses.hp: Desert needlegrass with long twisted awns. Deep roots stabilize sandy soils. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Mexican feathergrass (Poaceae). Ornamental feathergrass widely planted in LA landscapes.

fm.Leaves: Thread-thin, 0.5 mm wide, forming dense tussock

grasses.hp: Mexican feathergrass with silky plumes. Provides nesting material for Bushtit. Seeds eaten by Dark-eyed Junco. Potential invasive concern — wind-dispersed seeds spreading from ornamental plantings.

grasses.desc: Non-native Johnson grass (Poaceae). Aggressive perennial grass spreading by both seeds and deep rhizomes.

fm.Leaves: Broad, 1-2 cm wide, with white midvein
fm.Habitat: Fields, roadsides; extremely aggressive rhizome spreader

grasses.hp: Aggressive perennial grass spreading by both seeds and deep rhizomes. Difficult to eradicate once established. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Goose Grass (Eleusine) (Poaceae). Goose grass — one of the world's most common tropical weeds.
grasses.hp: Goose grass — one of the world's most common tropical weeds. Rapidly evolving herbicide resistance. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Sand Ricegrass (Poaceae). sand ricegrass with distinctive open panicle.

fm.Inflorescence: Delicate open panicle with widely spreading branches

grasses.hp: Sand ricegrass with distinctive open panicle. Seeds highly nutritious — indigenous peoples gathered them extensively ('Indian ricegrass'). Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Sorghums (Poaceae). Cultivated sorghum — occasional volunteer from birdseed and agricultural plantings.

fm.Leaves: Broad, flat, 5-8 cm wide, prominent white midrib

grasses.hp: Cultivated sorghum — occasional volunteer from birdseed and agricultural plantings. Fifth most important cereal crop globally. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native feathertop (Poaceae). ornamental grass with fluffy white seed heads.
grasses.hp: Less invasive than fountain grass but naturalizing in disturbed areas. Wind-pollinated. Extreme fire hazard — fine fuel grass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Common Barley (Poaceae). Cultivated barley — ancient grain crop occasionally volunteering from birdseed or agriculture.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense spike with long awns, 5-10 cm
fm.Leaves: Flat, 8-15 mm wide, with large auricles

grasses.hp: Cultivated barley — ancient grain crop occasionally volunteering from birdseed or agriculture. Minimal ecological impact. Wind-pollinated. Barbed awns disperse on animal fur. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Natal Grass (Poaceae). natal grass from Africa — pink-purple fluffy seed heads.
grasses.hp: Fire-adapted. Provides habitat structure for ground-nesting birds (Horned Lark, Western Meadowlark) and seed food for sparrows and finches. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch.

grasses.desc: Spreading Rush (Juncaceae). important streambank stabilizer.

fm.Habitat: Moist slopes, stream banks; popular in restoration

grasses.hp: Important streambank stabilizer. Common in restoration plantings. Dense tussocks provide ground-nesting bird cover. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Important wetland habitat. Spreading rush — important Black Phoebe perching substrate. Song Sparrow nests in dense rush clumps. Tongva used rush stems in basketry. Provides Pacific Tree Frog breeding habitat.

grasses.desc: Squirreltail (Poaceae). awned bunchgrass of dry open habitats.

fm.Inflorescence: Bristly spike, long spreading awns, 3-8 cm

grasses.hp: Awned bunchgrass of dry open habitats. Important rangeland species. Wind-pollinated. Important restoration bunchgrass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Creeping wild rye (Poaceae). Rhizomatous.  Important for wetland restoration.
grasses.hp: Rhizomatous. Salt-tolerant. Wind-pollinated. Important native bunchgrass for restoration. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Seeds harvested as food by indigenous peoples. Tough stems used in construction.

grasses.desc: Non-native Perennial Ryegrass (Poaceae). ubiquitous lawn grass.

fm.Leaves: Glossy below, folded in bud, prominent auricles

grasses.hp: Important pollen allergen. Rapidly establishing erosion control species. Wind-pollinated. Major lawn and turf grass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native orchard grass (Poaceae). European pasture grass.

fm.Leaves: Flat, folded at base, 5-12 mm wide

grasses.hp: European pasture grass. Important pollen allergen. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Provides cover for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Crested needle grass (Poaceae). Crested ricegrass — mountain bunchgrass with distinctive one-sided inflorescence.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle, spikelets with short crown-like lemma

grasses.hp: Crested ricegrass — mountain bunchgrass with distinctive one-sided inflorescence. Rocky slopes above 4,000 ft. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Kikuyu Grass (Cenchrus) (Poaceae). Kikuyu grass — extremely aggressive African grass.
grasses.hp: Kikuyu grass — extremely aggressive African grass. Dense mats smother native vegetation. Very difficult to eradicate. Wind-pollinated. Extreme fire hazard — fine fuel grass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Bread Wheat (Poaceae). world's most important cereal crop.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense spike, 5-12 cm, awned or awnless

grasses.hp: World's most important cereal crop. Occasional volunteer from birdseed or transport spills. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive common Mediterranean grass (Poaceae). Cal-IPC Limited.

fm.Habitat: Desert, sandy areas; fine fuel grass

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Mediterranean grass invading desert habitats. Creates continuous fine fuel in previously fuel-free desert, increasing fire frequency. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Foothill needle grass (Poaceae). Foothill needlegrass — common native bunchgrass.
grasses.hp: Foothill needlegrass — common native bunchgrass. More shade-tolerant than purple needlegrass. Important restoration species. Wind-pollinated. California's state grass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native barnyardgrass (Poaceae). Barnyard grass — common annual of moist disturbed areas.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense panicle of spiny racemes, often purplish
fm.Leaves: Flat, broad, 10-20 mm wide, no ligule

grasses.hp: Barnyard grass — common annual of moist disturbed areas. Seeds important waterfowl food despite non-native origin. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native longflower veldtgrass (Poaceae). South African grass spreading in coastal areas.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with distinctive long florets

grasses.hp: Less aggressive than E. erecta but expanding range. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Lesser Canary Grass (Poaceae). Mediterranean.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense ovoid spike, 2-4 cm, pale green

grasses.hp: Mediterranean. Important bird food (canary seed). Wind-pollinated. Contains toxic alkaloids. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive rattail sixweeks grass (Poaceae). Cal-IPC Moderate.

fm.Habitat: Dry grasslands; creating continuous fine fuel

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Annual fescue now dominant in many CA grasslands. Fine stems create continuous fire fuel. Displaces native bunchgrasses. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Rock melic (Poaceae). Delicate native grass of rocky slopes.
grasses.hp: Delicate native grass of rocky slopes. Important habitat structure in rock outcrops. Wind-pollinated. Chaparral understory grass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Knotroot bristlegrass (Poaceae). Warm-season grass of moist disturbed areas.
grasses.hp: Warm-season grass of moist disturbed areas. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Wind-pollinated. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Saint Augustine grass (Poaceae). St.  Augustine grass — tropical lawn grass occasionally persisting from old plantings.
grasses.hp: St. Augustine grass — tropical lawn grass occasionally persisting from old plantings. Dense stoloniferous growth. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Bobtail barley (Poaceae). Small native barley of grasslands.
grasses.hp: Small native barley of grasslands. Important component of intact native grassland. Wind-pollinated. Barbed awns disperse on animal fur. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native water beard grass (Poaceae). moist disturbed areas.
grasses.hp: Moist disturbed areas. Dense green inflorescences. Less invasive than P. monspeliensis. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Nodding needle grass (Poaceae). Nodding needlegrass — graceful nodding inflorescence.
grasses.hp: Nodding needlegrass — graceful nodding inflorescence. Native bunchgrass of grasslands and oak woodlands. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native swamp pricklegrass (Poaceae). Swamp dropseed — recently split from Crypsis.
grasses.hp: Swamp dropseed — recently split from Crypsis. Moist alkaline soils. Spreading in wetland margins. Wind-pollinated. Salt-tolerant — salt marsh and cordgrass habitats. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Toad rush (Juncaceae). tiny annual rush of seasonal wet areas.

fm.Flowers: Small greenish, in forked clusters along stem
fm.Habitat: Moist disturbed areas, vernal pool margins

grasses.hp: Tiny annual rush of seasonal wet areas. Cosmopolitan. Important vernal pool indicator. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Important wetland habitat.

grasses.desc: Annual hairgrass (Poaceae). Annual hairgrass of vernal pools and seasonal wet areas.
grasses.hp: Annual hairgrass of vernal pools and seasonal wet areas. Indicator of vernal pool habitat — absence signals habitat degradation. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Wind-pollinated. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Purple nutsedge (Cyperaceae). one of the world's worst weeds.

fm.Tubers: Deep rhizome-connected tubers; nearly impossible to eradicate
fm.Habitat: Gardens, fields; world's worst weed by some measures

grasses.hp: Deep persistent tubers make eradication nearly impossible. Wind-pollinated sedge. Tubers eaten by California Ground Squirrel. Despite being 'the world's worst weed,' provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees in grassland.

grasses.desc: California satintail (Poaceae). Silky white inflorescences.

fm.Leaves: Flat, 3-6 mm wide, with off-center midrib

grasses.hp: Seep and spring specialist. Increasingly rare due to groundwater depletion. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Ditch beard grass (Poaceae). Moist disturbed areas.
grasses.hp: Moist disturbed areas. Fluffy inflorescence. Less common than introduced P. monspeliensis. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Fragrant flatsedge (Cyperaceae). aromatic sedge of freshwater marsh margins.
grasses.hp: Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native insects in wetland communities. Fragrant flatsedge fills a distinctive ecological niche in its community.

grasses.desc: San Diego sedge (Cyperaceae). the largest Carex in LA County, forming massive clumps in riparian areas.

fm.Leaves: Very broad for a sedge, 8-15 mm

grasses.hp: The largest Carex in LA County, forming massive clumps in riparian areas. Important structural habitat for marsh birds and amphibians. Wind-pollinated sedge. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Non-native nit grass (Poaceae). small Mediterranean annual with shiny seed coats.
grasses.hp: Small Mediterranean annual with shiny seed coats. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: American three-square bulrush (Cyperaceae). triangular stems.
grasses.hp: Important salt marsh and freshwater marsh species. Nesting habitat for marsh wrens. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Non-native Sickle Grass (Poaceae). tiny Mediterranean grass of salt marshes and coastal disturbed areas.

fm.Spikelets: Embedded in rachis, disarticulating at maturity

grasses.hp: Tiny Mediterranean grass of salt marshes and coastal disturbed areas. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Wind-pollinated. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Shore grass (Poaceae). Salt-tolerant grass of upper marsh zones.
grasses.hp: Salt-tolerant grass of upper marsh zones. Important for salt marsh restoration and shore stabilization. Wind-pollinated. Salt-excreting leaves — salt marsh specialist. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Fern-grass (Poaceae). small stiff Mediterranean annual.
grasses.hp: Small stiff Mediterranean annual. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Sprangletop (Poaceae). Bearded sprangletop — grass of alkaline marshes and saline soils.
grasses.hp: Bearded sprangletop — grass of alkaline marshes and saline soils. Salt-tolerant. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native canary grass (Poaceae). birdseed plant occasionally volunteering.
grasses.hp: Birdseed plant occasionally volunteering. Mediterranean origin. Seeds are commercial canary food. Wind-pollinated. Contains toxic alkaloids. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Foxtail Barley (Hordeum) (Poaceae). Foxtail barley — iridescent awns catch sunlight.

fm.Inflorescence: Foxtail-like with very long silky awns, 5-12 cm

grasses.hp: Foxtail barley — iridescent awns catch sunlight. Moist alkaline soils. Barbed awns hazardous to livestock and pets. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: California dissanthelium (Poaceae). Thomas' bluegrass — mountain bunchgrass.
grasses.hp: Rocky slopes. Uncommon. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Bulbous Bluegrass (Poaceae). European grass with bulbous stem base.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense panicle, often viviparous (bulbils replace florets)

grasses.hp: European grass with bulbous stem base. Viviparous — produces bulbils instead of seeds. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Barb Grass (Poaceae). Coast hardgrass — salt-tolerant coastal grass.
grasses.hp: Coast hardgrass — salt-tolerant coastal grass. Mediterranean. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native hooked bristle grass (Poaceae). Bristly foxtail — barbed bristles cling to clothing.

fm.Inflorescence: Bristly spike, 5-12 cm; bristles with backward barbs
fm.Habitat: Gardens, disturbed areas; bristles stick to clothing

grasses.hp: Bristly foxtail — barbed bristles cling to clothing. Tropical. Common urban weed. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native tall fescue (Poaceae). major turf and pasture grass.

fm.Leaves: Broad, flat, 5-10 mm wide, rough above

grasses.hp: Major turf and pasture grass. Contains endophyte fungi producing alkaloids. Widely planted. Provides habitat structure for native wildlife and stabilizes microhabitat. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch.

grasses.desc: Southwestern bushy bluestem (Poaceae). Desert purple three-awn — tufted bunchgrass with purple spikelets.
grasses.hp: Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native bristly dogtail grass (Poaceae). Hedgehog dogtail — distinctive bristly one-sided inflorescence.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense one-sided spike with spiny sterile spikelets

grasses.hp: Hedgehog dogtail — distinctive bristly one-sided inflorescence. Mediterranean. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native smooth crabgrass (Poaceae). common lawn weed.

fm.Habitat: Lawns; less hairy than D. sanguinalis

grasses.hp: Common lawn weed. sanguinalis. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Jungle Rice (Poaceae). tropical annual grass of moist disturbed areas.
grasses.hp: Tropical annual grass of moist disturbed areas. Rice paddy weed worldwide. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Sixweeks grass (Poaceae). Six-weeks fescue — tiny annual completing life cycle in 6 weeks.
grasses.hp: Six-weeks fescue — tiny annual completing life cycle in 6 weeks. Dry open habitats. Important early seed source. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Shortleaf Spikesedge (Cyperaceae) — Non-native shortleaf flatsedge — tiny tropical sedge of moist lawns.
grasses.hp: Shortleaf flatsedge — tiny tropical sedge of moist lawns. Kyllinga-type flat inflorescence. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Non-native False Brome (Poaceae). Mediterranean grass increasingly invasive in oak woodlands.
grasses.hp: Mediterranean grass increasingly invasive in oak woodlands. Creates continuous fuel. Fire ecology concern. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Maize (Poaceae). corn/maize — cultivated crop occasionally volunteering.
grasses.hp: Corn/maize — cultivated crop occasionally volunteering. Ancient Mesoamerican domesticate. Minimal ecological impact. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Hairy Crabgrass (Poaceae). ubiquitous summer lawn weed.
grasses.hp: Ubiquitous summer lawn weed. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Yorkshire fog (Poaceae). Velvet grass — soft fuzzy European grass of moist areas.

fm.Inflorescence: Soft fluffy panicle, 8-15 cm, pale green to pink

grasses.hp: Velvet grass — soft fuzzy European grass of moist areas. Moderate ecological impact in grasslands. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Little quaking-grass (Poaceae). Lesser quaking grass — delicate pendulous heart-shaped spikelets.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands, meadows, and open areas; naturalized from cultivation

grasses.hp: Lesser quaking grass — delicate pendulous heart-shaped spikelets. Mediterranean. Ornamental value. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive Arabian grass (Poaceae). Cal-IPC Limited.
grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Limited. Arabian grass invading desert ecosystems. Fine fuel creator in previously fuel-free habitats. Fire ecology concern. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Panicled Bulrush (Cyperaceae). Small-fruited bulrush — tall marsh sedge.
grasses.hp: Small-fruited bulrush — tall marsh sedge. Important nesting habitat for marsh birds. Wetland restoration species. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Non-native Hare's Tail Grass (Poaceae). distinctive fluffy oval seed heads.

fm.Inflorescence: Soft egg-shaped fluffy head, 2-4 cm (bunny tail)

grasses.hp: Mediterranean. Common in wildflower mixes and coastal disturbed areas. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native yellow nutsedge (Cyperaceae). Edible tubers (chufa/tiger nut).
grasses.hp: Edible tubers (chufa/tiger nut). Extremely persistent weed. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native insects in wetland communities.

grasses.desc: California muhly (Poaceae). Clump-forming grass of mountain stream banks.
grasses.hp: Clump-forming grass of mountain stream banks. Important for stream bank stabilization. Wind-pollinated. Native bunchgrass of moist mountain slopes. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Fine stems used in Cahuilla and Tongva basket weaving — provides texture and pattern.

grasses.desc: Non-native Mediterranean Hair-grass (Poaceae). Mediterranean annual grass spreading in open areas.
grasses.hp: Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Mediterranean Hair-grass provides structural habitat for ground-nesting insects.

grasses.desc: Non-native Blue Fescue (Poaceae). blue-gray ornamental grass.
grasses.hp: European. Minimal naturalization. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Common Oat (Poaceae). Cultivated oat — crop plant occasionally volunteering from birdseed.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle with pendant spikelets, 15-30 cm

grasses.hp: Cultivated oat — crop plant occasionally volunteering from birdseed. Less weedy than wild oats (A. fatua). Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Barren Brome (Poaceae). European annual with large drooping spikelets.
grasses.hp: European annual with large drooping spikelets. Waste ground colonizer. Provides seed food for sparrows and finches. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Variable Flatsedge (Cyperaceae). rice paddy weed.
grasses.hp: Rice paddy weed. Moist disturbed areas. Small globose inflorescences. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Knot grass (Poaceae). Knotgrass — stoloniferous grass of wet areas.
grasses.hp: Knotgrass — stoloniferous grass of wet areas. Important wetland restoration species. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Lemmon's canarygrass (Poaceae). Lemmon's canary grass — uncommon native of vernal pools and seasonal wet areas.
grasses.hp: Lemmon's canary grass — uncommon native of vernal pools and seasonal wet areas. Vernal pool indicator. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Wind-pollinated. Contains toxic alkaloids. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Smooth Meadow-grass (Poaceae). Kentucky bluegrass — classic lawn grass.
grasses.hp: Kentucky bluegrass — classic lawn grass. European. Rhizomatous. Major pollen allergen.

grasses.desc: Non-native narrow-leaved cattail (Typhaceae). slimmer than native T.

fm.Habitat: Freshwater marshes and wetlands; naturalized from cultivation

grasses.hp: Slimmer than native T. latifolia. Narrowleaf cattail — hybridizes with native T. latifolia, creating vigorous invasive hybrid T. × glauca. Marsh Wren and Red-winged Blackbird nest in stands.

grasses.desc: Mariposa rush (Juncaceae). Dubious rush — mountain rush of moist meadows and stream banks.
grasses.hp: Dubious rush — mountain rush of moist meadows and stream banks. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Important wetland habitat.

grasses.desc: Long-leaved rush (Juncaceae). Large-leaved rush of moist mountain meadows.
grasses.hp: Large-leaved rush of moist mountain meadows. Stream bank stabilizer. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide nesting material for native bees. Important wetland habitat.

grasses.desc: Non-native Intermediate Wheatgrass (Poaceae). Eurasian grass planted for forage and erosion control.
grasses.hp: Eurasian grass planted for forage and erosion control. Rhizomatous spreader. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: California Cordgrass (Poaceae). Leafy dropseed — fine-textured grass of open areas.

fm.Inflorescence: Dense spike-like panicle within leaf sheaths

grasses.hp: Leafy dropseed — fine-textured grass of open areas. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native modest prickle grass (Poaceae). Nile dropseed — tropical African grass.
grasses.hp: Nile dropseed — tropical African grass. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Sand spikerush (Cyperaceae). Slender rush of sandy stream margins.
grasses.hp: Slender rush of sandy stream margins. Important wetland indicator. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Non-native Greater Quaking Grass (Poaceae). large pendulous heart-shaped spikelets.

fm.Habitat: Grasslands and meadows; naturalized from cultivation

grasses.hp: Mediterranean. Ornamental. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive purple false-brome (Poaceae). Purple false brome — Cal-IPC Moderate.
grasses.hp: Purple false brome — Cal-IPC Moderate. Mediterranean annual creating continuous fire fuel in grasslands. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Awned Canary-grass (Poaceae). Hood canary grass — Mediterranean.
grasses.hp: Hood canary grass — Mediterranean. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Asian Rice (Poaceae). Cultivated rice — rarely persisting from agricultural production or birdseed.
grasses.hp: Cultivated rice — rarely persisting from agricultural production or birdseed. Minimal ecological impact. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Rice cutgrass (Poaceae). Rough-edged grass of freshwater marshes.

fm.Inflorescence: Open panicle, partially enclosed in sheath

grasses.hp: Rough-edged grass of freshwater marshes. Important waterfowl habitat. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Chilean chess (Poaceae). Chilean brome — South American annual.
grasses.hp: Chilean brome — South American annual. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Tall brome (Poaceae). Large native brome of mountain slopes and shaded canyons.
grasses.hp: Large native brome of mountain slopes and shaded canyons. Less common than introduced bromes. Provides seed food for sparrows and finches. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Columbia brome (Poaceae). Shade-tolerant native grass of mountain forests.
grasses.hp: Shade-tolerant native grass of mountain forests. Important forest understory species. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Creates fine fuel load increasing fire frequency. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Western rough sedge (Cyperaceae). Large sedge of stream banks and marshes.
grasses.hp: Large sedge of stream banks and marshes. Wind-pollinated sedge. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Trigonous sedge (Cyperaceae). Triangular-fruit sedge — distinctive three-angled seed pods.

fm.Habitat: Coastal sage scrub, chaparral understory; unusual dry habitat for Carex

grasses.hp: Trigonous sedge contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Common spikerush (Cyperaceae). Creeping spikerush — rhizomatous marsh species.
grasses.hp: Creeping spikerush — rhizomatous marsh species. Important wetland restoration plant. Waterfowl habitat. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: Non-native Mediterranean lovegrass (Poaceae). Mediterranean lovegrass spreading on sandy disturbed ground.
grasses.hp: Mediterranean lovegrass spreading on sandy disturbed ground. Delicate inflorescence. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native australian rush (Juncaceae). common rush — Australian rush naturalizing in moist disturbed areas.

fm.Habitat: Disturbed areas and roadsides; naturalized from cultivation

grasses.hp: Common rush — Australian rush naturalizing in moist disturbed areas. Wind-pollinated rush. Stems provide habitat for native invertebrates.

grasses.desc: California orcutt grass (Poaceae). FEDERALLY ENDANGERED.

fm.Habitat: Vernal pools; federally endangered; CA endemic

grasses.hp: FEDERALLY ENDANGERED. Vernal pool endemic. One of California's rarest plants. Critical conservation priority. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Witch grass (Poaceae). Witchgrass — annual with delicate open panicle.

fm.Inflorescence: Very large open panicle, 1/3 of plant height

grasses.hp: Witchgrass — annual with delicate open panicle. Dry disturbed areas. Tumbles when mature. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native river oats (Poaceae). Inland sea oats — ornamental grass with flat drooping spikelets.

fm.Inflorescence: Pendant flat spikelets on arching branches

grasses.hp: Inland sea oats — ornamental grass with flat drooping spikelets. Eastern US native. Garden escape. Wind-pollinated — does not provide nectar. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow, Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch, and California Quail. Provides nesting habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Non-native Papyrus sedge (Cyperaceae). Papyrus — dramatic tall sedge of Egyptian antiquity.

fm.Habitat: Urban landscapes and gardens; naturalized from cultivation

grasses.hp: Papyrus — dramatic tall sedge of Egyptian antiquity. Frost-sensitive in LA. Wind-pollinated sedge. Provides habitat structure for native insects in wetland communities. Egyptian papyrus — one of civilization's most important plants (paper, boats, rope). Provides nesting structure for Marsh Wren when naturalized.

grasses.desc: Great Basin Wildrye (Poaceae). Basin wild rye — massive native bunchgrass to 6 ft.

fm.Habitat: Mountain meadows, sagebrush; tallest native grass

grasses.hp: Basin wild rye — massive native bunchgrass to 6 ft. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees. Seeds harvested as food by indigenous peoples. Tough stems used in construction.

grasses.desc: Non-native Palmgrass (Poaceae). Palm grass — broad pleated leaves resembling palm fronds.
grasses.hp: Palm grass — broad pleated leaves resembling palm fronds. Tropical. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Wind-pollinated. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

grasses.desc: Invasive buffelgrass (Poaceae) — Cal-IPC Moderate.

fm.Habitat: Desert, roadsides; Cal-IPC High, extreme fire hazard

grasses.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Buffel grass — extremely invasive in desert habitats. Wind-pollinated. Extreme fire hazard — fine fuel grass. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

ferns.desc: Largest fern in western North America — fronds to 9 ft. Shady canyon stream banks. Dramatic accent in moist forests.

fm.Size: Fronds 3–9 ft — largest western NA fern

ferns.hp: Largest fern in western North America — fronds to 9 ft. Provides microhabitat structure and moisture retention in canyon understory. Important for salamander and invertebrate habitat. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Fronds shelter Pacific Giant Salamander and California Newt. Fiddleheads edible when young. Tongva wove rhizomes into baskets.

ferns.desc: Small fern with triangular fronds — bright yellow waxy powder on underside. Rocky slopes, including very dry exposed sites. Drought-adapted.

fm.Season: Green in wet season, curls dry in summer

ferns.hp: Bright yellow waxy powder on underside protects from desiccation. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure. Tongva and Chumash used dried fronds medicinally. One of the most drought-tolerant ferns — survives by rolling fronds inward.

ferns.desc: Bluish-green fern of dry rocky slopes. Pinnae on wiry dark stems. Much more drought-tolerant than most ferns.
ferns.hp: Rocky slopes. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure. Among the most drought-tolerant CA ferns.

ferns.desc: Delicate fan-shaped pinnae on black wiry stems. Water beads and rolls off fronds ("maidenhair"). Shady moist grottos and seeps.

fm.Habitat: Moist grottos, shady seeps, waterfall spray zones

ferns.hp: California maidenhair — delicate fan-shaped pinnae on wiry black stems. Canyon seeps and waterfall spray zones. Exquisitely beautiful. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure. California maidenhair — indicator of year-round seepage. California Newt and Pacific Tree Frog breed in associated pools.

ferns.desc: Large sword-like fronds with eared pinnae. More common in NorCal — at southern range edge in San Gabriel canyons.

fm.Habitat: Mountain canyons — at southern range limit

ferns.hp: Large arching fronds. Important for erosion control on steep slopes. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important forest understory structure. Western sword fern — dominant understory fern in moist forests. Provides ground-nesting cover for Dark-eyed Junco. Fronds used as plates by indigenous peoples.

ferns.desc: Cosmopolitan fern of open woodlands and burns. Triangular fronds from underground rhizomes. Fire-following — dominates after burns.

fm.Ecology: Fire-follower — spreads by deep rhizomes
fm.Fire: Resprouter — deep rhizomes survive; dominates post-fire

ferns.hp: Cosmopolitan fern — found on every continent except Antarctica. Fire follower — deep rhizomes survive fire and dominate post-fire understory. Contains carcinogenic compounds (ptaquiloside). Provides ground-level habitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure. Dark-eyed Junco and Spotted Towhee forage in bracken fern understory. Ground-nesting habitat for Western Meadowlark. Tongva harvested fiddleheads as food (must be cooked — carcinogenic raw).

ferns.desc: Living fossil — unchanged for 300 million years. Jointed hollow stems with silica. Used as sandpaper. Riparian and seep areas.
ferns.hp: Living fossil — horsetails predate flowering plants by 300 million years. Important invertebrate habitat in riparian zones. Ancient lineage. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in moist areas. Important understory structure. Black Phoebe perches on scouring rush stems along streams to hawk insects. Song Sparrow nests at base. Tongva used scouring rush stems to polish arrowshafts and smooth wooden tools — silica-rich stems are naturally abrasive.

ferns.desc: Small fern of dry rock crevices. Woolly or scaly underside. Extreme drought tolerance — curls up completely when dry, revives with rain.

fm.Habitat: Dry rock crevices — extreme drought tolerance

ferns.hp: Coville's lip fern — woolly-brown scales. Curls up when dry, unfurls rapidly after rain. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure. Coville's lip fern — desert rock specialist. Curls tightly in drought, revives with rain. Provides crevice shelter for Western Fence Lizard.

ferns.desc: Small fern growing on mossy rocks and tree bases in shady canyons. Winter-green — appears with first rains, dies back completely in summer drought. Rhizome tastes and smells like licorice. One of the most seasonal ferns.
ferns.hp: Winter-green — appears with first rains, dies back in summer. Rhizome smells and tastes like licorice. Grows on mossy rocks and tree bases. Important understory structure. California polypody — epiphytic on oak branches and rocky outcrops. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates and Anna's Hummingbird nesting material.

ferns.desc: Blue-green pinnae with trident-shaped tips. Very drought-tolerant. Rocky chaparral.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — rhizome survives fire; one of first ferns to reappear post-fire

ferns.hp: Ferns provide habitat structure in moist microenvironments. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure. Bird's foot fern of dry rocky slopes. Provides shelter for Western Fence Lizard and side-blotched lizard among rocks.

ferns.desc: Small desert fern with star-shaped scales on underside. Extremely drought-tolerant. Desert rock crevices.
ferns.hp: Cochise cloak fern — star-shaped scales on undersurface. Among the most drought-tolerant ferns. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Tiny triangular fern of dry rocky slopes. Among the most drought-adapted ferns. Curls completely when dry.

fm.Behavior: Curls when dry, revives with rain

ferns.hp: Waxy-coated pinnae resist desiccation. Named for traditional use by indigenous peoples. Dry rocky slopes. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Delicate maidenhair fern of seeps and waterfalls. Fan-shaped pinnae on black stems. More common in SoCal than A. jordanii.
ferns.hp: Delicate fan-shaped pinnae. Named 'Venus hair.' Alkaline seeps and travertine deposits. Cosmopolitan species. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure. Venus-hair fern of dripping limestone. Indicator of calcium-rich seepage.

ferns.desc: Tiny triangular fern with lacy dissected fronds. Rock crevices at mid-elevations. Very drought-tolerant.
ferns.hp: Extremely dense pinnae creating lace-like appearance. Rock crevice specialist. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure. Indian's dream fern — extremely drought-tolerant. Tongva reportedly used as medicine.

ferns.desc: Tiny spikemoss forming dark mats on rock faces and seeps. Not a true fern or moss — an ancient lineage predating both. Resurrects from dried state.
ferns.hp: Not a true fern or moss — an ancient lineage predating both. Poikilohydric — revives from complete desiccation when wetted. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure. Resurrection plant — curls into a ball when dry, unfurls green within hours of rain. Desert rock specialist.

ferns.desc: Large woodland fern with triangular fronds and distinctive "wood fern" growth pattern. The most common large fern in LA mountain canyons. Evergreen in moist shade.
ferns.hp: Most common large fern in LA mountain canyons. Provides important microhabitat structure for invertebrates and amphibians in moist canyon understory. Coastal wood fern — provides shelter for California Newt and banana slugs in moist canyon bottoms.

ferns.desc: Small fern on shady rocky slopes in oak woodland. Narrow fronds from creeping rhizome. Uncommon but locally abundant in Santa Monica Mountains canyons.

fm.Habitat: Shady rocky slopes, oak woodland canyons

ferns.hp: Named for traditional use treating spleen ailments. Shaded rock crevices. Dark wiry stipes. Rock crevice specialist. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Smooth green jointed stems without whorled branches — looks like a thin bamboo rod. Ancient lineage — horsetails predate flowering plants by 300 million years. Stream banks.
ferns.hp: Ancient lineage predating flowering plants by 300 million years. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure. Black Phoebe perches on smooth scouring rush stems along streams to hawk insects.

ferns.desc: Largest horsetail — thick ivory stems with whorls of green branches like a tiny pine tree. Stream banks in Santa Monica Mountains canyons. Prehistoric appearance.

fm.Form: Thick ivory stems with whorled green branches
fm.Habitat: Stream banks, canyon bottoms — SMM canyons
fm.Season: Deciduous — dies back in dry season

ferns.hp: Largest horsetail species, with whorled branches creating bottle-brush appearance. Ancient lineage. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in moist areas. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Soft-textured fern of canyon streams. Larger and more lush than most SMM ferns. Fronds to 3 ft. At northern range limit in the Santa Monica Mountains.

fm.Habitat: Canyon streams — at northern range limit

ferns.hp: Large triangular fronds in shaded canyon bottoms. Subtropical origin. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates in canyon bottoms. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Stiff upright fronds with sword-shaped pinnae. Shady canyon slopes. More drought-tolerant than most ferns. Rocky areas in SGM canyons.
ferns.hp: Imbricate sword fern — overlapping pinnae with spiny tips. Rock ledges and talus. Smaller than P. munitum. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Delicate lacy fern of moist mountain stream banks. At extreme southern range limit in San Gabriel Mountain canyons. Twice-pinnate fronds with crescent-shaped sori.

fm.Habitat: Moist mountain stream banks — at southern range limit

ferns.hp: Moist canyon specialist providing microhabitat for salamanders and invertebrates. Important forest understory structure.

ferns.desc: Small rock fern with blue-green pinnae on dark wiry stipes. Rocky crevices in mountain chaparral. More compact than Coffee Fern.
ferns.hp: Rock crevice specialist stabilizing substrates and retaining moisture in montane habitats. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Small rock fern with beaded fronds — similar to Newberry's Lip Fern but with narrower, less woolly pinnae. Rocky outcrops in coastal and foothill chaparral.
ferns.hp: Drought-adapted — curls up when dry, revives with rain. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure. Cleveland's lip fern — chaparral rock crevice specialist. Among the most drought-tolerant ferns in North America.

ferns.desc: Small desert fern with wavy-margined pinnae covered in star-shaped scales. Desert rock crevices at eastern LA County edge. More heat-tolerant than A. cochisensis.

fm.Habitat: Desert rock crevices — eastern LA County edge
