ferns.hp: Desert rock fern colonizing extreme arid habitats. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Grass-like aquatic fern relative growing in seasonal pools. Ancient lineage — resembles early land plants. Rare in LA County vernal pool habitats.
ferns.hp: Ancient lineage related to earliest land plants. Vernal pool specialist — indicator of high-quality seasonal wetland habitat. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Larger spikemoss than S. bigelovii, forming mats on shady rock faces. Blue-green iridescent sheen in some light. Mountain canyons.

fm.Habitat: Shady rock faces in mountain canyons

ferns.hp: Retains moisture on rock faces enabling later plant colonization. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Non-native Introduced fern naturalizing on walls, mortar, and limestone. Remarkable ability to hyperaccumulate arsenic from soil — used in phytoremediation research.

fm.Ecology: Hyperaccumulates arsenic — phytoremediation research species

ferns.hp: Arsenic hyperaccumulator — absorbs toxic arsenic from contaminated soil. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Large triangular fern of deep mountain shade. At southern range limit in wet SGM canyons above 5,000 ft. Three-times-pinnate fronds.

fm.Habitat: Deep shade in wet SGM canyons above 5,000 ft

ferns.hp: Deep shade specialist at extreme southern range limit. Important microhabitat for mountain invertebrates and salamanders. Important forest understory structure.

ferns.desc: Five-finger maidenhair with fan-shaped pinnae on dark stems. Distinguished from A. jordanii by palmate (hand-shaped) frond arrangement. Rare in LA — wet mountain canyons.

fm.Shape: Palmate — fan of 5 pinnae (vs pinnate in A. jordanii)
fm.Habitat: Wet mountain canyons — rare in LA County

ferns.hp: Distinguished from related species jordanii by palmate (hand-shaped) frond arrangement. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Delicate small fern of moist rock crevices in mountain forests. Fronds easily broken — hence 'fragile.' Among the most widespread ferns worldwide.

fm.Habitat: Moist rock crevices in mountain forest

ferns.hp: Among the most widespread ferns worldwide — found on every continent. Moist rock crevice specialist in mountain forests. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Tiny rock fern that curls into a tight brown ball when dry and unfurls green within hours of rain. Extreme drought tolerance. Rock crevices in chaparral.

fm.Behavior: Curls into tight ball when dry, revives with rain

ferns.hp: Poikilohydric — tolerates complete desiccation and revives within hours of wetting. Rock crevice specialist. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Non-native skeleton fork fern (Psilotaceae). Whisk fern — among the most primitive living vascular plants.

fm.Habitat: Shaded slopes and canyon understory; naturalized from cultivation

ferns.hp: Whisk fern — among the most primitive living vascular plants. Living fossil resembling 400-million-year-old ancestors. Escaped from cultivation. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Newberry's lip fern (Pteridaceae). Newberry's lip fern of dry rocky crevices.

fm.Fronds: Drought-adapted — curls when dry, revives with moisture

ferns.hp: Newberry's lip fern of dry rocky crevices. Curls up when dry, revives rapidly with rain. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Sticky goldback fern (Pteridaceae). sticky goldback fern with glandular-viscid undersurface.
ferns.hp: Distinguished from related species triangularis by sticky (not waxy) coating. Coastal and foothill rock crevices. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Water clover (Marsileaceae). Aquatic fern with clover-shaped fronds.
ferns.hp: Seasonal pools and pond margins. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure. Hairy water clover — aquatic fern of vernal pools. Sporocarps can remain dormant in soil for decades, germinating when pools refill.

ferns.desc: Non-native house holly-fern (Dryopteridaceae). house holly-fern from East Asia — glossy dark green pinnae with holly-like shape.

fm.Habitat: Shaded slopes and canyon understory; naturalized from cultivation

ferns.hp: House holly-fern from East Asia — glossy dark green pinnae with holly-like shape. Escaping from cultivation to walls and shaded crevices. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important forest understory structure.

ferns.desc: California Cloak-fern (Pteridaceae). California cloak fern of desert rock crevices.

fm.Fronds: Drought-adapted — curls when dry, revives with moisture

ferns.hp: California cloak fern of desert rock crevices. Dense waxy coating on underside protects from extreme desiccation. Poikilohydric — revives after complete drying. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: American pillwort (Marsileaceae). tiny grass-like aquatic fern ally of vernal pools.
ferns.hp: Tiny grass-like aquatic fern ally of vernal pools. Spherical sporocarps at base. Extremely inconspicuous. Vernal pool indicator. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Field horsetail (Equisetaceae). Ancient lineage predating flowering plants.
ferns.hp: Ancient lineage predating flowering plants. Silica-rich stems used as scouring material. Spreads aggressively via deep rhizomes. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: California adder's-tongue (Ophioglossaceae). single fleshy leaf with spore-bearing spike.
ferns.hp: Single fleshy leaf with spore-bearing spike. Among the most overlooked native ferns. Mycorrhizal dependent. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Showy Maiden Fern (Thelypteridaceae). showy maiden fern of shaded mountain canyons.
ferns.hp: Showy maiden fern of shaded mountain canyons. Large triangular fronds. Moist habitat specialist at southern range limit. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates in canyon bottoms. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Non-native Fishbone Fern (Nephrolepidaceae). tuberous roots store water.

fm.Habitat: Shaded slopes and canyon understory; naturalized from cultivation

ferns.hp: Tuberous roots store water. Escaping from cultivation via tuber dispersal. Displaces native understory ferns. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Non-native water spangles (Salviniaceae). tiny floating fern forming dense mats on still water.

fm.Habitat: Shaded slopes and canyon understory; naturalized from cultivation

ferns.hp: Tiny floating fern forming dense mats on still water. Aquatic invasive. Floating aquatic — provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: Non-native spider brake (Pteridaceae). multi-fingered fronds.

fm.Habitat: Shaded slopes and canyon understory; naturalized from cultivation

ferns.hp: East Asian. Escaping from cultivation to walls and moist shaded areas. Provides habitat structure for native invertebrates in rock crevices. Drought-adapted. Important understory structure.

ferns.desc: San Diego Silverback Fern (Pteridaceae). sticky glandular underside.
ferns.hp: Distinguished from related species triangularis by viscid (not waxy) coating. At range edge. Provides moist microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important understory structure.

cacti.desc: Flat-padded cactus of coastal scrub. Yellow flowers. Purple-red fruit (tunas) eaten by wildlife and humans. Glochids are irritating.

fm.Pads: Flat, oval, with glochids and spines
fm.Fire: Resprouter — pads reroot after fire dislodges them; vegetative recovery

cacti.hp: Fruit eaten by coyotes, birds, and Desert Tortoise. Pads provide nest sites — Cactus Wren builds nests among spines for protection. Pollinated by cactus specialist bees (Diadasia rinconis) — large solitary bees that nest in ground near cactus. Fire-adapted — pads resprout from base. Fruit and pads provide post-fire food for American Robin and Cedar Waxwing. Coastal Cactus Wren (state Species of Special Concern) nests exclusively in prickly pear and cholla — population declining as coastal sage scrub is developed. Loggerhead Shrike impales prey (lizards, insects) on cactus spines — 'butcher bird' behavior serves as food cache and territorial display.

cacti.desc: Branching cylindrical cactus of coastal sage scrub. Pink-magenta flowers. Segments detach and root easily — vegetative spread.

fm.Spread: Segments detach and root on contact
fm.Fire: Resprouter — segments reroot; fire-resilient via vegetative spread

cacti.hp: Segments detach and root on contact — vegetative spread strategy. Pink-magenta flowers visited by native bees and beetles. Stem segments resprout after fire, rapidly re-establishing habitat structure.

cacti.desc: Dense silver-spined cholla of desert margins. Greenish-yellow flowers. More compact and silver than Coastal Cholla.
cacti.hp: Silvery-spined joints. Important Cactus Wren nesting habitat. Fruit spiny but edible after careful spine removal. Stem segments resprout after fire, rapidly re-establishing habitat structure. Greater Roadrunner shelters and nests in dense cholla thickets — spines provide predator protection.

cacti.desc: Spineless flat pads with hot pink flowers. Pads have irritating glochids but no visible spines. Desert species reaching LA County.
cacti.hp: Pad-shaped joints without visible spines (has glochids). Pollinated by native bees (especially cactus bees — Diadasia) and beetles. Fruit eaten by Cactus Wren, Desert Tortoise, and packrats. Fire-adapted — pads resprout from base. Fruit and pads provide post-fire food for American Robin and Cedar Waxwing. Desert Woodrat (Neotoma lepida) builds stick houses around beavertail cactus bases, incorporating spiny pads for predator protection. Loggerhead Shrike impales prey on beavertail spines. Costa's Hummingbird feeds from magenta flowers.

cacti.desc: Dramatic rosette sending up 6–15 ft flower stalk once in its life, then dying. Chaparral icon. Pollinated by Yucca Moth.

fm.Fire: Fire follower — dramatically increased flowering 1–2 years post-fire; rosettes killed but seed germination enhanced

cacti.hp: Neither can reproduce without the other. Obligate mutualism with California Yucca Moth (Tegeticula maculata) — the only pollinator. Moth larvae feed inside developing fruit. Scott's Oriole feeds from flowers in desert populations. Desert Woodrat gnaws yucca leaf bases for moisture. Coast Horned Lizard basks on open ground between yucca clumps. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

cacti.desc: Rosette succulent at extreme northern range limit. Massive flower stalk. Very rare in LA County — a few natural populations remain.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs — extreme northern range limit

cacti.hp: Flowers visited by native bees and hummingbirds. Historically important indigenous food plant. CA/Baja endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

cacti.desc: Succulent rosettes on rocky outcrops. Yellow-red flowers on arching stalks. Extremely drought-tolerant. Many species poaching-threatened.

fm.Flowers: Yellow to red on arching stalk
fm.Habitat: Dry slopes, banks, rock crevices — the most common Dudleya
fm.Conservation: Poaching is a major threat to Dudleya species
fm.vs: Canyon Dudleya (D. cymosa) has BROADER rounder leaves and grows specifically from rock crevices on canyon walls

cacti.hp: Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds. Host for rare Dudleya moth. CA endemic. Poaching is a major conservation threat to Dudleya species. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Dramatic chalk-white rosette succulent on cliff faces. Tall red flower stalk. Southern California specialty. Poaching-threatened.

fm.Height: Rosette to 1 ft; stalk to 3 ft

cacti.hp: The largest and most dramatically chalky-white dudleya. Pollinated by Anna's Hummingbird. Increasingly poached for international succulent trade. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Cylindrical with fierce hooked spines. Leans south ("compass cactus").
cacti.hp: California barrel cactus — Cactus Wren, Loggerhead Shrike, and Lesser Goldfinch perch on top to survey territory. Cahuilla ate young flower buds. Fruit eaten by Mule Deer and Desert Cottontail.

cacti.desc: Densely spined, golden in backlight. "Jumping cholla" — segments detach on contact.

fm.Cultural: Jumping cholla

cacti.hp: Densely spined — segments detach on lightest touch ('jumping cholla'). Cactus Wren builds nests in cholla for spine protection from predators. Important Cactus Wren nesting habitat — wrens build in dense spiny branches for predator protection. Pollinated by native bees. Stem segments resprout after fire, rapidly re-establishing habitat structure. Teddy bear cholla — segments detach and embed in passing animals (Mule Deer, coyote, hikers), dispersing the plant vegetatively.

cacti.desc: Thin-stemmed cholla with pencil-like branches. Pink-brown flowers. Desert species at western range edge.
cacti.hp: Diamond-patterned tubercles. Important Cactus Wren nesting habitat — wrens build in dense spiny branches for predator protection. Fruit eaten by desert wildlife. Stem segments resprout after fire, rapidly re-establishing habitat structure.

cacti.desc: Clustering barrel cactus with magenta flowers. Spiny clumps on desert rocky slopes. Fruit edible.
cacti.hp: Hedgehog cactus with showy flowers. Important Cactus Wren nesting habitat — wrens build in spiny stems for predator protection. Fruit edible — sweet, strawberry-like.

cacti.desc: Rosette succulent with pale green leaves (not chalky). White-green flowers. Coastal bluffs and Channel Islands.
cacti.hp: Channel Islands populations distinct. Conservation interest. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Increasingly threatened by international poaching for succulent trade. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Low clustering cactus with red-orange flowers. Mojave Desert edge of LA County. Spectacular spring display in good rain years.
cacti.hp: Pollinated by native bees, beetles, and hummingbirds. Fruit eaten by Cactus Wren and desert wildlife.

cacti.desc: Small globular cactus with hooked central spines. White-pink flowers. Coastal sage scrub and desert edges. Often hidden under shrubs.

fm.Habitat: Under shrubs — coastal scrub, desert edges
fm.Fire: Fire-sensitive — killed by fire; slow recovery

cacti.hp: Fish-hook cactus with hooked central spines. Fruit eaten by American Robin, Cactus Wren, Cedar Waxwing, and Northern Mockingbird. Slow-growing. Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds.

cacti.desc: Spiky yucca with shreddy fibrous trunk. Mojave Desert and Joshua tree woodland. Tongva used root for soap. Cream flower clusters. Yucca moth pollinated.

fm.Cultural: Tongva root soap plant
fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, Joshua tree woodland — Antelope Valley

cacti.hp: Obligate mutualism with Yucca Moth (Tegeticula). The moth is the sole pollinator; larvae feed inside developing fruit. Roots yield saponins used commercially in beverages and animal feed. Bulbs eaten by Botta's Pocket Gopher and California Ground Squirrel.

cacti.desc: Massive chalk-white rosette — the whitest of all Dudleyas. Rocky bluffs. Severely threatened by poaching for international succulent trade.
cacti.hp: Baja California origin, planted and naturalizing on cliffs. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Provides nectar on bare rock faces where few other flowers grow. Increasingly poached for international succulent trade. Regional endemic (CA/Baja) — conservation value. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Bright yellow-orange flowers on succulent rosettes growing from rock crevices. Multiple subspecies, several rare. Canyon walls in chaparral and oak woodland. Fire-adapted — survives on cliffs.

fm.Habitat: Rock crevices, canyon walls — specifically cliff faces
fm.Leaves: BROAD, rounded (vs narrow lance-shaped in D. lanceolata)
fm.vs: Lance-leaved Dudleya (D. lanceolata) has narrow lance-shaped leaves and grows on open slopes, not just cliffs

cacti.hp: Canyon cliff specialist with yellow-orange flowers. Pollinated by Anna's Hummingbird — one of few nectar sources on vertical rock faces. Increasingly threatened by international poaching for succulent trade. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Tiny dudleya with white-pink flowers on clay soils. CNPS 1B.1. Restricted to clay outcrops and openings. One of the most threatened dudleyas — habitat rapidly lost to development.
cacti.hp: CNPS 1B.1 — declining due to coastal development. Tiny rosettes on clay soils. Pollinated by native bees and small flies. Conservation priority. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Pale yellow flowers from multiple rosettes on clay soil. CNPS 1B.2. Often grows with Dudleya blochmaniae. Threatened by grassland conversion.
cacti.hp: CNPS 1B.2 — declining due to development. Multiple rosettes from single root on clay soils. Visited by native bees. Conservation priority. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Small green rosettes with yellow flowers on rocky granitic slopes. Named for early CA botanist LeRoy Abrams. Interior chaparral. Several subspecies.
cacti.hp: Abrams' dudleya — mountain rock specialist above 4,000 ft. Several varieties with different ranges. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Provides nectar on bare rock faces where few other flowers grow. Increasingly poached for international succulent trade. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Yellow flowers on fleshy rosettes of coastal bluffs and sea cliffs. Salt-spray tolerant. Forms dense clumps. Variable — integrades with D. lanceolata inland.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs, sea cliffs — salt-spray tolerant

cacti.hp: Salt-spray tolerant. Important coastal cliff community member. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Increasingly threatened by international poaching for succulent trade. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Federally threatened dudleya endemic to Conejo Valley/western LA County edge. Pink-red rosettes with yellow flowers. Known from ~14 populations.

fm.Conservation: Federally Threatened. CNPS 1B.1. Endemic to ~14 populations.

cacti.hp: FEDERALLY THREATENED — known from very few populations on Conejo Volcanics. Pollinated by hummingbirds. Critical conservation priority — active habitat management. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Chalky rosette succulent of coastal bluffs. Yellow flowers. Wind-and salt-tolerant. Poaching threat.
cacti.hp: Powdery-white rosettes on coastal bluffs. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Provides nectar on bare rock faces where few other flowers grow. Increasingly poached for international succulent trade. Regional endemic (CA/Baja) — conservation value. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Branching cholla with prominent spines and yellow-green to red flowers. Desert washes at eastern LA County edge. More open-branching than Teddy Bear Cholla.

fm.Habitat: Desert washes — eastern LA County edge

cacti.hp: Cactus Wren and Curve-billed Thrasher nest in cholla for spine protection from predators. Flowers visited by native bees. Stem segments resprout after fire, rapidly re-establishing habitat structure.

cacti.desc: Dense woolly-topped barrel cactus forming clumps. Mojave Desert specialist. Very slow-growing — some specimens centuries old. Yellow flowers emerge from woolly crown.

fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert rocky slopes — very slow-growing

cacti.hp: Cottontop cactus — clustered barrel form. Important Cactus Wren perching and survey point. Seeds eaten by harvester ants. Extremely slow-growing — large specimens may be centuries old.

cacti.desc: Tree-like prickly pear with woody trunk. Coastal sage scrub. Larger and more tree-like than O. littoralis. Important nesting habitat for Cactus Wren.
cacti.hp: Tree-like form provides important nesting structure for Cactus Wren and Loggerhead Shrike. Fruit eaten by coyotes and birds. Fire-adapted — pads resprout from base. Fruit and pads provide post-fire food for American Robin and Cedar Waxwing.

cacti.desc: Low-growing densely spined prickly pear. Mountain and desert-edge populations in LA County. More cold-tolerant than coastal species.
cacti.hp: Cold-tolerant cactus — survives freezing temperatures. Flowers visited by native bees. Fire-adapted — pads resprout from base. Fruit and pads provide post-fire food for American Robin and Cedar Waxwing.

cacti.desc: Succulent shrub of coastal bluffs — the only native succulent Euphorbia in LA County. Small and easily overlooked. At northern range limit.

fm.Habitat: Coastal bluffs — at northern range limit

cacti.hp: Visited by small native bees and parasitoid wasps. Latex deters herbivores.

cacti.desc: Finger-shaped cylindrical leaves on rosette succulent. White flowers. Coastal bluffs and rocky outcrops. Edible — hence the species name. Southern LA County edge.
cacti.hp: Edible succulent — 'edulis' means edible. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Provides nectar on bare rock faces where few other flowers grow. Increasingly threatened by international poaching for succulent trade. Poaching threat. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Channel Islands endemic dudleya with chalky rosettes and yellow flowers. Known only from Santa Catalina and a few mainland cliff sites. Severely threatened by poaching.

fm.Habitat: Catalina Island and a few mainland cliffs
fm.Conservation: Severely threatened by poaching — island endemic

cacti.hp: Channel Islands endemic threatened by international succulent trade poaching. Pollinated by hummingbirds and native bees. Provides nectar on bare rock faces where few other flowers grow. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Narrow-leaved dudleya with pale yellow flowers on coastal and inland rocky slopes. Southern LA County. Distinguished from D. lanceolata by narrower leaves.

fm.Habitat: Coastal and inland rocky slopes — southern LA County

cacti.hp: Pollinated by native bees and hummingbirds. Rock outcrop specialist. Survives fire on rock faces — succulent rosettes rehydrate and resprout from meristem.

cacti.desc: Brilliant red-orange flowers on clustering cactus. High desert and mountain-desert transition. Cold-tolerant — survives SGM winter freezes.
cacti.hp: Hummingbird-pollinated — brilliant red flowers attract Costa's and Black-chinned Hummingbirds. Cold-tolerant cactus.

cacti.desc: Thin-stemmed cholla with bright red fruits persisting into winter — hence 'Christmas cactus.' Desert washes at far eastern LA County edge.

fm.Habitat: Desert washes — far eastern LA County

cacti.hp: Red winter fruits eaten by Cactus Wren, thrashers, and other desert birds. Provides winter food when little else is available. Stem segments resprout after fire, rapidly re-establishing habitat structure.

cacti.desc: Low mat-forming succulent with spoon-shaped fleshy leaves and yellow star flowers. Rocky outcrops and cliff faces. At southern range limit.

fm.Leaves: Spoon-shaped, fleshy, often with waxy bloom
fm.Habitat: Rocky outcrops, cliff faces — at southern range limit

cacti.hp: Mat-forming succulent stabilizing rock surfaces. Pollinated by small native bees. At southern range limit.

vines.desc: Native grape of riparian corridors. Small tart fruit. Important wildlife food. Can completely cover large trees.

fm.Fruit: Small, dark purple — tart, wildlife food
fm.Color: Green, tiny — fruit is dark purple

vines.hp: Fruit eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and California Towhee including Northern Mockingbird and Cedar Waxwing. Host for Virginia Creeper Sphinx Moth and Grape Leaffolder. Can completely cover large trees.

vines.desc: Fast-growing vine with spiny fruit the size of a fist. Emerges from enormous underground tuber after winter rains. Toxic fruit.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — massive underground tuber survives fire; rapid post-fire regrowth
fm.vs: Man-root (M. fabacea) has SMOOTH round fruits; Wild Cucumber (M. macrocarpa) has SPINY prickly fruits. M. macrocarpa is the common LA County species.

vines.hp: Visited by native bees in winter/early spring when few other flowers available. Massive underground tuber survives fire — rapid post-fire regrowth. fabacea). Seeds and roots processed as food by Tongva after extensive leaching to remove toxic cucurbitacins. Root used to stun fish in streams. Ballistic seed dispersal — pods explode when ripe, flinging large seeds several meters.

vines.desc: White flower clusters followed by silky seed plumes. Vigorous native climber in riparian areas. Can form dense curtains.
vines.hp: Host for Clematis Clearwing Moth. Visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and beetles. Western clematis — Tongva used bark fiber as cordage. Bushtit builds pendant nests in clematis tangles. Feathery seed plumes disperse on wind.

vines.desc: Twining native honeysuckle with creamy yellow tubular flowers. Chaparral and woodland edges. Less showy than Japanese Honeysuckle but native.
vines.hp: Nectar source for hummingbirds and native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Fire-adapted resprouter — contributes to rapid post-fire habitat recovery.

vines.desc: Large white to pink morning glory flowers on vigorous native vine. Coastal and island slopes. Much larger flowers than bindweed.
vines.hp: Channel Islands endemic expanded to mainland. Vigorous twining vine. Visited by native bees and small butterflies.

vines.desc: Cream-white flowers and feathery seed heads. Vigorous chaparral climber.

fm.Fire: Resprouter — root crown; vigorous post-fire growth

vines.hp: Chaparral clematis — Tongva and Chumash used bark fiber as cordage. Feathery achene plumes disperse seeds long distances on wind. Visited by native bees. Provides nesting cover for Wrentit.

vines.desc: Tiny purple snapdragon flowers on twining stems. Delicate desert-edge vine. Climbing by twining leaf stalks.
vines.hp: Rock face specialist. Leaf petioles coil around supports for climbing. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds.

vines.desc: Orange parasitic vine forming tangled masses over host plants. No leaves, no chlorophyll — entirely parasitic. Native but often mistaken for invasive.

fm.Ecology: Native parasite — not invasive

vines.hp: Parasite — entirely parasitic with no chlorophyll. Often mistaken for invasive but is a natural part of the ecosystem. Can reduce vigor of invasive hosts. Visited by native bees and hummingbirds.

vines.desc: Native blackberry with delicious small sweet berries — far better flavor than Himalayan blackberry. Thorny arching canes in moist canyons. White flowers. Important wildlife food.

fm.Fruit: Small sweet black berries — superior flavor to non-native species

vines.hp: Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing, American Black Bear, raccoons, foxes, and coyotes. Superior flavor to non-native Himalayan blackberry. Flowers visited by native bees. Important riparian wildlife corridor cover.

vines.desc: Invasive Fast-growing South African vine smothering riparian trees. Cal-IPC High. Can grow 5+ ft per month in wet conditions. Toxic to livestock. One of LA County's most destructive riparian invasives.

fm.Ecology: Grows 5+ ft/month in wet conditions. Smothers riparian trees.
fm.Habitat: Riparian corridors — one of LA's worst riparian invasives

vines.hp: Cal-IPC High. One of California's most destructive invasive vines — smothers native riparian and coastal vegetation. Toxic to livestock. Active removal programs statewide. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies.

vines.desc: Invasive Aggressive climbing vine that smothers trees and ground. Cal-IPC Moderate. Aerial rootlets damage bark and mortar. Weight of ivy can topple trees in storms. Berry spread by birds.

fm.Ecology: Weight topples trees. Aerial rootlets damage structures.

vines.hp: Cal-IPC High. One of California's worst invasive vines. Smothers trees, ground covers, and structures. Berries bird-dispersed. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

vines.desc: Invasive Blue-violet pinwheel flowers on trailing groundcover. Cal-IPC Moderate. Escapes gardens into riparian areas where it forms dense mats excluding all native plants. Toxic if ingested.

fm.Ecology: Forms impenetrable mats excluding all natives

vines.hp: Cal-IPC Moderate. Aggressive vine smothering native riparian understory. Visited by native bees and other pollinators.

vines.desc: Pink tubular flowers on twining vine in mountain woodland. Less common than L. subspicata. Hairy leaves. Important hummingbird nectar plant in SGM canyons.
vines.hp: Important hummingbird nectar. Berries eaten by Hermit Thrush.

vines.desc: Yellow tubular flowers with distinctive fused leaf pairs below flower clusters — the upper leaves form a disc through which the stem passes. Dry chaparral. Less common than other honeysuckles.

fm.Leaves: Upper pairs fused into disc around stem (diagnostic)
fm.Habitat: Dry chaparral — less common than other Lonicera

vines.hp: Native vine climbing through shrubs. Yellow-orange flowers attract hummingbirds. Less common than L. hispidula.

vines.desc: Non-native Fragrant white-yellow flowers on aggressive twining vine. Cal-IPC Moderate. Widely planted but escaping into riparian areas. Smothers native shrubs. Extremely sweet fragrance.

fm.Ecology: Smothers native shrubs in riparian areas

vines.hp: Nectar source for hummingbirds and native bees. Berries eaten by American Robin, Northern Mockingbird, and Cedar Waxwing. Invasive — displaces native vegetation in riparian and woodland habitats.

vines.desc: Massive tuberous vine with white-green flowers and SMOOTH round fruits — the key difference from M. macrocarpa which has SPINY fruits. At the edge of its range in LA County (more common in NorCal/Central Coast). Enormous underground tuber can weigh over 100 lbs.

fm.Fruit: SMOOTH round — no spines (diagnostic vs M. macrocarpa)
fm.Habitat: Canyon bottoms, riparian — edge of range in LA County
fm.vs: Wild Cucumber (M. macrocarpa) has SPINY prickly fruits and is common in LA; Man-root (M. fabacea) has SMOOTH fruits and is rare in LA County (more common NorCal)

vines.hp: Visited by native bees in winter/early spring when few other flowers available.

aquatic.desc: Cattail (Typhaceae). Nesting habitat for Red-winged Blackbird, Marsh Wren, Least Bittern, and Virginia Rail.

fm.Seedhead: Fat brown cylinder — NO GAP between male (upper) and female (lower) sections (diagnostic vs T. domingensis)
fm.Habitat: Freshwater marshes, pond edges — less salt-tolerant
fm.Cultural: All parts used traditionally — root flour, leaf weaving, seedhead insulation
fm.Leaves: To 1 in wide — wider than Southern Cattail
fm.vs: Southern Cattail (T. domingensis) has a visible GAP between male and female spike sections, and narrower leaves

aquatic.hp: Nesting habitat for Red-winged Blackbird, Marsh Wren, Least Bittern, and Virginia Rail. All parts used traditionally — rhizome flour, leaf weaving, seedhead insulation, pollen food. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure. Pacific Tree Frog — whose 'ribbit' defines California nights — breeds in cattail marshes. California Treefrog calls from rocky stream margins.

aquatic.desc: Tiny floating plants forming green mats on still water. Each plant 1–5mm. Doubles in population every 2 days. Important waterfowl food.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food

aquatic.hp: Floating green discs doubling population in days. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure. Duckweed — important waterfowl food (especially for Mallard and American Coot). Provides cover for mosquitofish. One of world's smallest flowering plants (Guinness — related Wolffia is the absolute smallest).

aquatic.desc: Tiny floating fern turning red in sun. Hosts nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria — used as living fertilizer in rice paddies worldwide.

fm.Ecology: Nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria symbiont

aquatic.hp: Can cover pond surfaces rapidly. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure. Mosquito fern — tiny floating fern that fixes nitrogen via symbiotic cyanobacteria (Anabaena). Important waterfowl food.

aquatic.desc: White flower cone with petal-like bracts. Alkaline wetlands. Indigenous medicinal plant.

fm.Cultural: Indigenous remedy

aquatic.hp: Yerba mansa — important Tongva and Chumash medicinal plant for sore throat and skin conditions. Rhizomatous groundcover of moist alkaline soils. Visited by native bees.

aquatic.desc: Arrow-shaped leaves. Starchy tubers (wapato) — important indigenous food.

fm.Cultural: Tubers = wapato food

aquatic.hp: Edible tubers ('wapato') — important indigenous food. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Yellow flowers on floating stems. Can choke waterways when nutrients are high.
aquatic.hp: Can become aggressive in slow-moving water. Native but sometimes problematic. Pollinated by native bees and hawkmoths at dusk. Seeds eaten by Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch.

aquatic.desc: Non-native Peppery edible leaves in running streams. European introduction now widespread. Small white flowers.
aquatic.hp: Aquatic habitat supporting fish, amphibian, and invertebrate communities. Edible — peppery leaves — widely foraged. Visited by native bees and butterflies including Sara Orangetip. Watercress — edible. Provides habitat for Western Pond Turtle (state Species of Special Concern) and aquatic invertebrates.

aquatic.desc: Simple needle-like stems with single terminal spikelet. Freshwater margins. Important component of vernal pool and wetland habitats.

fm.Form: Single needle-like stem with terminal spikelet

aquatic.hp: Creeping spikerush of freshwater marshes. Rhizomatous — forms dense stands. Important waterfowl food and nesting habitat. Important wetland structure. Spikerush — provides underwater habitat for Pacific Tree Frog tadpoles and aquatic invertebrates.

aquatic.desc: Submerged aquatic with fine thread-like leaves. Important waterfowl food. Found in ponds, slow streams, and estuaries throughout LA County.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food

aquatic.hp: Sago pondweed — submerged aquatic with narrow leaves. Tubers are critical food for diving ducks and coots. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Small white three-petaled flowers in airy branching clusters above broad basal leaves. Shallow marsh and pond edges. Important waterfowl habitat plant.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl habitat plant

aquatic.hp: Wetland habitat species supporting marsh bird nesting and invertebrate communities. Important waterfowl food source. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: White umbel flowers on weak stems trailing in and along water. Shady stream margins. Caution: resembles toxic water hemlock. Verify ID carefully.

fm.Toxicity: Caution — resembles toxic Cicuta (water hemlock). ID carefully.

aquatic.hp: Sprawling aquatic herb. Pacific coast native. Provides shade and cover for fish fry in streams. Important host plant for Anise Swallowtail butterfly (Papilio zelicaon). Visited by diverse native pollinators.

aquatic.desc: Round umbrella-shaped leaves on slender stems in shallow water. Tiny white-green flower clusters. Wet margins of ponds and slow streams.
aquatic.hp: Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Succulent jointed green stems turning red in fall. Dominant salt marsh plant. Edible — salty crunch. Essential habitat for Belding's Savannah Sparrow. The iconic LA salt marsh plant.

fm.Form: Succulent jointed stems — turns red in fall
fm.Ecology: Essential nesting habitat for Belding's Savannah Sparrow

aquatic.hp: Essential habitat for Belding's Savannah Sparrow (state endangered). Edible — salty crunch. The iconic LA salt marsh species. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Narrow-leaved cattail with visible gap between male and female seed head sections — the key field mark vs T. latifolia. Leaves narrower (under 0.5 in). More salt-tolerant. The most common cattail in LA coastal marshes.

fm.Cultural: Tongva food plant — rhizomes, pollen, shoots all edible
fm.Habitat: Marshes, ponds, slow streams — more salt-tolerant than T. latifolia
fm.Seedhead: Brown cylinder with VISIBLE GAP between male (upper) and female (lower) sections (diagnostic vs T. latifolia)
fm.Leaves: Narrower than T. latifolia — under 0.5 in
fm.vs: Broadleaf Cattail (T. latifolia) has NO GAP between spike sections, and wider leaves to 1 in

aquatic.hp: More salt-tolerant than T. latifolia — the dominant cattail in LA coastal marshes. Tongva food plant — rhizomes, pollen, and shoots all edible. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Tall marsh sedge with round stems (vs triangular in S. californicus). Dense stands in marshes. Foundation wetland species.
aquatic.hp: The 'tule' of 'tule fog.' Dense marsh stands. Important marsh bird nesting habitat. Indigenous boat-building material. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure. Hardstem bulrush — Marsh Wren nests woven among stems. Red-winged Blackbird territories centered on bulrush stands. Tongva wove stems into mats.

aquatic.desc: Tall triangular-stemmed sedge of marshes and lake margins. Important nesting habitat for marsh birds. Cultural use for tule boats.

fm.Cultural: Tule — used for boats and shelters

aquatic.hp: Cultural significance — tule used by indigenous peoples for boats (balsas) and shelters. Critical nesting habitat for marsh birds. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure. Common Yellowthroat nests in dense tule stands. Marsh Wren builds multiple dummy nests woven among tule stems.

aquatic.desc: Tall grass with feathery plumes forming dense stands in wetlands. Both native and invasive genotypes exist in LA. The invasive form is displacing native in some areas.

fm.Ecology: Native and invasive genotypes — invasive form spreading

aquatic.hp: Common reed — complex ecology: native genotype (ssp. americanus) existed historically, but most LA County stands are the invasive European genotype. Important Marsh Wren and Song Sparrow habitat regardless of genotype. Chumash used stems for arrow shafts and thatching.

aquatic.desc: Invasive Beautiful lavender flowers on floating aquatic plant — one of the most destructive invasive plants on Earth. Cal-IPC High. Doubles biomass every 6-18 days. Clogs waterways worldwide. Present in LA River system.

fm.Origin: South America — Cal-IPC High. One of world's worst invasive species.
fm.Ecology: Doubles biomass every 6-18 days. Depletes oxygen, blocks light.
fm.Habitat: Slow waterways, ponds — LA River system

aquatic.hp: Aquatic habitat supporting fish, amphibian, and invertebrate communities. Cal-IPC High invasive — displaces native vegetation. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Tall sticky-leaved marsh plant with white flower clusters. Freshwater and brackish marshes. Similar to Mulefat but prefers wetter sites. Important marsh nectar source.
aquatic.hp: Important late-season nectar source in marsh habitats. Visited by native bees and butterflies.

aquatic.desc: Gray-green shrub of alkaline marshes and desert springs. Arrow-straight stems used by indigenous peoples for arrows and building. Pink flower clusters. Indicator of groundwater.

fm.Habitat: Alkaline marshes, desert springs — groundwater indicator
fm.Cultural: Tongva/Cahuilla used stems for arrows and construction

aquatic.hp: Groundwater indicator species. Tongva used straight stems for arrows and construction. Pink flowers visited by native bees.

aquatic.desc: Pink-purple flower clusters on aromatic marsh plant. Strong camphor-like odor. Important late-summer marsh nectar source. Freshwater and brackish wetlands.
aquatic.hp: Important late-summer nectar source in marsh habitats when few other wetland plants flower. Visited by butterflies and native bees.

aquatic.desc: Woolly-fruited sedge of stream banks and marsh margins. Rhizomatous — forms dense stands. Important for wetland restoration.

fm.Ecology: Important wetland restoration species

aquatic.hp: Wetland structural species providing nesting habitat for rails and Song Sparrows. Important for stream bank stabilization. Wind-pollinated sedge. Seeds eaten by sparrows. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Large robust sedge of shady stream banks. Among the most common LA County riparian sedges. Important for stream bank stabilization and restoration.

fm.Ecology: Stream bank stabilization

aquatic.hp: Important stream bank stabilizer. Dense stands provide cover for Song Sparrow and other riparian birds. Common restoration species. Wind-pollinated sedge. Seeds eaten by sparrows. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Small spikerush with blunt-tipped spikelet. Shallow marsh margins and vernal pool edges. Distinguished from E. macrostachya by blunt spikelet tip.

fm.Habitat: Shallow marsh margins, vernal pool edges

aquatic.hp: Vernal pool and marsh edge species. Provides habitat structure for aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Pink flower spikes floating on or emergent from water. Grows in both aquatic and terrestrial forms. Important waterfowl food — seeds and foliage eaten.

fm.Form: Floating or emergent — two growth forms
fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food

aquatic.hp: Seeds and foliage eaten by waterfowl including Mallard, Cinnamon Teal, and American Coot. Amphibious — grows in both water and on land. Important for native seed-harvesting ants and Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow. Nectar source for native bees and butterflies including Acmon Blue.

aquatic.desc: Invasive Feathery aquatic plant forming dense mats on still water. South American invasive choking ponds and slow streams. Fragments spread easily — nearly impossible to eradicate once established.

fm.Ecology: Forms dense mats blocking light and oxygen

aquatic.hp: Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure. Cal-IPC High — dense underwater mats deplete dissolved oxygen, killing native fish and amphibians. Fragments spread to new water bodies on boats and equipment.

aquatic.desc: Succulent salt marsh shrub — federally endangered. Once common in LA County salt marshes, now reduced to a few populations. Red-purple fall color.

fm.Leaves: Fleshy, linear, turning red-purple in fall
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered — few LA County populations remain

aquatic.hp: Federally endangered salt marsh specialist. Important habitat component for salt marsh invertebrates and Belding's Savannah Sparrow. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Hemiparasitic annual of salt marshes — federally endangered. Yellow-green hooded flowers. Depends on host plants (pickleweed, saltgrass) for water and nutrients. Very few remaining populations.

fm.Ecology: Hemiparasitic — taps pickleweed and saltgrass roots
fm.Conservation: Federally Endangered — very few populations remain

aquatic.hp: Federally endangered hemiparasite of salt marshes. Depends on pickleweed and saltgrass hosts. Critical indicator of high-quality salt marsh habitat. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Robust triangular-stemmed sedge of brackish and alkaline marshes. Important waterfowl food — tubers and seeds eaten by many duck species. Forms dense stands.

fm.Ecology: Important waterfowl food — tubers and seeds

aquatic.hp: Important waterfowl food — tubers eaten by Pintail, Canvasback, and Ruddy Duck. Dense stands provide nesting cover for marsh birds. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Iris-like leaves with spherical spiny seed heads. Freshwater marsh margins. Important food for waterfowl — seeds highly nutritious.
aquatic.hp: Seeds highly nutritious waterfowl food. Dense stands provide nesting habitat for marsh wrens and rails. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Low salt-tolerant annual of coastal salt marshes and alkali flats. Triangular fruiting bracts diagnostic. Important component of upper salt marsh zone.
aquatic.hp: Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow and White-crowned Sparrow and Lesser Goldfinch and House Finch in coastal habitats. Provides habitat for native aquatic invertebrates. Important wetland structure.

aquatic.desc: Low mat-forming grass of salt marsh upper zones. One of the most salt-tolerant grasses — grows where almost nothing else can. Important for salt marsh upper zone restoration.

fm.Habitat: Salt marsh upper zones — extreme salt tolerance

aquatic.hp: Extremely salt-tolerant — grows in uppermost salt marsh zone. Provides microhabitat for salt marsh invertebrates. Wind-pollinated. Seeds eaten by Song Sparrow, California Towhee, and Lesser Goldfinch. Provides habitat for ground-nesting native bees.

aquatic.desc: Invasive Bright yellow button-like flowers on fleshy stems in shallow water and marsh margins. South African introduction now common in coastal wetlands. Often the most conspicuous flower in salt marshes.

fm.Origin: South African — naturalized in coastal wetlands

aquatic.hp: Yellow button flowers are the most conspicuous bloom in many LA salt marshes. Composite heads visited by native bees, syrphid flies, and small butterflies. South African salt marsh invasive — displaces native Distichlis saltgrass and Salicornia. Despite ecological harm, provides nectar for native bees in degraded marsh habitats.

aquatic.desc: Succulent creeping shrub of salt marshes — one of the most salt-tolerant plants in LA County. Forms dense mats in lower salt marsh zone. Important food for salt marsh caterpillars.

fm.Habitat: Lower salt marsh zone — extreme salt tolerance

aquatic.hp: Lower salt marsh zone specialist — one of the most salt-tolerant plants in LA. Important habitat for salt marsh invertebrates and Western Snowy Plover. Important wetland structure.

mosses.desc: Tiny silver-green cushions in sidewalk cracks worldwide. One of the most urban-adapted mosses. Thrives in concrete environments.

fm.Season: Wet season only — desiccates in summer
fm.Ecology: Silver moss — one of most common urban mosses worldwide. Sidewalk cracks throughout LA.

mosses.hp: Pioneer species creating soil in concrete environments. Soil stabilizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Small moss on walls and rocks with hair-tipped leaves that twist when dry. The "corkscrew" motion gives it its name.

fm.Ecology: Wall screw moss — extremely common on concrete, walls, sidewalks.

mosses.hp: Wall screw-moss — tiny but extremely drought-tolerant. Survives on bare concrete and mortar. Twists when dry, unfurls within seconds when wetted. Retains moisture in rock crevices. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Pioneer moss after fire and disturbance. Bright green rosettes with curved pear-shaped capsules on long stalks. The capsule stalk twists with humidity changes.

fm.Size: Plants ½–1 in; stalks to 2 in
fm.Ecology: Bonfire moss — common in disturbed areas, post-fire sites.

mosses.hp: Pioneer after fire — one of the first organisms to colonize burned ground. Capsule stalk twists hygroscopically with humidity changes. Also called 'bonfire moss.'.

mosses.desc: Star-shaped rosettes that revive dramatically from complete desiccation. One of the most drought-tolerant plants on Earth.

fm.Color: Green when wet, brown/curled when dry
fm.Drought: Survives years of complete desiccation — poikilohydric
fm.Ecology: Star moss — common desert and semi-arid moss. SoCal verified.

mosses.hp: Among the most drought-tolerant plants on Earth — survives years of complete desiccation and revives within minutes of wetting. Poikilohydric. Retains moisture in rock crevices. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Relatively large moss with stiff juniper-like leaves. Male plants with splash-cup structures. Mountain trails and banks.

fm.Color: Dark green with reddish capsule caps
fm.Ecology: Juniper haircap — mountain areas. Well-documented on iNat for SoCal.

mosses.hp: Among the tallest mosses — upright growth form resembling miniature juniper grove. Provides microhabitat for springtails and mites. Important pioneer on disturbed soil after fire.

mosses.desc: Dark green moss growing on tree bark, especially oaks. Erect capsules among leaves. Most common epiphytic moss in LA.

fm.Ecology: Common on bark in SoCal oak woodland.

mosses.hp: Lyell's tree moss — fuzzy tufts on bark. Important bark microhabitat for mites and springtails. Bark epiphyte providing microhabitat for native invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Creeping feathery moss in shady moist areas. Irregularly branching. One of the most common woodland floor mosses.

fm.Habitat: Shady moist woodland floor, rotting wood

mosses.hp: Creates humid microhabitat for springtails. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important soil moisture retention.

mosses.desc: Flat green thallus with umbrella-like reproductive structures. Not a true moss — a liverwort. Greenhouse and nursery floors.

fm.Habitat: Moist shady ground, greenhouses, nursery pots

mosses.hp: Not a true moss — a liverwort (distinct evolutionary lineage). Umbrella-like reproductive structures are diagnostic. Important soil stabilizer in nurseries and greenhouses.

mosses.desc: Forms pincushion-like mounds on tree bark. Dry-adapted — more common in SoCal than most mosses. Epiphytic.
mosses.hp: Dense rounded cushions on tree branches. Distinctive julaceous (worm-like) branches. Fog-moisture specialist.

mosses.desc: Small gray-green cushions on rocks with distinctive white hair-tips. Very common on boulders and walls. Drought-adapted.

fm.Ecology: Cushion moss — common on exposed rock in SoCal mountains.

mosses.hp: Grey cushion moss — silvery cushions on rock and concrete. Hair-point leaf tips create distinctive silvery appearance. Desiccation-tolerant.

mosses.desc: Dark green to black cushions on exposed rock. Very drought-tolerant. One of the most common rock mosses in SoCal.
mosses.hp: Smooth rock moss — glossy dark cushions on exposed rock. pulvinata by smooth (not hair-pointed) leaf tips.

mosses.desc: Small green cushions with nodding pear-shaped capsules on long red stalks. Very common on walls and paths.
mosses.hp: Among the most common urban mosses — on sidewalks, walls, and tree bases. Pioneer colonizer of bare surfaces. Provides microhabitat for urban invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Gray-green moss with white hair points. Forms extensive mats on sandy/rocky ground. Mountain trails.
mosses.hp: Sandy and gravelly soils. Important sand stabilizer. Pioneer rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates and retains moisture.

mosses.desc: Distinctive swept-to-one-side leaves. Mountain forest floors. Named for its wind-blown appearance.

fm.Ecology: Broom moss — mountain conifer forest understory.

mosses.hp: Distinctive one-sided leaf orientation. Looks 'combed' by wind. Important nesting material for hummingbirds.

mosses.desc: Named for its spherical apple-like capsules. Mountain rock crevices and soil banks. Green glaucous cushions.
mosses.hp: Spherical capsules resembling tiny green apples. Shaded rock ledges and banks. Beautiful under hand lens. Rock ledge specialist. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Very common moss with distinctive reddish stalks (setae). Pioneer on bare soil, rooftops, and cracks. Cosmopolitan — found on every continent including Antarctica.

fm.Habitat: Bare soil, rooftops, paths — extremely common
fm.Ecology: Redshank moss — cosmopolitan. Found on every continent.

mosses.hp: First moss to colonize burned ground after fire. Provides microhabitat for pioneer invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Golden-green mats on dry rocks and soil in chaparral. One of the most common SoCal mosses. Distinctive golden sheen in sun. Branches irregular, not feathery.

fm.Substrate: Dry rocks, soil banks in chaparral
fm.Habitat: Very common in chaparral and oak woodland throughout LA County

mosses.hp: Mat moss — golden-brown mats on tree trunks. Somewhat drought-tolerant for a moss. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates. Important soil moisture retention.

mosses.desc: Tiny bright green cushions on rock and mortar. Leaves twist spirally when dry (diagnostic). One of the most common rock mosses in urban LA - grows on walls, concrete, old brick.

fm.Color: Bright green when moist, yellow-brown when dry
fm.Habitat: Urban walls and rocks - very common

mosses.hp: Rock crevice specialist. Extremely desiccation-tolerant — curls tightly when dry, revives within seconds when wetted. Provides microhabitat for rock-dwelling invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Large springy moss with leaves bent sharply backward from stems - distinctive "squarrose" look. Mountain meadows. Touch it and it springs back.
mosses.hp: Bouncy when walked on. Forest floor moisture retention. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Dense dark green mats draping tree bases and lower trunks in moist canyons. One of the most conspicuous mosses in SMM/SGM canyon bottoms. Glossy appearance.

fm.Habitat: Moist canyon bottoms - SMM and SGM

mosses.hp: Forms skirt-like mats around tree bases. Pacific coast specialist.

mosses.desc: Long trailing stems with irregularly branched shoots in mountain forests. Moist conifer duff. Named for its lanky, sprawling growth habit.
mosses.hp: Important water retention in forest floor. Sensitive to air pollution. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

mosses.desc: Dense rounded cushions of pale blue-green moss. Unmistakable color - no other local moss is blue-green. Unique cell structure holds water like a sponge.

fm.Ecology: Sponge-like water retention - unique cell structure

mosses.hp: Distinctive white-green cushions resembling miniature palm trees. Color from specialized hyaline cells storing water.

mosses.desc: The only truly aquatic moss in LA County - grows fully submerged in flowing streams. Long dark green trailing stems anchored to rocks. Name means "against fire" - historically used to fireproof roofs.

fm.Form: Aquatic - long trailing stems fully submerged
fm.Cultural: Name means "against fire" - historically packed around chimneys

mosses.hp: The only truly aquatic moss in LA County — grows fully submerged. Provides habitat for stream invertebrates. Name means 'against fire' — historically packed around chimneys.

mosses.desc: The iconic bog moss - pale green with unique water-holding cells. Extremely rare in SoCal - known from a few mountain seeps in SGM. Creates its own acidic habitat.

fm.Ecology: Creates its own acidic habitat. Holds 20x its weight in water.
fm.Habitat: Mountain seeps - extremely rare in SoCal
fm.Conservation: Very rare in LA County - a few SGM seeps only

mosses.hp: Creates its own acidic habitat (pH 4–5). Extremely rare in SoCal — a few SGM seeps only. Peat formation basis.

mosses.desc: One of the most common mosses worldwide. Curved yellow-green shoots in neat mats on bark, rock, and soil. The "default" moss people imagine. Curved leaves diagnostic.
mosses.hp: Curved leaves resembling miniature cypress fronds. Among the most common mosses worldwide. Forms dense mats on logs.

mosses.desc: Tiny bright green tufts in sidewalk cracks and between pavers. Related to the common Silver Moss but greener. Among the first colonizers of disturbed urban ground.
mosses.hp: Tiny silver-green cushions in sidewalk cracks. Urban specialist. Soil stabilizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Bright orange-yellow foliose lichen on trees and rocks. Nitrogen-loving — common near bird perches and dog-walking areas.

fm.Habitat: Tree bark, rocks, concrete — nitrogen-enriched sites
fm.Season: Year-round — grows fastest in wet months
fm.Ecology: Very common on urban trees and fences across LA.

lichens.hp: Nitrogen-loving — common near bird perches, dog walks, and vehicle exhaust. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Gray foliose lichen with net-like ridges (sulcate). Common on tree bark. Moderate air pollution tolerance.

fm.Ecology: Widespread in N. Hemisphere but uncommon in SoCal lowlands. Verify.

lichens.hp: One of the most studied lichens globally — key species in biomonitoring for heavy metals and air pollutants. Provides hummingbird nesting material. Ridged thallus traps water and invertebrates.

lichens.desc: California state lichen. Dramatic hanging net-like strands from tree branches. Fog-dependent. Air quality indicator — absent from polluted areas.

fm.Form: Fruticose — hanging net-like strands to 3 ft
fm.Conservation: CA state lichen — air quality indicator
fm.Ecology: CA State Lichen. Hasse collected extensively in LA. Common on coast live oaks.

lichens.hp: CA state lichen. Fog-dependent — absorbs moisture directly from marine layer. Absence from formerly occupied areas indicates declining air quality. Important nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow foliose lichen on conifer bark in mountain forests. Contains vulpinic acid — toxic yellow pigment historically used as wolf poison in Europe.

fm.Toxicity: Vulpinic acid — historically used as wolf poison
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer bark — San Gabriel/San Bernardino Mts

lichens.hp: Contains vulpinic acid — historically used as wolf poison in Scandinavia. Mountain conifer bark specialist. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates. Air quality bioindicator.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green crustose lichen forming map-like patterns on rock. Extremely slow-growing — used for dating rock surfaces (lichenometry). Some colonies centuries old.

fm.Growth: Extremely slow — used for rock dating (lichenometry)
fm.Ecology: Map lichen on high-elevation granite. San Gabriel Mountains.

lichens.hp: The iconic 'map-like' pattern used for lichenometric dating. Fluorescent yellow-green areoles on black hypothallus. Among the world's longest-lived organisms — some thalli estimated 8,000+ years old.

lichens.desc: Small podetia with bright red apothecia on tips. On decaying wood and humus in mountain forests. The SoCal equivalent of eastern British Soldiers.

fm.Habitat: Decaying wood, humus in mountain conifer forest

lichens.hp: Acid rain indicator. Forest floor habitat for native invertebrates. Important soil crust component.

lichens.desc: Large gray-brown foliose lichen growing on soil and mossy banks. Undersides with white veins and rhizines. Contains cyanobacteria partner.

fm.Ecology: Dog lichen on soil and moss in mountain forests.

lichens.hp: Large foliose with dog-tooth apothecia. Cyanolichen fixing atmospheric nitrogen. Important soil ecology — provides habitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Large single-lobed lichen attached to rock by a central cord. Dark brown-black. Eaten as emergency survival food in some cultures.

fm.Form: Foliose — single large lobe, attached centrally
fm.Habitat: Rock faces, boulders in mountain areas
fm.Use: Emergency survival food in some traditions
fm.Ecology: Rock tripe on granite. San Gabriel Mountains.

lichens.hp: Single central holdfast. Brown-black foliose on exposed granitic rocks. Edible in emergencies (soaked and boiled). Rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates on boulder surfaces.

lichens.desc: Small orange foliose lichen with powdery soredia on margins. Very common on city trees and fences. Pollution-tolerant.

fm.Ecology: Very common orange lichen on bark. Well-documented.

lichens.hp: Common powder sunburst on urban trees and concrete. Nitrogen-loving indicator. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates. Moderately pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Pendant greenish beard-like lichen hanging from tree branches. Central elastic cord inside strands (pull to test — unique to Usnea). Air quality indicator.

fm.ID: Pull strand gently — central white elastic cord is diagnostic for Usnea

lichens.hp: Pendant lichen with elastic central cord (pull gently to test). Multiple species in LA County mountains. Extremely slow-growing — large specimens may be decades old.

lichens.desc: Pale greenish-gray crustose lichen forming rosettes on concrete and rock. One of the most common urban lichens. Very pollution-tolerant.

fm.Ecology: Extremely common on concrete/sidewalks in LA. Among most-observed lichens on iNat.

lichens.hp: Concentric rosettes of olive-green lobes on rock and concrete. Pioneer colonizer of urban walls and sidewalks. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with crater-like fruiting bodies (apothecia). Rock surfaces. The craters are distinctive with a hand lens.

fm.Ecology: Common on limestone and concrete in SoCal.

lichens.hp: Immersed crustose with sunken crater-like apothecia. Parasitizes other lichens when young, then becomes independent. Bark colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Tiny goblet-shaped structures on soil. Green granular surface. Often among mosses. The "cups" collect rainwater and dispersal propagules.

fm.Ecology: Pixie cup lichen on soil. Widely distributed.

lichens.hp: Pixie cup — goblet-shaped podetia collecting rainwater. Forest floor specialist. Reproduces via granular soredia inside cups.

lichens.desc: Powdery blue-green crust on tree bark and shady rock. No distinct form — just powder. Very common but overlooked.

fm.ID: Powdery texture distinguishes from crustose lichens
fm.Ecology: Common dust lichen but name may be misapplied for SoCal populations.

lichens.hp: Entirely leprose (powdery), never forming distinct lobes. Shade specialist. Pioneer rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow-green crustose lichen on exposed rock. Very common on granite boulders at all elevations.

fm.Ecology: Common on exposed rock. San Gabriel Mts.

lichens.hp: Alpine rock specialist with golden pruinose discs. UV-protective pigments. Provides microhabitat for alpine invertebrates at extreme elevation.

lichens.desc: Gray-white branching crust on volcanic rock and concrete. Looks like tiny coral. Interesting dual symbiosis — green algae plus cyanobacteria.

fm.Ecology: Primarily volcanic substrates. Verify for LA County.

lichens.hp: Rock foam — stalked pseudopodetia with cephalodia containing nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria. Volcanic rock specialist.

lichens.desc: Branching fruticose lichen used historically in perfumery (oakmoss). Hanging from oak branches. Air quality indicator.

fm.Cultural: Oakmoss — used in perfumery
fm.Ecology: Reported from oak woodland in SoCal mountains.

lichens.hp: Oak moss — important perfumery ingredient. Pendant lichen on oaks. Used as fixative in fragrances since ancient times. Air quality bioindicator — sensitive to pollution. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Smooth white crustose lichen giving trees a whitewashed appearance. Very common on smooth-barked trees. Often overlooked as lichen.

fm.ID: Looks like white paint on bark
fm.Ecology: Primarily NorCal/Pacific NW. May not reach LA County.

lichens.hp: Whitewash lichen — smooth white crustose resembling paint on bark. Distinguished from Lepraria by smooth surface. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling mites.

lichens.desc: Tiny bright yellow-green foliose lichen on tree bark. Very small but very common in urban areas. Often grows alongside Sunburst Lichen.

fm.Ecology: Very common on bark in urban areas. Well-documented.

lichens.hp: Candleflame lichen — bright yellow on urban trees and fences. Nitrogen-loving indicator of enriched substrates. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Dark olive-black gelatinous lichen forming biological soil crust in desert. Swells dramatically when wet. Critical for soil stability and nitrogen fixation in desert ecosystems.

fm.Form: Foliose — swells when wet, shrivels when dry
fm.Ecology: Biological soil crust — nitrogen fixing, soil stabilizing

lichens.hp: Critical component of biological soil crust — stabilizes soil, fixes nitrogen, and prevents erosion in desert. EXTREMELY FRAGILE — decades to recover from a single footstep. Nitrogen-fixing cyanolichen. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Gray-green foliose lichen with distinctive network of ridges on surface (isidia). Very common on rocks and tree bark. One of the most frequently encountered lichens in SoCal mountains.

fm.Habitat: Common on rocks and bark - SMM and SGM

lichens.hp: Network of ridges creating hammered-metal appearance. Important biomonitor for heavy metals. Air quality bioindicator — sensitive to pollution. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Small gray-white rosettes with distinctive hooded lobe tips (soralia). Extremely common on urban trees and fences. One of the most pollution-tolerant lichens - thrives in LA.

fm.Ecology: Very pollution-tolerant - thrives in urban LA

lichens.hp: Hooded rosette — distinctive hood-shaped lobe tips (soredia inside hoods). Common on urban trees. Moderately pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Neat gray-white star-shaped rosettes on tree bark. Common on oaks throughout LA County. Among the first lichens beginners learn to recognize.

fm.Habitat: Oak woodland - very common throughout LA

lichens.hp: Star rosette — distinctive star-shaped gray lobes radiating from center. Moderately pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Brown foliose lichen with tiny dark isidia (pepper-like). Moist shady habitats. Contains cyanobacteria - fixes nitrogen. Sensitive to air pollution.

fm.Ecology: Contains cyanobacteria - nitrogen fixer. Air pollution indicator.

lichens.hp: Large foliose with cyphellae (pores) on lower surface. Cyanolichen — fixes atmospheric nitrogen.

lichens.desc: Large gray-green foliose lichen with broad ruffled lobes and soredia along margins. Common on oak bark. One of the largest and most conspicuous SoCal lichens.

fm.Size: 4-12 in across - among the largest SoCal lichens
fm.Color: Gray-green upper, black below with brown bare zone at margins

lichens.hp: Large ruffled gray-green lobes with dark cilia. Pollution-sensitive. Air quality bioindicator — sensitive to pollution. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen tightly pressed to rock surfaces. Very common on exposed boulders and outcrops throughout SoCal. Tolerates full sun and heat.

fm.Ecology: Tolerates full sun, heat, and drought
fm.Habitat: Exposed boulders and outcrops - very common

lichens.hp: Loosely attached foliose on rock. Brown below. Can detach and blow as 'vagrant lichen.'.

lichens.desc: Large dark brown single-lobed lichen attached to rock by central point (umbilicus). Blistered upper surface. Mountain rocks. Edible in emergencies but bitter.

fm.Form: Foliose, single lobe attached by central point
fm.Cultural: Edible in emergencies but very bitter

lichens.hp: Single central holdfast on alpine rocks. Edible in emergencies. Rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates on boulder surfaces.

lichens.desc: Brilliant chartreuse-yellow shrubby lichen on conifer bark. Toxic - historically used to poison wolves and foxes in Europe (hence the name). One of the most eye-catching mountain lichens.

fm.Toxicity: Contains vulpinic acid - historically used as wolf/fox poison

lichens.hp: Brilliant chartreuse-yellow — unmistakable. Contains vulpinic acid — historically used to poison wolves and foxes in Europe (hence the name). Toxic but not through skin contact. Air quality bioindicator — sensitive to pollution. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Long pale green-yellow pendulous strands hanging from conifer branches. Mountain forests. Distinguished from Usnea by lack of central cord. Important bird nesting material.

fm.Ecology: Important bird nesting material
fm.Habitat: Mountain conifer forest - SGM upper elevations

lichens.hp: Long pendant strands in mountain forests. Indicator of old-growth forest. Extremely slow-growing. Air quality bioindicator — sensitive to pollution. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Erect gray-green finger-like branches in coastal fog zone. Fog-dependent - absorbs moisture from marine layer. Sensitive to air pollution. Indicator of clean coastal air.

fm.Ecology: Fog-dependent - absorbs moisture from marine layer. Air quality indicator.

lichens.hp: Photosynthetic symbiosis between fungus and algae/cyanobacteria. Bioindicator of air quality. CA endemic — range-restricted and of conservation concern. Important epiphyte. Provides microhabitat for native insects on tree branches.

lichens.desc: Small bright yellow-green foliose lichen on mountain conifer bark. Closely related to V. canadensis. SGM pine forest.
lichens.hp: Bright yellow-green foliose on conifers. Contains pinastric acid.

lichens.desc: White crust on smooth bark with black line-like fruiting bodies resembling Egyptian hieroglyphics. Common on smooth-barked trees. Among the most distinctive and easily recognized crustose lichens.

fm.Color: White crust with black script-like lines

lichens.hp: Script Lichen contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources.

lichens.desc: Brilliant orange-red crustose lichen forming small rosettes on rocks. Common on calcareous rock and concrete. One of the most colorful and conspicuous crustose lichens.

fm.Habitat: Very common on rocks, walls, concrete throughout LA

lichens.hp: Important pioneer on cement. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow-orange powdery crust on concrete, mortar, and bird-perching rocks. The most common urban lichen - look at any concrete wall in LA. Nitrogen-loving.

fm.Ecology: Nitrophilous - loves nitrogen from bird droppings and urban runoff
fm.Habitat: Every concrete wall in LA - the most common urban lichen

lichens.hp: Among the most common urban lichens in LA — on concrete, mortar, and building stone. Nitrogen-loving indicator. Provides microhabitat for urban invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Gray-white crustose lichen with small wart-like soralia. Tastes extremely bitter (the name "amara" means bitter). Common on oak bark. Chemical test: C+ red.

fm.Taste: Extremely bitter (diagnostic - but do not taste regularly)

lichens.hp: Extremely bitter taste (diagnostic). White crustose with conspicuous soralia.

lichens.desc: Gray-white crust with striking blood-red fruiting discs (apothecia). Shady rocks and bark. The red discs make this one of the most visually dramatic crustose lichens.
lichens.hp: Blood-spot lichen — distinctive red-brown apothecia from haematommone pigment. Important bioindicator of old-growth forest conditions.

lichens.desc: Pink-brown squamulose lichen forming soil crusts in desert. Part of the cryptobiotic soil community critical for desert ecosystem function. Extremely fragile - destroyed by foot traffic.

fm.Ecology: Part of cryptobiotic soil crust - critical for desert nitrogen fixation and erosion control
fm.Toxicity: EXTREMELY FRAGILE - decades to recover from footstep

lichens.hp: Part of cryptobiotic soil crust community critical for desert nitrogen fixation and erosion control. Destroyed by foot and vehicle traffic.

lichens.desc: Unattached lichen that tumbles across desert flats with the wind - a lichen tumbleweed. Yellow-green, crumpled. One of the most unusual lichen life strategies anywhere.

fm.Form: Foliose but UNATTACHED - rolls like tumbleweed
fm.Ecology: Vagrant life strategy - tumbles across desert with wind

lichens.hp: Unattached, tumbles across desert flats with wind like a tumbleweed.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen with tiny granular isidia covering surface like black pepper. Very common on exposed rocks and boulders in full sun. Loosely attached.

fm.Color: Yellow-green with dark isidia like pepper
fm.Substrate: Exposed rocks, boulders in full sun
fm.Habitat: Very common on sunny rocks throughout LA County

lichens.hp: One of the most common rock lichens in temperate regions. Pioneer rock surface colonizer. Provides microhabitat for rock-dwelling mites and springtails.

lichens.desc: Single large leathery lobe attached to rock by central point. Smooth dark brown upper surface, black below. Mountain rocks. Closely related to U. phaea.

fm.Form: Foliose, single lobe from central umbilicus
fm.Color: Dark brown upper, black below, smooth

lichens.hp: Multiple leaf-like lobes from single point. Edible in emergencies. Rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates on boulder surfaces.

lichens.desc: Tiny gray-white rosettes with powdery lobe tips (soredia). Common on urban trees, fences, and concrete. Very similar to P. adscendens but with flatter lobe tips.

fm.Ecology: Pollution-tolerant urban lichen
fm.Habitat: Urban trees and structures - very common

lichens.hp: Narrow gray lobes with powdery tips. Common on deciduous trees. adscendens by open (not hooded) soralia.

lichens.desc: Large yellow-green foliose lichen with tiny raised dots (soredia) on surface. Common on oaks and other hardwoods. One of the more conspicuous bark lichens in LA.

fm.Habitat: Oak woodland - common throughout LA mountains

lichens.hp: One of the most common foliose lichens on urban trees. Moderately pollution-tolerant — its presence indicates acceptable air quality. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Brilliant orange bushy lichen on tree twigs and branches. Becoming rare due to air pollution sensitivity. One of the most spectacular SoCal lichens when found.

fm.Ecology: Air pollution sensitive - declining
fm.Habitat: Coastal trees - becoming rare in LA

lichens.hp: Declining due to air pollution — formerly common, now increasingly rare. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Pale yellow-green hanging strands with a central cord (pull apart a strand to see white elastic cord inside). Mountain conifers. The central cord distinguishes all Usnea from Alectoria.

fm.Test: Pull strand apart - white elastic central cord visible (diagnostic for Usnea)

lichens.hp: Yellow beard lichen with elastic central cord (diagnostic for Usnea). Mountain conifer specialist. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds and small birds.

lichens.desc: Tiny bright yellow-green rosettes on conifer bark at mountain elevations. Forms dense colonies. Distinctive color against dark bark makes it easy to spot.

fm.Form: Tiny foliose rosettes in dense colonies

lichens.hp: Subnival (under-snow) specialist of high-elevation conifer forests. One of few lichens active under snowpack. Provides microhabitat for winter-active invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Blue-gray crust with conspicuous black fruiting discs. Very common on shaded rocks. The black discs on pale background are distinctive.
lichens.hp: Granitic rock specialist with distinctive smoky-blue discs. Acid-loving. Provides microhabitat for rock-dwelling invertebrates. Important pioneer on boulders.

lichens.desc: White crust dotted with small brown fruiting bodies resembling chocolate chips. Coastal rocks and mortar. Charming appearance.
lichens.hp: Dark apothecia on pale crustose thallus. Mediterranean limestone specialist.

lichens.desc: Brilliant orange rosettes on rock, especially where birds perch (nitrogen-enriched). More vivid and neatly circular than X. parietina. Mountain rocks.

fm.Ecology: Nitrophilous - indicates bird droppings
fm.Habitat: Mountain rocks - especially near raptor perches

lichens.hp: Brilliant orange rosettes on nitrogen-enriched rocks. Used for lichenometric dating in Arctic.

lichens.desc: Dark olive-black gelatinous crust on desert soil. Part of cryptobiotic crust community. Swells dramatically when wet, shrinks when dry. Nitrogen fixer via cyanobacteria.

fm.Form: Gelatinous when wet, crustose when dry
fm.Ecology: Cryptobiotic soil crust - nitrogen fixer via cyanobacteria
fm.Habitat: Mojave Desert, Antelope Valley - do not step on

lichens.hp: Soil crust jelly lichen — swells dramatically when wet. Cyanolichen fixing nitrogen. Critical for desert soil crust integrity. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Another vagrant (unattached) lichen of desert flats. Darker green than X. chlorochroa. Accumulates in wind-deposited piles against fences and rocks in Antelope Valley.

fm.Ecology: Vagrant - tumbles across desert; accumulates against obstacles

lichens.hp: Loosely attached foliose that detaches and blows across desert. One of few vagrant (unattached) lichens.

lichens.desc: Large wrinkled yellow-green foliose lichen on tree bark — the most common large lichen on oaks in LA County. Wrinkled surface with granular soredia in center. Closely related to F. flaventior but wrinkled (not dotted).

fm.Surface: Wrinkled with granular soredia in center (vs raised dots in F. flaventior)
fm.Size: 3-10 in across — often the largest lichen on a given oak
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — the most common large lichen on LA oaks
fm.vs: Speckled Greenshield (F. flaventior) has raised dot soredia; Common Greenshield has wrinkled surface soredia

lichens.hp: One of the most frequently encountered foliose lichens worldwide. Important bioindicator of air quality — sensitive to sulfur dioxide. Provides hummingbird nesting material.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen with powdery soredia along lobe margins (vs laminal soredia in F. flaventior). On bark and rock. Distinguished from Speckled Greenshield by marginal rather than surface soredia placement.

fm.Surface: Soredia along LOBE MARGINS (vs on surface in F. flaventior)
fm.vs: Speckled Greenshield (F. flaventior) has soredia arising from surface pseudocyphellae; Powder-edged has soredia concentrated at lobe margins
