lichens.hp: Sorediate variant of the Flavopunctelia complex. Common on oaks and hardwoods.

lichens.desc: Yellow-green foliose lichen on ROCK (not bark). Distinguished from Common Greenshield (F. caperata) by pustular isidia-like outgrowths instead of granular soredia. Prefers exposed rock in chaparral.

fm.Habitat: Exposed rock in chaparral and woodland
fm.vs: Common Greenshield (F. caperata) grows on BARK with granular soredia; Rock Greenshield grows on ROCK with pustular outgrowths

lichens.hp: Distinguished from bark-dwelling congeners by rock substrate.

lichens.desc: Tiny greenish-gray horn-shaped stalks (podetia) growing from a crust of squamules on rotting wood and bark bases. Powdery soredia cover the horns. One of the most common Cladonia species in moist SoCal mountain habitats.

fm.Form: Fruticose — tiny horn-shaped podetia 0.5-2 cm tall rising from basal squamules
fm.Substrate: Rotting wood, bark bases, mossy stumps
fm.Habitat: Moist mountain forest — SMM and SGM canyons
fm.vs: Pixie Cup (C. pyxidata) has CUP-SHAPED tops; Powderhorn has POINTED tops. Lipstick Cladonia (C. macilenta) has RED tips.

lichens.hp: Important ground-cover lichen. Forest floor habitat for native invertebrates. Important soil crust component.

lichens.desc: Slender gray-green stalks topped with neat trumpet-shaped cups — like tiny goblets. Powdery soredia cover the cups. On soil, rotting wood, and mossy banks. More delicate than Pixie Cup (C. pyxidata).

fm.Form: Fruticose — slender stalks with neat trumpet/goblet cups at top
fm.Color: Gray-green with powdery soredia on cups
fm.vs: Pixie Cup (C. pyxidata) has wider, rougher cups with granular cortex; Trumpet Lichen has slender, neat cups with powdery soredia

lichens.hp: Elegant goblet-shaped podetia flaring to trumpet form. Indicator of intact forest. Forest floor habitat for native invertebrates. Important soil crust component.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow cobblestone-like crustose lichen on desert rock — one of the most common lichens in the Mojave. Pioneer species. Yellow color from rhizocarpic acid.

fm.Form: Crustose, areolate to squamulose — cobblestone pattern
fm.Habitat: Desert rock surfaces — extremely common in Mojave portion of LA County
fm.vs: Map Lichen (Rhizocarpon geographicum) is also yellow-green on rock but has black prothallus lines between areoles; Cobblestone lacks black lines

lichens.hp: Bright yellow areoles on desert pavement. Important component of biological soil crust preventing erosion. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Dark brown cobblestone crustose lichen — the brown counterpart to A. socialis. Among the most common rock lichens worldwide. Often overlooked due to drab color.

fm.Substrate: Rock — extremely common on any siliceous rock
fm.vs: Yellow Cobblestone (A. socialis) is bright yellow; Brown Cobblestone is dark brown. Both have similar areolate texture.

lichens.hp: Brown cobblestone — small brown areoles creating cobblestone pattern. Among the most common crustose rock lichens worldwide.

lichens.desc: Vivid orange-red crustose lichen on rock and concrete — the most conspicuous firedot lichen. Often the brightest spot of color on urban walls and gravestones.

fm.Color: Vivid ORANGE-RED (brighter and redder than C. flavovirescens)
fm.Substrate: Rock, concrete, mortar — loves calcareous substrates
fm.Habitat: Urban walls, gravestones, concrete bridges — very common
fm.vs: Firedot Lichen (C. flavovirescens) is more yellow-orange; Red Firedot is distinctly REDDER. Sidewalk Firedot (C. citrina) is powdery yellow.

lichens.hp: Red firedot — deep orange-red crustose on calcareous rock. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Tiny orange foliose lichen with hooded lobe tips on bark — named after early LA lichenologist Hermann Hasse. Very common on urban trees throughout LA.

fm.Form: Foliose, small, with hooded lobe tips
fm.Habitat: City trees, oak bark — one of the most common bark lichens in LA
fm.vs: Powder Sunburst (X. fallax) has powdery soredia; Hooded Sunburst has hooded lobe tips without soredia

lichens.hp: Orange foliose with hood-shaped lobe tips. Named for pioneering LA lichenologist Hasse. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Black crustose lichen on limestone, concrete, and mortar — probably the most overlooked lichen in LA because it looks like a dark stain. Tiny pimple-like perithecia.

fm.Form: Crustose — thin black crust, often cracked
fm.Surface: Tiny raised dots (perithecia) — look like pimples
fm.Substrate: Limestone, concrete, mortar — very common on urban surfaces
fm.Habitat: Sidewalks, walls, bridges — everywhere in urban LA
fm.vs: Looks like a dark stain, not a lichen. Black shield lichens (Buellia) have disc-shaped apothecia instead of pimple-like perithecia.

lichens.hp: Black crustose lichen on limestone and concrete. Endolithic — penetrates rock surface. Extremely common on urban structures.

lichens.desc: Aquatic lichen forming black patches on rocks in streams and seeps — one of the few truly aquatic lichens. Often mistaken for wet rock discoloration.

fm.Form: Crustose — thin black film on submerged or splash-zone rock
fm.Habitat: Permanently wet rock — streams, waterfalls, seeps
fm.vs: Looks like wet rock discoloration. Distinguished by tiny raised perithecia visible with hand lens.

lichens.hp: Black crustose on periodically submerged stream rocks. Semi-aquatic lifestyle unusual for lichens.

lichens.desc: Bright yellow powdery crust on bark in shaded locations — looks like someone sprinkled gold dust on the tree. No organized thallus structure.

fm.Form: Leprose — pure powder, no organized thallus
fm.vs: Not a true lichen by some definitions (no organized thallus). Distinguished from Candelariella by purely powdery form with no crust beneath.

lichens.hp: Gold dust lichen — entirely powdery (leprose), never forming lobes. Resembles yellow spray paint on bark. Provides microhabitat for minute bark invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Tiny bright egg-yolk yellow crustose lichen on rock, concrete, and bark. Extremely common but easily overlooked due to small size. Often the first lichen to colonize bare rock.

fm.Substrate: Rock, concrete, bark, wood — very versatile
fm.Habitat: Everywhere — one of the most common urban lichens
fm.vs: Smaller and more granular than Sunburst Lichen (Xanthoria). Gold Dust (Chrysothrix) is purely powdery; Egg-yolk has a granular crust.

lichens.hp: Brilliant egg-yolk yellow on nitrogen-enriched rocks — diagnostic indicator of bird perching sites. Provides microhabitat for rock-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with prominent black button-like apothecia on bark — one of the most common gray crustose lichens on trees in LA.

fm.Form: Crustose — gray thallus with black disc apothecia
fm.vs: Distinguished from Lecidella by bark substrate and larger apothecia. Hooded Rosette (Physcia) is foliose, not crustose.

lichens.hp: Button lichen — tiny black apothecia on gray-green crustose thallus. Common but requires hand lens. Provides microhabitat for minute bark-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Thin gray crust with tiny black discs — perhaps the single most common crustose lichen on bark in temperate regions. Usually identified only to genus in the field.

fm.Color: Gray with tiny black disc apothecia
fm.Habitat: Any bark surface — one of the most common bark crustose lichens worldwide
fm.vs: Very similar to B. griseovirens; distinguished mainly by thallus chemistry (field ID to genus is usually sufficient)

lichens.hp: Tiny black-dotted crustose on bark and wood. Important early colonizer of exposed wood surfaces. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with olive-brown to black discs — extremely common on bark. Usually the most abundant crustose lichen on deciduous trees.

fm.Color: Gray crust with olive-brown to black disc apothecia
fm.vs: Rim Lichen (Lecanora muralis) has rimmed apothecia; Lecidella has flat discs without a rim

lichens.hp: Common epiphyte. Important component of bark lichen communities. Pioneer rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Small dark olive-green foliose lichen forming neat rosettes on bark and rock — very common in urban environments. Tolerates high nitrogen from vehicle emissions.

fm.Color: Dark olive-green to brown when dry, greener when wet
fm.vs: Hooded Rosette (Physcia adscendens) is gray-white; Shadow Lichen is distinctly darker olive-green

lichens.hp: Dark gray-brown foliose forming neat orbicular rosettes. Extremely common on urban trees. Very pollution-tolerant.

lichens.desc: Tiny dark brown foliose lichen tightly pressed to bark — one of the most common but most overlooked urban bark lichens. So small and flat it looks crustose.

fm.Form: Foliose but very tightly appressed — appears almost crustose
fm.Habitat: City trees — very common, very overlooked
fm.vs: Appears crustose but has tiny lobe tips visible with hand lens. Shadow Lichen (Phaeophyscia) is similar color but larger and more obviously foliose.

lichens.hp: Tightly appressed dark foliose. Among the most pollution-tolerant foliose lichens. Thrives on urban trees.

lichens.desc: Medium brown foliose lichen with tiny isidia on the surface — common on oaks. Distinguished from similar species by brown color and granular isidia.

fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — common in SGM and SMM canyons
fm.vs: Shield Lichen (Parmelia sulcata) is gray with ridges; Brown-eyed Sunshine is distinctly brown with isidia

lichens.hp: Brown-eyed sunshine lichen — foliose with distinctive isidia. Distinguished from M. californica by chemical tests. Bark specialist providing invertebrate microhabitat.

lichens.desc: White-fringed strap-like foliose lichen on trees in fog-belt habitats — dramatic ciliate lobe margins. A coastal fog indicator species.

fm.Form: Foliose, strap-like lobes with dramatic white cilia on margins
fm.Habitat: Coastal fog-belt — indicator of persistent fog
fm.vs: Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) is pendant; Fringed Strap is appressed with fringed margins

lichens.hp: Tropical lichen at northern range edge in LA County. Important biogeographic indicator. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates on mature tree canopy.

lichens.desc: White to chalky gray crustose lichen on limestone and concrete — extremely common on LA sidewalks and retaining walls. White rimmed apothecia.

fm.Form: Crustose — thick chalky white to gray
fm.Habitat: Urban sidewalks, retaining walls — very common on calcareous surfaces
fm.vs: Rim Lichen (Lecanora muralis) has lobed margins; Chalk-rim is more uniformly crustose without lobes

lichens.hp: Important pioneer on limestone. Extremely long-lived. Pioneer rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Gray crustose lichen with sunken dimple-like apothecia — one of the most common gray crustose lichens on siliceous rock worldwide.

fm.vs: Chalk-rim (A. calcarea) prefers calcareous rock and is whiter; Rock Dimple prefers siliceous rock and is grayer

lichens.hp: Common crustose on siliceous rocks. Pioneer colonizer slowly weathering rock into soil. Provides microhabitat for rock-dwelling mites. Used in lichenometric dating of rock surfaces.

lichens.desc: Gray-white foliose lichen with black peppery soredia — extremely common on urban trees in warm areas. A nitrogen-lover indicative of vehicle exhaust.

fm.Habitat: City trees near roads — nitrogen pollution indicator
fm.vs: Similar to Physcia but has characteristic dark/peppery soredia vs the white-tipped soredia of Physcia species

lichens.hp: Tropical to warm-temperate bark specialist at range edge in LA County. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates. Biogeographic indicator.

lichens.desc: Thin gray crustose lichen with dark-rimmed apothecia on bark — very common but requires a hand lens to appreciate. Often overlooked.

fm.Color: Gray with brown-black apothecia with pale rim
fm.vs: Similar to Buellia but apothecia have a pale rim (lecanorine). Buellia has unrimmed (lecideine) apothecia.

lichens.hp: Tiny crustose with brown-ringed apothecia. Common but requires hand lens.

lichens.desc: Native Stonewall Rim Lichen (Lecanoraceae) — Common rock lichen with distinctive concentric rings.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Common rock lichen with distinctive concentric rings. Important in urban ecology — among the most pollution-tolerant lichens. Pioneer species on concrete and mortar. Pioneer rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Non-native Golden Hair-lichen (Teloschistaceae). Golden hair lichen — bright orange-yellow filamentous tufts on coastal shrubs.
lichens.hp: Indicator of clean coastal air. Becoming rarer due to air quality decline. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Brown-eyed Wolf Lichen (Parmeliaceae). similar to L.
lichens.hp: Vulpina but with brown apothecia instead of yellow. Contains vulpinic acid.

lichens.desc: Green Rock-posy (Lecanoraceae). Rock-posy lichen forming distinctive green-yellow rosettes with dark centers on exposed alpine rocks.
lichens.hp: Rock-posy lichen forming distinctive green-yellow rosettes with dark centers on exposed alpine rocks. Important pioneer on bare rock surfaces. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Farinose Cartilage Lichen (Ramalinaceae). Cartilage lichen with sorediate (powdery-surfaced) straps on tree bark.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Nitrogen cycling contributor. Sensitive to air pollution — indicator of air quality. Important epiphyte. Provides microhabitat for native insects on tree branches.

lichens.desc: Native Lesser Salted Rocktripe Lichen (Umbilicariaceae) — Rock tripe lichen anchored by single central holdfast.
lichens.hp: Rock tripe lichen anchored by single central holdfast. Edible in emergencies. Extremely slow-growing — large specimens may be centuries old. Rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates on boulder surfaces.

lichens.desc: Native Elegant Sunburst Lichen (Teloschistaceae) — Brilliant orange lichen on nitrogen-enriched rocks — especially bird perching stones.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Brilliant orange lichen on nitrogen-enriched rocks — especially bird perching stones. Color from anthraquinone pigments provides UV protection. Cosmopolitan distribution. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Native gold cobblestone lichen (Acarosporaceae) — Bright chartreuse-yellow cobblestone lichen on exposed acidic rocks.
lichens.hp: Bright chartreuse-yellow cobblestone lichen on exposed acidic rocks. Forms distinctive small lobes. UV-protective pigments.

lichens.desc: Pin-cushion Sunburst Lichen (Teloschistaceae). Pin-cushion sunburst lichen forming small orange-yellow cushions on bark and rock.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Pin-cushion sunburst — common on urban trees. Tolerates moderate air pollution. Nitrogen indicator. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Non-native White Pleated Lichen (Caliciaceae). white pleated lichen forming distinctive white crustose patches on bark and concrete.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: White pleated lichen common near agricultural areas. Nitrogen-loving — indicator of atmospheric nitrogen enrichment. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Soil Paint Lichen (Acarosporaceae). soil paint lichen forming bright yellow-green crusts on desert rocks.
lichens.hp: Soil paint lichen forming bright yellow-green crusts on desert rocks. Among the most conspicuous desert rock lichens. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Sierra Rim Lichen (Lecanoraceae). crustose lichen on mountain rocks.
lichens.hp: High-elevation specialist. Related to the common P. muralis.

lichens.desc: Mealy Pixie Cup (Cladoniaceae). goblet-shaped podetia on forest floor and rotting wood.
lichens.hp: Goblet-shaped podetia on forest floor and rotting wood. Indicator of intact forest understory ecology. Forest floor habitat for native invertebrates. Important soil crust component.

lichens.desc: Beaded Rosette Lichen (Physciaceae). gray-green foliose lichen on bark.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Beaded rosette lichen — diagnostic beaded margins on narrow lobes. Moderately pollution-tolerant. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Waxy Firedot Lichen (Teloschistaceae). bright orange crustose lichen on coastal rocks.
lichens.hp: Bright orange crustose lichen on coastal rocks. UV-protective anthraquinone pigments.

lichens.desc: Red Firedot Lichen (Polycauliona) (Teloschistaceae). Red firedot lichen — bright orange-red crustose lichen on rock.
lichens.hp: Red firedot lichen — bright orange-red crustose lichen on rock. UV-protective anthraquinone pigments. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Orange Atoms (Teloschistaceae). tiny orange squamules on rock.
lichens.hp: Among the smallest visible lichens — orange squamules on alkaline rock. Provides microhabitat for minute invertebrates. Pioneer colonizer of fresh rock surfaces.

lichens.desc: Frosted Rim-lichen (Lecanoraceae). gray-blue crustose lichen on bark.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Frosted rim-lichen on urban tree bark. Moderately pollution-tolerant. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates. Important component of urban lichen communities.

lichens.desc: Non-native Bushy Fog Lichen (Ramalinaceae). Fruticose fog lichen of coastal habitats.
lichens.hp: Fruticose fog lichen of coastal habitats. Depends on marine fog moisture. Important epiphyte. Provides microhabitat for native insects on tree branches.

lichens.desc: Hoary cobblestone lichen (Acarosporaceae). Brown-gray crustose lichen on exposed rocks.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Brown-gray crustose lichen on exposed rocks. Pioneer species on bare rock surfaces. Desert soil crust pioneer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Mortar Rim Lichen (Lecanoraceae). crustose lichen colonizing mortar, concrete, and limestone.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Crustose lichen colonizing mortar, concrete, and limestone. Common on urban structures. Pioneer rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Rock Nipple Lichen (Caliciaceae). tiny dark fruiting bodies on rock.
lichens.hp: Rock Nipple Lichen contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources. Bark and wood colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Powdery Sea-Fog Lichen (Ramalinaceae). coastal fog-dependent.
lichens.hp: Important for coastal nutrient cycling. Important epiphyte. Provides microhabitat for native insects on tree branches.

lichens.desc: Rosette Cobblestone Lichen (Acarosporaceae). — yellow-green rosette-forming cobblestone lichen on rock.
lichens.hp: Rosette cobblestone lichen — yellow-green rosettes on sun-exposed rock. Pioneer colonizer. Provides microhabitat for rock-dwelling mites and springtails.

lichens.desc: Non-native Radiate Lichen (Caliciaceae). — radiating crustose lichen on rock.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: — radiating crustose lichen on rock. Rock colonizer. Bark and wood colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Non-native Flame Firedot Lichen (Teloschistaceae). bright orange crustose on rock.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: Flame firedot — brilliant orange-red crustose spreading on urban substrates. UV-protective parietin pigment. Pioneer colonizer of concrete and mortar.

lichens.desc: Parasitic Firedot Lichen (Teloschistaceae). grows parasitically ON other lichens.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Grows parasitically ON other lichens. Fascinating biology — one of few parasitic lichens. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Black Sheet Lichen (Parmeliaceae). large foliose lichen with distinctive netted upper surface.
lichens.hp: Black sheet lichen — large foliose with distinctive netted (reticulate) upper surface. Important old-growth indicator. Provides nesting material for hummingbirds.

lichens.desc: Non-native Inelegant Strap Lichen (Ramalinaceae). pendant lichen on coastal trees.
lichens.hp: Pendant lichen on coastal trees. Canary Islands origin. Fog-dependent.

lichens.desc: Non-native Honey Rim Lichen (Lecanoraceae). — honey-colored rim lichen on bark.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: Honey Rim Lichen contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources.

lichens.desc: Yellow-edged Frost Lichen (Physciaceae). gray foliose with yellow medulla visible at margins.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Yellow-edged Frost Lichen contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources.

lichens.desc: Non-native Acarospora robiniae (Acarosporaceae). — cobblestone lichen possibly spreading on urban substrates.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: — cobblestone lichen possibly spreading on urban substrates.

lichens.desc: Non-native Stalked Firedot Lichen (Teloschistaceae). — stalked yellow crustose lichen.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: Rock and soil specialist. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Coral Firedot Lichen (Teloschistaceae). orange coral-like growth form.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Rock specialist. Unusual branching morphology for Teloschistaceae.

lichens.desc: Frosted Grain-spored Lichen (Acarosporaceae). black crustose lichen with frosted surface.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Black crustose lichen with frosted surface. Rock colonizer. Desert soil crust pioneer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Non-native Bouquet Sea-Fog Lichen (Ramalinaceae). branching fruticose fog lichen.
lichens.hp: Fog-dependent fruticose lichen of coastal headlands. Captures atmospheric moisture — important water source for bark invertebrates. Indicator of healthy fog-belt microclimate.

lichens.desc: Sulphured Crimson Dot Lichen (Lecanoraceae). tiny red-orange fruiting bodies on bark.
lichens.hp: Oak bark specialist with distinctive red-orange apothecia. Important bioindicator — presence indicates stable old-growth oak canopy. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling mites and springtails.

lichens.desc: Cowpie Lichen (Thelotremataceae). crustose lichen growing over mosses and soil.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Crustose lichen growing over mosses and soil. Important soil crust component. Bark colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Bare-bottomed Sunburst Lichen (Teloschistaceae). orange foliose lichen lacking rhizines on lower surface (hence 'bare-bottomed').

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Orange foliose lichen lacking rhizines on lower surface (hence 'bare-bottomed').

lichens.desc: Rock Disk Lichen (Lecanoraceae). dark crustose on rock and concrete.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Dark crustose on rock and concrete. Common but overlooked. Urban colonizer. Pioneer rock colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Granite-speck Rim Lichen (Lecanoraceae). yellow-green crustose on granitic rock.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Important pioneer on bare rock. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Brown Tile Lichen (Lecideaceae). dark brown crustose on exposed rocks.
lichens.hp: Dark brown crustose on exposed mountain rocks. Pioneer colonizer at high elevation. Provides microhabitat for alpine invertebrates. Extremely long-lived — centuries old.

lichens.desc: Western Pixie-cup (Cladoniaceae). goblet-shaped podetia.
lichens.hp: Western pixie-cup lichen of moist forest floors. Related to British Soldier lichen. Provides microhabitat for forest floor invertebrates. Indicator of intact forest.

lichens.desc: Non-native Firm Cup Lichen (Cladoniaceae). robust pixie-cup.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: Robust pixie-cup. Soil and rotting wood colonizer. Forest floor habitat for native invertebrates. Important soil crust component.

lichens.desc: Flat Disk Lichen (Lecideaceae). — dark crustose lichen on rock.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Flat disc lichen — dark crustose on exposed rock surfaces. Pioneer colonizer contributing to rock weathering and soil formation. Provides microhabitat for rock-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Frosted Rosette Lichen (Physciaceae). gray foliose with frosted tips.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil

lichens.hp: Moderately pollution-tolerant. Frosted Rosette Lichen contributes to habitat structure and pollinator resources.

lichens.desc: Non-native Santesson's Rock Lichen (Caliciaceae). — minute dark lichen on rock and wood.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: — minute dark lichen on rock and wood. Named for Swedish lichenologist Santesson.

lichens.desc: Elegant Camouflage Lichen (Parmeliaceae). brown foliose with isidia.
lichens.hp: Bark specialist with distinctive isidia. Air quality bioindicator — populations track changes in atmospheric nitrogen deposition. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Non-native Dark Aspicilia (Lecanoraceae). — dark crustose lichen on rock.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: — dark crustose lichen on rock. Rock surface colonizer. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Non-native Santa Monica Leprocaulon (Leprocaulaceae). — described from Santa Monica Mountains.

fm.Habitat: Mountain slopes and meadows; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: — described from Santa Monica Mountains. Leprose crustose on soil and rock. Taxonomically interesting.

lichens.desc: Non-native California Camouflage Lichen (Parmeliaceae). brown foliose on bark.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: California camouflage lichen — brown foliose blending with bark. Important air quality bioindicator. Provides microhabitat for bark-dwelling invertebrates.

lichens.desc: Non-native Deceptive Firedot Lichen (Teloschistaceae). — orange firedot lichen.

fm.Habitat: Rock surfaces, tree bark, and soil; naturalized from cultivation

lichens.hp: — orange firedot lichen. Rock specialist. Spreading on urban substrates. Nitrogen-enriched habitat indicator. Provides microhabitat for native insects.

lichens.desc: Wolf Lichen (Brown) (Parmeliaceae). — related to wolf lichen but with different chemistry.
lichens.hp: — related to wolf lichen but with different chemistry. Contains vulpinic acid.

lichens.desc: Non-native Isidiate Fog Lichen (Ramalinaceae). — isidiate fog lichen.
lichens.hp: — isidiate fog lichen. Coastal specialist dependent on marine fog.
